Email 1 delivers extra value beyond the magnet, email 2 tells a relevant story, email 3 teaches something that reveals why my offer matters, email 4 shares proof: [PROOF POINT], email 5 makes a soft first offer. Space them over [DAYS]. Each builds trust and moves them closer without pressure. Tone [TONE].
description: Nurture new leads from a lead magnet toward a first purchase over several emails.
useCase: Use this to warm up leads who grabbed a freebie but are not ready to buy. A nurture sequence builds the trust and belief a cold lead lacks, so by the soft offer they see your paid solution as the obvious next step rather than a pitch.

title: Re Engage a Prospect Who Went Quiet
promptText: A warm prospect went quiet after [WHAT HAPPENED, E.G. A GOOD CALL OR REQUESTING A PROPOSAL]. It has been [TIME SINCE]. Write 2 re engagement options. Option 1 is a light, low pressure check in that assumes good timing issues, not disinterest. Option 2 is a pattern interrupt: a short, slightly unexpected message that breaks the silence, such as a relevant resource or a simple are you still interested or should I close your file question. Both under 80 words. My offer: [YOUR OFFER].
description: Break the silence with a prospect who went quiet after showing interest.
useCase: Use this when an interested prospect stops replying, which happens constantly. A well judged re engagement that removes pressure or gently forces a decision often revives deals that would otherwise quietly die in the pipeline.
variables: WHAT HAPPENED, E.G. A GOOD CALL OR REQUESTING A PROPOSAL

title: Write a Gentle Decision Nudge Email
promptText: A prospect is interested but stuck in indecision about [YOUR OFFER]. Their situation: [WHAT IS HOLDING THEM BACK]. Write an email that gently helps them decide without pressure. Acknowledge that the decision matters, reframe the cost of staying stuck versus acting, offer to remove whatever friction is in the way, and give a clear simple next step. Under 110 words. It should feel like help making a decision, not a push to buy. Give me one warmer version and one more direct version.
description: Help a stuck prospect make a decision without applying sales pressure.
useCase: Use this when a prospect keeps stalling despite clear interest. Reframing the cost of inaction and removing friction helps them past the indecision that traps warm leads, without the pressure that would make them retreat.

title: Write a Free Trial to Paid Conversion Sequence
promptText: Write an email sequence to convert free trial or free tier users of [YOUR PRODUCT] to paid. The trial lasts [TRIAL LENGTH], the key value they should experience is [KEY VALUE], and the main reason people do not convert is [MAIN DROP OFF REASON]. Email 1 early in the trial drives them to the aha moment, email 2 midway shares a use case or tip, email 3 near the end shows what they lose by not upgrading, email 4 makes the upgrade ask with any incentive: [INCENTIVE OR WRITE NONE]. Keep each focused on their success, not my revenue.
description: Convert free users to paid with a sequence built around their product success.
useCase: Use this for any free trial or freemium offer. Trials fail to convert when users never hit the aha moment, and a sequence that drives them to real value first, then makes the upgrade ask, converts far better than a countdown of pushy reminders.

title: Follow Up on an Abandoned Inquiry
promptText: Someone inquired about [YOUR OFFER] through [CHANNEL, E.G. A CONTACT FORM OR DM] then went quiet before I could close. Their original question or interest was: [WHAT THEY ASKED]. Write a 2 message follow up that reconnects while their interest may still be warm. Message 1 answers their original question fully and helpfully, message 2 a few days later offers the clear next step. Keep it helpful and low pressure so it feels like good service, not chasing. Under 90 words each.
description: Recover an inquiry that went cold before you could respond or close.
useCase: Use this for form fills, DMs, or inquiries that stalled before a sale. These leads raised their hand once, so a helpful follow up that answers their question and offers a next step recovers revenue that would otherwise be lost to slow or missed responses.
variables: CHANNEL, E.G. A CONTACT FORM OR DM

title: Write a Post Webinar Follow Up Sequence
promptText: Write a follow up sequence for attendees of my webinar or workshop on [WEBINAR TOPIC] where I offered [YOUR OFFER]. Email 1, same day, thanks them, links the replay: [REPLAY LINK], and recaps the key takeaway. Email 2 addresses the top question or objection that came up: [TOP OBJECTION]. Email 3 shares a success story or proof: [PROOF POINT]. Email 4 is the final call for the offer with the deadline: [DEADLINE]. Include a separate short version for people who registered but did not attend. Tone [TONE].
description: Convert webinar attendees with a follow up sequence tied to what you taught.
useCase: Use this after any webinar, workshop, or live training with an offer. Most webinar sales happen in the follow up, not live, and a sequence that recaps value then handles objections captures the attendees who were interested but did not buy in the moment.

title: Follow Up After a Specific Objection
promptText: A prospect raised this specific objection to [YOUR OFFER]: [THE OBJECTION]. I did not fully resolve it in the moment. Write a follow up email that addresses it thoughtfully after the fact. Validate the concern as reasonable, then answer it with logic, evidence, a story, or a reframe: [RELEVANT PROOF OR ANGLE]. End by checking whether that addresses it and offering the next step. Under 130 words. It should feel like I listened and came back with a real answer, not a rebuttal.
description: Send a considered follow up that resolves a specific objection raised earlier.
useCase: Use this after a conversation where an objection came up that you did not fully handle live. A thoughtful written follow up gives you the space to address the concern properly, and coming back with a real answer signals you listened, which itself builds trust.

title: Build a Long Term Nurture for Not Yet Ready Leads
promptText: Design a long term nurture approach for leads who are a good fit for [YOUR OFFER] but genuinely not ready to buy for months. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the eventual trigger that makes them ready: [READINESS TRIGGER]. Give me: a low frequency cadence that keeps me present without annoying them, the types of value to send between now and readiness, how to stay top of mind so I am the obvious choice when they are ready, and the signal to watch for to know when to re engage actively. Outline the first 4 touches.
description: Keep good fit but not ready leads warm until they are ready to buy.
useCase: Use this for qualified leads on a long buying timeline. Most solo sellers drop these leads and lose them to whoever stays present, and a light long term nurture makes you the obvious choice when their readiness trigger finally arrives.

title: Write a New Customer Activation Sequence
promptText: Write an onboarding sequence for new customers of [YOUR OFFER] to get them to their first win fast and reduce refunds or churn. The first meaningful result they should reach is [FIRST WIN], and the most common early stumbling block is [COMMON STUMBLE]. Email 1 welcomes and sets them up for the quickest first step, email 2 helps them past the common stumble, email 3 celebrates or checks on their first win and points to what is next. Keep it about their success. Tone [TONE].
description: Activate new customers fast with an onboarding sequence built around their first win.
useCase: Use this for every new customer to reduce churn and refunds. Customers who reach a first win early stay and buy again, and a deliberate activation sequence that removes early friction protects the revenue you already earned.

title: Write a Post Purchase Upsell Sequence
promptText: Write a follow up sequence to upsell or cross sell existing customers who bought [INITIAL PURCHASE]. The natural next offer is [UPSELL OFFER], which helps them because [WHY IT HELPS THEM NEXT]. Email 1 makes sure they are getting value from the first purchase, email 2 surfaces the next problem they will hit that the upsell solves, email 3 presents the upsell as the logical next step with any customer incentive: [INCENTIVE OR WRITE NONE]. Time it for after they have seen value, not immediately. Under 130 words each.
description: Upsell existing customers with a sequence timed to their success, not the sale.
useCase: Use this after customers have seen value from a first purchase. Existing customers are the easiest sale you have, and an upsell framed as the logical next step to solve their next problem converts far better than an immediate pitch after buying.

title: Write a Customer Check In Email
promptText: Write a genuine check in email to an existing customer of [YOUR OFFER] who has been with me for [TIME AS CUSTOMER]. The goal is retention and relationship, not an immediate sale. Ask how things are going in a specific way tied to what they bought, offer help or a resource, and open the door to feedback. It should feel like I actually care about their outcome, because I do, not like a disguised sales email. Under 90 words. Give me one version for a happy customer and one for a quiet one I have not heard from.
description: Strengthen retention with a genuine customer check in that is not a disguised pitch.
useCase: Use this periodically with existing customers to protect retention. A sincere check in that offers help catches problems before they cause churn and deepens the relationship, which is what earns renewals, referrals, and future upsells.

title: Write a Referral Request Sequence
promptText: Write a short sequence asking happy customers of [YOUR OFFER] for referrals. Trigger it after [POSITIVE MOMENT, E.G. A GOOD RESULT OR PRAISE]. Message 1 acknowledges their result and asks if they know one person who has the same problem I solved for them, making the ask specific and easy. Message 2 provides a ready to forward blurb they can send with no effort, plus any referral incentive: [INCENTIVE OR WRITE NONE]. Keep it warm and low pressure. The best time to ask is right after they have experienced value.
description: Turn happy customers into referrals with a well timed, easy to act on ask.
useCase: Use this right after a customer gets a result or praises your work, the peak moment for a referral ask. A specific request plus a ready to forward blurb removes the effort that stops most happy customers from actually referring you.
variables: POSITIVE MOMENT, E.G. A GOOD RESULT OR PRAISE

title: Write a Testimonial Request Sequence
promptText: Write a sequence to collect a testimonial from a satisfied customer of [YOUR OFFER] who achieved [THEIR RESULT]. Message 1 asks if they would be open to sharing their experience, framing it as quick and easy. Message 2, once they agree, provides simple guiding questions that draw out a specific story driven testimonial (before, turning point, after) rather than a vague nice job. Make it effortless for them to give a testimonial I can actually use. Keep the tone appreciative, not transactional. Under 100 words each.
description: Collect specific, usable testimonials with a sequence that makes it easy to say yes.
useCase: Use this to systematically gather the social proof your marketing needs. Most customers will give a testimonial if asked well, and guiding them with story based questions produces specific, persuasive testimonials instead of the generic praise that convinces no one.

title: Re Engage a List of Old Leads
promptText: I have a list of old leads who once showed interest in [YOUR OFFER] but never bought, going back [HOW OLD]. Write a re engagement sequence to revive them. Email 1 reconnects with a fresh reason: [WHAT IS NEW, E.G. NEW OFFER, RESULT, OR APPROACH]. Email 2 delivers value and reminds them of the problem I solve. Email 3 makes a clear invitation to talk or buy, with a simple way to opt out if they are truly not interested. Warm, not guilt driven. Tone [TONE].
description: Revive a list of cold old leads with a fresh reason to re engage.
useCase: Use this on the dormant leads sitting in your list or CRM. These people already expressed interest once, so a re engagement sequence with a fresh angle is one of the cheapest ways to generate sales, mining value from leads you already paid to acquire.
variables: WHAT IS NEW, E.G. NEW OFFER, RESULT, OR APPROACH

title: Plan the Timing and Cadence of a Follow Up
promptText: Help me plan the timing for following up with [PROSPECT TYPE] after [STARTING EVENT, E.G. A CALL, A PROPOSAL, OR AN INQUIRY]. My sales cycle is typically [SALES CYCLE LENGTH] and the offer is [YOUR OFFER]. Recommend: how soon to send the first follow up, how many follow ups before I stop, how many days between each, and how the message purpose should shift across the sequence. Explain the reasoning so I understand the balance between staying persistent and becoming annoying. Give me a simple schedule I can reuse.
description: Plan how soon, how often, and how many times to follow up without overdoing it.
useCase: Use this when you are unsure how persistent to be. Following up too little loses deals and too much burns goodwill, and a deliberate cadence tuned to your sales cycle gets the timing right so no prospect is dropped early or chased into annoyance.
variables: STARTING EVENT, E.G. A CALL, A PROPOSAL, OR AN INQUIRY

title: Build a Reusable Follow Up Template Bank
promptText: Build me a bank of reusable follow up message templates for common situations in my sales process for [YOUR OFFER] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Create short templates for: the first check in after a call, the second no response bump, the after proposal nudge, the gone quiet revival, the value add touch, and the graceful breakup. For each, give a fill in the blank template with placeholders so I can personalize fast. Keep them human and non pushy. These should save me from writing every follow up from scratch.
description: Create a bank of reusable, personalizable follow up templates for common situations.
useCase: Use this once to stop writing every follow up from scratch. A template bank for your recurring sales situations makes follow up fast and consistent, which means you actually do it, and consistent follow up is where most solo sales revenue is won or lost.

title: Plan a Multi Channel Follow Up
promptText: Help me follow up with a warm prospect across more than one channel without being overwhelming. I can reach them via [CHANNELS, E.G. EMAIL, LINKEDIN, TEXT]. My offer: [YOUR OFFER], where things stand: [CURRENT STATUS]. Design a coordinated multi channel follow up: which channel to use for which touch, how to vary the message per channel so it does not feel like the same thing three times, and how to space it so it reads as attentive rather than stalking. Give me the sequence with the channel and purpose for each touch.
description: Coordinate follow ups across email, LinkedIn, and text without smothering the prospect.
useCase: Use this for high value prospects reachable on several channels. A coordinated multi channel approach increases the odds of a response, but only when the touches are varied and spaced deliberately, otherwise it reads as pestering rather than diligence.

title: Write a Value Add Follow Up Touch
promptText: I want to follow up with a prospect considering [YOUR OFFER] without just asking if they have decided. Write a value add follow up that gives them something useful: an insight, resource, article, or tip relevant to [THEIR SITUATION OR GOAL], with no ask attached beyond staying in touch. It should keep me top of mind and demonstrate my expertise while they decide. Under 90 words. Give me 3 different value add angles I can rotate so my follow ups never feel like nagging.
description: Follow up by giving value instead of asking if they have decided yet.
useCase: Use this between direct follow ups to stay present without nagging. Value add touches keep you top of mind and demonstrate expertise while a prospect deliberates, which builds trust and makes the eventual ask land better than a string of did you decide emails.

title: Adjust the Tone of a Follow Up Message
promptText: Here is my follow up message: [PASTE MESSAGE]. It does not feel right. Rewrite it in 3 tones so I can pick the right fit for this prospect: one warm and relationship led, one confident and direct, one light and casual. For each, keep the core purpose intact but shift the energy. Then tell me which tone suits which kind of prospect or situation. My relationship with them is [RELATIONSHIP CONTEXT] and my offer is [YOUR OFFER].
description: Rewrite a follow up in warm, direct, and casual tones to match the prospect.
useCase: Use this when a follow up feels off but you cannot say why. Seeing the same message in three tones lets you match the energy to the specific prospect and relationship, since the right words in the wrong tone can stall a deal that better calibration would advance.

title: Diagnose Why a Prospect Is Not Responding
promptText: A prospect has stopped responding about [YOUR OFFER]. Here is the full context of our interactions so far: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE HISTORY]. Help me diagnose the likely reason for the silence: lost interest, price, competing priority, needs internal buy in, bad timing, or something I did. Based on the most likely cause, recommend the specific follow up move that fits that reason, since the right message depends on why they went quiet. Give me the diagnosis, the reasoning, and the recommended next message.
description: Diagnose the likely reason for prospect silence and match the right follow up to it.
useCase: Use this when a prospect goes dark and you do not know why. The right follow up depends entirely on the cause of the silence, and diagnosing whether it is price, timing, or lost interest before you respond stops you from sending a message that misreads the situation.

title: Write the Final Follow Up That Forces a Decision
promptText: Write a final follow up to a prospect I have pursued for [YOUR OFFER] across several touches with no clear yes or no. It is time to get a decision either way. The email should respectfully signal this is my last follow up, make it easy to say yes with a clear next step, make it just as easy to say not now without awkwardness, and remove any lingering pressure. The goal is a clean answer so I can either move forward or move on. Under 90 words. Give me one firm version and one gentle version.
description: Write the respectful final follow up that forces a clean yes or no.
useCase: Use this to close out a prospect who has gone unanswered across multiple follow ups. A clean final message that makes both yes and no easy gets you a decision so you can stop spending energy on a dead lead or capture one that just needed the nudge.

title: Handle the Price Objection
promptText: A prospect says [YOUR OFFER] priced at [PRICE] is too expensive. Their situation: [THEIR CONTEXT]. Write 3 ways to respond that reframe price around value rather than defending or discounting. Response 1 anchors the price against the cost of their problem staying unsolved, response 2 breaks the price into the outcome per unit of time or result, response 3 gently uncovers whether it is a budget issue or a value perception issue. Keep each conversational and non defensive. End each with a question that moves the conversation forward.
description: Respond to the too expensive objection by reframing price around value.
useCase: Use this when a prospect balks at price. The instinct to discount or defend loses either margin or the deal, and reframing around the cost of inaction or breaking price into outcome terms keeps the conversation on value where you can actually win it.

title: Handle the No Budget Objection
promptText: A prospect says they do not have the budget for [YOUR OFFER] right now. I believe they genuinely need it and it solves [PROBLEM]. Write responses that respect the budget reality without immediately discounting. Include: a way to uncover whether budget is the real issue or a polite no, a reframe of the offer as an investment that pays for itself through [ROI OR PAYBACK], an option to explore a smaller starting scope, and a graceful way to stay in touch if the timing is truly wrong. Keep it collaborative, not pushy.
description: Address a genuine budget objection without reflexively discounting.
useCase: Use this when a prospect cites budget. Sometimes budget is real and sometimes it is a soft no, and these responses help you tell the difference while offering paths forward that protect your pricing and keep good fit prospects warm.

title: Build the Value Justification for Your Price
promptText: Help me build a clear value justification for [YOUR OFFER] priced at [PRICE]. It helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] achieve [OUTCOME], and the cost of them not solving this problem is [COST OF INACTION]. Give me: the core value narrative that makes the price feel obviously worth it, 3 concrete ways to quantify the return, the comparison points that make my price look reasonable, and how to present all of this without sounding like I am justifying or apologizing for the price. I want to walk into pricing conversations confident.
description: Construct the value case that makes your price feel obviously worth it.
useCase: Use this before pricing conversations, not during them. Having a rehearsed value justification means you present your price with confidence rather than flinching, and prospects take their cue on whether a price is reasonable largely from how you carry it.

title: Handle I Need to Think About It
promptText: A prospect says they need to think about [YOUR OFFER]. This often hides a real unspoken concern. Write responses that uncover what is actually holding them back without pressuring them. Include: a warm way to give them space while surfacing the real hesitation, 2 or 3 gentle questions that reveal whether it is price, trust, timing, or a specific doubt, and a way to make it easy for them to voice the true objection. Then give me a follow up message to send if they leave without resolving it. Keep it low pressure and genuinely curious.
description: Uncover the real concern hiding behind I need to think about it.
useCase: Use this when a prospect stalls with I need to think about it, which almost always masks a specific unspoken objection. Gently surfacing the real concern lets you address it, whereas letting them leave to think usually means letting the deal quietly die.

title: Handle the Timing Objection
promptText: A prospect says now is not the right time for [YOUR OFFER]. Write responses that test whether timing is real or an excuse, without dismissing their situation. Include: a way to explore what specifically makes now wrong and when would be right, a reframe of the cost of waiting given their problem [PROBLEM], and a genuine path to stay connected if the timing truly is off: [FUTURE FOLLOW UP APPROACH]. If waiting genuinely serves them better, I want to acknowledge that honestly rather than pressure a bad fit. Keep it consultative.
description: Test whether a timing objection is real and respond to each case honestly.
useCase: Use this for the not right now objection. Timing is sometimes genuine and sometimes a polite deflection, and these responses help you distinguish the two so you either address the real hesitation or set up an honest future follow up rather than forcing a bad fit sale.

title: Handle Already Have a Solution
promptText: A prospect says they already have a solution or provider for what [YOUR OFFER] does. My genuine advantages over the typical alternative: [YOUR ADVANTAGES], and the common frustrations people have with existing solutions: [COMMON FRUSTRATIONS]. Write responses that respect their current choice while opening the door. Include: questions that surface whether their current solution is actually working well, a way to plant doubt without trashing the competitor, and a low commitment way to demonstrate the difference. Never disparage their existing provider directly. Keep it curious and confident.
description: Open a door with prospects who already have a solution, without bashing it.
useCase: Use this when a prospect says they are already covered. Attacking their current choice makes them defensive, but curious questions about whether it truly works, paired with a low risk way to show your difference, can surface dissatisfaction they had not voiced.

title: Handle a Competitor Comparison
promptText: A prospect is comparing [YOUR OFFER] to a competitor: [COMPETITOR OR ALTERNATIVE]. They mentioned this concern: [WHAT THEY SAID OR ASKED]. My real differentiators: [YOUR DIFFERENTIATORS], and where the competitor is genuinely stronger: [COMPETITOR STRENGTHS, IF ANY]. Write a response that positions me honestly without trashing them. Acknowledge the competitor fairly where warranted, redirect to the dimensions where I clearly win, and help the prospect choose based on what matters most for their specific situation. Confident, honest, never desperate or dismissive.
description: Position honestly against a named competitor without disparaging them.
useCase: Use this when a prospect is weighing you against a specific competitor. Trashing rivals backfires and looks insecure, while honest acknowledgment paired with a clear redirect to where you genuinely win builds the trust that actually closes comparison shoppers.

title: Handle a Trust or Credibility Objection
promptText: A prospect is hesitant because they do not fully trust that [YOUR OFFER] will deliver, or they do not know me well enough yet. This shows up as [HOW THE DOUBT APPEARS, E.G. WILL THIS ACTUALLY WORK FOR ME]. My available proof: [PROOF, TESTIMONIALS, RESULTS, OR GUARANTEE]. Write responses that build trust rather than argue for it. Include: how to acknowledge their caution as smart, how to offer relevant proof without an info dump, and a risk reversal or small first step that lets them verify before fully committing. Warm and reassuring, never defensive.
description: Build trust with a skeptical prospect using proof and risk reversal.
useCase: Use this when the real objection is doubt about whether you can deliver, common for newer businesses or higher priced offers. Validating their caution and offering proof plus a low risk first step builds trust far better than insisting they should believe you.
variables: HOW THE DOUBT APPEARS, E.G. WILL THIS ACTUALLY WORK FOR ME

title: Design a Risk Reversal to Kill Objections
promptText: Help me design a risk reversal that removes the fear of buying [YOUR OFFER] priced at [PRICE]. The main risk prospects feel is [PERCEIVED RISK], and what I can realistically stand behind is [WHAT YOU CAN OFFER, E.G. GUARANTEE, TRIAL, MILESTONE]. Give me: 3 risk reversal options ranging from conservative to bold, how each shifts the risk from the buyer to me, the downside I need to consider for each, and how to present the risk reversal so it boosts confidence rather than signaling desperation. I want prospects to feel safe saying yes.
description: Design a guarantee or risk reversal that makes saying yes feel safe.
useCase: Use this when perceived risk is stalling sales. A well designed risk reversal shifts the fear of a bad decision off the buyer, which often does more to lift conversion than any argument, and this helps you craft one you can actually stand behind.
variables: WHAT YOU CAN OFFER, E.G. GUARANTEE, TRIAL, MILESTONE

title: Handle Questions About Your Guarantee
promptText: A prospect is asking pointed questions about my guarantee or refund policy for [YOUR OFFER], such as [THEIR QUESTION]. My actual policy: [YOUR POLICY]. Write responses that address their question with total transparency and turn the guarantee into a confidence builder. Include: how to explain the policy clearly and honestly, how to convey that I stand behind my work without sounding worried about refunds, and how to handle it if they seem to be looking for an easy exit. The goal is that my confidence in the guarantee reassures them. Straightforward and secure.
description: Answer guarantee questions transparently and turn your policy into reassurance.
useCase: Use this when a prospect probes your guarantee, which usually signals they want to buy but need to feel safe. Answering with transparent confidence turns the policy into a trust signal, while hedging or vagueness raises the exact doubt they were testing for.

title: Diagnose the Real Objection Behind the Stated One
promptText: A prospect gave me this objection to [YOUR OFFER]: [STATED OBJECTION]. Here is the context of our conversation: [CONVERSATION CONTEXT]. Help me figure out what is really going on. Analyze whether the stated objection is likely the true one or a socially acceptable cover for something else like price, fear, lack of authority, or low priority. Give me: the most likely real objection and why, the question I should ask to confirm it, and how my response should differ depending on what the true objection turns out to be.
description: Figure out whether a stated objection is the real one or a polite cover.
useCase: Use this when an objection does not quite add up. Prospects often give a socially easy objection instead of the real one, and diagnosing the true blocker before responding stops you from expertly answering a concern the prospect never actually had.

title: Handle a Prospect Who Cannot Decide
promptText: A prospect clearly wants [YOUR OFFER] but is paralyzed by indecision. There is no clear objection, just hesitation. Their situation: [THEIR CONTEXT]. Write responses that help them get unstuck without pressure. Include: how to normalize that the decision feels big, a way to simplify the choice down to what actually matters, a question that helps them picture the outcome of acting versus not acting, and a clear easy next step that lowers the stakes of committing. The goal is to help a genuine fit make a decision they will feel good about.
description: Help a genuinely interested but paralyzed prospect make a confident decision.
useCase: Use this when a good fit prospect is stuck in indecision with no real objection. Simplifying the choice and helping them picture both outcomes gets them past the paralysis, which serves them as much as you when the offer is genuinely right for their situation.

title: Handle I Need to Check with My Partner
promptText: A prospect says they need to check with a spouse, business partner, or boss before buying [YOUR OFFER]. Write responses that respect the real approval process without letting the deal drift. Include: how to tell whether this is genuine or a soft no, how to equip them to make the case to the other decision maker (the key points and answers to likely questions), an offer to join a conversation with the other party if helpful, and a way to set a clear next step and timeline so it does not vanish. Supportive, not suspicious. The decision maker they must convince cares most about [WHAT THE OTHER PARTY CARES ABOUT].
description: Handle the need approval objection by equipping your prospect to sell internally.
useCase: Use this when a prospect needs sign off from a partner or boss. Rather than losing control of the deal, you equip your champion with the arguments and answers to convince the real decision maker, and set a timeline so the deal does not evaporate in someone else's inbox.
variables: WHAT THE OTHER PARTY CARES ABOUT

title: Handle a Missing Feature or Scope Objection
promptText: A prospect says [YOUR OFFER] is missing something they want: [THE MISSING FEATURE OR SCOPE]. In reality this is [TRUE SITUATION, E.G. NOT SOMETHING I OFFER, COMING SOON, OR SOLVABLE ANOTHER WAY]. Write honest responses that handle it without overpromising. Include: how to acknowledge the request genuinely, how to explore whether it is a real dealbreaker or a nice to have, how to redirect to whether my offer solves their core problem regardless, and how to handle it honestly if I truly cannot meet the need. Never promise something I cannot deliver to close a sale.
description: Handle a missing feature objection honestly without overpromising to close.
useCase: Use this when a prospect wants something your offer does not do. The temptation to overpromise creates refunds and bad reviews later, so this helps you separate real dealbreakers from nice to haves and redirect to your core value, or walk away honestly when the fit is wrong.
variables: TRUE SITUATION, E.G. NOT SOMETHING I OFFER, COMING SOON, OR SOLVABLE ANOTHER WAY

title: Build an Objection Prep Sheet for Your Offer
promptText: Build me a complete objection handling prep sheet for selling [YOUR OFFER] priced at [PRICE] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Anticipate the top objections I will face given [YOUR CONTEXT AND KNOWN CONCERNS]. For each objection, give me: the likely real reason behind it, my best response framed around value or trust, a clarifying question to ask, and the proof or story that supports my answer. Organize it so I can review it before any sales conversation and walk in prepared for anything. I want to stop being caught off guard.
description: Create a full prep sheet of anticipated objections and your best responses.
useCase: Use this once per offer and review it before sales conversations. Being caught off guard by an objection loses deals, and a prep sheet of anticipated objections with your best responses means you handle each one calmly instead of improvising under pressure.

title: Preempt Objections Inside Your Pitch
promptText: Help me build the top objections to [YOUR OFFER] into my pitch so I handle them before the prospect raises them. The recurring objections are: [OBJECTION 1], [OBJECTION 2], [OBJECTION 3]. Show me how to naturally address each one within my pitch flow, so that by the time I finish, the prospect's main doubts are already resolved. For each, give me the framing or line to weave in, and where in a typical pitch it fits best. Done well this should feel like thoroughness, not defensiveness. My pitch covers [PITCH STRUCTURE OR KEY POINTS].
description: Weave objection handling into your pitch so doubts resolve before they are voiced.
useCase: Use this to strengthen your pitch or sales page. Preempting known objections within the pitch means prospects reach the ask with their main doubts already answered, which converts better than a clean pitch followed by a scramble to handle objections after the fact.

title: Respond to an Objection Raised Over Email
promptText: A prospect raised an objection to [YOUR OFFER] over email. Here is their message: [PASTE THEIR EMAIL]. Write my email response that handles the objection thoughtfully in writing. Match their tone, validate the concern, address it with a clear value or trust based answer plus any relevant proof: [PROOF OR ANGLE], and move toward the next step without pressure. Keep it concise since it is email, and end with a question or clear action. Give me the response plus a note on what their objection likely signals about how close they are to buying.
description: Craft a written email response to an objection that keeps the deal moving.
useCase: Use this when objections arrive by email, where you have time to respond well but the medium can feel cold. A concise, validating written response with proof handles the concern while keeping momentum, and reading what the objection signals helps you gauge how hard to push.

title: Reframe an Objection with Empathy
promptText: A prospect has this objection to [YOUR OFFER]: [THE OBJECTION]. Help me respond with genuine empathy rather than a scripted rebuttal. Show me how to acknowledge and validate their concern sincerely, relate it to others who felt the same way, and share what those people discovered: [WHAT OTHERS FOUND OR THE REALITY]. Make it feel authentic and human, not like a manipulative sales formula. The prospect should feel understood, not handled. Give me 2 versions at different warmth levels so I can match the person.
description: Respond to an objection with sincere empathy instead of a canned rebuttal.
useCase: Use this when an objection needs a human touch rather than logic. Prospects can feel a scripted rebuttal and it erodes trust, whereas genuine acknowledgment that others shared the concern and what they discovered lets people lower their guard and reconsider without feeling handled.
variables: WHAT OTHERS FOUND OR THE REALITY

title: Handle Silence After You State the Price
promptText: I told a prospect the price for [YOUR OFFER] and there was an awkward silence or a noncommittal reaction. I am not sure if it is sticker shock, thinking, or hesitation. Help me handle this moment well. Give me: what to say to break the silence without immediately discounting or backpedaling, how to invite their honest reaction to the price, questions that surface whether the number itself is the issue, and how to hold my price with calm confidence rather than filling the silence with concessions. The moment after the price is where I tend to cave.
description: Navigate the tense silence after stating a price without caving on it.
useCase: Use this for the critical moment right after you name a price. The silence tempts sellers to discount or backpedal before the prospect even reacts, and learning to hold the space with calm confidence, then invite their honest response, protects both the price and the deal.

title: Hold Your Position Without Over Discounting
promptText: I tend to cave and offer discounts too quickly when I feel objection pressure selling [YOUR OFFER] at [PRICE]. Help me build the mindset and language to hold my position. Give me: phrases to buy time and stay calm when pressured, ways to acknowledge a concern without conceding on price, how to distinguish a real negotiation from a test of my confidence, and if a concession is truly warranted, how to trade rather than simply give (asking for something in return). I want to negotiate from confidence, not fear.
description: Build the language and mindset to hold your price instead of discounting from fear.
useCase: Use this if you routinely cave on price under pressure. Reflexive discounting signals low confidence and trains prospects to push, and learning to hold calmly, concede only in trades, and separate real negotiation from confidence tests protects your margins and your positioning.

title: Handle a Direct Discount Request
promptText: A prospect is directly asking for a discount on [YOUR OFFER] priced at [PRICE]. Write responses for different situations. Include: how to decline gracefully while holding the value when a discount is not warranted, how to trade for a concession (longer commitment, upfront payment, case study, referral) if I am open to it, how to offer a smaller scope at a lower price instead of discounting the full offer, and how to tell when walking away is the right call. I do not want to reflexively discount, but I also do not want to lose a good fit over a small gap. My flexibility level: [HOW FLEXIBLE YOU CAN BE].
description: Respond to discount requests with graceful holds, trades, or scoped alternatives.
useCase: Use this when a prospect asks for a discount outright. Reflexive yes trains buyers to always push and reflexive no can lose a good fit, and having graceful ways to hold, trade, or offer a smaller scope lets you protect margin while keeping deals that are worth keeping.

title: Turn a Resolved Objection into a Close
promptText: I just handled a prospect's objection to [YOUR OFFER]: the objection was [THE OBJECTION] and I addressed it by [HOW YOU ADDRESSED IT]. Now I want to move toward closing without losing the moment or reopening doubts. Write me: a way to confirm the objection is truly resolved before moving on, a natural transition from handling the objection to asking for the decision, and the actual closing ask phrased confidently. I often handle an objection well then fumble the transition to the close. Help me capture the moment. The next step I want is [DESIRED NEXT STEP].
description: Bridge cleanly from a handled objection to a confident closing ask.
useCase: Use this right after resolving an objection, the moment sellers often fumble by hesitating or reopening the topic. Confirming the concern is resolved and transitioning smoothly to the ask captures the momentum, since a well handled objection is often the exact point a prospect is ready to commit.

title: Handle This Feels Overwhelming
promptText: A prospect is hesitant about [YOUR OFFER] because it feels like too much, too complicated, or more than they can take on right now. Write responses that reduce the perceived overwhelm. Include: how to acknowledge the feeling as valid, how to break the offer or process down into a simple manageable starting point, how to reassure them about the support or guidance they will get: [SUPPORT YOU PROVIDE], and how to shrink the mental size of the commitment. The prospect should leave feeling it is doable, not daunting. Keep it calm and reassuring.
description: Reduce the overwhelm that stalls prospects who feel your offer is too much.
useCase: Use this when a prospect stalls because the offer feels like too much to take on. Breaking it into a manageable first step and reassuring them about the support they will get shrinks the perceived commitment, converting hesitation rooted in overwhelm rather than doubt about value.

title: Match Your Objection Response to the Person
promptText: Here is my standard response to a common objection about [YOUR OFFER]: [PASTE YOUR RESPONSE]. Help me adapt it to different personality types so it lands with each. Rewrite it for: an analytical prospect who wants data and logic, a relationship driven prospect who wants trust and rapport, a fast moving decisive prospect who wants it brief and direct, and a cautious risk averse prospect who wants reassurance. For each, note what shifted and why it fits that type. The same objection needs different handling depending on who is raising it.
description: Adapt one objection response to fit different buyer personality types.
useCase: Use this to sharpen how you handle a recurring objection across different personalities. The same logical answer that convinces an analytical buyer can fall flat with a relationship driven one, and matching your response style to the person is often what determines whether the objection actually gets resolved.

title: Write a Full Client Proposal
promptText: Write a client proposal for [YOUR OFFER] for [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE]. Their problem: [CLIENT PROBLEM], their goal: [CLIENT GOAL], my proposed solution: [YOUR SOLUTION], the scope: [SCOPE], the investment: [PRICE], and the timeline: [TIMELINE]. Structure it to open with their situation and goal so they feel understood, then present my solution as the clear path, the scope and deliverables, the investment framed against the value, and a clear next step to move forward. Lead with their outcome, not my process. Confident and clear, not padded. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a complete client proposal that leads with their outcome and closes on a next step.
useCase: Use this to turn a sales conversation into a written proposal that closes. Proposals that open with the client's situation and frame investment against value convert far better than ones that lead with your process, and a clear next step stops the proposal from going silent.

title: Write a Proposal Executive Summary
promptText: Write a compelling executive summary for a proposal to [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE] for [YOUR OFFER]. The key points: their core problem is [PROBLEM], my solution delivers [KEY OUTCOME], and the main reason to choose me is [KEY DIFFERENTIATOR]. Keep it to a few tight paragraphs that a busy decision maker could read in under a minute and grasp the whole proposal. It should make them want to read the rest, or be enough to say yes on its own. Lead with their outcome. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write the executive summary that sells the whole proposal in under a minute.
useCase: Use this for the opening page of any proposal. Decision makers often read only the summary, so a tight one that captures the problem, outcome, and differentiator either earns their attention for the full document or closes the deal on its own.

title: Write a One Page Proposal
promptText: Write a one page proposal for [YOUR OFFER] for [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE]. Their problem: [PROBLEM], my solution: [SOLUTION], deliverables: [KEY DELIVERABLES], investment: [PRICE], timeline: [TIMELINE], next step: [NEXT STEP]. Fit it all on a single page: brief, scannable, and focused on their outcome. Cut anything that does not help them decide. This is for clients who do not want a long document and just want to know what they get, what it costs, and how to start. Clear and confident. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a tight one page proposal for clients who want the essentials fast.
useCase: Use this for smaller engagements or fast moving clients who will not read a long document. A focused one pager that covers outcome, deliverables, price, and next step removes friction and often closes quicker than an elaborate proposal that sits unread.

title: Write a Clear Scope of Work
promptText: Write a clear scope of work for [YOUR OFFER] for [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE]. The work involves [WHAT THE WORK INCLUDES], the deliverables are [DELIVERABLES], the timeline is [TIMELINE], and what is explicitly not included is [OUT OF SCOPE ITEMS]. Write it so both sides have a shared, unambiguous understanding of what will and will not be delivered. Include what I need from the client to deliver: [CLIENT RESPONSIBILITIES]. Make boundaries clear enough to prevent scope creep without sounding rigid or distrustful. Professional and precise.
description: Write a precise scope of work that prevents scope creep before it starts.
useCase: Use this for any client engagement to protect yourself from scope creep. Clearly defining what is included, excluded, and required from the client sets shared expectations upfront, which prevents the mid project disputes and unpaid extra work that erode solo business margins.

title: Present Pricing Options in a Proposal
promptText: Help me present pricing in a proposal for [YOUR OFFER] to [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE]. My pricing: [YOUR PRICING, ONE PRICE OR TIERS]. Show me how to present it so the price feels justified and easy to say yes to. If tiered, structure the options so the recommended one stands out. Include: how to frame each price against the value or outcome it delivers, how to order and anchor the options, and the language around the numbers that builds confidence rather than inviting negotiation. The value should be established before the number appears. My tone: [TONE].
description: Present proposal pricing so the number feels justified, not negotiable.
useCase: Use this for the pricing section of any proposal, where deals are often won or lost. Establishing value before the number and structuring options with a clear recommendation makes the price feel reasonable and guides the client toward the choice you want them to make.
variables: YOUR PRICING, ONE PRICE OR TIERS

title: Design Tiered Proposal Packages
promptText: Help me design 3 tiered packages for [YOUR OFFER] to present to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My full offering includes [WHAT YOU CAN OFFER], and the outcomes clients want are [DESIRED OUTCOMES]. Design good, better, and best tiers where the middle is the intended choice. For each tier give: a name, what is included, who it suits, and the price if I provide one: [PRICING GUIDANCE OR WRITE SUGGEST]. Explain the psychology of how the tiers guide the client toward the middle and how to make the best tier anchor the value. Clear and easy to compare.
description: Design good better best packages that steer clients toward the tier you want.
useCase: Use this to structure your offer into tiers that increase average deal size. A well designed three tier structure anchors value with the top option and guides most clients to the profitable middle, converting better than a single take it or leave it price.

title: Write a Sales Pitch for a Quick Conversation
promptText: Write a short spoken sales pitch for [YOUR OFFER] I can deliver in a quick conversation or when someone asks what I do. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the problem I solve: [PROBLEM], the outcome I deliver: [OUTCOME], what makes me different: [DIFFERENTIATOR]. Give me a version I can say in about 30 seconds that hooks interest and invites a follow up, plus a slightly longer 60 second version for when there is more time. Natural spoken language, leads with their problem or outcome, ends with a question or hook that opens a real conversation.
description: Write a spoken sales pitch that hooks interest and opens a conversation.
useCase: Use this for networking, referrals, or any moment someone asks what you do. A pitch that leads with the prospect's problem and ends with a hook turns a casual question into a sales conversation, which is where much of a solo business's pipeline actually begins.

title: Write a Cold Pitch Message
promptText: Write a cold pitch for [YOUR OFFER] to send to [PROSPECT TYPE] who does not know me. Their likely problem: [PROBLEM], the result I deliver: [RESULT], my proof: [PROOF POINT]. Open with a personalized or relevant hook, name their problem in their words, present my offer as the solution briefly, add credibility, and close with a low pressure ask: [SOFT ASK]. Keep it concise and confident, focused on them not me. This is a pitch, so it should create enough interest to earn a conversation, not close the whole deal in one message. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a concise cold pitch that earns a conversation, not a full close.
useCase: Use this to pitch your offer cold to a prospect who does not know you. A pitch that leads with their problem and offers a low pressure next step creates enough interest to open a dialogue, which is the realistic goal of a cold pitch rather than closing in one message.

title: Outline a Sales Pitch Deck
promptText: Outline a sales pitch deck for [YOUR OFFER] presented to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The problem I solve: [PROBLEM], my solution: [SOLUTION], the outcomes: [OUTCOMES], proof: [PROOF], and the ask: [THE ASK]. Give me a slide by slide outline that tells a persuasive story: hook the problem, agitate the cost of it, present the solution, show proof, handle the likely objection, and close with a clear ask. For each slide give the headline and the one key point it makes. Keep it lean, no filler slides. The story should build to an obvious yes.
description: Outline a lean pitch deck that tells a persuasive problem to solution story.
useCase: Use this before building any sales presentation or pitch deck. A deck structured as a story that moves from problem to proof to ask persuades far better than a feature dump, and outlining the narrative first ensures every slide earns its place.

title: Build a Story Into Your Pitch
promptText: Help me build a story into my pitch for [YOUR OFFER] to make it memorable and persuasive. A relevant story I can tell: [YOUR STORY, E.G. A CLIENT TRANSFORMATION OR YOUR ORIGIN]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the point the story should prove: [THE POINT]. Shape it into a tight pitch ready narrative with a clear beginning, turning point, and result that naturally leads to my offer. Show me where in a pitch it lands best and how to transition from the story to the ask. Keep it concise, stories in pitches must earn their length.
description: Shape a real story into a tight, persuasive element of your pitch.
useCase: Use this to make your pitch stick. Facts are forgotten but stories are remembered, and weaving a client transformation or origin story into your pitch makes the value tangible and emotional in a way that a list of benefits never achieves.
variables: YOUR STORY, E.G. A CLIENT TRANSFORMATION OR YOUR ORIGIN

title: Turn Discovery Call Notes into a Proposal
promptText: Turn my discovery call notes into a proposal for [YOUR OFFER]. Here are my notes: [PASTE CALL NOTES]. Use their exact language about their problem and goals so the proposal shows I listened. Structure it: their situation and goals in their words, my proposed solution mapped directly to what they told me, scope and deliverables, investment, and next step. Where the notes are thin, flag what I should confirm before sending. The proposal should feel custom built from our conversation, not a template with their name pasted in. My tone: [TONE].
description: Convert raw discovery call notes into a tailored proposal in their own words.
useCase: Use this right after a discovery call while the conversation is fresh. Building the proposal from their actual language proves you listened and makes the solution feel custom, which converts dramatically better than a template, and it turns messy notes into a polished document fast.

title: Write a Response to an RFP
promptText: Help me respond to this RFP or request for proposal for [YOUR OFFER]. Here are the requirements or questions they asked: [PASTE RFP REQUIREMENTS]. My relevant experience and approach: [YOUR EXPERIENCE AND APPROACH]. Write responses that directly answer each requirement, demonstrate I understand their real need beneath the stated requirements, and differentiate me from other likely bidders. Where a requirement invites it, show insight rather than just compliance. Flag any requirement I should ask a clarifying question about before finalizing. Professional but not generic. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write an RFP response that answers requirements while showing real insight.
useCase: Use this when responding to a formal RFP or bid. Most responses simply comply with requirements, so one that answers precisely while demonstrating understanding of the deeper need and differentiating your approach stands out in a stack of interchangeable submissions.

title: Write the Why Me Section of a Proposal
promptText: Write the why choose me section of a proposal for [YOUR OFFER] for [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE], who is likely also considering [ALTERNATIVES OR COMPETITORS]. My genuine differentiators: [YOUR DIFFERENTIATORS], my relevant proof: [PROOF OR RESULTS]. Write it to make the case for me specifically without disparaging alternatives or listing generic strengths every provider claims. Focus on what genuinely sets me apart for this client's situation. Confident but not boastful. It should answer the client's real question of why you over the others they are looking at.
description: Write the why me section that differentiates you without generic claims.
useCase: Use this for the section of a proposal that addresses the client's unspoken question of why choose you. Generic strengths every provider claims do nothing, while genuine differentiators tied to this client's situation and backed by proof are what win competitive deals.

title: Write Clear Terms for a Proposal
promptText: Help me write plain language terms to include in a proposal for [YOUR OFFER]. Cover: payment terms [PAYMENT TERMS], what happens with revisions or changes [REVISION POLICY], timeline expectations and what could shift them, cancellation or refund terms [CANCELLATION TERMS], and what I need from the client to stay on schedule. Write these in clear, friendly language that protects me without feeling like legal fine print or signaling distrust. These set expectations, not adversarial boundaries. Note this is general business language, not legal advice, and I should have a professional review anything binding.
description: Write clear, friendly proposal terms that set expectations and protect you.
useCase: Use this to add protective terms to a proposal without souring the relationship. Plain language terms on payment, revisions, and cancellation set clear expectations that prevent disputes, and framing them as mutual understanding rather than fine print keeps the tone collaborative.

title: Write a Follow Up After Pitching
promptText: Write a follow up message to send after I pitched [YOUR OFFER] to [PROSPECT TYPE]. The pitch covered [WHAT YOU PITCHED] and their reaction was [THEIR REACTION]. Write a follow up that reinforces the key value without repeating the whole pitch, addresses any hesitation they showed, provides anything I promised to send, and proposes a clear next step. Keep it concise and confident. Give me one version for a warm reaction and one for a lukewarm reaction so I can match how the pitch actually landed. My tone: [TONE].
description: Follow up after a pitch to reinforce value and drive the next step.
useCase: Use this right after delivering a pitch. The follow up is where interested prospects convert and lukewarm ones can be recovered, and a message that reinforces value and proposes a clear next step keeps momentum instead of letting the pitch fade into silence.

title: Nudge a Client on a Sent Proposal
promptText: I sent a proposal for [YOUR OFFER] to [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE] [TIME SINCE SENT] ago and have not heard back. Write a follow up nudge that checks in without sounding desperate or pushy. Offer to answer questions or walk through anything unclear, gently reinforce the value or a key benefit, and make it easy to respond even if the answer is not yet or no. Confident, assuming the proposal has value. Give me a first nudge and a firmer second nudge if the first gets no response. Keep each short. My tone: [TONE].
description: Nudge a silent proposal forward without sounding desperate.
useCase: Use this when a sent proposal goes quiet, which happens constantly. A confident nudge that offers help and makes any answer easy revives proposals stalled by client busyness rather than disinterest, recovering deals that would otherwise be lost to silence.

title: Write Closing Lines for a Verbal Pitch
promptText: Help me close a verbal pitch for [YOUR OFFER] to [PROSPECT TYPE]. I tend to trail off or get vague at the end instead of clearly asking for the business. The next step I want is [DESIRED NEXT STEP]. Give me several confident closing lines to end a pitch: a direct ask, an assumptive close that moves to logistics, and a choice close that offers two ways forward. For each, note when it fits best. Also give me a line to comfortably handle if they say they need to think about it. I want to end pitches with a clear ask, not a fizzle.
description: End a verbal pitch with a confident, clear ask instead of trailing off.
useCase: Use this if you deliver a good pitch then fumble the close by going vague. Confident closing lines that clearly ask for the next step capture the momentum you built, since a strong pitch that ends without a clear ask often leaves the prospect unsure what to do and the deal drifts.

title: Add an Upsell or Add On to a Proposal
promptText: Help me add a natural upsell or add on to my proposal for [YOUR OFFER] to [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE]. The core offer solves [CORE PROBLEM], and a relevant add on that would genuinely help them is [ADD ON OFFER] which addresses [WHAT THE ADD ON SOLVES]. Show me how to present this add on within the proposal so it feels like a helpful option, not a cash grab. Include how to frame it as optional, tie it to a real benefit for them, and price or position it so it increases deal value without complicating the main decision. Genuine and low pressure.
description: Add an optional upsell to a proposal that feels helpful, not greedy.
useCase: Use this to increase deal value within a proposal. A relevant add on presented as a genuine optional benefit, rather than a pushy upsell, raises your average project size while still serving the client, and framing it as optional keeps it from complicating the core yes.

title: Tighten a Bloated Proposal
promptText: Here is my proposal draft: [PASTE PROPOSAL]. It feels too long and padded. Tighten it significantly while keeping everything that helps the client decide. Cut filler, redundant sections, and anything that is about me rather than their outcome. Ensure it still covers their problem, my solution, deliverables, price, and next step clearly. Show me the tightened version and note what you cut and why. Clients decide faster on lean proposals, so I want this sharp and scannable, not exhaustive. Keep my voice and tone intact.
description: Cut a padded proposal down to only what helps the client decide.
useCase: Use this on any proposal that feels bloated before sending. Long proposals delay decisions and hide the key points, and cutting to only what helps the client decide produces a sharper, more scannable document that closes faster than an exhaustive one.

title: Adapt Your Pitch for Different Stakeholders
promptText: I am pitching [YOUR OFFER] into an organization where multiple people influence the decision. The stakeholders are [LIST STAKEHOLDERS, E.G. THE USER, THE BUDGET HOLDER, THE BOSS]. Each cares about different things: [WHAT EACH CARES ABOUT]. Help me adapt my pitch for each. For each stakeholder, give me the angle and the specific value points that matter to them, and the concern I need to preempt. Then show me how to build a single proposal or pitch that speaks to all of them at once without losing focus. My core offer value: [CORE VALUE].
description: Tailor your pitch to each stakeholder who influences a buying decision.
useCase: Use this when selling into a business with multiple decision influencers. A user, budget holder, and boss each care about different things, and a pitch that speaks to each one's priorities converts complex deals that a single generic message would stall.
variables: LIST STAKEHOLDERS, E.G. THE USER, THE BUDGET HOLDER, THE BOSS

title: Pitch a Productized Service
promptText: Help me pitch my productized service [YOUR OFFER] which delivers [WHAT IT DELIVERS] for a fixed scope and price of [PRICE]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], the problem it solves: [PROBLEM]. Write a pitch that sells the clarity and simplicity of a fixed offer as a benefit: they know exactly what they get, what it costs, and when. Emphasize the outcome and the removal of the uncertainty that comes with custom work. Include how to present the fixed scope as an advantage, not a limitation. Confident and clear. My tone: [TONE].
description: Pitch a productized service by selling its clarity and fixed scope as benefits.
useCase: Use this to pitch a fixed scope productized offer. The predictability of a set scope and price is a genuine selling point for clients tired of custom project uncertainty, and framing the fixed nature as a benefit rather than a limitation is what makes productized services convert.

title: Preempt Objections Inside a Proposal
promptText: Help me build objection handling into my proposal for [YOUR OFFER] for [CLIENT NAME OR TYPE]. The objections or concerns they are likely to have are [LIKELY OBJECTIONS]. Show me how to address each one naturally within the proposal so their doubts are resolved as they read, before they can become reasons to say no. For each, give me the section it fits in and the framing to use. Done well this feels like thoroughness and understanding, not defensiveness. The client should finish the proposal with their concerns already answered.
description: Resolve likely objections inside the proposal before the client raises them.
useCase: Use this to strengthen a proposal for a deal with known hesitations. Addressing likely objections within the document means the client reaches the decision with their doubts already answered, which converts better than a clean proposal that leaves concerns to surface and stall the deal later.

title: Rewrite a Weak Pitch to Sound Confident
promptText: Here is my pitch or proposal language: [PASTE YOUR PITCH]. It sounds tentative, apologetic, or unsure of its own value. Rewrite it to sound confident without being arrogant. Remove hedging words, apologetic phrasing, and anything that undersells what I offer. Make it own its value and state the price and terms without flinching. Show me the confident version alongside a note on the specific weak patterns you fixed so I learn to stop doing them. Keep it authentic to me, just remove the self doubt leaking through. My offer: [YOUR OFFER].
description: Rewrite a tentative pitch into confident language that owns its value.
useCase: Use this when your pitch or proposal reads as unsure of itself. Hedging and apologetic phrasing quietly signal low value and invite negotiation, and stripping out the self doubt so your offer owns its worth changes how prospects perceive both the price and you.

title: Build a Full Discovery Call Question Bank
promptText: Build me a complete discovery call question bank for selling [YOUR OFFER] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The problem I solve is [PROBLEM] and the outcome I deliver is [OUTCOME]. Organize questions into stages: opening and rapport, understanding their current situation, uncovering the real problem and its cost, exploring their desired outcome, surfacing budget and decision process, and transitioning to next steps. For each stage give 4 to 6 questions and a one line note on what each is designed to uncover. I want a reusable bank I can pull from on any call. My tone: [TONE].
description: Build a complete, staged bank of discovery questions you can reuse on any call.
useCase: Use this once to create a reusable discovery framework. A staged question bank means every call systematically uncovers the problem, cost, outcome, and buying process, so you stop winging discovery and start walking into every call prepared to diagnose and qualify.

title: Write Questions That Uncover the Real Problem
promptText: Write discovery questions that dig past surface symptoms to the real problem for [TARGET AUDIENCE] considering [YOUR OFFER]. The surface problem they usually describe is [SURFACE PROBLEM], but the deeper issue is often [DEEPER PROBLEM]. Give me a sequence of questions that starts with their stated problem and layers deeper to reveal the root cause, the emotional and business impact, and what they have already tried. Include follow up probes for when they give a shallow answer. The goal is that by the end they see their problem more clearly than before the call.
description: Write layered questions that dig past symptoms to the true root problem.
useCase: Use this to run discovery that diagnoses rather than skims. Prospects describe symptoms, not root causes, and questions that layer from the surface problem to the real one make you the person who understood their situation best, which is often who they buy from.

title: Write Questions About the Cost of Doing Nothing
promptText: Write discovery questions that help a prospect articulate the cost of not solving [PROBLEM] that [YOUR OFFER] addresses. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me questions that lead them to quantify or feel what the problem is costing them in money, time, stress, or missed opportunity, and what happens if it continues unsolved for another 6 or 12 months. The questions should let them reach the conclusion themselves rather than me telling them. Include gentle probes that make the cost concrete without being manipulative. This builds the urgency that justifies my price.
description: Write questions that lead prospects to quantify the cost of their unsolved problem.
useCase: Use this to build genuine urgency during discovery. When prospects articulate the cost of inaction themselves, they create their own motivation to buy, and it establishes the value context that makes your price feel reasonable long before you name it.

title: Write Questions to Qualify Budget and Authority
promptText: Write discovery questions that surface budget, decision authority, and timeline for [YOUR OFFER] without feeling intrusive or transactional. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE], typical price range: [PRICE RANGE]. Give me natural, conversational ways to learn: whether they have budget or how they fund solutions like this, who else is involved in the decision, and what their timeline or urgency is. Frame each so it feels like helping them plan rather than interrogating them. Include how to gracefully handle it if they deflect. I need to qualify without killing rapport.
description: Write questions that qualify budget, authority, and timeline without feeling pushy.
useCase: Use this to qualify prospects gracefully during discovery. Learning budget, decision authority, and timeline early stops you from wasting hours on deals that cannot close, and conversational framing lets you qualify without the transactional feel that damages rapport.

title: Write Questions to Qualify if They Are a Good Fit
promptText: Write qualifying questions to quickly determine whether a prospect is a good fit for [YOUR OFFER]. My ideal client looks like [IDEAL CLIENT], and the signs of a bad fit are [BAD FIT SIGNALS]. Give me questions early in a call that reveal whether they match my ideal client profile, have the problem I actually solve, and are the kind of client I want to work with. Include the specific answers that would tell me to qualify them in or out. I want to protect my time by identifying poor fits early and politely, not 40 minutes into a call.
description: Write early questions that reveal whether a prospect is worth pursuing.
useCase: Use this to protect your time on sales calls. Qualifying questions early in a call reveal whether a prospect fits your ideal client and has the problem you solve, so you can invest fully in good fits and gracefully exit poor ones before they consume your day.

title: Write Questions About Their Desired Outcome
promptText: Write discovery questions that help a prospect vividly describe the outcome they want from solving [PROBLEM] with something like [YOUR OFFER]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me questions that draw out what success looks like to them specifically, how their business or life changes when the problem is solved, what they would do with the time or results they gain, and how they will know it worked. The goal is that they paint a clear picture of the after so my offer becomes the obvious bridge to it. Make the questions curious and forward looking.
description: Write questions that get prospects to vividly describe the outcome they want.
useCase: Use this during discovery to make the value tangible. When prospects describe their desired outcome in vivid detail, they build their own emotional case for change, and your offer becomes the clear bridge to the after they just painted rather than a cost to weigh.

title: Write Pain Point Discovery Questions
promptText: Write questions that surface the specific pain points [TARGET AUDIENCE] experiences around [PROBLEM AREA] that [YOUR OFFER] resolves. Give me questions that uncover their day to day frustrations, the moments the problem hurts most, what has been the last straw for others in their position, and the ripple effects the problem creates elsewhere. Include probes that get them talking about real specific situations rather than abstractions. Specific pain that they name themselves is far more motivating than pain I describe. Keep the questions empathetic, not leading.
description: Write questions that surface the specific, concrete pains your offer resolves.
useCase: Use this to uncover the specific frustrations that drive a purchase. Prospects who describe their concrete daily pains, rather than abstract problems, motivate themselves to act, and naming those pains empathetically positions you as someone who truly understands their situation.

title: Write a Full Discovery Call Script
promptText: Write a discovery call script for selling [YOUR OFFER] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The call should run about [CALL LENGTH]. Structure it: a warm opening that sets the agenda and puts them at ease, transition into discovery questions that uncover their problem, cost, and desired outcome, a natural point to determine fit and budget, and a close that either transitions to presenting my solution or sets a clear next step. Give me the actual language and questions for each phase, plus notes on when to listen versus talk. I want a repeatable structure that does not feel scripted. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a complete, repeatable discovery call script that flows naturally.
useCase: Use this to run consistent, effective discovery calls. A structured script that flows from rapport through diagnosis to next steps ensures you cover what matters on every call, while notes on when to listen keep it from feeling robotic, which is where most rapport is won or lost.

title: Write the Opening of a Discovery Call
promptText: Write the opening minutes of a discovery call with [PROSPECT TYPE] about [YOUR OFFER]. Give me: a warm genuine opener that builds quick rapport without forced small talk, a way to set the agenda so they know what to expect and feel in control, a line that earns permission to ask questions, and a transition into discovery. The opening should make them comfortable and position the call as a two way conversation to see if we are a fit, not a sales pitch they need to defend against. Keep it natural and human. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write a discovery call opening that builds rapport and sets a collaborative frame.
useCase: Use this to start discovery calls on the right footing. The opening sets whether the prospect is relaxed and open or defensive, and a warm opener that frames the call as a mutual fit conversation gets you honest answers instead of guarded ones for the rest of the call.

title: Write Questions That Reveal the Gap
promptText: Write discovery questions that help a prospect see the gap between where they are now and where they want to be regarding [PROBLEM] that [YOUR OFFER] solves. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me questions that establish their current state clearly, then their desired state, and then naturally highlight the distance between them and why they have not closed it on their own. The gap they perceive is the space my offer fills. Include how to summarize the gap back to them so they feel the need to bridge it. Keep it consultative, not manipulative.
description: Write questions that make prospects feel the gap between now and their goal.
useCase: Use this to create motivation through contrast during discovery. Questions that establish the current state, the desired state, and why the gap persists make the prospect feel the distance they need to close, and your offer becomes the natural bridge rather than an expense.

title: Write Questions That Surface Objections Early
promptText: Write discovery questions that surface a prospect's likely objections and hesitations before the close for [YOUR OFFER]. Common objections in my sales are [COMMON OBJECTIONS]. Give me questions that draw out their concerns, past bad experiences, what would make them hesitant, and what has stopped them from solving this before. The goal is to get objections on the table during discovery when I have time to address them, rather than having them ambush me at the close. Frame the questions as genuine curiosity about their situation, not as fishing for objections.
description: Write questions that bring objections to the surface early, not at the close.
useCase: Use this to handle objections when you have time to, not under pressure at the close. Surfacing concerns during discovery lets you address them naturally within the conversation, so they do not ambush you at the decision moment when the prospect is already reaching for a reason to say no.

title: Write a Pre Call Qualification Form
promptText: Write a short pre call qualification form or application for people booking a discovery call about [YOUR OFFER]. My ideal client is [IDEAL CLIENT], and I want to filter out [WHO TO FILTER OUT]. Give me 5 to 8 questions that surface whether they are a fit, what their main problem is, their rough budget or seriousness, and enough context that I walk into the call prepared. Balance qualifying rigor with keeping the form short enough that good prospects still complete it. Note which questions are the key disqualifiers. I want fewer, better calls.
description: Write a pre call form that filters bad fits and prepares you for good ones.
useCase: Use this to raise the quality of your discovery calls before they happen. A short qualification form filters out poor fits, so you spend calls only on serious prospects, and the answers let you walk in prepared rather than spending the first ten minutes gathering basic context.

title: Write the Transition from Discovery to Pitch
promptText: Help me transition smoothly from discovery to presenting my solution on a call for [YOUR OFFER]. After discovery I will know their problem, cost, and desired outcome. Give me: how to summarize what I heard back to them so they feel understood and confirm I got it right, a transition line that earns permission to share how I can help, and a way to frame my solution as the direct answer to what they just told me. This bridge is where I tend to fumble, jumping into a generic pitch instead of connecting it to their words. My tone: [TONE].
description: Write the bridge from discovery into a pitch tailored to what you just heard.
useCase: Use this for the pivotal moment between discovery and your pitch. Summarizing their situation back before presenting makes them feel understood and lets you frame your solution as the direct answer to their words, which converts far better than jumping into a generic pitch.

title: Write Follow Up Probes for Deeper Answers
promptText: Give me a set of follow up probes I can use on any discovery call for [YOUR OFFER] to get prospects to go deeper when they give short or surface answers. I need natural phrases that dig further without interrogating: ways to ask them to say more, to give an example, to explain the impact, to clarify what they mean, and to reveal the feeling behind a fact. Give me 12 to 15 reusable probe phrases and a note on when each works best. These are the moments where the real information comes out, and I tend to move on too fast.
description: Build a set of reusable probes that draw out deeper answers on any call.
useCase: Use this to get past surface answers in discovery. The real information comes from follow up probes that ask prospects to go deeper, and having reusable phrases ready means you slow down and dig at the exact moments most sellers move on too quickly.

title: Write Questions About Their Current Approach
promptText: Write discovery questions that reveal how a prospect currently handles [PROBLEM AREA] that [YOUR OFFER] addresses, and why their current approach falls short. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me questions that uncover what they do now, what tools or providers they use, what is working and what is not, what they have tried before and abandoned, and what specifically frustrates them about the status quo. This reveals whether the pain is strong enough to drive change and where my offer clearly beats their current approach. Keep it non judgmental so they are honest about what is not working.
description: Write questions that expose the gaps in the prospect's current approach.
useCase: Use this to understand what you are really selling against, which is usually the status quo, not a competitor. Learning how they handle the problem now and where it falls short reveals whether their pain is strong enough to drive change and exactly where your offer wins.

title: Write Questions About Their Decision Process
promptText: Write discovery questions that map out how a prospect will actually make the decision to buy [YOUR OFFER]. My audience: [TARGET AUDIENCE].
