Note whether it works better as a free lead generation event or a paid offer, and how it could lead into a larger offer afterward. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a workshop or live training offer with a clear transformation.
useCase: Use this to create a live offer that generates revenue or leads. Workshops leverage the urgency and value of live teaching, and they can either sell directly or warm attendees toward a larger offer afterward.

title: Bundle Existing Offers into a New One
promptText: Help me bundle my existing offers into a new higher value offer. My current offers are [LIST YOUR OFFERS]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Design a bundle: which offers combine well and why, the transformation the bundle delivers as a package, how to price it so it feels like a deal while raising my average sale, and how to position it as more than the sum of its parts. Note which customers the bundle suits best. Explain why bundling these makes sense rather than selling them separately. My tone is [TONE].
description: Combine existing offers into a higher value bundle that raises average sale.
useCase: Use this to create a new offer without building anything new. Bundling existing offers raises your average sale, gives customers a complete solution, and creates a fresh thing to promote from assets you already have.

title: Refine How Your Offer Is Positioned
promptText: Help me refine the positioning of my offer so it stands out. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and competitors or alternatives include [ALTERNATIVES]. My genuine strengths are [YOUR STRENGTHS]. Help me position it distinctly: the angle that sets it apart, who it is specifically for and not for, the one core promise to lead with, and how to frame it so it does not look like every similar offer. Give me a positioning statement and the key messaging points. Explain the strategic reasoning. My tone is [TONE].
description: Refine your offer's positioning so it stands apart from alternatives.
useCase: Use this when your offer is good but blends in with similar ones. Sharp positioning that names who it is for and leads with one distinct promise is often what determines whether an offer sells, regardless of its quality.

title: Design a Done For You Offer
promptText: Help me design a done for you offer where I handle everything for the client. I help [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT YOU DO], and many of them would rather pay to have it handled than do it themselves. Design the offer: exactly what I take off their plate, the end result I deliver, the process and their minimal involvement, what justifies the premium price of full service, and how to position the convenience and outcome. Note the delivery boundaries so it stays profitable. Explain who this suits versus a cheaper do it yourself option. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a premium done for you offer where you handle everything for the client.
useCase: Use this to serve clients who would rather pay than do it themselves. Done for you offers command premium prices because they sell convenience and a finished result, appealing to busy clients who value their time over saving money.

title: Design a Guarantee for Your Offer
promptText: Help me design a guarantee for my offer that reduces buyer risk and boosts conversions without exposing me to abuse. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [PRICE], and the main risk buyers feel is [PERCEIVED RISK]. What I can realistically stand behind is [WHAT YOU CAN OFFER OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Design guarantee options from conservative to bold, explain how each shifts risk from buyer to me, the downside of each I should weigh, and how to present the guarantee so it builds confidence. Keep it something I can genuinely honor. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a conversion boosting guarantee you can genuinely stand behind.
useCase: Use this when perceived risk is holding back sales of an offer. A well designed guarantee shifts risk off the buyer and lifts conversions, often more than any argument, and this helps you craft one that boosts trust without inviting abuse.
variables: WHAT YOU CAN OFFER OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Brainstorm a Seasonal or Limited Offer
promptText: Help me brainstorm a seasonal or limited time offer. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the season, holiday, or moment coming up is [THE SEASON OR EVENT]. Design a timely offer that fits the moment genuinely: what to offer, why it fits this specific season for my audience, how to use the natural deadline for urgency, the price or promotion, and how it connects to my core offers. Avoid a forced seasonal tie in. Note whether this should be a discount, a special edition, or a bonus. My tone is [TONE].
description: Brainstorm a timely seasonal or limited offer with genuine relevance.
useCase: Use this ahead of a season, holiday, or event relevant to your audience. A limited offer with a real deadline drives urgency and gives you a fresh reason to promote, capturing sales from buyers who need a nudge to act.

title: Brainstorm Ways to Make Your Offer Unique
promptText: Help me brainstorm ways to make my offer genuinely different from competitors. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the typical way competitors deliver this is [HOW COMPETITORS DO IT]. Brainstorm differentiation angles: a different delivery method, a unique inclusion or bonus, a distinct guarantee, a narrower specialization, a different pricing model, or a unique experience. For each, explain how it would make my offer stand out and whether it fits my strengths, which are [YOUR STRENGTHS]. Mark the most promising and realistic ones. My tone is [TONE].
description: Brainstorm concrete ways to make your offer stand apart from competitors.
useCase: Use this when your offer competes in a crowded space. Concrete differentiation through delivery, inclusions, specialization, or experience gives buyers a clear reason to choose you, which matters more than being marginally better at the same thing.

title: Decide What Offer to Create Next
promptText: Help me decide what offer to build next. My current offers are [YOUR CURRENT OFFERS], my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my business goal right now is [YOUR GOAL, E.G. MORE REVENUE, MORE SCALE, OR MORE STABILITY]. Based on where my customers naturally want to go next and the gaps in my current lineup, recommend what offer would have the biggest impact to build next. Weigh what serves my goal, what my existing customers would buy, and what is realistic to create. Give me your top recommendation with reasoning, plus a runner up. My tone is [TONE].
description: Decide which offer to build next based on your goals and customer gaps.
useCase: Use this when you have offers but are unsure what to build next. A recommendation grounded in your goal, your customers' next need, and what is realistic prevents wasted effort on the wrong offer and focuses you on the highest impact move.
variables: YOUR GOAL, E.G. MORE REVENUE, MORE SCALE, OR MORE STABILITY

title: Run an Idea Through a Validation Framework
promptText: Run my business or offer idea through a structured validation framework. The idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], solving [THE PROBLEM]. Assess it across the key validation dimensions: is the problem real and painful, is the audience reachable and willing to pay, is the market big enough, is my solution meaningfully better than alternatives, and can I actually deliver it. For each dimension give an honest read, the risk level, and what evidence would confirm or kill it. End with a clear verdict on whether this is worth pursuing and the single riskiest assumption to test first. My tone is [TONE].
description: Assess a business idea across the core validation dimensions with an honest verdict.
useCase: Use this before investing time or money into an idea. A structured pass across problem, market, willingness to pay, and delivery surfaces the fatal flaws early and tells you the riskiest assumption to test first, saving you from building something nobody wants.

title: Find the Riskiest Assumption in Your Idea
promptText: Help me find the riskiest assumption behind my idea so I test the right thing first. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. List the core assumptions this idea depends on to succeed, covering whether the problem matters, whether people will pay, whether I can reach them, and whether I can deliver. For each assumption, rate how risky it is (how likely it is to be false and how badly it would hurt if it were) and how easy it is to test. Then tell me the one assumption to validate first and the fastest cheap way to test it. My tone is [TONE].
description: Identify and rank the assumptions your idea depends on so you test the riskiest first.
useCase: Use this when you have an idea but limited time to validate it. Testing the assumption most likely to be false and most damaging if wrong is the fastest path to knowing whether an idea is viable, rather than validating easy things that do not matter.

title: Validate That the Problem Is Real
promptText: Help me validate that the problem behind my idea is real and worth solving. The problem is [THE PROBLEM] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my idea to solve it is [YOUR IDEA]. Help me pressure test the problem itself before the solution: how to tell if this is a painful urgent problem or a mild annoyance, signs that people are already spending time or money trying to solve it, where to look for evidence the problem exists at scale, and questions to ask real people that reveal the true pain without leading them. A great solution to a weak problem still fails. My tone is [TONE].
description: Pressure test whether the problem your idea solves is real and painful enough.
useCase: Use this before validating a solution, because a great solution to a weak problem still fails. Confirming the problem is painful and urgent enough that people already try to solve it is the foundation that determines whether any idea built on it can succeed.

title: Write Customer Validation Interview Questions
promptText: Write interview questions to validate my idea with real potential customers. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], solving [THE PROBLEM]. Give me questions that uncover the truth without leading people to say what I want to hear: questions about how they currently handle the problem, what they have tried, what it costs them, and how they feel about it, rather than asking if they like my idea. Include the mistake of pitching too early and how to avoid it. Structure the questions so the answers reveal real demand or its absence. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write non leading interview questions that reveal real demand from customers.
useCase: Use this before talking to potential customers about an idea. Non leading questions about their actual behavior and spending reveal genuine demand, while asking whether they like your idea produces polite lies that give false confidence.

title: Design a Demand Test
promptText: Design a demand test for my idea before I build it. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] at roughly [PRICE], and my reachable channels are [YOUR CHANNELS]. Give me a plan to test whether real demand exists using the smallest possible effort: options like a landing page with a waitlist or preorder, a presale, a manual offer to a small group, or a paid ad test. For each, explain what a pass or fail looks like, the numbers to watch, and how much signal it gives. Recommend the best first test for my situation and what result would justify building. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design the smallest test that proves real demand before you build.
useCase: Use this to test demand with real signals rather than opinions. A well designed demand test using a waitlist, presale, or small manual offer shows whether people will actually act, which is the only validation that reliably predicts whether an idea will sell.

title: Plan a Presale to Validate an Idea
promptText: Help me plan a presale to validate my idea by getting people to pay before I fully build it. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] at [PRICE]. Design the presale: how to position an unbuilt or partly built offer honestly, what to promise and what to be transparent about, the price and any early bird incentive, how many sales would validate it, and how to deliver or refund gracefully depending on the result. Explain how a presale is the strongest validation because it tests real money, not opinions. Note the ethical line to stay on. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan an honest presale that validates an idea with real money before building.
useCase: Use this for the strongest form of validation, real payment before building. A presale proves people will pay, funds the build, and delivers early customers, while transparent positioning keeps it ethical, making it the highest confidence test an idea can pass.

title: Test Willingness to Pay
promptText: Help me figure out whether my audience will actually pay for my idea and roughly how much. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], solving [THE PROBLEM]. Give me ways to test willingness to pay without just asking would you buy this, which people answer dishonestly: methods like gauging what they already spend on the problem, testing price reactions, using a fake door or preorder, and reading behavioral signals versus stated intent. Explain the gap between people saying they would pay and actually paying, and how to get past it. My tone is [TONE].
description: Test whether people will really pay, and how much, beyond stated intent.
useCase: Use this because the gap between saying yes and paying is where most ideas die. Testing willingness to pay through actual spending and behavioral signals, rather than survey answers, reveals whether real demand exists at a price that works.

title: Write Copy for a Validation Landing Page
promptText: Write landing page copy to validate my idea by measuring how many people sign up or express interest. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the outcome it promises is [THE OUTCOME]. Write a page that clearly presents the offer as if it exists, leads with the transformation, and drives to a single action like joining a waitlist, preordering, or requesting early access. Keep it honest about the stage it is at. Give me a headline, the key value points, and the call to action. Tell me what signup rate would suggest real demand. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write validation landing page copy that measures real interest in an idea.
useCase: Use this to run a landing page demand test. Clear copy that presents the offer and drives one action lets you measure genuine interest through signups, giving you real behavioral data on demand before you commit to building.

title: Use a Waitlist to Validate Demand
promptText: Help me use a waitlist to validate my idea. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my reach is [YOUR CHANNELS OR AUDIENCE SIZE]. Design a waitlist validation: how to promote it, what to promise waitlist members, how many signups would signal real demand given my reach, and how to gauge the quality of interest, not just the quantity, since signups are cheaper than sales. Include how to survey or warm the waitlist to test how serious they are, and how to convert them when I launch. Note the limits of waitlist signals. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a waitlist test that measures genuine demand, not just cheap signups.
useCase: Use this to gauge demand while building an early audience. A waitlist tests interest and warms future buyers, but signups are cheaper than sales, so this helps you read the quality of interest and avoid the false confidence of a big but lukewarm list.

title: Design Cheap Validation Experiments
promptText: Give me a set of cheap, fast experiments to validate my idea without building anything. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I have limited time and budget: [YOUR CONSTRAINTS]. Suggest lean experiments such as a concierge or manual version, a fake door test, a single offer to a small group, a content test to gauge interest, or a simple survey plus behavioral follow up. For each, explain what it tests, what a pass or fail looks like, and how much it costs in time and money. Rank them by signal strength per effort. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design lean, cheap experiments to validate an idea without building it.
useCase: Use this when you want to validate on a tight budget of time and money. Lean experiments like a manual concierge version or a fake door test give real signal for almost no cost, letting you learn whether an idea works before committing serious resources.

title: Plan a Concierge Version to Validate
promptText: Help me validate my idea by delivering it manually as a concierge version before building anything automated or scalable. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], solving [THE PROBLEM]. Design the manual version: how to deliver the outcome by hand for a few early customers, what to charge, what I would learn from doing it manually, and how this tests both demand and delivery at once. Explain how doing it manually first reveals what to build and whether it is worth automating. Note when to graduate from concierge to a real product. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan a manual concierge version that validates demand and delivery at once.
useCase: Use this to test an idea by delivering it by hand before building anything. A concierge version validates both that people will pay and that you can deliver the outcome, while teaching you exactly what to build and whether automation is even worth it.

title: Scope a Minimum Viable Version
promptText: Help me scope the smallest version of my idea I can launch to learn from real customers. My full idea is [YOUR FULL IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the core outcome it must deliver is [CORE OUTCOME]. Strip it down to a minimum viable version: what is the one core thing it must do to deliver value, what to cut or fake for now, and what to add only after it is validated. Help me resist the urge to build everything. Give me the lean first version and the roadmap of what to add only once demand is proven. My tone is [TONE].
description: Scope the smallest launchable version of an idea that still delivers real value.
useCase: Use this to avoid overbuilding before you know an idea works. Scoping to the one thing that delivers core value lets you launch fast, learn from real customers, and add features only after demand is proven, rather than building everything on a guess.

title: Analyze Customer Interview Insights
promptText: Help me analyze what I learned from talking to potential customers about my idea. Here is what people said: [PASTE INTERVIEW NOTES OR QUOTES]. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Analyze the responses objectively: what patterns suggest real demand, what signals suggest weak interest or polite encouragement, where people revealed the true problem versus what I hoped to hear, and what the notable objections or hesitations mean. Warn me if I am reading validation into responses that do not actually support it. Give me an honest read on whether to proceed, pivot, or drop it. My tone is [TONE].
description: Analyze interview responses objectively for real demand versus polite encouragement.
useCase: Use this after customer conversations to avoid fooling yourself. Founders hear encouragement they want to hear, and an objective read of the responses separates genuine demand signals from politeness, giving you an honest basis to proceed, pivot, or drop the idea.

title: Interpret Whether Competitors Validate or Warn
promptText: Help me interpret what existing competitors mean for my idea. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and existing players or alternatives include [COMPETITORS OR ALTERNATIVES]. Help me read the signal: whether their existence validates the market or warns of saturation, whether the absence of competitors means opportunity or means nobody wants it, where the gaps in their offerings suggest room for me, and how I could position differently. Be honest if the market looks too crowded or if the lack of competition is a red flag rather than a green light. My tone is [TONE].
description: Read whether existing competitors validate your market or warn against it.
useCase: Use this to interpret the competitive landscape correctly. Competitors can signal healthy demand or dangerous saturation, and an empty market can mean opportunity or that nobody wants it, so reading the signal accurately shapes whether and how you enter.

title: Reality Check the Market Size
promptText: Help me do a reality check on whether the market for my idea is big enough to be worth pursuing. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my revenue goal is [YOUR REVENUE GOAL]. Help me think through: roughly how many people have this problem and are reachable, what fraction might realistically buy, at what price, and whether that math supports my goal. Do a rough back of the envelope calculation. Be honest if the market is too small or too hard to reach to hit my goal, and note whether a niche can still work at my scale. My tone is [TONE].
description: Run a back of the envelope check on whether the market supports your goal.
useCase: Use this to check whether an idea can realistically hit your revenue goal. A rough market size calculation reveals whether the reachable audience and likely conversion at your price add up to your target, catching ideas that are viable but too small before you commit.

title: Plan a Smoke Test Ad Campaign
promptText: Help me plan a small smoke test ad campaign to validate demand for my idea. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my test budget is [BUDGET]. Design a lean test: what to advertise, where to send clicks such as a landing page or signup, what metrics indicate real interest like click through and signup rate, and what results would justify building versus dropping the idea. Keep the spend minimal since this is a signal test, not a launch. Tell me what numbers would count as a pass. Note the limits of what an ad test can and cannot prove. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan a low budget ad smoke test to measure demand signals for an idea.
useCase: Use this to buy real demand signal fast with a small budget. A smoke test ad measures whether cold strangers click and sign up, giving quantitative evidence of interest before you build, while staying cheap because it is a signal test rather than a launch.

title: Check for False Validation Signals
promptText: Help me sanity check whether my validation signals are real or misleading. Here is the evidence I have gathered for my idea [YOUR IDEA]: [PASTE YOUR VALIDATION EVIDENCE, E.G. SIGNUPS, INTEREST, FEEDBACK]. Help me spot false positives: encouragement from friends and family that does not count, vanity signups that will not convert, people saying yes without commitment, and any way I might be fooling myself. Then tell me what stronger evidence I still need before I can trust this idea, and whether my current signals actually justify moving forward. Be direct. My tone is [TONE].
description: Spot false validation signals and identify the stronger evidence you still need.
useCase: Use this before betting on an idea based on early enthusiasm. Friends, vanity signups, and non committal yeses create false confidence, and catching these false positives, then defining the stronger evidence you actually need, protects you from building on validation that is not real.
variables: PASTE YOUR VALIDATION EVIDENCE, E.G. SIGNUPS, INTEREST, FEEDBACK

title: Build a Validation Survey
promptText: Build a survey to help validate my idea with my audience. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], solving [THE PROBLEM]. Write survey questions that surface real signal rather than polite answers: questions about their current behavior and spending on the problem, the severity of the pain, and revealed preferences instead of hypothetical intentions. Keep it short enough that people finish it. Note which questions matter most and how to interpret the answers, including the trap of hypothetical questions that overstate demand. Include one question that reveals genuine urgency. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build a short validation survey that surfaces real signal, not polite answers.
useCase: Use this to gather validation input at scale from your audience. A well built survey focused on actual behavior and spending, rather than hypothetical intent, gives useful signal, while knowing how to interpret the answers keeps you from mistaking politeness for demand.

title: Decide Whether to Pivot or Persevere
promptText: Help me decide whether to pivot, persevere, or drop my idea based on what I have learned. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here is what validation has shown so far: [WHAT YOU HAVE LEARNED]. Help me interpret it honestly: whether the signals justify pushing forward, whether a specific pivot in audience, problem, or solution is warranted by the evidence, or whether the responsible move is to drop it. Lay out the case for each option based on my actual evidence, not optimism. Then give your honest recommendation and the reasoning. My tone is [TONE].
description: Decide whether to pivot, persevere, or drop an idea based on real evidence.
useCase: Use this at the crossroads after gathering validation data. An honest analysis of whether the evidence supports pushing forward, pivoting, or stopping prevents both stubbornly pursuing a dead idea and abandoning a good one too early, grounding the decision in evidence rather than emotion.

title: Run a Pre Mortem on Your Idea
promptText: Run a pre mortem on my idea to surface how it could fail before I commit. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Imagine it is a year from now and the idea failed. Walk backward through the most likely reasons it did not work: wrong audience, weak problem, poor reach, pricing, delivery, competition, or something I am not seeing. For each failure mode, note how likely it is and what I could do now to prevent or de risk it. Be brutally honest, since the point is to find the killers while I can still act. Rank the failure modes by likelihood. My tone is [TONE].
description: Imagine your idea failed and work backward to surface the likely killers.
useCase: Use this before committing to an idea to expose blind spots. Imagining failure and working backward surfaces the risks optimism hides, and identifying the most likely killers while you can still act lets you de risk the idea before betting real time and money on it.

title: Validate an Idea with Your Existing Audience
promptText: Help me validate my idea using the audience I already have. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my existing audience is [DESCRIBE YOUR AUDIENCE, E.G. EMAIL LIST, FOLLOWERS, PAST CLIENTS]. Give me low risk ways to test the idea with them: content that gauges interest, a direct question or poll, a soft offer or early access invite, and reading their engagement as signal. Explain how to test without damaging trust or looking like I am constantly selling, and how to tell real interest from polite engagement. Note the advantage and the bias of testing on a warm audience. My tone is [TONE].
description: Validate an idea using your existing warm audience without damaging trust.
useCase: Use this when you already have an audience to test on. Your existing followers or list can validate an idea fast through content, polls, and soft offers, and this shows how to read genuine interest while accounting for the warm audience bias and protecting trust.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR AUDIENCE, E.G. EMAIL LIST, FOLLOWERS, PAST CLIENTS

title: Define Your Validation Success Metrics
promptText: Help me define upfront what would count as validation for my idea, so I do not move the goalposts later. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the test I am running is [YOUR TEST, E.G. LANDING PAGE, PRESALE, INTERVIEWS]. Help me set clear success criteria before I start: the specific number, rate, or outcome that would count as a genuine pass, what would count as a clear fail, and the murky middle and how to handle it. Setting the bar in advance stops me from rationalizing weak results into a yes. Give me the concrete threshold to commit to. My tone is [TONE].
description: Define clear pass or fail validation criteria before you run the test.
useCase: Use this before running any validation test to avoid rationalizing weak results. Setting a concrete success threshold in advance stops you from moving the goalposts when the data disappoints, keeping your validation honest and your decision grounded in the bar you committed to.
variables: YOUR TEST, E.G. LANDING PAGE, PRESALE, INTERVIEWS

title: Validate by Narrowing Your Niche
promptText: Help me test whether narrowing my idea to a tighter niche improves its chances. My current idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [BROAD AUDIENCE]. Help me explore niching down: which specific segment within my audience has the most acute version of the problem, would pay the most, and is easiest to reach and convince. Suggest 3 narrower niches to consider, the case for each, and how validating within one tight niche first is often easier and more revealing than testing broadly. Then recommend the strongest niche to validate first and why. My tone is [TONE].
description: Test whether narrowing to a tighter niche makes your idea easier to validate.
useCase: Use this when a broad idea is hard to validate or sell. Narrowing to the segment with the most acute problem often makes an idea far easier to prove and market, and testing within one tight niche first gives clearer signal than a broad audience.

title: Plan Your Next Validation Steps
promptText: Help me build a step by step validation plan for my idea from where I am now. My idea is [YOUR IDEA] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and so far I have [WHAT YOU HAVE DONE OR LEARNED SO FAR]. My constraints are [YOUR TIME AND BUDGET]. Give me an ordered validation roadmap: the specific tests to run in sequence from cheapest and riskiest assumption first, what each should prove, the decision gate after each, and when I would have enough confidence to build. Make it realistic for a solo operator. Keep me from either overthinking validation or skipping it entirely. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build an ordered, realistic validation roadmap from where you are now.
useCase: Use this to turn validation from vague worry into a clear plan. An ordered roadmap that tests the riskiest assumptions first with decision gates keeps a solo operator from both endless overthinking and reckless building, so you reach a confident build or drop decision efficiently.
variables: WHAT YOU HAVE DONE OR LEARNED SO FAR

title: Generate Business Name Ideas
promptText: Generate 25 business name ideas for my business. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and what I do is [WHAT YOU DO]. My brand personality is [BRAND PERSONALITY], and names I like or dislike are [NAMES YOU LIKE OR DISLIKE OR WRITE NONE]. Give me a mix: some descriptive that say what I do, some evocative or metaphorical, some invented or coined words, and some based on a real word with a twist. For each, note the style and why it could work. Keep them easy to say, spell, and remember. Mark the 5 strongest and flag any that might be hard to trademark or find a domain for.
description: Generate a wide range of business name ideas across naming styles.
useCase: Use this when starting a business or rebrand and staring at a blank page. A broad set of names across different styles gives you real options to react to, and reacting to specific names clarifies your direction faster than abstract brainstorming.
variables: NAMES YOU LIKE OR DISLIKE OR WRITE NONE

title: Explore Different Naming Styles
promptText: Help me explore different naming styles for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. What I do is [WHAT YOU DO]. Walk me through the main naming approaches with 4 to 5 example names for each applied to my business: descriptive names that state what I do, evocative names that suggest a feeling, invented or coined names, founder or personal names, compound names that join two words, and metaphorical names. For each style, explain the tradeoffs, such as memorability versus clarity or trademark ease versus distinctiveness. Then recommend which style best fits my brand and why.
description: Explore each naming style with examples and tradeoffs for your business.
useCase: Use this early in naming to understand your options before committing to a direction. Seeing each style applied to your business with its tradeoffs helps you choose an approach deliberately rather than defaulting to the first idea that sounds okay.

title: Weigh Descriptive Versus Abstract Names
promptText: Help me decide between a descriptive name and an abstract name for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. What I do is [WHAT YOU DO], and my growth plans are [YOUR PLANS, E.G. STAY NICHE OR EXPAND BROADLY]. Give me: 5 strong descriptive name options that clearly say what I do, 5 strong abstract or evocative options that are more brandable, the pros and cons of each direction for my specific situation, and a recommendation based on my growth plans. Explain how a descriptive name helps early discovery but can limit later expansion, and how an abstract name is harder to launch but more ownable long term.
description: Weigh the descriptive versus abstract name tradeoff for your specific plans.
useCase: Use this when torn between a name that explains what you do and one that is more brandable. The right choice depends on your growth plans, and understanding how each affects early discovery versus long term flexibility prevents a naming regret you cannot easily undo later.
variables: YOUR PLANS, E.G. STAY NICHE OR EXPAND BROADLY

title: Evaluate Your Name Shortlist
promptText: Help me evaluate my shortlist of business name candidates. The names are [YOUR SHORTLIST]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my brand should feel [BRAND PERSONALITY]. Score each name across the criteria that matter: memorability, ease of spelling and pronunciation, distinctiveness from competitors, brand fit, room to grow, and likely domain and trademark availability. Give each a quick rating and honest notes on its strengths and risks. Then rank them and tell me your top pick with reasoning. Be direct about which names have real problems.
description: Score and rank your name shortlist across the criteria that matter.
useCase: Use this when you have narrowed to a few candidates but cannot decide. An objective evaluation across memorability, distinctiveness, and practical factors like trademark risk cuts through the emotional attachment that makes naming decisions hard and surfaces the strongest genuine choice.

title: Plan Around Domain Availability
promptText: Help me handle domain availability for my business name. My favorite names are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES], but I am worried the exact dot com is taken. Give me strategies: how to check and think about domain options, creative variations that keep the name strong when the exact match is unavailable such as adding a word, using a different extension, or a slight tweak, when it is worth pursuing a taken domain versus adapting, and how much a perfect domain actually matters today. For each of my candidates, suggest domain approaches. Be practical about what a solo business really needs versus nice to have.
description: Plan practical domain options when your ideal name's dot com is taken.
useCase: Use this when a name you love has a taken domain. Practical strategies for variations and extensions let you keep a strong name without overpaying for the exact match, and understanding how much the perfect domain really matters keeps you from discarding good names needlessly.

title: Spot Trademark Risks in a Name
promptText: Help me think through potential trademark risks with my business name candidates before I commit. The names are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES], and my business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] in [YOUR INDUSTRY]. Point out general risk factors to consider: names that seem similar to well known brands, names that are generic and hard to protect, and names likely to have crowded trademark space in my field. Suggest what to search for and what would make a name safer or riskier to build on. Be clear that this is general guidance to help me screen candidates, not legal advice, and that I should consult a trademark attorney before finalizing anything.
description: Screen name candidates for general trademark risk factors before committing.
useCase: Use this to catch obvious trademark problems early in the naming process. Screening candidates for risk factors before you invest in branding avoids the costly mistake of building a business on a name you may have to abandon, while still leaving the legal clearance to a professional.

title: Test a Name for Pronunciation and Spelling
promptText: Help me test my business name candidates for pronunciation and spelling problems. The names are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES]. For each, tell me: whether people will know how to say it on first sight, whether they can spell it correctly after hearing it aloud, any ambiguity that could cause confusion, and whether it works when said over the phone or in a podcast. Flag names that will constantly need spelling out or correcting, since that friction hurts word of mouth and discovery. Rank them from easiest to hardest on this dimension and note any simple fixes.
description: Test name candidates for the pronunciation and spelling friction that hurts growth.
useCase: Use this before finalizing a name, since pronunciation and spelling friction quietly hurts word of mouth. A name people cannot say or spell after hearing it creates constant small barriers to being found and referred, and catching this early avoids a persistent drag on growth.

title: Test How a Name Sounds in Real Sentences
promptText: Help me test how my business name candidates sound in real use. The names are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES], and my business is a [BUSINESS TYPE]. For each name, show me how it sounds in the real sentences I will say constantly: answering the phone, introducing myself at an event, in an email signature, and in a tagline. Flag any name that sounds awkward, unclear, or unprofessional in these everyday contexts, or that clashes with common words. A name can look good written down but feel wrong spoken, so I want to catch that now. Rank them by how naturally they work in real speech.
description: Test how each name candidate sounds in the sentences you will say daily.
useCase: Use this to catch names that look fine written but feel wrong spoken. Testing a name in the real phrases you will repeat constantly, like answering the phone or introducing yourself, reveals awkwardness that only shows up in speech, before you commit to saying it thousands of times.

title: Decide Between Your Name and a Brand Name
promptText: Help me decide whether to name my business after myself or create a separate brand name. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], my name is [YOUR NAME], and my long term vision is [YOUR VISION, E.G. STAY SOLO, BUILD A TEAM, OR SELL ONE DAY]. Give me the case for using my personal name, the case for a separate brand name, and how each choice affects personal branding, scaling, hiring, selling the business later, and my ability to step back. Then recommend which fits my vision and explain the reasoning. Note any hybrid approach that could work.
description: Decide between naming the business after yourself or building a separate brand.
useCase: Use this pivotal decision when starting a solo business. Whether to use your own name or a brand name shapes your ability to scale, hire, step back, or sell later, and thinking it through against your long term vision prevents a choice that limits options you will want down the road.
variables: YOUR VISION, E.G. STAY SOLO, BUILD A TEAM, OR SELL ONE DAY

title: Check if a Name Will Grow With You
promptText: Help me check whether my business name candidates will still fit as my business grows. The names are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES], I currently do [WHAT YOU DO NOW], and I might expand into [POSSIBLE FUTURE DIRECTIONS]. For each name, assess: whether it locks me into one service, location, or niche, whether it leaves room to add offerings or shift focus later, and whether it would feel wrong if my business evolved. Flag names that are too narrow for my ambitions and ones that stay flexible. Explain how to balance a name that is specific enough to be clear now with one that is broad enough to grow into.
description: Check whether name candidates leave room for your business to evolve.
useCase: Use this before committing to a name if you have any ambition to grow or evolve. A name tied too tightly to one service or location can force a costly rebrand later, and checking for this room to grow now saves you from outgrowing your own name.

title: Make Your Name Stand Out from Competitors
promptText: Help me choose a business name that stands out in my market. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and here are the names of my main competitors: [COMPETITOR NAMES]. Analyze the naming patterns competitors use, such as common words, styles, or themes they all share. Then generate 15 name ideas for me that deliberately break from those patterns to stand out, while still fitting my industry. Explain how sounding different from competitors helps me get noticed and remembered. Mark the ideas that best balance distinctiveness with still feeling appropriate for my field.
description: Generate a name that breaks from competitor naming patterns to stand out.
useCase: Use this when competitors in your space all sound alike. Analyzing their shared naming patterns then deliberately breaking from them produces a name that stands out and gets remembered, turning a naming choice into a real differentiation advantage.

title: Generate Invented Word Names
promptText: Help me create invented or coined word names for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. I want something ownable and brandable that is not a real dictionary word. Generate 20 coined name ideas using techniques like blending two relevant words, modifying a real word, using evocative sounds or roots, or adding a suffix. The words should feel [DESIRED FEELING, E.G. MODERN, TRUSTWORTHY, PLAYFUL] and connect loosely to [RELEVANT CONCEPTS OR WORDS]. For each, note what it evokes and how to pronounce it. Coined names are more trademark and domain friendly, so mark the ones that feel most natural to say despite being invented.
description: Generate ownable invented word names that are trademark and domain friendly.
useCase: Use this when you want a highly ownable, brandable name. Invented words are far easier to trademark and secure a domain for than real words, and generating them from relevant roots and sounds gives you distinctive options that competitors cannot copy or crowd.
variables: DESIRED FEELING, E.G. MODERN, TRUSTWORTHY, PLAYFUL

title: Build Compound and Blended Names
promptText: Help me build compound and blended business names for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The key concepts or words relevant to my business are [RELEVANT WORDS OR CONCEPTS]. Generate 20 names by combining or blending these and related words in different ways: full compounds joining two words, blends merging parts of two words, and word plus modifier combinations. For each, note the two ideas it joins and whether it reads cleanly. Compound names can be descriptive and brandable at once, so mark the ones that are easy to say, not too long, and still feel distinctive rather than clunky.
description: Build compound and blended names that are descriptive and brandable at once.
useCase: Use this to create names that both hint at what you do and stay brandable. Combining relevant words into clean compounds or blends is a reliable way to generate distinctive names, and this produces many combinations to find the ones that read smoothly rather than clunky.

title: Find the Story Behind a Name
promptText: Help me find or shape the story and meaning behind my chosen business name [YOUR NAME]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and what I stand for is [YOUR VALUES OR MISSION]. Help me articulate: what the name can mean or symbolize in connection to my mission, a short origin story I can tell about why I chose it, and how to explain its meaning in a way that adds depth to my brand. Even if the name was somewhat arbitrary, help me find a genuine, non forced meaning I can stand behind. A name with a story is more memorable and gives people a reason to care.
description: Shape a genuine story and meaning behind your chosen business name.
useCase: Use this once you have a name to give it depth and memorability. A genuine story behind a name makes it stick and gives customers a reason to care, and articulating the meaning, even for a name that started arbitrary, turns a label into part of your brand narrative.

title: Pair a Tagline with Your Name
promptText: Help me pair a tagline with my business name [YOUR NAME] to clarify what I do. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I help them [WHAT YOU DO]. This matters especially if my name is abstract and does not say what I do. Give me 10 tagline options that work alongside the name to make my offering clear and appealing, ranging from purely descriptive to more evocative. Each should be short and pair naturally with the name. Mark the ones that best fill the clarity gap the name leaves, and show how the name and tagline read together as a lockup.
description: Pair a clarifying tagline with your name, especially if the name is abstract.
useCase: Use this when your name is memorable but does not explain what you do. A tagline paired with the name fills that clarity gap, letting you have a brandable abstract name while still telling people instantly what you offer, which is how strong brands get both distinctiveness and clarity.

title: Name Your Products or Offers
promptText: Help me name my products or offers under my business [YOUR BUSINESS NAME]. My offers are [DESCRIBE YOUR OFFERS], serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Generate name ideas for each offer that fit together as a coherent family and complement the parent brand. Give me options that follow a consistent naming logic across the line so customers see how they relate, while each name still signals what that specific offer does or who it is for. Note the naming system or pattern you used so I can name future offers consistently. Mark the strongest name for each offer.
description: Name your products or offers as a coherent family under your brand.
useCase: Use this when naming multiple offers so they feel like a related family. A consistent naming system across your product line signals professionalism, helps customers understand how offers relate, and gives you a pattern to name future offers, rather than a jumble of unrelated names.

title: Plan a Business Rename
promptText: Help me think through renaming my business. My current name is [CURRENT NAME] and I want to change it because [REASON FOR RENAME]. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me: decide whether renaming is truly worth the disruption, what I would gain versus lose in recognition and equity, how to choose a stronger new name that fixes the current problem, and how to transition without losing existing customers or search presence. Generate a few new name directions that address my reason for changing. Be honest if my current name is fine and the rename is not worth it.
description: Weigh whether to rename your business and plan the transition if you do.
useCase: Use this when your current name feels wrong but renaming is disruptive. Weighing the real gain against the lost recognition, and planning the transition carefully, ensures a rename actually solves the problem rather than trading one issue for the cost of starting brand recognition over.

title: Plan How to Test a Name With Your Audience
promptText: Help me test my business name candidates with real people before I commit. My candidates are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES], and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Give me a simple way to gather useful feedback: the right questions to ask that reveal genuine reactions rather than polite approval, who to ask and who to ignore, how to test first impressions and memorability, and how to interpret the responses without just going with the crowd. Warn me about the trap of testing names to death or letting a committee pick a bland safe option. Give me a lightweight test plan I can run quickly.
description: Plan a lightweight test to get honest name feedback without over testing.
useCase: Use this to sanity check a name with real people before committing. A focused test that asks the right questions catches problems you cannot see yourself, while a lightweight approach avoids the trap of testing a name to death or letting a committee water it down to something bland.

title: Check the First Impression a Name Gives
promptText: Help me understand the first impression each of my business name candidates gives. The names are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES], and I want my brand to feel [DESIRED BRAND FEELING]. For each name, tell me honestly what it likely signals to someone hearing it cold: the vibe, the perceived quality or price level, the kind of business they would assume it is, and any unintended associations or connotations. Flag any name that sends the wrong signal or could be misread. Rank them by how well their instant impression matches the brand feeling I want. Point out any name that feels cheaper, more generic, or different from my intent.
description: Check the instant impression and connotations each name candidate gives.
useCase: Use this to catch names that send the wrong signal before you commit. A name creates an instant impression of quality, price, and business type, and checking that each candidate's connotations match your intended brand feeling prevents a name that quietly misrepresents you or carries unintended associations.

title: Decide on a Location Based Name
promptText: Help me decide whether to include my location in my business name. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [CITY OR AREA], and my growth plans are [YOUR PLANS, E.G. STAY LOCAL OR EXPAND BEYOND]. Give me: the benefits of a location in the name such as local trust and local search relevance, the drawbacks such as limiting expansion or sounding generic, some strong location based name options and some location free alternatives for my business, and a recommendation based on my plans. Explain how a local name helps a local business get found but can trap a business that later wants to grow beyond the area.
description: Decide whether to put your location in your name based on your growth plans.
useCase: Use this for a local business weighing whether to name after its area. A location in the name boosts local trust and search relevance but can limit expansion, so deciding deliberately based on your growth plans captures the local benefit or preserves your freedom to grow beyond it.
variables: YOUR PLANS, E.G. STAY LOCAL OR EXPAND BEYOND

title: Plan Social Handles Around Your Name
promptText: Help me plan social media handles for my business name candidates. The names are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES], and the platforms I care about are [YOUR PLATFORMS]. Give me: how to think about handle availability and consistency across platforms, creative handle variations to try when the exact name is taken such as adding a prefix or word, how much matching handles across platforms actually matters for a solo business, and which of my candidates are likely to have cleaner handle availability. Suggest a consistent handle approach I could use. Be practical about what matters versus what is just nice to have.
description: Plan consistent social handles for your name candidates across platforms.
useCase: Use this alongside domain checking when finalizing a name. Consistent social handles make a brand easier to find and look more professional, and planning handle variations for when the exact name is taken lets you keep a strong name while still securing a clean, findable presence.

title: Make the Final Naming Decision
promptText: Help me make a final decision between my top business name candidates because I am stuck. My finalists are [YOUR FINALISTS], my business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my gut is leaning toward [YOUR LEANING OR WRITE UNSURE]. Give me a clear decision framework: the few criteria that truly matter most for my situation, how each finalist scores on them, which practical factors like domain or trademark should break a tie, and permission to stop overthinking once a good enough name clears the bar. Then give me your recommendation with honest reasoning. Help me commit and move forward rather than endlessly deliberating.
description: Use a clear framework to commit to a final name and stop deliberating.
useCase: Use this when you are stuck deliberating between good name finalists. A decision framework that weights what truly matters and lets practical factors break ties helps you commit, since endless naming deliberation stalls the actual business and a good name acted on beats a perfect name never chosen.

title: Check if a Name Fits Your Industry
promptText: Help me check whether my business name candidates fit the expectations of my industry. The names are [YOUR NAME CANDIDATES], and my industry is [YOUR INDUSTRY], serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. For each name, tell me: whether it signals credibility and belonging in my field or feels out of place, whether it matches the tone my audience expects such as professional, creative, or approachable, and whether it strikes the right balance between fitting in enough to be trusted and standing out enough to be noticed. Flag any name that is too casual, too corporate, or otherwise mismatched for my industry. Rank them by industry fit.
description: Check whether each name fits your industry's tone and credibility expectations.
useCase: Use this to ensure a name signals belonging in your field. A name that clashes with industry expectations can undermine trust before a prospect learns anything else, and checking each candidate's fit balances standing out with the credibility that comes from feeling appropriate for your field.

title: Generate Names from Your Values and Mission
promptText: Help me generate business names rooted in my values and mission rather than just what I do. My business is a [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. What I deeply believe or stand for is [YOUR VALUES OR MISSION], and the change I want to create is [YOUR MISSION OR IMPACT]. Generate 20 name ideas that draw on these values, the feeling I want my brand to evoke, and the deeper why behind my business, not just the surface service. Explain the meaning or connection behind each. Mark the ones that best capture my mission while still being practical, memorable, and easy to use. I want a name with genuine meaning.
description: Generate meaningful names rooted in your values and mission, not just your service.
useCase: Use this when you want a name with genuine meaning rather than a descriptive label. Names rooted in your values and mission carry a deeper story that resonates and lasts, and generating from your why produces options that reflect what your business truly stands for.

title: Choose the Right Pricing Model
promptText: Help me choose the right pricing model for my offer. I am a [BUSINESS TYPE] and my offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], delivering [OUTCOME]. Walk me through the main pricing models that could fit: one time, hourly, project or flat rate, retainer, subscription, tiered, value based, and usage based. For each relevant one, explain how it would work for my offer, the pros and cons for me and the customer, and which suits my situation best. Then recommend a primary model with reasoning, and note whether a hybrid makes sense. My tone is [TONE].
description: Choose the pricing model that best fits your offer and situation.
useCase: Use this when deciding how to structure pricing for an offer. The model you choose, whether hourly, flat, retainer, or value based, shapes your income stability and how customers perceive value, so matching it deliberately to your offer avoids leaving money and clarity on the table.

title: Set Your Actual Price Point
promptText: Help me set the actual price for my offer. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], delivering [OUTCOME AND VALUE]. What similar offers charge is [MARKET RANGE OR WRITE NOT SURE], my costs and time are [YOUR COSTS OR EFFORT], and my positioning goal is [POSITIONING, E.G. PREMIUM, MID, OR ACCESSIBLE]. Help me arrive at a specific price or range: what the value justifies, what the market supports, what my positioning demands, and where my costs set the floor. Give me a recommended price with reasoning, and flag if I am likely underpricing. My tone is [TONE].
description: Arrive at a specific price for your offer grounded in value, market, and positioning.
useCase: Use this when you have an offer but do not know what to charge. Triangulating from value, market rates, positioning, and your cost floor produces a defensible price, and the underpricing flag catches the most common and costly mistake solo businesses make.
variables: MARKET RANGE OR WRITE NOT SURE
POSITIONING, E.G. PREMIUM, MID, OR ACCESSIBLE

title: Price Based on Value, Not Time
promptText: Help me price my offer based on the value it creates rather than the time it takes. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The tangible value it delivers is [THE VALUE, E.G. REVENUE GAINED, TIME SAVED, OR COST AVOIDED], and the emotional or strategic value is [DEEPER VALUE]. Help me: quantify the value in the customer's terms, calculate a price that captures a fair share of that value, and frame the price against the value so it feels like an obvious return. Show me how value based pricing can dramatically raise what I charge versus cost plus or hourly. Note when value based pricing works and when it does not. My tone is [TONE].
description: Price your offer on the value it creates rather than the hours it takes.
useCase: Use this to escape the ceiling of hourly or cost based pricing. Quantifying the value your offer creates and capturing a fair share of it often justifies a far higher price, since customers pay for the outcome, not your time, and this reframes pricing around what they actually gain.
variables: THE VALUE, E.G. REVENUE GAINED, TIME SAVED, OR COST AVOIDED

title: Move from Hourly to Fixed or Value Pricing
promptText: Help me transition from hourly pricing to fixed or value based pricing. I currently charge [YOUR HOURLY RATE] per hour for [YOUR SERVICE], serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Hourly caps my income and penalizes my efficiency. Help me: package my service into a fixed price offer, calculate a fair fixed price that reflects value rather than hours, handle the scope so I do not lose money on efficiency, and explain the change to existing clients without alarming them. Show me why moving off hourly benefits both me and the client. Note the risks to manage in the shift. My tone is [TONE].
description: Transition from hourly billing to fixed or value based pricing that rewards efficiency.
useCase: Use this when hourly billing is capping your income and punishing your speed. Packaging into a fixed price rewards your efficiency instead of penalizing it and removes the client's incentive to watch the clock, and this handles both the pricing math and the client conversation the shift requires.

title: Design Tiered Pricing
promptText: Help me design tiered pricing for my offer. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and what I can include or vary across tiers is [WHAT YOU CAN OFFER]. Design three tiers, good, better, and best, where the middle is the intended choice: for each give a name, what is included, who it suits, and a price or price logic. Use pricing psychology so the tiers anchor value and guide most buyers to the middle while the top tier makes the middle look reasonable. Explain the reasoning behind the structure and the gaps between tiers. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design good better best tiers that anchor value and guide buyers to the middle.
useCase: Use this to structure pricing into tiers that raise your average sale. A well designed three tier structure anchors value with the top option and steers most buyers to a profitable middle, converting better than a single take it or leave it price by giving customers a choice they control.

title: Use Price Anchoring Honestly
promptText: Help me use price anchoring honestly to make my offer's price feel reasonable. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [PRICE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Show me legitimate anchoring approaches: anchoring against the cost of the problem staying unsolved, against the value delivered, against a higher tier or premium option, or against the cost of alternatives. For each, give me the framing and language to establish the anchor before revealing my price so the number feels small by comparison. Keep it honest with real comparisons, not fabricated inflated prices. Explain why anchoring shapes perceived value. My tone is [TONE].
description: Use honest price anchoring so your price feels reasonable by comparison.
useCase: Use this to make a price feel reasonable before you reveal it. Establishing an honest anchor, whether the cost of inaction, the value delivered, or a premium option, shapes how buyers perceive your number, so the same price feels like a bargain rather than a stretch, without any deceptive fake discounts.

title: Check if You Are Underpricing
promptText: Help me honestly assess whether I am underpricing my offer. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [CURRENT PRICE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], delivering [OUTCOME AND VALUE]. Signs I have noticed are [ANY SIGNS, E.G. EVERYONE SAYS YES INSTANTLY, I FEEL RESENTFUL, OR CLIENTS SEEM SURPRISED IT IS SO CHEAP]. Assess: whether my price reflects the value I deliver, the signals that suggest I am charging too little, what raising my price could do to my income and client quality, and roughly how much room I likely have to raise it. Be direct if I am significantly underpricing, since undercharging is the most common solo business mistake. My tone is [TONE].
description: Get an honest assessment of whether you are charging too little.
useCase: Use this if you suspect you are undercharging, which most solo operators are. An honest read of the signals, like instant yeses or your own resentment, and how much room you have to raise prices catches the most common and costly pricing mistake before it caps your income for years.
variables: ANY SIGNS, E.G. EVERYONE SAYS YES INSTANTLY, I FEEL RESENTFUL, OR CLIENTS SEEM SURPRISED IT IS SO CHEAP

title: Plan a Price Increase
promptText: Help me plan a price increase. My offer is [YOUR OFFER], currently priced at [CURRENT PRICE], and I want to move toward [TARGET PRICE OR WRITE NOT SURE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me: decide on the right new price and increase size, time the increase well, decide whether to grandfather existing clients or raise their rates too, and communicate it confidently without over apologizing. Give me the plan and the reasoning. Also give me the exact language to announce the increase to existing clients in a way that holds the relationship. Note how to handle any pushback. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan and communicate a price increase without losing clients or nerve.
useCase: Use this when you know you need to charge more but dread the change. A clear plan for the new price, timing, grandfathering, and confident communication removes the fear that keeps solo operators underpriced for years, and the ready to use announcement language protects the client relationships through the change.
variables: TARGET PRICE OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Write a Price Increase Announcement
promptText: Write an announcement telling existing clients about my price increase. My offer is [YOUR OFFER], the price is moving from [CURRENT PRICE] to [NEW PRICE], effective [WHEN], for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The honest reason is [REASON, E.G. GROWING DEMAND, RISING VALUE, OR COSTS]. Write it to: lead with appreciation and what stays the same for them, state the change and reason plainly without over apologizing, spell out exactly what it means and when, offer any grandfathering or transition if applicable, and reinforce the value they get. Confident and warm, not defensive. Give me one firmer version and one softer version. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write a confident, warm price increase announcement for existing clients.
useCase: Use this to announce a price increase without souring client relationships. A message that leads with appreciation, states the change plainly, and reinforces value retains far more goodwill than an apologetic or defensive one, and having it written removes the anxiety that makes people avoid raising prices at all.
variables: REASON, E.G. GROWING DEMAND, RISING VALUE, OR COSTS

title: Analyze Competitor Pricing
promptText: Help me analyze competitor pricing to position my own. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Here is what I know about competitor pricing: [COMPETITOR PRICES AND WHAT THEY INCLUDE]. Help me interpret it: where my offer fits in the market range, whether to price above, at, or below competitors and why, how to avoid a race to the bottom, and how to justify a higher price through differentiation rather than matching low prices. Warn me against simply undercutting. Recommend a positioning and price approach based on the landscape and my strengths, which are [YOUR STRENGTHS]. My tone is [TONE].
description: Analyze competitor pricing to position yours without racing to the bottom.
useCase: Use this to price relative to competitors intelligently. Understanding where you fit and choosing to compete on differentiated value rather than the lowest price protects your margins, since undercutting starts a race to the bottom that a solo business rarely survives.
variables: COMPETITOR PRICES AND WHAT THEY INCLUDE

title: Position Your Price Strategically
promptText: Help me decide where to position my price in the market and align everything with it. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I am considering positioning as [POSITIONING, E.G. PREMIUM, MID MARKET, OR BUDGET]. Help me: understand what each positioning signals and attracts, what customers, margins, and volume each implies, whether premium or budget better fits my strengths and goals, and how price positioning must align with my branding, marketing, and quality signals to be believable. Recommend the positioning that best serves my business and explain how to make the whole experience match the price. My tone is [TONE].
description: Choose a price positioning and align your whole brand experience with it.
useCase: Use this to decide whether to be the premium, mid, or budget option. Price positioning determines the customers, margins, and volume you attract, and this helps you choose deliberately then align your branding and experience so the price is believable rather than at odds with everything else.
variables: POSITIONING, E.G. PREMIUM, MID MARKET, OR BUDGET

title: Justify Premium Pricing
promptText: Help me justify and confidently charge premium pricing for my offer. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [PREMIUM PRICE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and what makes it worth a premium is [YOUR PREMIUM VALUE OR DIFFERENTIATORS]. Help me: build the case for why my offer commands a premium, identify what to emphasize so buyers feel the higher price is justified, spot any gaps where the experience does not yet match the price, and develop the confidence to state a premium price without flinching. Explain how premium buyers think differently from bargain hunters. Give me the value narrative that supports the premium. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build the case and confidence to charge and defend premium pricing.
useCase: Use this when you want to charge premium but feel unsure you can defend it. Building the value case, closing gaps between price and experience, and developing the confidence to state the number without flinching is what lets you serve premium buyers, who think differently and often prefer the higher priced option.

title: Frame How You Present Your Price
promptText: Help me frame how I present my price so it lands as well as possible. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [PRICE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], delivering [VALUE]. Show me presentation techniques that ethically improve how the price is received: establishing value before the number, breaking the price into smaller units where it helps, comparing it to relevant everyday costs, bundling versus itemizing, and the words around the number that build or undermine confidence. Give me the specific framing and language to present my price in a proposal or conversation. Keep it honest, not manipulative. My tone is [TONE].
description: Frame and present your price so it lands with maximum confidence and clarity.
useCase: Use this to present a price so it is received well, since how you frame a number matters as much as the number. Establishing value first, choosing the right comparisons, and using confident language around the price improves acceptance ethically, whereas fumbling the presentation can sink a fair price.

title: Build Confidence in Your Pricing
promptText: Help me build genuine confidence in my pricing so I stop undercharging or caving. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [PRICE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I struggle with [YOUR PRICING STRUGGLE, E.G. QUOTING NERVOUSLY, DISCOUNTING TOO FAST, OR FEELING IT IS TOO MUCH]. Help me: reframe my mindset around what my price represents and the value behind it, prepare language to state my price calmly and hold it, recognize that the right clients respect confident pricing, and stop the self sabotage of pre discounting or apologizing. Give me the internal reframes and the external phrases to price with confidence. My tone is [TONE].
description: Build the mindset and language to state and hold your price with confidence.
useCase: Use this if you undercharge, discount too fast, or quote nervously. The reframes and ready language to state a price calmly and hold it address the confidence problem that undermines pricing more than the numbers themselves, since clients take their cue on your value from how you carry your price.
variables: YOUR PRICING STRUGGLE, E.G. QUOTING NERVOUSLY, DISCOUNTING TOO FAST, OR FEELING IT IS TOO MUCH

title: Prepare for Price Objections
promptText: Help me prepare for price objections on my offer so I am not caught off guard. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [PRICE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Anticipate the price objections I will face, such as too expensive, no budget, or it costs less elsewhere, given my context [YOUR CONTEXT]. For each likely objection, give me the calm confident response that reframes around value rather than defending or discounting, and a clarifying question to uncover the real concern. Help me hold my price without becoming defensive. Give me a prep sheet I can review before pricing conversations. My tone is [TONE].
description: Prepare confident responses to the price objections you will face.
useCase: Use this before pricing conversations so objections do not rattle you. A prep sheet of confident value based responses to too expensive, no budget, and costs less elsewhere lets you handle each calmly instead of reflexively discounting, which is where fair prices are most often lost.

title: Design a Discount Policy
promptText: Help me design a discount policy so I stop discounting reactively. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [PRICE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me decide: when if ever a discount is warranted, what to ask for in return so a discount is a trade not a giveaway, such as a longer commitment, upfront payment, case study, or referral, how to offer a smaller scope at a lower price instead of discounting the full offer, and how to hold firm gracefully when no discount is justified. Give me a clear policy I can follow so discounting becomes a deliberate strategy rather than a reflex. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design a deliberate discount policy so discounts become trades, not reflexes.
useCase: Use this to stop discounting reactively and protect your margins. A clear policy that treats discounts as trades for commitment or referrals, or offers a smaller scope instead, turns discounting into a deliberate tool rather than a reflex that trains buyers to always push for less.

title: Design Your Payment Terms
promptText: Help me design payment terms for my offer that improve cash flow and reduce risk. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at [PRICE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the delivery timeline is [TIMELINE]. Help me decide: whether to require a deposit or full upfront payment, how to structure installments or milestones if relevant, whether to offer a discount for paying in full upfront, and how to protect myself from non payment. Explain how each choice affects my cash flow and the client's willingness to say yes. Recommend payment terms that balance getting paid well with staying easy to buy from. Note this is general business guidance, not legal advice. My tone is [TONE].
description: Design payment terms that improve cash flow while staying easy to buy from.
useCase: Use this to structure how and when you get paid. Deposits, milestones, and upfront discounts protect your cash flow and reduce non payment risk, and designing terms that balance getting paid well with remaining easy to buy from is a quiet but major factor in a solo business staying solvent.

title: Write Pricing Page Copy
promptText: Write copy for my pricing page. My offer or tiers are [YOUR OFFER OR TIERS AND PRICES] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], delivering [OUTCOME]. Write a pricing page that: leads with value before the numbers, presents each option clearly with what is included and who it suits, highlights the recommended choice, addresses the main hesitation, and drives to a clear next step. Make the pricing feel justified and the choice easy. Include a short reassurance element like a guarantee or FAQ line if relevant: [GUARANTEE OR REASSURANCE OR WRITE NONE]. Give me the full page copy. My tone is [TONE].
description: Write pricing page copy that justifies the price and makes choosing easy.
useCase: Use this for the pricing page where buyers decide. Copy that leads with value, presents options clearly, highlights a recommended choice, and handles hesitation converts far better than a bare price table, since the pricing page is often the last thing standing between interest and purchase.
variables: YOUR OFFER OR TIERS AND PRICES
GUARANTEE OR REASSURANCE OR WRITE NONE

title: Apply Pricing Psychology
promptText: Help me apply pricing psychology to my offer ethically. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] at roughly [PRICE OR RANGE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Explain the psychological pricing tactics worth considering for my situation: charm pricing versus round numbers and when each suits a premium versus value brand, the decoy effect, anchoring, the way price endings signal quality or discount, and reducing the pain of paying. For each, tell me whether it fits my positioning and how to apply it. Keep every tactic honest and appropriate for my brand, not manipulative. Recommend which few tactics to actually use. My tone is [TONE].
description: Apply honest pricing psychology tactics that fit your brand and positioning.
useCase: Use this to make small pricing choices work in your favor. Whether to use charm pricing or round numbers, decoys, or specific price endings measurably affects perception, and knowing which tactics fit your positioning lets you apply pricing psychology ethically rather than leaving these levers to chance.

title: Plan Launch or Early Bird Pricing
promptText: Help me plan launch pricing for my new offer. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my eventual regular price is [TARGET PRICE OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Help me decide: whether to use early bird or launch pricing to drive initial sales and proof, how much to discount for early buyers without undervaluing the offer, how to structure the price increase from launch to regular price, and how to communicate the limited launch pricing honestly to create real urgency. Explain the tradeoffs of launching low versus at full price. Give me a launch pricing plan with the timeline and prices. My tone is [TONE].
description: Plan launch or early bird pricing that drives initial sales without undervaluing.
useCase: Use this when launching a new offer and deciding on introductory pricing. A structured early bird plan drives initial sales and proof through honest urgency, and planning the ramp from launch to regular price captures early momentum without permanently anchoring your offer too low.
variables: TARGET PRICE OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Check Your Profit Margins
promptText: Help me check whether my pricing actually leaves healthy profit. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] priced at [PRICE], and my costs to deliver it are [YOUR COSTS, INCLUDING TIME, TOOLS, AND EXPENSES]. Help me: calculate my real margin including the value of my own time, identify hidden costs I may be ignoring like revisions, admin, or acquisition, determine whether this price is genuinely profitable at the volume I can handle, and find where my pricing or delivery is quietly eating my profit. Be direct if my margins are too thin to sustain the business. Recommend a price or cost adjustment if needed. My tone is [TONE].
description: Check whether your pricing leaves healthy profit after all real costs.
useCase: Use this to confirm your prices are actually profitable, not just revenue. Calculating real margin including your time and hidden costs like revisions and admin reveals whether a price sustains the business, catching the trap of being busy and broke because thin margins quietly eat every sale.
variables: YOUR COSTS, INCLUDING TIME, TOOLS, AND EXPENSES

title: Price a Bundle
promptText: Help me price a bundle of my offers. The offers in the bundle are [OFFERS AND THEIR INDIVIDUAL PRICES] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me: set a bundle price that feels like a clear deal while raising my average sale, decide how much of a discount versus buying separately makes sense, frame the savings so the bundle is the obvious smart choice, and avoid discounting so deeply that I undercut my individual offer prices. Explain how bundle pricing psychology works and what savings level motivates without devaluing. Recommend a bundle price with the reasoning. My tone is [TONE].
description: Price a bundle so it feels like a deal while raising your average sale.
useCase: Use this when combining offers into a bundle. Setting a bundle price that shows clear savings while protecting your individual prices raises average order value and gives customers a complete solution, but only when the discount motivates without devaluing what the components cost alone.

title: Design Subscription or Recurring Pricing
promptText: Help me design pricing for my subscription or recurring offer. My offer is [YOUR RECURRING OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], delivering [ONGOING VALUE].
