Give me one version that leans warmer and one that is more matter of fact.
description: Write a warm, professional response that makes a complaining customer feel heard.
useCase: Use this when a customer raises a complaint directly. A response that acknowledges frustration and offers a clear resolution turns a moment of tension into a chance to build loyalty, since how you handle a complaint often matters more to the relationship than the problem itself.

title: De Escalate an Angry Customer
promptText: Write a reply to de escalate this angry customer message for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE MESSAGE]. They are upset about [THE ISSUE]. Write a response that lowers the temperature: lead with genuine empathy, stay completely calm and never match their tone, validate the emotion without necessarily agreeing with every claim, and move toward a concrete next step. The goal is to defuse the anger and show I am on their side in solving this. Do not be defensive or dismissive. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a measured, human response that calms the situation.
description: Write a calm, empathetic reply that de escalates an angry customer.
useCase: Use this when a customer contacts you angry. Leading with empathy and staying calm defuses the emotion so you can actually solve the problem, since matching their frustration or getting defensive escalates a recoverable situation into a lost customer or a public complaint.

title: Respond to a Refund Request
promptText: Write a response to this refund request for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE REQUEST]. The reason they want a refund is [THE REASON], my refund policy is [YOUR POLICY], and my decision is [APPROVE, DECLINE, OR OFFER AN ALTERNATIVE]. Write a reply that is gracious and professional regardless of the outcome, clearly communicates my decision and the reasoning, stays warm even if declining, and where possible preserves the relationship and leaves the door open. Avoid sounding rigid or bureaucratic. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the response for my chosen decision.
description: Respond to a refund request graciously whether you approve, decline, or offer an alternative.
useCase: Use this when handling any refund request. A gracious response that clearly communicates your decision preserves goodwill regardless of the outcome, since how you handle a refund, even a declined one, shapes whether the customer leaves as a quiet detractor or a respectful former customer who might return.
variables: APPROVE, DECLINE, OR OFFER AN ALTERNATIVE

title: Write a Refund Approval Message
promptText: Write a message approving a refund for a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The refund is for [WHAT AND HOW MUCH], and the reason is [THE REASON]. Write a message that grants the refund graciously and without friction, confirms the details and timing they can expect, avoids making them feel bad for asking, and leaves the relationship warm so they might return or still speak well of me. Where genuine, keep a door open for the future without being pushy. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a message that turns a refund into a positive final impression.
description: Write a gracious refund approval that leaves a positive final impression.
useCase: Use this when approving a refund. Granting it graciously without making the customer feel bad turns a potentially negative moment into a positive final impression, since a smoothly handled refund often earns more goodwill and future business than a grudging one that leaves the customer feeling like a problem.

title: Decline a Refund Gracefully
promptText: Write a message declining a refund request for my [BUSINESS TYPE], while keeping the relationship as intact as possible. The request is for [WHAT], the reason it does not qualify is [WHY IT DOES NOT QUALIFY UNDER YOUR POLICY], and any alternative I can offer is [ALTERNATIVE OR WRITE NONE]. Write a reply that declines clearly but kindly, explains the reasoning without hiding behind policy coldly, acknowledges their disappointment, and offers any alternative or goodwill gesture that might help. Stay firm but human, since a graceless decline can trigger a public complaint. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the response.
description: Decline a refund clearly but kindly while protecting the relationship.
useCase: Use this when you must decline a refund. A clear but kind decline that explains the reasoning and offers an alternative holds your position while limiting the damage, since a cold policy driven refusal often provokes the angry public complaint that costs far more than the refund would have.
variables: WHY IT DOES NOT QUALIFY UNDER YOUR POLICY

title: Offer a Resolution to a Complaint
promptText: Help me craft a resolution to offer this complaining customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE COMPLAINT]. The issue is [THE ISSUE], and what I am able to offer is [YOUR OPTIONS, E.G. A FIX, A REPLACEMENT, A PARTIAL REFUND, A CREDIT, OR AN APOLOGY]. Help me present a resolution that genuinely addresses their problem, feels fair or even generous to them, and is sustainable for my business. Write the message offering the resolution warmly and confidently, framing it as me wanting to make things right. Give me the resolution message plus a note on how to decide which option fits when I have several. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Craft and present a fair resolution that genuinely addresses a complaint.
useCase: Use this when a complaint needs a concrete resolution, not just words. Offering a fair fix framed as wanting to make things right resolves the problem and rebuilds trust, since a well handled resolution can create more loyalty than if the issue had never occurred, turning a complaint into a retention win.
variables: YOUR OPTIONS, E.G. A FIX, A REPLACEMENT, A PARTIAL REFUND, A CREDIT, OR AN APOLOGY

title: Write a Genuine Apology
promptText: Write a genuine apology to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] for [WHAT WENT WRONG]. The impact on them was [THE IMPACT], and how I am making it right is [YOUR RESOLUTION]. Write an apology that takes real responsibility without excuses or a fake sounding sorry you feel that way, acknowledges the specific impact on them, explains briefly how I am fixing it and preventing a repeat, and feels sincere rather than corporate. Avoid over apologizing or groveling, which undermines confidence. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me an apology that rebuilds trust through genuine accountability.
description: Write a sincere apology that takes real responsibility and rebuilds trust.
useCase: Use this when you genuinely got something wrong. A real apology that owns the specific impact and shows how you are fixing it rebuilds trust, since customers forgive mistakes handled with genuine accountability but resent the hollow non apologies that dodge responsibility while pretending to accept it.

title: Plan a Service Recovery
promptText: Help me plan a full service recovery for a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] after a significant problem. What went wrong was [WHAT HAPPENED], the customer is [HOW THEY FEEL OR WHAT THEY WANT], and my constraints are [WHAT YOU CAN AND CANNOT DO]. Help me plan the recovery: the immediate response to stabilize the situation, the resolution that addresses their problem, any goodwill gesture that turns the experience around, and the follow up to confirm they are satisfied. The goal is not just to fix the issue but to recover the relationship. Give me the step by step recovery plan and the key messages. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Plan a full service recovery that fixes the problem and recovers the relationship.
useCase: Use this after a serious problem that risks losing a customer. A structured recovery that stabilizes, resolves, and follows up can turn an upset customer into a loyal one, since customers whose problems are recovered well often become more loyal than those who never had an issue, a pattern worth deliberately pursuing.
variables: HOW THEY FEEL OR WHAT THEY WANT
WHAT YOU CAN AND CANNOT DO

title: Set a Boundary with a Difficult Customer
promptText: Help me respond to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who is being unreasonable, abusive, or demanding something I cannot provide. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION], and what they are demanding is [WHAT THEY WANT]. Write a response that stays professional and calm, holds a firm boundary about what I can and cannot do, does not reward abusive behavior or cave to unreasonable demands, and remains respectful while protecting myself and my business. Include how to disengage if they continue past a reasonable point. I want to be fair but not a doormat. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the boundaried response.
description: Respond to an unreasonable or abusive customer with a firm, professional boundary.
useCase: Use this when a customer is being abusive or demanding the impossible. A calm firm boundary protects you and your business without escalating, since caving to unreasonable demands or matching hostility both make things worse, and knowing how to hold a professional line, and when to disengage, preserves your wellbeing and standards.

title: Follow Up After Resolving a Complaint
promptText: Write a follow up message to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] after I resolved their complaint. The original issue was [THE ISSUE], and how I resolved it was [YOUR RESOLUTION]. Write a message that checks they are now genuinely satisfied, confirms the problem is fully behind us, thanks them for their patience and for giving me the chance to fix it, and reinforces that I value them. This should close the loop warmly and, if they are happy, leave them feeling better about my business than before. Do not reopen the issue unnecessarily. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the follow up message.
description: Follow up after resolving a complaint to confirm satisfaction and close the loop warmly.
useCase: Use this after resolving a complaint. A follow up that confirms satisfaction and thanks them for the chance to fix it closes the loop and often leaves the customer feeling better than before the problem, since the thoughtful extra touch signals genuine care that most businesses skip once the immediate issue is handled.

title: Win Back a Refunded or Lost Customer
promptText: Help me win back a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who requested a refund or left unhappy. Why they left was [THE REASON], and what has changed or what I can offer to bring them back is [WHAT CHANGED OR YOUR OFFER]. Write a genuine reconnection message that acknowledges their past experience honestly, shows what has improved or what I can do differently, and invites them back without pressure or desperation. Keep the focus on genuinely serving them better, not just recovering the sale. Only reach out if there is a real reason to. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the win back message.
description: Write a genuine message to win back a refunded or unhappy former customer.
useCase: Use this to reconnect with a customer who left unhappy once things have genuinely improved. A sincere message acknowledging their experience and showing real change can recover a relationship that seemed lost, since some unhappy customers will return when they see genuine improvement and feel their earlier concern was actually heard.

title: Respond to a Chargeback or Payment Dispute
promptText: Help me respond to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who has filed or is threatening a chargeback or payment dispute. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION], the customer's claim is [THEIR CLAIM], and the facts are [WHAT ACTUALLY HAPPENED]. Help me: write a calm professional message to the customer aiming to resolve it directly and avoid the chargeback if possible, and separately organize the factual account and evidence I would need if the dispute proceeds. Keep the customer message non confrontational since escalation rarely helps. Note this is general guidance, not legal or payment processor advice. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me both the customer message and the evidence outline.
description: Respond to a chargeback threat calmly while organizing your factual case.
useCase: Use this when facing a chargeback or payment dispute. A calm message aiming to resolve it directly often prevents the chargeback entirely, which is far better than fighting one, and organizing your factual account prepares you in case it proceeds, all while avoiding the escalation that hostility invites.

title: Respond to a Complaint About Your Genuine Mistake
promptText: Write a response to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] complaining about something that was genuinely my fault. What I got wrong was [WHAT YOU DID WRONG], the impact was [THE IMPACT ON THEM], and how I am making it right is [YOUR RESOLUTION]. Write a reply that owns the mistake fully and quickly without excuses, apologizes sincerely, makes the resolution generous enough to match the error, and reassures them it will not happen again. Owning a real mistake gracefully builds more trust than deflecting. Avoid defensiveness or minimizing. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the accountable response.
description: Respond to a complaint about your genuine mistake with full accountability.
useCase: Use this when a complaint is genuinely your fault. Owning the mistake fully and making a resolution that matches the error rebuilds trust faster than any defense, since customers respect a business that takes real accountability far more than one that minimizes or deflects, often becoming more loyal after a well handled failure.

title: Handle a Complaint That Is Not Your Fault
promptText: Write a response to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] complaining about something that is not actually my fault, such as their own error, a misunderstanding, or a third party. The complaint is [PASTE OR DESCRIBE IT], and the real cause is [THE ACTUAL CAUSE]. Write a reply that stays empathetic and helpful without accepting blame that is not mine, gently clarifies what actually happened without making them feel stupid, and focuses on helping them move forward or resolve it. Avoid being defensive or making them wrong, since being right matters less than keeping the relationship. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the response.
description: Handle a complaint that is not your fault with empathy and without accepting blame.
useCase: Use this when a complaint stems from the customer's error or a misunderstanding. Staying empathetic and helpful while gently clarifying what happened resolves it without making them feel stupid or accepting blame you do not own, since winning the argument matters far less than keeping the customer and avoiding a public grievance.

title: Offer a Partial Refund or Credit
promptText: Help me offer a partial refund or credit as a middle ground for a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [THE SITUATION], a full refund is not warranted because [WHY], but I want to acknowledge their concern with [PARTIAL REFUND OR CREDIT AMOUNT OR OFFER]. Write a message that frames the partial resolution as a fair and genuine gesture of goodwill, not a grudging compromise, explains it clearly, and aims to leave them satisfied rather than shortchanged. Position it as me valuing the relationship. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the message offering the partial resolution.
description: Offer a partial refund or credit framed as a genuine goodwill gesture.
useCase: Use this when a full refund is not warranted but the customer deserves acknowledgment. A partial refund or credit framed as genuine goodwill, rather than a grudging compromise, resolves the tension fairly, keeping the customer satisfied and the relationship intact while protecting your business from giving away more than the situation truly calls for.
variables: PARTIAL REFUND OR CREDIT AMOUNT OR OFFER

title: Analyze Recurring Complaints for Root Causes
promptText: Help me analyze recurring complaints and refund reasons for my [BUSINESS TYPE] to fix the root causes. Here are the complaints and refund reasons I have been getting: [PASTE OR LIST THEM]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me: identify the recurring themes and patterns, distinguish surface complaints from the underlying root problems, pinpoint what in my offer, process, messaging, or expectations is generating them, and recommend specific fixes prioritized by impact. The goal is to stop the complaints at the source rather than just handling each one. Give me the analysis and the fix list.
description: Analyze recurring complaints and refunds to find and fix their root causes.
useCase: Use this when the same complaints or refund reasons keep recurring. Finding the root cause, whether in your offer, process, or the expectations your messaging sets, lets you stop problems at the source, which is far more valuable than skillfully handling the same complaint over and over while it keeps costing you customers.

title: Prevent Complaints Before They Happen
promptText: Help me prevent common complaints before they happen in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The complaints I most want to prevent are [YOUR COMMON COMPLAINTS]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] and my process is [BRIEF DESCRIPTION OF YOUR PROCESS]. Help me: identify where in the customer journey each complaint originates, what expectation gaps or friction points cause them, and the proactive steps to prevent them, such as clearer expectations upfront, better onboarding, or process changes. Focus on the highest leverage prevention moves. The goal is fewer complaints reaching me at all. Give me the prevention plan mapped to each complaint. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Build a plan to prevent common complaints before they reach you.
useCase: Use this to reduce complaints at the source rather than just handling them. Identifying the expectation gaps and friction points that cause complaints, then closing them proactively, means fewer problems reach you at all, freeing your time and improving the experience, since the best complaint handling is preventing the complaint from arising.

title: Write a Clear Refund Policy
promptText: Help me write a clear refund policy for my [BUSINESS TYPE] offering [YOUR OFFER] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. What I am willing to offer is [YOUR REFUND TERMS, E.G. TIMEFRAME, CONDITIONS, EXCLUSIONS]. Write a policy that is clear and easy to understand, fair to both me and the customer, protects me from abuse while building buyer confidence, and is written in plain friendly language rather than cold legalese. Explain any conditions in a way that feels reasonable, not restrictive. Note this is general business guidance, not legal advice, and I should have anything binding reviewed. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the refund policy.
description: Write a clear, fair refund policy that builds confidence and prevents abuse.
useCase: Use this to set a refund policy that works for both sides. A clear policy in plain language builds buyer confidence, since a fair visible refund policy actually increases sales by reducing risk, while well defined conditions protect you from abuse, replacing the ambiguity that causes disputes with terms everyone understands upfront.
variables: YOUR REFUND TERMS, E.G. TIMEFRAME, CONDITIONS, EXCLUSIONS

title: Set Expectations to Prevent Complaints
promptText: Help me write clear expectation setting communication for new customers of my [BUSINESS TYPE] to prevent complaints from mismatched expectations. My offer is [YOUR OFFER], and the areas where customers often misunderstand or expect more than I deliver are [WHERE MISMATCHES HAPPEN]. Write messaging, for onboarding or the point of purchase, that clearly and honestly sets expectations about what they will get, what to expect and when, what is and is not included, and how things work, in a warm confident way that reassures rather than lists caveats defensively. Setting honest expectations upfront prevents most complaints. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the expectation setting message.
description: Write expectation setting communication that prevents complaints before they start.
useCase: Use this to prevent the complaints that come from mismatched expectations, one of the most common causes. Setting honest clear expectations at purchase or onboarding, about what customers get and when, heads off the disappointment that fuels complaints and refunds, since much dissatisfaction is really a gap between what was expected and what was delivered.

title: Build a Complaint Response Template Bank
promptText: Build me a bank of complaint response templates for my [BUSINESS TYPE] so I can respond quickly and consistently while staying personal. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE] and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Create adaptable templates for the common situations: a general complaint, an angry customer, a refund request I will approve, a refund request I will decline, a genuine mistake on my part, and an issue that is not my fault. Each should have a warm on brand structure with fill in the blank spots to personalize, not a canned script. Include notes on what to customize each time. Give me the template bank.
description: Build adaptable complaint and refund response templates that stay personal.
useCase: Use this once to handle complaints faster without sounding robotic. A bank of adaptable templates for each situation lets you respond quickly and consistently even when stressed or busy, which matters since complaints often arrive at inconvenient moments and a fast, calm, personalized response beats a slow or emotional one every time.

title: Get a Quick Complaint Response Fast
promptText: I need to respond to this customer complaint quickly for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE THE COMPLAINT]. The issue is [THE ISSUE] and what I can offer is [YOUR RESOLUTION OR WRITE STILL DECIDING]. Give me a calm, professional, empathetic response ready to send with minimal editing, that acknowledges their concern, addresses the issue, and offers a path forward. Keep it warm and human, not corporate. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Just give me a solid ready to use response I can send now.
description: Get a calm, ready to send complaint response quickly when you need it fast.
useCase: Use this when a complaint needs a good response right away. A calm ready to send reply helps you respond promptly and professionally even under time pressure or emotion, since a fast composed response prevents a complaint from festering, and quick acknowledgment alone often defuses much of a customer's frustration.
variables: YOUR RESOLUTION OR WRITE STILL DECIDING

title: Respond to a Complaint Made Publicly
promptText: Help me respond to a complaint a customer made publicly about my [BUSINESS TYPE], such as on social media or in a comment: [PASTE THE PUBLIC COMPLAINT]. The issue is [THE ISSUE] and my context is [YOUR CONTEXT]. Write a public reply that responds with grace and composure knowing others are watching, acknowledges their concern genuinely, avoids arguing or airing details publicly, and invites them to continue privately to resolve it. The public response should reassure onlookers that I handle problems well. Give me the public reply plus a private message to send if they engage. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Respond to a public complaint with composure and move resolution offline.
useCase: Use this when a complaint is made publicly where others can see. A composed public reply that acknowledges the concern and moves resolution offline protects your reputation with the onlookers who matter most, since how you handle a public complaint signals to every potential customer watching how you would treat them too.

title: Decide When to Let a Customer Go
promptText: Help me decide whether to part ways with a difficult customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] and how to do it professionally if so. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION], and the pattern of behavior is [WHAT KEEPS HAPPENING]. Help me: honestly assess whether this customer is worth keeping given the cost to my time, energy, and wellbeing, distinguish a difficult but salvageable customer from one I should release, and if releasing them is right, write a professional respectful message to end the relationship cleanly, including any refund or transition. Firing a bad customer can be the healthiest business decision. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the assessment and, if warranted, the message.
description: Decide whether to part ways with a difficult customer and do it professionally.
useCase: Use this when a customer costs more in stress and time than they are worth. Honestly assessing whether to keep or release them, and ending it professionally if warranted, protects your wellbeing and business, since keeping a genuinely toxic customer drains energy from good ones and firing a bad customer is sometimes the healthiest decision you can make.

title: Respond to an Emotionally Charged Complaint
promptText: Help me respond to a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] whose complaint is emotionally charged because the issue affected them deeply. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION], and why it matters so much to them is [WHY IT IS EMOTIONAL FOR THEM]. Write a response that leads with real human empathy for what they are feeling, takes their emotion seriously rather than treating it as an overreaction, addresses both the practical issue and the emotional impact, and makes them feel genuinely cared for as a person. The emotional acknowledgment matters as much as the fix here. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a deeply empathetic response.
description: Respond to an emotionally charged complaint with genuine human empathy.
useCase: Use this when a complaint carries real emotional weight because the issue mattered deeply to the customer. Leading with genuine empathy and taking their feelings seriously, alongside fixing the problem, makes them feel cared for as a person, since in emotionally charged moments the acknowledgment of their feelings often matters as much as the practical resolution.
variables: WHY IT IS EMOTIONAL FOR THEM

title: Write a New Customer Welcome Email
promptText: Write a welcome email for a new customer who just bought [YOUR OFFER] from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Write an email that warmly confirms their smart decision, sets the tone for the relationship, tells them exactly what happens next, points them to their first step, and reassures them they are in good hands. Keep it warm, clear, and free of overwhelming detail. This is their first impression after buying, so it should feel personal and build confidence. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a warm welcome email that confirms the purchase and sets up a great start.
useCase: Use this the moment someone buys, when their excitement and open rate are highest. A warm welcome that confirms their decision and points to the first step reduces buyer regret and sets the relationship on the right foot, since a strong first impression after purchase shapes whether they stay engaged or drift.

title: Build a Full Onboarding Email Sequence
promptText: Build a complete onboarding email sequence for new customers of [YOUR OFFER] from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The first meaningful result they should reach is [FIRST WIN], the common early stumbling point is [COMMON STUMBLE], and the sequence should span [NUMBER OF EMAILS OR DAYS]. Design each email: a warm welcome and setup, guidance to their first step, help past the common stumble, encouragement as they progress, and a check in on their first win. For each give the purpose, timing, subject line, and key message. Keep every email focused on their success. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Build a complete onboarding sequence that drives new customers to their first win.
useCase: Use this to onboard every new customer systematically. A sequence that guides them to a first win and past common stumbles reduces churn and refunds, since customers who reach early success stay and buy again, while those who get stuck early quietly disappear or ask for their money back.

title: Write a First Step Guidance Email
promptText: Write an email guiding a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] through their very first step to get started. The first thing they need to do is [THE FIRST STEP], and it matters because [WHY IT MATTERS]. Write an email that makes the first step feel easy and unintimidating, gives clear simple instructions, explains the quick benefit of doing it, and removes any friction or hesitation. Keep it focused on this one step so they are not overwhelmed. The goal is to get them to actually take the first action. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a focused email that gets a new customer to take their crucial first step.
useCase: Use this to drive the single most important onboarding action, the first step. Making that step feel easy and giving clear instructions gets customers moving, since the first action predicts whether they will succeed, and many customers who never take it simply stall and disengage entirely.

title: Set Expectations in an Onboarding Email
promptText: Write an onboarding email that sets clear expectations for new customers of [YOUR OFFER] from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Help them understand: what to expect and when, how the process or timeline works, what is included and what is not, what I need from them, and how to get help. Write it warmly and confidently so it reassures rather than reads as a list of caveats. Clear expectations upfront prevent confusion, frustration, and complaints later. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an onboarding email that sets clear expectations and prevents later confusion.
useCase: Use this early in onboarding to prevent the confusion and complaints that come from mismatched expectations. Clearly explaining what to expect, the timeline, and what is included reassures new customers and heads off the friction that arises when reality does not match what they assumed they were buying.

title: Write an Early Quick Win Email
promptText: Write an email designed to give a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] a fast early win so they feel momentum. The quick win I can help them achieve early is [THE QUICK WIN], and how they get it is [HOW THEY ACHIEVE IT]. Write an email that guides them to this early success, makes it feel achievable and worthwhile, and celebrates the momentum once they get it. The goal is to give them a taste of results fast so they stay engaged and believe in the value. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that guides new customers to a fast early win and builds momentum.
useCase: Use this to deliver an early quick win, one of the strongest drivers of retention. A fast taste of results makes customers believe in the value and stay engaged, since momentum early on carries them forward, while a slow start where they see no results yet is where doubt and disengagement creep in.

title: Write an Onboarding Check In Email
promptText: Write a check in email for a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] a few days or weeks into onboarding. Write an email that genuinely asks how things are going, offers help if they are stuck, invites them to ask questions or share obstacles, and shows I care about their success rather than just their payment. Keep it warm and low pressure, and make it easy for them to reply honestly if something is not working. Catching a stuck customer here can prevent a refund or churn. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a warm check in email that catches stuck customers before they churn.
useCase: Use this partway through onboarding to catch problems before they become refunds. A genuine check in that invites honest replies surfaces the customers quietly struggling, letting you help before they give up, since a stuck customer who feels supported stays while one who feels ignored quietly disappears or disputes the charge.

title: Write an Onboarding Resources Email
promptText: Write an email pointing new customers of [YOUR OFFER] to the key resources they need to succeed. The most useful resources are [YOUR RESOURCES, E.G. GUIDES, TUTORIALS, SUPPORT, COMMUNITY]. Write an email that introduces the resources clearly, explains what each is for and when to use it, and helps them know where to turn without overwhelming them with everything at once. Prioritize the resources that matter most early. The goal is to equip them to help themselves and reduce confusion. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that points new customers to the key resources for success.
useCase: Use this to equip new customers with the resources they need without overwhelming them. Introducing the right resources at the right time helps customers help themselves, reducing support questions and confusion, since customers who know where to find answers stay confident while those who feel lost get frustrated and disengage.
variables: YOUR RESOURCES, E.G. GUIDES, TUTORIALS, SUPPORT, COMMUNITY

title: Onboard a New Service Client
promptText: Write an onboarding email for a new client who just hired my [BUSINESS TYPE] for [YOUR SERVICE]. Write an email that welcomes them warmly, confirms the engagement and what happens next, outlines the process and timeline, tells them clearly what I need from them to begin, and sets a professional confident tone. Include the first action or information I need from them. This kickoff sets the tone for the whole working relationship, so it should feel organized and reassuring. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a professional kickoff email that onboards a new service client smoothly.
useCase: Use this when a new client hires you for a service. A clear kickoff that confirms next steps and what you need from them starts the engagement organized and professionally, since a smooth confident onboarding sets client expectations and trust for the entire project, while a chaotic start makes them doubt their decision immediately.

title: Request Information to Begin Onboarding
promptText: Write an email requesting the information or materials I need from a new client of my [BUSINESS TYPE] to begin their [YOUR SERVICE OR PROJECT]. What I need from them is [WHAT YOU NEED, E.G. ACCESS, ASSETS, DETAILS, A FORM]. Write an email that clearly lists what I need and why, makes it as easy as possible for them to provide it, sets a friendly deadline so things do not stall, and keeps it organized rather than overwhelming. The goal is to get everything I need promptly so the work can start smoothly. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a clear email that gathers everything you need to start client work.
useCase: Use this to collect what you need before starting client work. A clear organized request with a friendly deadline gets materials promptly so projects do not stall, since the most common cause of a slow start is waiting on information the client did not realize you needed or kept meaning to send.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED, E.G. ACCESS, ASSETS, DETAILS, A FORM

title: Write an Onboarding FAQ Email
promptText: Write an email answering the common early questions new customers of [YOUR OFFER] have, to reduce confusion and support requests. The questions new customers usually ask are [COMMON EARLY QUESTIONS, OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Write an email that proactively addresses these questions in a friendly digestible way, so customers get answers before they need to ask. Keep it scannable and reassuring. The goal is to prevent the repetitive early questions and help customers feel confident and informed. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that proactively answers common early customer questions.
useCase: Use this to answer the questions new customers always ask before they ask them. Proactively addressing common early questions reduces the repetitive support requests eating your time while making customers feel informed and confident, since answers delivered before the question arises prevent both frustration and inbox overload.
variables: COMMON EARLY QUESTIONS, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Re Engage a Stalled New Customer
promptText: Write an email to re engage a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] who bought but has not started or gone quiet during onboarding. They have not yet [WHAT THEY HAVE NOT DONE]. Write an email that gently nudges them without guilt, reminds them of the value and outcome waiting for them, removes any friction or hesitation that might be holding them back, and makes it easy to take the next step now. Keep it warm and encouraging, not nagging. The goal is to reactivate them before they disengage entirely and become a refund or a detractor. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that re engages a new customer who stalled during onboarding.
useCase: Use this when a new customer bought but never started or went quiet. A warm nudge that reminds them of the value and removes friction reactivates them before they disengage entirely, since a customer who never begins is the most likely to request a refund, forget the value, or become a quiet detractor of your business.

title: Celebrate an Onboarding Milestone
promptText: Write an email celebrating a milestone a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] has reached during onboarding. The milestone is [THE MILESTONE, E.G. COMPLETED SETUP, GOT THEIR FIRST RESULT, FINISHED THE FIRST MODULE]. Write an email that genuinely celebrates their progress, reinforces the value of what they have accomplished, builds their confidence and momentum, and points them toward the next step. The goal is to make them feel good about their progress and motivated to continue. Recognition of progress keeps customers engaged. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that celebrates an onboarding milestone and builds momentum.
useCase: Use this when a customer hits a milestone during onboarding. Celebrating their progress reinforces the value and motivates them to continue, since recognition of progress is a powerful retention lever, and customers who feel their momentum acknowledged push forward while those whose effort goes unnoticed lose steam.
variables: THE MILESTONE, E.G. COMPLETED SETUP, GOT THEIR FIRST RESULT, FINISHED THE FIRST MODULE

title: Add a Personal Touch to Onboarding
promptText: Write a personal, human onboarding email from me to a new customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] that stands apart from automated corporate onboarding. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE], and something genuine I want to convey is [WHAT YOU WANT TO SHARE, E.G. WHY YOU DO THIS, A PERSONAL WELCOME, OR YOUR COMMITMENT TO THEM]. Write an email that feels like it came from a real person who genuinely cares, not a template, that welcomes them personally and makes them feel like more than a transaction. As a solo business, this personal touch is a real advantage over bigger competitors. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a genuinely personal onboarding email that makes customers feel valued.
useCase: Use this to leverage the personal touch a solo business can offer that big companies cannot. A genuine human onboarding email that makes customers feel like more than a transaction builds the connection and loyalty that becomes your competitive edge, since people remember and stay with businesses where they feel personally valued.
variables: WHAT YOU WANT TO SHARE, E.G. WHY YOU DO THIS, A PERSONAL WELCOME, OR YOUR COMMITMENT TO THEM

title: Onboard a New Member or Subscriber
promptText: Write an onboarding email for a new member or subscriber to my [MEMBERSHIP OR SUBSCRIPTION]. What they get access to is [WHAT IS INCLUDED], and where I want them to start is [THE BEST FIRST ACTION]. Write an email that welcomes them warmly, helps them get oriented to everything available without overwhelming them, points them to the best place to start getting value, and encourages them to engage early. For recurring offers, early engagement is critical to reducing churn, so this email should get them active fast. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an onboarding email that gets new members active early to reduce churn.
useCase: Use this to onboard new members or subscribers, where early engagement determines retention. Helping them get oriented and active fast reduces churn, since for recurring offers a member who engages early sees ongoing value and renews, while one who never gets started cancels at the first billing they notice.

title: Onboard a New Course or Program Student
promptText: Write an onboarding email for a new student who enrolled in my [COURSE OR PROGRAM] that teaches [WHAT IT TEACHES]. Write an email that welcomes them, helps them access the material, sets expectations for how to approach the course for best results, encourages them to commit to actually completing it, and points them to the first lesson or module. Course completion is the biggest predictor of results and referrals, so this email should motivate them to start and stick with it. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an onboarding email that motivates new students to start and complete a course.
useCase: Use this to onboard new course or program students. Motivating them to actually start and commit to finishing drives the completion that produces results, since students who complete a course get outcomes, leave testimonials, and refer others, while the many who never start beyond enrolling produce nothing and often regret the purchase.

title: Introduce Support Options in Onboarding
promptText: Write an onboarding email introducing new customers of [YOUR OFFER] to how they can get help when they need it. My support options are [YOUR SUPPORT CHANNELS, E.G. EMAIL, A HELP DOC, A COMMUNITY, OR A CONTACT FORM]. Write an email that clearly explains how and where to get help, reassures them that support is available and I want them to succeed, sets expectations for response times, and makes them comfortable reaching out. The goal is that a customer who hits a problem knows exactly where to turn instead of getting frustrated and giving up. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that introduces support options so customers know where to get help.
useCase: Use this so new customers know how to get help before they need it. Clearly explaining support options and response times means a customer who hits a problem knows exactly where to turn, preventing the frustration and silent churn that happens when a stuck customer cannot find help and simply gives up on the product.
variables: YOUR SUPPORT CHANNELS, E.G. EMAIL, A HELP DOC, A COMMUNITY, OR A CONTACT FORM

title: Audit Your Onboarding Sequence
promptText: Audit my current onboarding email sequence for my [BUSINESS TYPE] offering [YOUR OFFER]. Here is the sequence: [PASTE YOUR EMAILS OR DESCRIBE THEM]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the first win a customer should reach is [FIRST WIN]. Assess it honestly: whether it drives customers to their first win effectively, where it might lose or overwhelm people, gaps where customers get stuck without support, emails that are unclear or unnecessary, and whether the timing and tone work. Then give me prioritized improvements and rewrite the weakest emails. The goal is an onboarding that reliably activates customers. Be direct about what is not working.
description: Audit an existing onboarding sequence and get prioritized improvements.
useCase: Use this to improve an onboarding sequence that is not activating customers well. An honest audit reveals where the sequence loses people, overwhelms them, or leaves gaps, since a weak onboarding quietly drives churn and refunds, and fixing it is often the highest leverage improvement a business can make to retention.
variables: PASTE YOUR EMAILS OR DESCRIBE THEM

title: Write an Onboarding Next Steps Email
promptText: Write an email guiding a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] on what to do next after they have completed the initial setup or first step. They have already done [WHAT THEY HAVE DONE], and the next thing they should focus on is [THE NEXT STEP]. Write an email that acknowledges their progress, clearly directs them to the next action, explains why it matters and what it leads to, and keeps their momentum going. Keep it focused so they know exactly what to do next without feeling overwhelmed by everything at once. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that guides a customer to their next step and sustains momentum.
useCase: Use this to keep customers moving forward after the first step. Clearly directing them to the next action and explaining what it leads to sustains momentum, since onboarding works as a chain and a customer who is not told what to do next often stalls, even after a strong start, simply because the path forward was unclear.

title: Remind Customers of the Value They Bought
promptText: Write an onboarding email that reinforces the value and outcome a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] is working toward, to keep them motivated. The transformation or result they want is [THE OUTCOME], and the reason they bought was likely [THEIR MOTIVATION]. Write an email that reconnects them with why they bought, paints the outcome they are heading toward, and renews their motivation to keep going through the onboarding. This is useful when early effort feels like work before results appear. The goal is to remind them the effort is worth it. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that reconnects customers with the outcome they are working toward.
useCase: Use this when early onboarding requires effort before results appear, the moment motivation dips. Reconnecting customers with why they bought and the outcome ahead renews their drive to continue, since customers who lose sight of the goal during the effortful early phase are the ones who quit before reaching the results that would have kept them.

title: Humanize an Onboarding Email
promptText: Here is an onboarding email I drafted for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE YOUR EMAIL]. It sounds robotic, corporate, or generic. Rewrite it to sound warm and human, like it came from a real person who cares, while keeping all the necessary information and instructions intact. Match my voice, which is [DESCRIBE YOUR VOICE OR PASTE A SAMPLE], and my audience, which is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Show me the humanized version and note what changed so I can keep future onboarding emails personal. Do not make it more formal than my real voice. The goal is onboarding that feels personal, not automated.
description: Rewrite a robotic onboarding email to sound warm, human, and on brand.
useCase: Use this when your onboarding emails read as automated or corporate. Rewriting them to feel personal and human strengthens the connection new customers came to a small business for, since warm onboarding builds the loyalty that big competitors with sterile automated sequences cannot match, at the exact moment the relationship is forming.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR VOICE OR PASTE A SAMPLE

title: Onboard a Returning Customer
promptText: Write an onboarding email for a returning customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who bought again or came back after a break. What they bought or returned to is [WHAT THEY BOUGHT], and their history with me is [THEIR HISTORY]. Write an email that welcomes them back warmly and acknowledges the returning relationship rather than treating them like a stranger, catches them up on anything new since last time, and gets them started again smoothly. Returning customers deserve recognition of their loyalty. The tone should feel like reconnecting with someone you know, not a cold generic welcome. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a welcome back email that recognizes and re onboards a returning customer.
useCase: Use this when a past customer buys again or returns after a break. Recognizing their history rather than treating them like a stranger honors their loyalty and gets them restarted smoothly, since returning customers are among your most valuable and treating them with a generic cold welcome misses the chance to deepen a proven relationship.

title: Get a Quick Onboarding Email Fast
promptText: I need an onboarding email for a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] from my [BUSINESS TYPE], quickly. The purpose of this email is [THE PURPOSE, E.G. WELCOME, FIRST STEP, OR NEXT STEP], and the key thing they need to know or do is [KEY MESSAGE OR ACTION]. Give me a warm, clear, ready to use email with a subject line, written for [TARGET AUDIENCE] in a [TONE] tone. Keep it focused and ready to send with minimal editing. Just give me a solid ready to use onboarding email.
description: Get a warm, ready to send onboarding email quickly for any purpose.
useCase: Use this when you need an onboarding email fast without building a full sequence. A quick ready to send email for any onboarding purpose lets you communicate with new customers promptly, since a fast warm response during onboarding keeps momentum, and a missing or delayed email at this stage is where new customers start to disengage.
variables: THE PURPOSE, E.G. WELCOME, FIRST STEP, OR NEXT STEP

title: Request Early Feedback During Onboarding
promptText: Write an email asking a new customer of [YOUR OFFER] for early feedback on their onboarding experience. Write an email that invites them to share how the start has gone, whether anything was confusing or difficult, and what would help them succeed. Keep it short, genuine, and easy to reply to, framing it as me wanting to improve their experience, not a formal survey. Early feedback catches problems while they are fixable and shows the customer I care. The goal is honest input I can act on and a customer who feels heard. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email that gathers early onboarding feedback customers will actually give.
useCase: Use this to catch onboarding problems while they are still fixable. A genuine easy to answer feedback request surfaces confusion and friction early and shows the customer you care, since early feedback both improves your onboarding for everyone and makes the individual customer feel heard, which itself builds loyalty and reduces churn.

title: Plan Your Onboarding Email Timeline
promptText: Help me plan the timing and cadence of my onboarding emails for new customers of [YOUR OFFER] from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The journey a customer takes from purchase to first win is [DESCRIBE THE JOURNEY OR KEY MILESTONES]. Help me map: how many onboarding emails to send, the ideal timing of each relative to purchase and to their progress, what each email should accomplish at that point, and how to space them so they guide without overwhelming. Give me a clear timeline I can build my sequence around. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The goal is a well paced onboarding that reliably activates customers.
description: Plan the timing and cadence of your onboarding emails around the customer journey.
useCase: Use this before building an onboarding sequence to get the pacing right. Mapping how many emails to send and when, relative to the customer journey, ensures onboarding guides without overwhelming, since even great emails fail if they arrive too fast and flood the customer or too slow and let them stall between steps.
variables: DESCRIBE THE JOURNEY OR KEY MILESTONES

title: Win Back a Lapsed Customer
promptText: Write a win back message for a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who has not bought or engaged in a while. They previously [WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED], and it has been about [HOW LONG]. Write a warm message that reconnects genuinely without guilt tripping, reminds them of the value they got or could get, and gives them an easy low pressure reason to come back. Keep it human and personal, not a generic we miss you blast. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write a warm win back message that reconnects with a lapsed customer.
useCase: Use this to re engage customers who drifted away. Reconnecting warmly with people who already know and trust you is far cheaper than finding new customers, and a genuine reason to return often revives dormant relationships that would otherwise be lost for good.
variables: WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED

title: Build a Win Back Email Sequence
promptText: Build a win back email sequence for lapsed customers of my [BUSINESS TYPE]. They previously bought [WHAT THEY BOUGHT], and the sequence should span [NUMBER OF EMAILS]. Design each email with a distinct angle: a warm reconnection, a reminder of value or what is new, an incentive or offer to return, and a final gentle last call. For each give the purpose, timing, subject line, and key message. The sequence should escalate gently from soft reconnection to a clear reason to act, without becoming pushy or desperate. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE].
description: Build a multi email win back sequence that escalates from reconnection to offer.
useCase: Use this to systematically re engage lapsed customers rather than relying on one message. A sequence gives multiple genuine touchpoints with escalating reasons to return, recovering far more dormant customers than a single email, since most who come back do so after a second or third nudge, not the first.

title: Write a Genuine We Miss You Message
promptText: Write a we miss you message for a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] that feels genuine rather than a canned marketing template. They have been away for [HOW LONG], and what I genuinely valued about serving them was [WHAT YOU VALUED OR WRITE GENERAL]. Write a message that expresses authentic warmth, makes them feel individually noticed rather than mass mailed, and reopens the door without pressure or a hard sell. The goal is to make them feel remembered and welcome back. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write an authentic we miss you message that feels personal, not canned.
useCase: Use this to reconnect emotionally with a customer who drifted. A genuine we miss you note that feels individually noticed rebuilds the personal connection a solo business thrives on, standing apart from the generic automated version everyone ignores and reminding the customer why they chose you in the first place.
variables: WHAT YOU VALUED OR WRITE GENERAL

title: Write a Win Back Offer Message
promptText: Write a win back message with an incentive to bring back a lapsed customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The incentive I can offer is [YOUR OFFER, E.G. A DISCOUNT, BONUS, OR SPECIAL DEAL], and they previously bought [WHAT THEY BOUGHT]. Write a message that reconnects warmly first, presents the incentive as a genuine welcome back gesture rather than a desperate discount, and makes returning feel easy and worthwhile. Frame the offer around their benefit, not my need for the sale. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write a win back message that presents an incentive as a genuine welcome back.
useCase: Use this when an incentive can tip a lapsed customer into returning. Framing the offer as a welcome back gesture rather than a desperate discount preserves your value while giving a concrete reason to act, which often converts the on the fence dormant customers who needed just a small nudge.
variables: YOUR OFFER, E.G. A DISCOUNT, BONUS, OR SPECIAL DEAL

title: Win Back With What Is New
promptText: Write a win back message for a lapsed customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] built around what is new or improved since they left. What has changed is [WHAT IS NEW, E.G. NEW OFFERS, IMPROVEMENTS, OR FEATURES], and they previously bought [WHAT THEY BOUGHT]. Write a message that reconnects warmly, shares what is new in a way that gives them a fresh reason to return, and, if what changed addresses why they may have left, gently notes that. The goal is to show the business has evolved and is worth another look. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write a win back message that gives lapsed customers a fresh reason to return.
useCase: Use this when your business has genuinely improved since a customer left. Sharing what is new gives a fresh reason to return beyond nostalgia, and if the changes address why they drifted, it directly removes the original reason, making another look feel worthwhile rather than a repeat of what they already left.
variables: WHAT IS NEW, E.G. NEW OFFERS, IMPROVEMENTS, OR FEATURES

title: Win Back by Asking What Went Wrong
promptText: Write a win back message for a lapsed customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] that leads with genuinely asking what went wrong or why they left, rather than immediately selling. They previously [WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED]. Write a message that sincerely invites their honest feedback, shows I actually want to understand and improve, and makes it easy and low pressure to reply. The win back happens through caring first, with any return being a natural result of feeling heard. Do not bury a sales pitch under a fake feedback request. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Win back a customer by sincerely asking why they left before selling anything.
useCase: Use this to re engage a customer while learning why they left. Leading with genuine curiosity rather than a pitch makes them feel heard, which itself rebuilds the relationship, and their answers reveal fixable problems that may be quietly costing you other customers too.
variables: WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED

title: Write a Personal Win Back Note
promptText: Write a personal one to one win back note for a specific former customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE], the kind of message that feels handwritten rather than automated. Their name and history is [CUSTOMER NAME AND WHAT YOU REMEMBER ABOUT THEM], and they have been away for [HOW LONG]. Write a short genuine note that references something specific about them, reconnects as a real person reaching out, and warmly leaves the door open. This is for a valuable customer worth a personal touch, not a mass message. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the note.
description: Write a personal, handwritten style win back note for a valuable former customer.
useCase: Use this for a high value former customer worth individual effort. A genuinely personal note that references specifics about them lands far harder than any mass campaign, leveraging the personal relationship advantage a solo business has to recover the customers most worth winning back.
variables: CUSTOMER NAME AND WHAT YOU REMEMBER ABOUT THEM

title: Write a Last Chance Win Back Message
promptText: Write a final last chance win back message for a lapsed customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who has not responded to earlier attempts. This is the last message in the sequence before I stop reaching out or remove them from my list. Write a message that is honest about it being the last one, respectful of their choice either way, gives a final clear reason or offer to return if they want to, and closes warmly rather than guilt tripping. A genuine last call sometimes prompts action from those who kept meaning to respond. My offer or reason to return is [YOUR OFFER OR REASON]. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write an honest final win back message that respects the customer's choice.
useCase: Use this as the last touch for customers who did not respond to earlier win back attempts. An honest respectful last call often prompts action from those who kept meaning to reply, and closing warmly protects the relationship and your reputation even with those who choose not to return.

title: Win Back an Expired Subscriber
promptText: Write a win back message for a customer whose subscription or membership to my [SUBSCRIPTION OR MEMBERSHIP] expired or was cancelled. They were a member for [HOW LONG THEY WERE A MEMBER], and what they get by rejoining is [WHAT REJOINING OFFERS]. Write a message that warmly invites them back, reminds them of the value they are missing, notes anything new since they left, and makes rejoining easy. If appropriate, include a returning member incentive. Keep it warm and confident, not desperate. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write a win back message that invites an expired subscriber to rejoin.
useCase: Use this to recover churned subscribers, who are prime win back targets. They already valued membership enough to join once, so reminding them what they are missing and easing the path back recovers recurring revenue far more efficiently than acquiring brand new members from scratch.
variables: HOW LONG THEY WERE A MEMBER

title: Follow Up After a Cancellation
promptText: Write a follow up message to send shortly after a customer cancels my [SUBSCRIPTION OR SERVICE], aimed at leaving the door open for a future return. The reason they gave for cancelling was [CANCELLATION REASON OR WRITE UNKNOWN]. Write a message that graciously accepts their decision without pressure, thanks them for their time as a customer, addresses their reason if there is something I can note or offer, and makes clear they are welcome back anytime. A gracious cancellation experience makes a future return far more likely. Do not try to hard sell them out of the cancellation. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write a gracious post cancellation message that leaves the door open to return.
useCase: Use this right after a customer cancels to preserve the relationship. A gracious exit that accepts their choice without pressure makes a future return far more likely, since customers remember how they were treated on the way out, and a respectful goodbye keeps the door open that a desperate save attempt would slam shut.

title: Win Back an Abandoned Cart
promptText: Write a message to win back a customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who added [PRODUCT OR OFFER] to their cart or started checkout but did not complete the purchase. Write a message that gently reminds them what they left behind, addresses the likely hesitation that stopped them such as price, doubt, or distraction, reassures them, and makes it easy to complete the purchase. Keep it helpful rather than pushy, as if nudging a friend who got sidetracked. If useful, note where a small incentive could help. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write a message that recovers a customer who abandoned their cart or checkout.
useCase: Use this to recover the near sales lost at checkout. People who abandoned a cart showed strong intent and got sidetracked or hesitated, so a helpful nudge that addresses the likely hesitation recovers revenue that was almost yours, often more easily than converting a cold prospect from scratch.

title: Follow Up With a Past Inquiry Who Never Bought
promptText: Write a message to reconnect with someone who inquired about my [BUSINESS TYPE] a while ago but never became a customer. They originally asked about [WHAT THEY INQUIRED ABOUT], and it has been about [HOW LONG]. Write a message that warmly reconnects without being awkward about the gap, checks whether their need is still relevant, and reopens the conversation with a low pressure next step. Since they never bought, this reintroduces me rather than assumes a relationship. If anything new might address why they did not proceed, note it. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Reconnect with a past inquiry who showed interest but never became a customer.
useCase: Use this to revive warm leads who inquired but never bought. Their original interest may still be relevant, and a low pressure reconnection reopens a conversation that timing or circumstance interrupted, recovering prospects who were genuinely interested but slipped through before converting.

title: Win Back With a Seasonal Reason
promptText: Write a win back message for lapsed customers of my [BUSINESS TYPE] that uses a seasonal or timely moment as a natural reason to reach out. The occasion is [THE SEASON, HOLIDAY, OR MOMENT], and it connects to my business because [THE CONNECTION]. Write a message that uses the occasion as a warm genuine reason to reconnect, ties it naturally to a reason to return, and avoids feeling like a forced seasonal sales blast. The timeliness should make the outreach feel natural rather than random. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Write a win back message that uses a seasonal moment as a natural reason to reconnect.
useCase: Use this when a season or event gives a natural, non random reason to reach out to lapsed customers. A timely occasion makes win back outreach feel genuine rather than out of the blue, giving you a warm pretext to reconnect and a built in reason to return that fits the moment.

title: Win Back by Reminding Them of the Value
promptText: Write a win back message for a lapsed customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] centered on reminding them of the value or results they got or could get. When they were active, the value they experienced was [THE VALUE OR RESULTS], and the outcome they are missing now is [WHAT THEY ARE MISSING]. Write a message that reconnects them with the tangible benefit of what I offer, reminds them of the results without bragging, and frames returning as getting back to that value. The goal is to reawaken their memory of why it was worth it. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and my brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Win back a customer by reawakening their memory of the value they got.
useCase: Use this when a customer drifted away and simply forgot the value they were getting. Reminding them of the concrete results reawakens why it was worth it, since people often lapse not from dissatisfaction but from inertia, and reconnecting them with the tangible benefit gives a compelling reason to return.

title: Win Back a High Value Customer
promptText: Help me win back a high value former customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who was one of my best but has gone quiet. Their history and value to my business was [THEIR HISTORY AND VALUE], and they have been away for [HOW LONG]. Write a thoughtful personal outreach that treats them with the importance they warrant, reconnects genuinely, acknowledges the relationship, and opens the door to working together again, potentially with a personal gesture. This customer justifies real effort, so make it feel individual and considered, not a campaign. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me the message and a note on any gesture worth including.
description: Win back one of your best former customers with thoughtful, individual outreach.
useCase: Use this for a top former customer whose return would meaningfully impact your business. High value customers justify real individual effort, and a thoughtful personal approach that honors the relationship recovers the accounts that matter most, where a generic campaign would waste the opportunity.

title: Analyze Why Customers Leave
promptText: Help me analyze why customers of my [BUSINESS TYPE] lapse or leave, so my win back efforts address the real reasons. Here is what I know about customers who left: [WHAT YOU KNOW, E.G. PATTERNS, FEEDBACK, OR CANCELLATION REASONS]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER] for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me identify the likely reasons customers drift away, distinguish reasons I can address in win back messaging from deeper issues to fix in the business, and recommend how to tailor win back outreach to each type of lapsed customer. The goal is win back that speaks to why they actually left. Give me the analysis and recommendations.
description: Analyze why customers lapse so win back messaging addresses the real reasons.
useCase: Use this before building win back campaigns to ground them in why customers actually leave. Understanding the real reasons lets you tailor outreach that resonates and separates fixable messaging issues from deeper business problems, making win back far more effective than generic we miss you messages that ignore the cause.
variables: WHAT YOU KNOW, E.G. PATTERNS, FEEDBACK, OR CANCELLATION REASONS

title: Segment Lapsed Customers for Win Back
promptText: Help me segment my lapsed customers so I can win them back with the right message for each group rather than one generic blast. My types of lapsed customers include [DESCRIBE YOUR LAPSED CUSTOMERS, E.G. ONE TIME BUYERS, LONG TERM CUSTOMERS WHO LEFT, CANCELLED SUBSCRIBERS, OR OLD INQUIRIES]. My offer is [YOUR OFFER]. Help me define the meaningful segments, what likely drove each to lapse, and the win back angle and message type that fits each. The goal is targeted win back that speaks to each group's situation. Prioritize the segments most worth the effort. Give me the segments and the recommended approach for each. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Segment lapsed customers so each group gets a relevant win back approach.
useCase: Use this to make win back efforts targeted rather than one size fits all. Different lapsed customers left for different reasons, and segmenting them so each gets a relevant message dramatically improves results, while focusing effort on the segments most worth recovering respects a solo operator's limited time.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR LAPSED CUSTOMERS, E.G. ONE TIME BUYERS, LONG TERM CUSTOMERS WHO LEFT, CANCELLED SUBSCRIBERS, OR OLD INQUIRIES

title: Plan When to Send Win Back Messages
promptText: Help me figure out the right timing for win back outreach to lapsed customers of my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My typical customer buying or engagement cycle is [DESCRIBE YOUR CYCLE, E.G. HOW OFTEN THEY NORMALLY BUY OR ENGAGE]. Help me determine: when a customer should be considered lapsed and worth a win back message, the best timing to reach out so it is not too soon or too late, how to space a win back sequence, and any natural trigger points that signal the right moment. The goal is to reach lapsed customers when a win back is most likely to work. Give me a timing plan I can follow. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Plan the right timing and triggers for win back outreach.
useCase: Use this to time win back messages for maximum effect. Reaching out too soon feels pushy and too late loses the window entirely, so knowing when a customer counts as lapsed and the best moment to re engage, based on your buying cycle, makes win back outreach land when it is most likely to succeed.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR CYCLE, E.G. HOW OFTEN THEY NORMALLY BUY OR ENGAGE

title: Write a Win Back Text Message
promptText: Write a short win back text message for a lapsed customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] who has opted in to texts. They previously [WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED], and my reason or offer to bring them back is [YOUR REASON OR OFFER]. Write a brief, warm, personal text that reconnects and gives an easy reason to return, respecting that texts must be short and non intrusive. Keep it friendly and human, not a salesy blast, and include a clear simple next step. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me two or three short versions to choose from.
description: Write a short, warm win back text message for opted in lapsed customers.
useCase: Use this to win back customers through text, where opened in messages get high visibility. A brief warm text respects the channel's personal immediate nature and can re engage customers who ignore emails, provided it stays friendly and non intrusive rather than reading as a spammy sales blast.
variables: WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED

title: Get a Quick Win Back Message Fast
promptText: I need a win back message fast for a lapsed customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE]. They previously [WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED], and my reason or offer to bring them back is [YOUR REASON OR OFFER]. Give me a warm, ready to send message with a subject line, written for [TARGET AUDIENCE] in a [TONE] tone, that reconnects genuinely and gives an easy reason to return without being pushy. Keep it ready to send with minimal editing. Just give me a solid ready to use win back message.
description: Get a warm, ready to send win back message quickly with minimal effort.
useCase: Use this when you need a solid win back message right away without building a full campaign. A quick ready to send message lets you re engage a lapsed customer promptly, since acting on the impulse to reach out while it is top of mind means the outreach actually happens rather than staying on a someday list.
variables: WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED

title: Fix a Win Back Message That Sounds Desperate
promptText: Here is a win back message I drafted for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [PASTE YOUR MESSAGE]. It sounds desperate, needy, or guilt trippy, which I do not want. Rewrite it to feel warm and confident rather than pleading, keeping the genuine reconnection while removing anything that sounds like begging for the sale or laying on guilt. Match my voice, which is [DESCRIBE YOUR VOICE OR PASTE A SAMPLE], and my audience, which is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Show me the improved version and note what created the desperate tone so I can avoid it going forward. The goal is win back that respects both of us.
description: Rewrite a win back message that sounds desperate into one that is warm and confident.
useCase: Use this when a win back draft comes across as needy or guilt tripping, which repels customers. Rewriting it to feel warm and confident preserves your dignity and the relationship, since desperation signals weakness and pushes lapsed customers further away, while a confident invitation makes returning feel appealing rather than obligatory.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR VOICE OR PASTE A SAMPLE

title: Decide Who to Win Back and Who to Let Go
promptText: Help me decide which lapsed customers of my [BUSINESS TYPE] are worth win back effort and which to let go or remove from my list. My types of lapsed contacts are [DESCRIBE THEM, E.G. GOOD PAST CUSTOMERS, ONE TIME BARGAIN BUYERS, UNRESPONSIVE OLD LEADS, OR PROBLEM CUSTOMERS]. Help me think through: which lapsed customers have the best return on win back effort, which are unlikely to return or not worth recovering, and how to focus my limited time on the highest potential contacts. Also advise when cleaning up an unresponsive list is healthier than chasing everyone. Give me a framework for prioritizing and pruning. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE].
description: Decide which lapsed customers are worth winning back and which to let go.
useCase: Use this to focus win back effort where it pays off. Not every lapsed customer is worth recovering, and knowing which to pursue versus release protects a solo operator's limited time, while pruning genuinely unresponsive contacts keeps your list healthy rather than chasing everyone indiscriminately.
variables: DESCRIBE THEM, E.G. GOOD PAST CUSTOMERS, ONE TIME BARGAIN BUYERS, UNRESPONSIVE OLD LEADS, OR PROBLEM CUSTOMERS

title: Write a Win Back Direct Message
promptText: Write a win back direct message to send on social media to a lapsed customer or follower of my [BUSINESS TYPE] I have a genuine connection with. They previously [WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED], and my reason to reconnect is [YOUR REASON]. Write a casual, personal DM that fits the informal nature of social messaging, reconnects genuinely as a real person, and reopens the door naturally without feeling like a copy paste sales pitch. Keep it short and human, since DMs that feel automated get ignored or reported. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a couple of versions to choose from.
description: Write a casual, personal win back DM for social media that does not feel automated.
useCase: Use this to win back a customer through a social DM where you have a genuine connection. A casual personal message fits the informal channel and can re engage someone who ignores email, but it must feel human rather than a copy paste pitch, since automated feeling DMs get ignored or reported as spam.
variables: WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED

title: Re Engage a Lapsed Customer Through a Referral or Update
promptText: Write a message that re engages a lapsed customer of my [BUSINESS TYPE] through a non salesy angle like sharing a useful update, a helpful resource, or a genuine reason unrelated to immediately selling. The valuable thing I can share is [WHAT YOU CAN SHARE, E.G. A HELPFUL RESOURCE, AN UPDATE, OR NEWS RELEVANT TO THEM], and they previously [WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED]. Write a message that leads with genuine value or connection rather than a pitch, rebuilds the relationship warmly, and lets any return happen naturally from re establishing goodwill. The goal is relationship first re engagement. My brand voice is [BRAND VOICE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Re engage a lapsed customer by leading with genuine value instead of a pitch.
useCase: Use this to reconnect with lapsed customers through goodwill rather than a direct pitch. Leading with something genuinely useful rebuilds the relationship without pressure, which often works better than an offer, since re establishing warmth and value first makes any eventual return feel natural rather than transactional.
variables: WHAT YOU CAN SHARE, E.G. A HELPFUL RESOURCE, AN UPDATE, OR NEWS RELEVANT TO THEM
WHAT THEY BOUGHT OR HOW THEY ENGAGED

title: Plan Your Daily Priorities
promptText: Help me plan my priorities for today. Here is everything on my plate: [BRAIN DUMP OF TASKS AND COMMITMENTS]. My most important goal right now is [YOUR MAIN GOAL], and I have roughly [HOURS AVAILABLE] of focused work time today. Help me identify the few tasks that actually move the needle versus busywork, decide what to do first, what to defer, and what to drop, and lay out a realistic order for the day.
