Do not overload the day. Flag anything that looks urgent but is not actually important. Give me a focused daily plan I can follow.
description: Turn a messy task list into a focused, realistic daily plan built around what matters.
useCase: Use this at the start of a day when everything feels urgent and you are not sure where to begin. Separating the needle movers from busywork and setting a realistic order keeps a solo operator focused on what actually grows the business rather than reacting to whatever shouts loudest.
variables: BRAIN DUMP OF TASKS AND COMMITMENTS

title: Run a Weekly Planning Session
promptText: Guide me through a weekly planning session for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My bigger goals this month or quarter are [YOUR GOALS], what I did not finish last week is [CARRYOVER TASKS], and known commitments and deadlines this week are [THIS WEEK COMMITMENTS]. Help me set the handful of outcomes that would make this week a success, map the key tasks to reach them, spread the workload realistically across the days, and protect time for the important but not urgent work I usually neglect. Keep it focused and achievable, not an overloaded wish list. Give me a clear plan for the week.
description: Run a structured weekly planning session that sets realistic priorities and protects key work.
useCase: Use this at the start of each week to work proactively instead of reactively. A weekly plan that ties daily work to bigger goals and protects important but not urgent tasks stops the drift where a solo operator stays busy all week yet never advances what actually matters.

title: Prioritize a Overwhelming Task List
promptText: I have too much to do and feel overwhelmed. Here is my full task list: [YOUR TASK LIST]. My current top priority is [YOUR PRIORITY], and my hard deadlines are [ANY DEADLINES]. Help me sort these by what is both urgent and important versus what only feels urgent, what is important but not time sensitive, and what I can drop or delegate entirely. Point out anything on this list that is not actually worth doing. Then give me a clear prioritized order and tell me the single thing to start with right now. Cut through the overwhelm and make this manageable.
description: Sort an overwhelming task list by true priority and get a clear starting point.
useCase: Use this when your task list feels paralyzing and everything seems important. Sorting by genuine urgency and importance, and cutting what does not deserve doing, turns overwhelm into a clear ordered path, which is often the difference between a productive day and a frozen one.

title: Build a Time Blocked Daily Schedule
promptText: Help me build a time blocked schedule for my day. My tasks and their rough time needs are [TASKS AND ESTIMATED DURATIONS], my fixed commitments are [MEETINGS OR FIXED EVENTS], and my energy tends to be highest in the [MORNING, AFTERNOON, OR EVENING]. Help me assign tasks to specific time blocks, placing demanding focus work in my peak energy window and lighter tasks elsewhere, with realistic buffers between blocks. Do not pack it so tight it falls apart at the first interruption. Give me a realistic time blocked schedule for the day.
description: Build a realistic time blocked daily schedule matched to your energy and commitments.
useCase: Use this to turn a task list into an actual schedule that respects your energy and leaves room for reality. Placing demanding work in your peak window with realistic buffers means the plan survives contact with the day instead of collapsing at the first interruption.

title: Run a Weekly Review and Reflection
promptText: Guide me through a weekly review to reflect and learn before I plan next week. Here is how this week went: [WHAT YOU ACCOMPLISHED, WHAT SLIPPED, AND HOW YOU FELT]. Ask me or walk through the right reflection points: what went well and why, what did not get done and the real reason, what I learned, where I wasted time or energy, and what to adjust. Keep it honest and constructive, not self critical. End with the two or three lessons or changes to carry into next week. Help me actually learn from the week rather than just moving on.
description: Run an honest weekly review that turns the past week into lessons for the next.
useCase: Use this at the end of a week to extract lessons before planning the next one. An honest constructive review of what worked and what slipped, and why, compounds into steady improvement, since a solo operator who reflects gets better each week while one who just moves on repeats the same patterns.
variables: WHAT YOU ACCOMPLISHED, WHAT SLIPPED, AND HOW YOU FELT

title: Assign Themes to Your Days
promptText: Help me set up themed days to reduce context switching in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The main types of work I do are [YOUR WORK CATEGORIES, E.G. CLIENT WORK, MARKETING, ADMIN, CONTENT, SALES], and my week has [NUMBER OF WORKING DAYS] working days. Help me group similar work onto dedicated days or half days so I batch like tasks together, protect focus, and stop switching between unrelated modes all day. Account for any work that cannot be confined to one day. Explain how theming reduces the cost of context switching. Give me a suggested themed week structure.
description: Group similar work onto themed days to cut context switching and protect focus.
useCase: Use this if you switch constantly between unrelated types of work and lose focus. Assigning themes to days batches similar work so your brain stays in one mode longer, cutting the hidden cost of context switching that quietly drains a solo operator juggling every role.
variables: YOUR WORK CATEGORIES, E.G. CLIENT WORK, MARKETING, ADMIN, CONTENT, SALES

title: Protect Time for Deep Work
promptText: Help me carve out and protect deep work time for my most important focus work. The demanding work that needs uninterrupted focus is [YOUR DEEP WORK], my typical daily interruptions and obligations are [YOUR INTERRUPTIONS AND COMMITMENTS], and my sharpest hours are [YOUR PEAK HOURS]. Help me design where deep work blocks fit, how long they should be, how to protect them from meetings, messages, and distractions, and how to defend them consistently. Be realistic given my obligations. Give me a plan to reliably get real focus time each day or week for the work that matters most.
description: Carve out and defend regular deep work blocks for your most important focus work.
useCase: Use this when shallow tasks and interruptions crowd out the focused work that actually grows your business. Deliberately protecting deep work blocks in your peak hours ensures the important high value work gets done, rather than perpetually losing to the endless small stuff that fills a day.

title: Check if Your Plan Is Realistic
promptText: Check whether my plan for the day or week is actually realistic or if I am overcommitting. Here is what I have planned: [YOUR PLANNED TASKS WITH ANY TIME ESTIMATES], and the time I actually have available is [YOUR AVAILABLE TIME]. Be honest about whether this fits, where I am likely underestimating how long things take, what will realistically not get done, and what to cut or move so the plan is achievable. Account for interruptions, breaks, and the tasks that always run long. I would rather face reality now than fail the plan by noon. Give me an honest reality check and a trimmed realistic version.
description: Get an honest reality check on whether your plan fits the time you actually have.
useCase: Use this before committing to a day or week that may be overloaded. An honest check on whether the work fits the available time, accounting for interruptions and underestimated durations, prevents the demoralizing pattern of setting plans you cannot possibly finish and feeling behind before lunch.
variables: YOUR PLANNED TASKS WITH ANY TIME ESTIMATES

title: Decide What to Delegate or Drop
promptText: Help me decide what on my plate to keep, delegate, automate, or drop. Here is what I am doing regularly: [YOUR RECURRING TASKS AND RESPONSIBILITIES]. My highest value work, what only I can do well, is [YOUR HIGH VALUE WORK], and my delegation options are [WHO OR WHAT YOU COULD DELEGATE TO, OR WRITE LIMITED]. Help me sort each task: what only I should do, what someone else could do, what could be automated, and what does not need doing at all. Point out where I am spending time on low value work I should offload. Give me a clear keep, delegate, automate, or drop decision for each.
description: Sort your recurring tasks into keep, delegate, automate, or drop.
useCase: Use this when you are doing everything yourself and drowning in low value work. Deciding what only you can do versus what to offload, automate, or drop frees your time for the high value work that actually grows the business, which is the core discipline of scaling as a solo operator.
variables: WHO OR WHAT YOU COULD DELEGATE TO, OR WRITE LIMITED

title: Reset When You Feel Overwhelmed
promptText: I am feeling overwhelmed and scattered and cannot think clearly about what to do. Here is everything swirling in my head: [BRAIN DUMP OF EVERYTHING ON YOUR MIND]. Help me reset: get it all out of my head and organized, separate the genuine priorities from the noise, identify what actually needs attention today versus what can wait, and give me one small concrete first step to regain momentum. Keep it calm and grounding, not another overwhelming list. Help me get unstuck and moving again with a clear, manageable next action.
description: Reset from overwhelm by organizing the mental clutter into a calm, clear next step.
useCase: Use this in a moment of overwhelm when you cannot think straight. Getting everything out of your head, separating real priorities from noise, and finding one small first step breaks the paralysis, since overwhelm usually comes from carrying it all mentally rather than from the work itself being impossible.
variables: BRAIN DUMP OF EVERYTHING ON YOUR MIND

title: Design a Productive Morning Routine
promptText: Help me design a morning routine that sets up a productive day for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My mornings currently look like [YOUR CURRENT MORNING], the time I realistically have is [AVAILABLE MORNING TIME], and what a good day needs from me is [WHAT HELPS YOU WORK WELL]. Help me design a realistic morning routine that eases me into focused work, includes a quick planning step to set the day's priorities, and avoids starting the day reactive in my inbox. Keep it achievable for who I actually am, not an idealized guru routine. Give me a simple morning routine I can stick to.
description: Design a realistic morning routine that starts your day focused and intentional.
useCase: Use this if your days start reactive and scattered in the inbox. A simple realistic morning routine with a quick planning step sets an intentional tone that shapes the whole day, and starting focused rather than reactive is one of the highest leverage habits for a solo operator.

title: Create an End of Day Shutdown Routine
promptText: Help me create an end of day shutdown routine so I can stop working and actually disconnect. Right now my workday ends with [HOW YOUR DAY CURRENTLY ENDS], and I struggle with [YOUR STRUGGLE, E.G. NEVER FEELING DONE, WORKING LATE, OR CARRYING WORK STRESS HOME]. Help me design a short closing routine that reviews what I did, captures loose ends and tomorrow's priorities so nothing is lost, and creates a clear mental line between work and rest. This matters especially since my work and life blur together. Give me a simple shutdown routine that lets me truly stop for the day.
description: Create an end of day shutdown routine that captures loose ends and lets you disconnect.
useCase: Use this if you never feel done and struggle to switch off, common when work and home blur. A shutdown routine that captures loose ends and sets tomorrow's priorities creates a clear stopping line, protecting the rest that sustains a solo operator and prevents the slow burn toward burnout.
variables: YOUR STRUGGLE, E.G. NEVER FEELING DONE, WORKING LATE, OR CARRYING WORK STRESS HOME

title: Organize Your Recurring Tasks
promptText: Help me organize the recurring tasks that keep my [BUSINESS TYPE] running so they stop cluttering my mind and slipping through cracks. My recurring tasks are [LIST YOUR DAILY, WEEKLY, MONTHLY RECURRING TASKS]. Help me sort them by frequency, suggest the best day or time to attach each to so it becomes routine, identify which could be batched together, and flag any I do out of habit that may not be worth continuing. The goal is a clear recurring task rhythm I can rely on rather than remembering everything ad hoc. Give me an organized recurring task schedule.
description: Organize recurring tasks into a reliable rhythm so nothing slips through the cracks.
useCase: Use this when the routine tasks that keep your business running clutter your mind or get forgotten. Organizing them into a reliable rhythm attached to specific days frees mental space and prevents dropped balls, since trying to remember every recurring obligation ad hoc is a constant low grade drain.
variables: LIST YOUR DAILY, WEEKLY, MONTHLY RECURRING TASKS

title: Protect Your Focus from Distractions
promptText: Help me protect my focus from the distractions that derail my workday. My biggest distractions are [YOUR DISTRACTIONS, E.G. PHONE, EMAIL, SOCIAL MEDIA, MESSAGES, INTERRUPTIONS], and they tend to hit when [WHEN DISTRACTIONS STRIKE]. Help me build a practical plan to defend my focus: which distractions to eliminate versus manage, concrete tactics for each, how to set boundaries around interruptions, and how to build focus as a habit. Keep the tactics realistic for how I actually work, not extreme. Give me a focus protection plan I can put in place today.
description: Build a practical plan to defend your focus from your specific distractions.
useCase: Use this when distractions constantly derail your work. A practical plan targeting your specific distractions protects the sustained focus that quality work requires, since for a solo operator every interruption costs not just the moment but the time to regain concentration, adding up to a large hidden tax on the day.
variables: YOUR DISTRACTIONS, E.G. PHONE, EMAIL, SOCIAL MEDIA, MESSAGES, INTERRUPTIONS

title: Reduce Costly Context Switching
promptText: Help me reduce the context switching that fragments my day and drains my energy. A typical day for me involves switching between [THE DIFFERENT TYPES OF WORK AND ROLES YOU JUGGLE], often many times. Help me see where the costly switches happen, how to batch similar tasks to switch less, how to group communications and small tasks instead of scattering them, and how to structure my day around fewer, longer stretches of one type of work. Explain why constant switching is so costly. Give me a concrete plan to cut context switching and keep more of my mental energy.
description: Cut costly context switching by batching similar work into longer stretches.
useCase: Use this when juggling many roles leaves you drained and scattered by day's end. Batching similar work to switch less preserves the mental energy that constant switching quietly burns, a major hidden cost for solo operators who wear every hat and jump between unrelated tasks all day.
variables: THE DIFFERENT TYPES OF WORK AND ROLES YOU JUGGLE

title: Batch Similar Tasks Together
promptText: Help me batch my similar tasks together to work more efficiently. Here are the recurring tasks and small jobs that fill my week: [LIST YOUR TASKS]. Help me group them into logical batches that make sense to do in one focused session, such as all my content, all my admin, all my client communication, or all my errands. For each batch suggest a good cadence and when to do it. Explain how batching reduces setup time and switching costs. Point out anything I currently do one off that would be far more efficient batched. Give me a batching plan for my recurring work.
description: Group similar tasks into focused batches to cut setup time and work more efficiently.
useCase: Use this when you handle small recurring tasks one at a time throughout the day. Batching similar work into focused sessions cuts the repeated setup and switching costs, letting a solo operator clear the same work in less time and with less mental fatigue than scattering it across the week.

title: Break a Big Goal into Weekly Tasks
promptText: Help me break a big goal down into weekly and daily tasks so I actually make progress on it. My goal is [YOUR GOAL], I want to reach it by [YOUR TIMEFRAME], and where I am now is [YOUR STARTING POINT]. Help me work backward: the milestones between here and the goal, what needs to happen each week to stay on track, and the concrete next tasks to start with. Make it realistic alongside my regular work. The goal is to turn a big intimidating goal into clear weekly actions I can actually schedule. Give me the milestone breakdown and the first week of tasks.
description: Break a big goal into realistic weekly milestones and concrete daily tasks.
useCase: Use this when a big goal feels stuck because it is too large to act on. Breaking it into weekly milestones and concrete next tasks turns an intimidating ambition into schedulable actions, which is how important goals actually get achieved rather than perpetually postponed behind daily busywork.

title: Analyze Your Productivity Patterns
promptText: Help me understand my productivity patterns so I can work with them instead of against them. Here is what I have noticed about my energy, focus, and output through the day and week: [YOUR OBSERVATIONS, E.G. WHEN YOU FEEL SHARP, WHEN YOU CRASH, WHAT DRAINS YOU]. Help me identify my patterns, when I should schedule demanding versus easy work, what conditions help me do my best work, and what consistently derails me. Then give me practical adjustments to structure my days around my natural rhythms. Help me stop fighting my patterns and design my schedule around how I actually work best.
description: Analyze your energy and focus patterns to schedule work around your natural rhythms.
useCase: Use this to stop fighting your natural rhythms and design your schedule around them. Understanding when you focus best and what derails you lets you place demanding work in peak windows, which for a solo operator with full control over their schedule is a large and underused productivity advantage.
variables: YOUR OBSERVATIONS, E.G. WHEN YOU FEEL SHARP, WHEN YOU CRASH, WHAT DRAINS YOU

title: Clean Up a Bloated To Do List
promptText: Help me clean up my bloated to do list that has become an overwhelming graveyard of tasks. Here is my current list: [PASTE YOUR TO DO LIST]. Help me ruthlessly clean it: identify what is genuinely worth doing versus what has been sitting there so long it should just be deleted, group what remains sensibly, flag anything that is actually important and getting buried, and separate real actionable tasks from vague someday ideas. Give me back a lean, honest, workable list plus a short separate someday list for the rest. Help me feel in control of my tasks again rather than buried by them.
description: Ruthlessly clean up a bloated to do list into a lean, honest, workable one.
useCase: Use this when your to do list has become an overwhelming graveyard you avoid looking at. Ruthlessly cutting the dead weight and surfacing what actually matters restores a list you can trust and act on, since a bloated list creates guilt and paralysis rather than the clarity a good list should provide.

title: Protect a Meeting Free Focus Day
promptText: Help me set up and protect a meeting free day each week for focused work in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My meetings and calls currently fall on [WHEN YOUR MEETINGS HAPPEN], and the deep work I need uninterrupted time for is [YOUR FOCUS WORK]. Help me choose which day to keep meeting free, how to structure my scheduling so meetings cluster on other days, how to communicate this boundary to clients and contacts, and how to defend the day when requests come in. Give me a plan to reliably protect one full focus day a week and make the most of it.
description: Set up and protect one meeting free day each week for uninterrupted focus work.
useCase: Use this when scattered meetings fragment every day and leave no long focus stretches. Protecting one meeting free day a week gives you the uninterrupted time deep work requires, and clustering meetings on other days is a simple structural change that dramatically increases how much meaningful work gets done.

title: Get a Quick Daily Plan Fast
promptText: I need to quickly plan my day. Here is my task list and commitments: [YOUR TASKS AND COMMITMENTS], and I have about [HOURS AVAILABLE] to work. Give me a fast, clear plan: the top three priorities that matter most, a sensible order to tackle everything, and what to cut if I run out of time. Keep it quick and practical, no long explanation. Just give me a focused plan for the day I can start on right now.
description: Get a fast, focused daily plan with top priorities and a sensible order.
useCase: Use this when you just need to plan the day quickly and start working. A fast plan naming your top priorities and a sensible order gets you moving without a long planning session, ideal for busy mornings when the goal is simply to act with focus rather than deliberate at length.

title: Plan Your Week Around Real Capacity
promptText: Help me plan my week around my real capacity so I stop overcommitting. This week my available working hours are [YOUR AVAILABLE HOURS], my fixed commitments take up [FIXED COMMITMENTS AND THEIR TIME], and the work I want to fit in is [WHAT YOU WANT TO ACCOMPLISH]. Help me calculate how much discretionary time I actually have after commitments, whether my intended work realistically fits, and how to prioritize if it does not. Account for the buffer real life always needs. Give me an honest capacity based plan so I commit to what I can actually deliver this week.
description: Plan your week around your real available capacity to stop overcommitting.
useCase: Use this when you routinely plan more than a week can hold and end up behind. Calculating your real discretionary time after fixed commitments, with a buffer for reality, lets you commit only to what you can deliver, replacing the chronic overcommit and disappointment cycle with achievable, honest weekly plans.

title: Prioritize When Everything Feels Urgent
promptText: Everything on my plate feels urgent right now and I do not know what to tackle first. Here is the situation: [DESCRIBE WHAT IS ALL HAPPENING AT ONCE]. Help me cut through it: which of these are truly urgent with real consequences versus which just feel urgent, what carries the biggest cost if it slips, what can wait longer than it feels like it can, and the actual order to handle things. Help me think clearly under pressure rather than react to whatever is loudest. Give me a clear triage and the one thing to do first.
description: Triage competing urgent demands and find the one thing to do first under pressure.
useCase: Use this in the pressured moment when everything seems to demand attention at once. Triaging genuine urgency from perceived urgency and finding the true first action restores clear thinking under pressure, preventing the reactive scramble where a solo operator handles whatever shouts loudest rather than what matters most.
variables: DESCRIBE WHAT IS ALL HAPPENING AT ONCE

title: Build Buffer Time into Your Schedule
promptText: Help me build buffer time into my schedule so unexpected things do not blow up my whole plan. Right now I schedule [HOW YOU CURRENTLY SCHEDULE, E.G. BACK TO BACK, EVERY HOUR FILLED], and what regularly disrupts my plans is [WHAT THROWS OFF YOUR DAY, E.G. OVERRUNNING TASKS, URGENT REQUESTS, INTERRUPTIONS]. Help me design a schedule with realistic buffers: how much unstructured time to leave, where to place it, and how to use it to absorb overruns and surprises without falling behind. Explain why a fully packed schedule always breaks. Give me a plan to build in breathing room that makes my days more resilient.
description: Build realistic buffer time into your schedule so surprises do not derail everything.
useCase: Use this if your days fall apart the moment anything runs long or unexpected. Building in buffer time absorbs the overruns and surprises that are inevitable, making your schedule resilient, since a fully packed plan with no slack breaks at the first disruption and cascades delays through the whole day.
variables: HOW YOU CURRENTLY SCHEDULE, E.G. BACK TO BACK, EVERY HOUR FILLED
WHAT THROWS OFF YOUR DAY, E.G. OVERRUNNING TASKS, URGENT REQUESTS, INTERRUPTIONS

title: Turn Rough Notes into Clean Meeting Notes
promptText: Turn my rough meeting notes into clean, organized notes. Here is what I jotted down: [PASTE YOUR ROUGH NOTES]. The meeting was about [MEETING TOPIC] with [WHO WAS THERE]. Organize this into a clear structure: the key points discussed, decisions made, action items with who owns them, and any open questions or follow ups. Fill in obvious gaps from context but flag anything unclear rather than inventing it. Keep it concise and scannable so I or anyone else can quickly see what happened and what comes next. Give me the cleaned up notes.
description: Turn messy meeting scribbles into clean, structured, scannable notes.
useCase: Use this after a meeting to convert rushed scribbles into notes you can actually use. Clean structured notes capture decisions and action items before they fade from memory, which prevents the dropped follow ups and forgotten commitments that cost a solo operator time and credibility.

title: Extract Action Items from a Meeting
promptText: Extract the action items from this meeting. Here are the notes or transcript: [PASTE NOTES OR TRANSCRIPT]. Pull out every action item, task, and commitment mentioned, and for each note who owns it, any deadline mentioned, and what exactly needs to happen. Separate clear commitments from vague maybes that need confirming. Flag anything that sounded like a task but lacks an owner so nothing falls through the cracks. Put the most important or time sensitive items first. Give me a clean action item list I can work from and share.
description: Pull every action item, owner, and deadline out of meeting notes or a transcript.
useCase: Use this to make sure nothing agreed in a meeting gets forgotten. Extracting every action item with its owner and deadline turns discussion into accountability, catching the ownerless tasks and vague commitments that otherwise slip through and become the things nobody remembered to do.

title: Write a Concise Meeting Summary
promptText: Write a concise summary of this meeting for people who need the outcome without the full detail. Here are the notes or transcript: [PASTE NOTES OR TRANSCRIPT]. The meeting was about [MEETING TOPIC]. Write a short summary that captures what was discussed, what was decided, and what happens next, in a few tight paragraphs or a brief bulleted recap. Lead with the most important outcome. Keep it clear enough that someone who was not there understands what matters. Give me the summary.
description: Write a tight meeting summary that captures outcomes for those who need the gist.
useCase: Use this to share a meeting outcome with people who need the result but not every detail. A concise summary that leads with what was decided and what comes next respects everyone's time and keeps stakeholders aligned without making them read full notes or attend a recap.

title: Summarize a Long Meeting Transcript
promptText: Summarize this long meeting transcript into something useful. Here is the transcript: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT]. The meeting was about [MEETING TOPIC]. Distill it into: a short overview of what the meeting covered, the key points and discussion, all decisions made, action items with owners, and any unresolved questions. Cut the filler, tangents, and repetition, keeping only what matters. Preserve important specifics like numbers, dates, and commitments exactly. Give me a summary that saves me from ever rereading the full transcript.
description: Distill a long meeting transcript into a useful summary with decisions and actions.
useCase: Use this to make sense of a long recorded meeting without rereading the whole transcript. Distilling it into key points, decisions, and action items while preserving exact specifics saves significant time and turns an unwieldy transcript into a reference you can actually act on.

title: Write a Post Meeting Follow Up Email
promptText: Write a follow up email to send after this meeting. The meeting was with [WHO] about [MEETING TOPIC], and here is what was discussed and agreed: [KEY POINTS, DECISIONS, AND ACTION ITEMS]. Write an email that thanks them, recaps the key decisions and next steps clearly, confirms who is doing what by when, and keeps momentum toward the outcome. Keep it professional and concise. The recap should leave no ambiguity about what was agreed. My tone is [TONE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a post meeting follow up email that confirms decisions and next steps.
useCase: Use this after a meeting to confirm what was agreed in writing. A clear recap email that spells out decisions and who does what by when prevents the misunderstandings and dropped commitments that arise when everyone leaves a meeting with a slightly different memory of what was decided.
variables: KEY POINTS, DECISIONS, AND ACTION ITEMS

title: Build a Meeting Agenda
promptText: Help me build a focused agenda for an upcoming meeting. The meeting is about [MEETING PURPOSE] with [WHO IS ATTENDING], and it needs to last no more than [MEETING LENGTH]. What I need to accomplish is [DESIRED OUTCOMES]. Build an agenda that opens with the goal, orders the topics sensibly with rough time allocations, keeps the discussion on track toward the outcomes, and ends with clear next steps. Cut anything that does not need a meeting. Keep it tight so the meeting is efficient and actually achieves its purpose. Give me the agenda.
description: Build a focused, time boxed meeting agenda that drives to clear outcomes.
useCase: Use this before a meeting to keep it efficient and purposeful. A tight agenda with time allocations and clear goals prevents the meandering, overrunning meetings that waste time, ensuring the meeting achieves its outcome rather than eating an hour a solo operator cannot spare.

title: Prepare for an Important Meeting
promptText: Help me prepare for an important meeting. The meeting is with [WHO] about [MEETING TOPIC], and what I want to get out of it is [YOUR GOAL]. What I know about the situation and the other party is [CONTEXT]. Help me prepare: the key points I need to make, the questions I should ask, the likely questions or objections I will face and how to handle them, the outcome I am steering toward, and anything I should have ready. Help me walk in clear and confident rather than winging it. Give me a prep sheet for the meeting.
description: Prepare for an important meeting with key points, questions, and likely objections.
useCase: Use this before a meeting that matters, like a client pitch or negotiation. Preparing your key points, questions, and responses to likely objections lets you walk in clear and confident, which for a solo operator often makes the difference between a meeting that advances the business and one that drifts.

title: Prepare Talking Points for a Meeting
promptText: Help me prepare clear talking points for a meeting so I communicate what I need to without rambling or forgetting something. The meeting is with [WHO] about [MEETING TOPIC], and the things I need to get across are [WHAT YOU NEED TO SAY]. Turn these into concise, well ordered talking points I can glance at during the meeting, phrased so I make each point clearly and confidently. Note which points matter most in case time runs short. Keep it brief and usable in the moment, not a script. Give me my talking points.
description: Turn what you need to say into concise, glanceable talking points for a meeting.
useCase: Use this before a meeting where you need to communicate specific things clearly. Concise ordered talking points keep you on message and ensure nothing important gets forgotten, so you come across as prepared and articulate rather than rambling or realizing afterward you left out a key point.

title: Write a Client Meeting Recap
promptText: Write a professional recap of a client meeting to send to the client. The client is [CLIENT], the meeting was about [MEETING TOPIC], and here is what was discussed and agreed: [KEY POINTS, DECISIONS, DELIVERABLES, AND TIMELINES]. Write a recap that confirms my understanding of what was agreed, lists the deliverables and timelines clearly, notes what I need from them and what they can expect from me, and reinforces confidence that things are handled. This protects both sides by putting the agreement in writing. My tone is [TONE]. Give me a subject line and the recap.
description: Write a professional client meeting recap that confirms deliverables and timelines.
useCase: Use this after a client meeting to put the agreement in writing. A clear recap confirming deliverables, timelines, and mutual expectations protects both sides from misunderstandings and scope disputes, while reinforcing the client's confidence that they hired someone organized and reliable.
variables: KEY POINTS, DECISIONS, DELIVERABLES, AND TIMELINES

title: Log Decisions from a Meeting
promptText: Help me create a clean decision log from this meeting. Here are the notes or transcript: [PASTE NOTES OR TRANSCRIPT]. Pull out every decision that was made, and for each record what was decided, the reasoning or context behind it, who made or approved it, and the date. Separate firm decisions from things that were merely discussed but not decided. The goal is a clear record I can refer back to later when someone asks why we chose something or what we agreed. Give me the decision log.
description: Create a clean log of every decision made in a meeting with its reasoning.
useCase: Use this to keep a record of decisions and why they were made. A decision log prevents the frustrating situation of relitigating settled questions or forgetting the reasoning behind a choice, giving a solo operator or a team a reliable reference when someone later asks why something was decided.

title: Structure Notes for a One on One
promptText: Help me structure notes from a one on one meeting with [WHO, E.G. A TEAM MEMBER, CONTRACTOR, OR PARTNER]. Here is what we discussed: [PASTE YOUR ROUGH NOTES]. Organize it into: what is going well, any concerns or blockers raised, decisions or agreements reached, action items for each of us, and anything to follow up on next time. Keep a clear record so I can track progress and continuity across our one on ones. Flag anything I committed to so I do not forget. Give me the structured one on one notes.
description: Structure one on one meeting notes to track progress and follow ups over time.
useCase: Use this after a one on one with a team member, contractor, or partner. Structured notes that capture concerns, agreements, and action items maintain continuity across meetings, so you follow up reliably and track progress rather than starting fresh each time and dropping commitments you made.
variables: WHO, E.G. A TEAM MEMBER, CONTRACTOR, OR PARTNER

title: Decide if a Meeting Is Even Needed
promptText: Help me decide whether this needs to be a meeting or could be handled another way. What I need to accomplish is [WHAT YOU NEED TO DO OR DECIDE], the people involved are [WHO IS INVOLVED], and the context is [ANY CONTEXT]. Help me assess whether a live meeting is actually the best format, or whether an email, a message, a shared document, or an async update would work better and save everyone's time. If a meeting is warranted, note what would make it worthwhile. If not, suggest the better alternative and how to handle it. Give me your recommendation.
description: Decide whether something needs a meeting or could be handled async instead.
useCase: Use this before scheduling a meeting to protect your time and others'. Checking whether the goal could be met by an email, message, or shared doc avoids the unnecessary meetings that fragment a workday, since much of what becomes a meeting could be handled faster asynchronously without a scheduled call.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED TO DO OR DECIDE

title: Write an Async Update Instead of Meeting
promptText: Help me write a clear async update to replace a meeting. What I need to communicate is [WHAT YOU NEED TO SHARE OR ALIGN ON], the audience is [WHO], and any decision or input I need from them is [WHAT YOU NEED BACK, OR WRITE NONE]. Write an update that conveys everything clearly enough that no live meeting is needed, is easy to skim, makes any request for input or a decision explicit, and gives them what they need to respond async. Keep it complete but concise. Give me the async update.
description: Write a clear async update that replaces a meeting and requests any needed input.
useCase: Use this to handle alignment or updates without a meeting. A clear skimmable async update that makes any needed decision explicit lets people respond on their own time, replacing a scheduled call with something faster for everyone and freeing the calendar space that back to back meetings would otherwise consume.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED TO SHARE OR ALIGN ON
WHAT YOU NEED BACK, OR WRITE NONE

title: Prepare for a Difficult Meeting
promptText: Help me prepare for a difficult or high stakes meeting. The meeting is with [WHO] about [THE DIFFICULT TOPIC], the tension or challenge is [WHAT MAKES IT HARD], and the outcome I want is [YOUR DESIRED OUTCOME]. Help me prepare to handle it well: how to open, the key points to make calmly and clearly, how to anticipate and respond to their perspective or pushback, how to stay composed if it gets tense, and how to steer toward a constructive outcome. Help me go in prepared rather than anxious. Give me a prep plan for the difficult conversation.
description: Prepare to handle a difficult or high stakes meeting calmly and constructively.
useCase: Use this before a tense meeting like addressing a problem or a hard negotiation. Preparing how to open, respond to pushback, and stay composed lets you handle it constructively rather than reactively, since difficult conversations go far better with a plan than when improvised under stress.

title: Build a Reusable Meeting Notes Template
promptText: Build me a reusable meeting notes template so I capture the right things consistently every time. The meetings I most often have are [YOUR COMMON MEETING TYPES, E.G. CLIENT CALLS, PARTNER CHECK INS, PLANNING SESSIONS]. Create a clean template with clear sections for the essentials such as attendees, topic, key points, decisions, action items with owners and deadlines, and follow ups. Make it quick to fill in during or right after a meeting. If different meeting types need different templates, note that. Give me the template or templates.
description: Build a reusable meeting notes template that captures the essentials every time.
useCase: Use this once to standardize how you take meeting notes. A consistent template ensures you always capture decisions, action items, and owners rather than relying on memory in the moment, making your notes reliable and fast to complete, which is how commitments stop slipping through the cracks.
variables: YOUR COMMON MEETING TYPES, E.G. CLIENT CALLS, PARTNER CHECK INS, PLANNING SESSIONS

title: Get a Quick Meeting Summary Fast
promptText: I need a quick summary of this meeting fast. Here are my notes or the transcript: [PASTE NOTES OR TRANSCRIPT]. Give me a quick rundown: the main points, what was decided, and the action items with owners. Keep it brief and clear, no long formatting. Just give me a fast usable summary I can reference or share right now.
description: Get a fast, usable meeting summary with main points and action items.
useCase: Use this when you need a meeting summarized quickly to reference or share. A fast rundown of points, decisions, and action items captures the essentials without a lengthy process, ideal right after a meeting when you want the takeaways recorded before moving straight into the next thing.

title: Turn a Meeting Recording into Notes
promptText: I have a transcript from a meeting recording that I need turned into useful notes. Here is the raw transcript: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT]. It is messy with filler, crosstalk, and tangents. Clean it into organized notes: an overview, the key discussion points, decisions, action items with owners and any deadlines, and open questions. Cut the noise but preserve important specifics exactly. If speakers are labeled, attribute commitments to the right person. Give me clean notes that make the recording worth having captured.
description: Turn a messy meeting recording transcript into clean, organized notes.
useCase: Use this when you recorded a meeting and have a raw transcript to work from. Cleaning the messy transcript into organized notes with attributed action items makes the recording genuinely useful, saving you from rewatching or rereading it while ensuring commitments are captured against the right people.

title: Track Action Items Across Meetings
promptText: Help me track open action items and follow ups across my recent meetings so nothing gets lost. Here are the action items and commitments from my recent meetings: [PASTE ACTION ITEMS FROM RECENT MEETINGS]. Help me consolidate them into one clear tracker: what is outstanding, who owns each, any deadlines, and what is overdue or needs a nudge. Group or prioritize sensibly so I can see at a glance what needs attention. Flag anything that has been sitting too long. Give me a consolidated follow up tracker.
description: Consolidate action items across meetings into one tracker so nothing gets lost.
useCase: Use this when action items are scattered across multiple meetings and hard to track. Consolidating them into one prioritized tracker shows at a glance what is outstanding and overdue, preventing the dropped follow ups that happen when commitments live in separate sets of notes a solo operator never revisits.
variables: PASTE ACTION ITEMS FROM RECENT MEETINGS

title: Synthesize a Brainstorming Session
promptText: Help me synthesize the output of a brainstorming session into something useful. Here are all the ideas and notes from the session: [PASTE THE RAW IDEAS AND NOTES]. The session was about [THE TOPIC OR GOAL]. Help me organize the scattered ideas into themes, surface the strongest and most promising ones, note any that build on each other, and identify clear next steps or ideas worth pursuing. Do not lose good ideas in the mess. Turn the raw brainstorm into an organized summary I can actually act on. Give me the synthesis.
description: Synthesize a messy brainstorm into organized themes and actionable next steps.
useCase: Use this after a brainstorming session that produced lots of scattered ideas. Organizing them into themes and surfacing the strongest ones turns raw creative output into something actionable, so the good ideas get pursued rather than lost in the mess, which is where most brainstorm value quietly evaporates.
variables: PASTE THE RAW IDEAS AND NOTES

title: Capture Notes from Thinking Sessions
promptText: Help me organize the notes from a solo thinking or planning session with myself. Here is my brain dump from thinking through [THE TOPIC OR DECISION]: [PASTE YOUR RAW THINKING]. Help me make sense of it: organize my scattered thoughts, clarify what I actually concluded or decided, surface the open questions I still need to resolve, and pull out any action items for myself. Turn my messy thinking into a clear record I can act on and revisit. Give me the organized notes.
description: Organize a solo thinking session into clear conclusions, questions, and actions.
useCase: Use this after thinking through a decision or plan on your own. Organizing your raw thinking into clear conclusions, open questions, and action items captures the value of the session, so your reasoning is preserved and actionable rather than a jumble you have to reconstruct later when you revisit the topic.

title: Brief Someone Who Missed the Meeting
promptText: Help me brief someone who missed a meeting on what they need to know. Here are the meeting notes or summary: [PASTE NOTES OR SUMMARY]. The person who missed it is [WHO AND THEIR ROLE OR INTEREST], so focus on what is relevant to them. Write a brief that catches them up on the outcomes that affect them, anything they need to act on or be aware of, and any decisions relevant to their work, without making them read the full notes. Keep it focused on what matters to them specifically. Give me the catch up brief.
description: Brief someone who missed a meeting on just what is relevant to them.
useCase: Use this to catch up someone who missed a meeting. A focused brief covering only what affects them and what they need to act on respects their time and keeps them aligned, far more useful than forwarding full notes and leaving them to find the parts that actually matter to their work.
variables: WHO AND THEIR ROLE OR INTEREST

title: Optimize or Cut a Recurring Meeting
promptText: Help me evaluate a recurring meeting to make it better or cut it. The meeting is [DESCRIBE THE RECURRING MEETING, ITS FREQUENCY, AND WHO ATTENDS], its purpose is [ITS PURPOSE], and my honest feeling about it is [HOW USEFUL IT ACTUALLY IS]. Help me assess whether it still earns its place, whether it could be shorter, less frequent, async, or eliminated, and if it should continue, how to make it tighter and more valuable. Be honest if it sounds like a meeting that exists out of habit rather than need. Give me a recommendation and, if keeping it, how to improve it.
description: Evaluate a recurring meeting to tighten it, reduce it, or cut it entirely.
useCase: Use this to audit a recurring meeting that may have outlived its usefulness. Honestly assessing whether it still earns its place, and whether it could be shorter, less frequent, or cut, reclaims the standing time that recurring meetings consume out of habit, which is often among the easiest time savings to find.
variables: DESCRIBE THE RECURRING MEETING, ITS FREQUENCY, AND WHO ATTENDS

title: Prepare Questions to Ask in a Meeting
promptText: Help me prepare the right questions to ask in an upcoming meeting so I get what I need from it. The meeting is with [WHO] about [MEETING TOPIC], and what I most need to learn or clarify is [WHAT YOU NEED TO FIND OUT]. Generate the questions that will surface the information I need, in a sensible order, including any follow up questions to dig deeper and questions that uncover things I might not think to ask. Prioritize the must ask questions in case time is short. Give me my question list for the meeting.
description: Prepare a prioritized list of questions to get what you need from a meeting.
useCase: Use this before a meeting where you need to learn or clarify specific things. A prepared prioritized question list ensures you leave with the information you came for, avoiding the common regret of realizing afterward you forgot to ask the one thing that actually mattered most.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED TO FIND OUT

title: Turn Meeting Notes into Scheduled Tasks
promptText: Help me turn my meeting notes into scheduled tasks in my own workflow. Here are the notes with my action items: [PASTE NOTES OR ACTION ITEMS]. Help me convert my commitments into concrete tasks: break any big or vague action items into specific doable steps, suggest a sensible order and rough timing based on deadlines, and flag anything urgent. The goal is to move from notes to a clear plan of what I actually need to do and when. Give me my action items as scheduled, ready to do tasks.
description: Convert your meeting action items into concrete, scheduled, ready to do tasks.
useCase: Use this to bridge the gap between meeting notes and actually doing the work. Converting vague commitments into specific scheduled tasks means action items get done rather than sitting in notes, closing the common gap where a solo operator captures what they need to do but never translates it into their actual workflow.

title: Write an SOP from Scratch
promptText: Help me write a standard operating procedure for a process in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The process is [THE PROCESS], the goal it achieves is [THE GOAL OR OUTPUT], and who will follow it is [WHO WILL USE IT]. Write a clear SOP that includes the purpose, when to use it, anything needed before starting, the numbered steps in order written plainly enough for someone else to follow without asking me, and how to know it was done right. Flag any step where mistakes commonly happen. Keep it detailed but not bloated. Give me the finished SOP.
description: Write a clear standard operating procedure someone else can follow without asking you.
useCase: Use this to document a process so it runs without you. A clear SOP lets you delegate or automate work and ensures consistency, which is the foundation of scaling beyond doing everything yourself and the first step toward a business that does not depend on you for every task.

title: Turn a Brain Dump into an SOP
promptText: I am going to describe how I do a task in my own messy words, and I want you to turn it into a clean SOP. Here is how I do it: [BRAIN DUMP HOW YOU ACTUALLY DO THE TASK]. Organize my description into a proper standard operating procedure: a clear purpose, ordered numbered steps, any tools or access needed, and notes on how to do it right. Fill obvious gaps but flag anything unclear or missing rather than guessing. Preserve the specific details and quirks that matter. Give me the finished SOP based on how I actually work.
description: Turn a messy description of how you do a task into a clean, structured SOP.
useCase: Use this to document a process you do by habit without stopping to write formal steps. Brain dumping how you actually do it and having it structured into an SOP is far faster than writing one cold, capturing the real quirks and details that matter before they live only in your head.
variables: BRAIN DUMP HOW YOU ACTUALLY DO THE TASK

title: Build a Checklist from a Process
promptText: Turn this process into a simple checklist I can run through each time. The process is [THE PROCESS], and here are the steps or how it works: [THE STEPS OR DESCRIPTION]. Convert it into a clean checklist: each item a clear actionable check, in the right order, phrased so it is obvious whether it is done. Keep it concise and scannable for repeated use, not a long document. Flag any step that is easy to forget or skip. Give me the checklist.
description: Turn a process into a concise, repeatable checklist for consistent execution.
useCase: Use this for a recurring process where a full SOP is more than you need. A concise checklist ensures nothing gets skipped each time you run the process, catching the easy to forget steps that cause errors, and works better than a long document for tasks you repeat and just need to get right consistently.

title: Document a Process You Do by Habit
promptText: Help me document a process I do automatically but have never written down. The process is [THE PROCESS]. Ask me the right questions or walk me through capturing it: the trigger that starts it, each step including the small things I do without thinking, the decisions I make along the way and how I make them, the tools involved, and how I know it is finished and done well. Help me surface the tacit knowledge I would otherwise forget to mention. Then organize it all into clear documentation. Give me the documented process.
description: Capture and document a process you do on autopilot, including the tacit steps.
useCase: Use this to document work you do on autopilot, where the hardest part is noticing the steps you take without thinking. Surfacing that tacit knowledge produces documentation complete enough for someone else to follow, avoiding the gaps that make a rushed SOP fail when a habitual expert writes down only the obvious parts.

title: Build a Reusable SOP Template
promptText: Build me a reusable SOP template so all my procedures follow a consistent format. My [BUSINESS TYPE] has processes like [YOUR TYPES OF PROCESSES]. Create a clean SOP template with standard sections such as title, purpose, when to use it, prerequisites or tools needed, the step by step procedure, quality checks, and who owns it, with a short note on what goes in each section. Make it easy to fill in for any process. Consistency across SOPs makes them easier to write, follow, and maintain. Give me the template.
description: Build a consistent, reusable template for writing all your SOPs.
useCase: Use this once before writing multiple SOPs. A consistent template makes every procedure easier to write, follow, and maintain, and standardized documentation is what lets a solo operator build a real operations library rather than a scattered pile of differently formatted notes nobody can rely on.

title: Write an SOP for Delegating a Task
promptText: Help me write an SOP specifically so I can hand a task off to someone else. The task is [THE TASK], I want to delegate it to [WHO, E.G. A VA, CONTRACTOR, OR NEW HIRE], and their skill level with this is [THEIR EXPERIENCE LEVEL]. Write an SOP detailed enough that they can do it correctly without constantly asking me: clear steps, the context and why behind key decisions, examples of right and wrong, common mistakes to avoid, and when to check with me versus proceed. Pitch the detail to their experience level. The goal is confident handoff. Give me the delegation ready SOP.
description: Write an SOP detailed enough to confidently hand a task off to someone else.
useCase: Use this when delegating a task for the first time. An SOP written for handoff, with context and examples pitched to the person's level, lets them do it right without constant questions, which is what makes delegation actually save you time rather than creating a stream of interruptions that costs more than doing it yourself.
variables: WHO, E.G. A VA, CONTRACTOR, OR NEW HIRE

title: Check an SOP for Gaps
promptText: Review my SOP for gaps that would trip up someone following it. Here is the SOP: [PASTE YOUR SOP]. The person following it will be [WHO WILL USE IT AND THEIR LEVEL]. Check it critically: where a step assumes knowledge they may not have, where instructions are vague or could be misread, missing steps or prerequisites, decision points that are not explained, and anything I take for granted that they would not know. Point out exactly where someone would get stuck or make a mistake. Give me the gaps and how to fix each.
description: Review an SOP for the gaps and assumptions that would trip up a new user.
useCase: Use this before handing off an SOP to check it actually works for someone else. Finding where it assumes knowledge or leaves steps vague catches the gaps that cause errors and questions, since the expert who wrote it cannot easily see what a first time user would not know, which is exactly where handoffs fail.
variables: WHO WILL USE IT AND THEIR LEVEL

title: Simplify an Overcomplicated SOP
promptText: My SOP is too long, complicated, or confusing and people do not follow it well. Here it is: [PASTE YOUR SOP]. Simplify it: cut unnecessary words and steps, clarify anything convoluted, tighten the structure so the actual actions are easy to find and follow, and make it genuinely usable in practice rather than exhaustive on paper. Keep every step that matters but remove the bloat that makes people ignore it. Flag anything that could be a quick checklist instead. Give me the simplified SOP.
description: Simplify a bloated or confusing SOP into one people will actually follow.
useCase: Use this when an SOP is technically complete but too dense for anyone to follow. Cutting the bloat and clarifying the structure makes it genuinely usable, since an overcomplicated SOP gets ignored and defeats its purpose, and a simple procedure people actually follow beats an exhaustive one that sits unread.

title: Update an Outdated SOP
promptText: Help me update an SOP that is now out of date. Here is the current SOP: [PASTE YOUR SOP], and what has changed since it was written is [WHAT CHANGED, E.G. NEW TOOLS, STEPS, OR A DIFFERENT WAY OF DOING IT]. Update it to reflect how the process actually works now: revise the affected steps, remove anything obsolete, add new steps or tools, and make sure the whole thing is consistent and current. Flag anything where I need to confirm the new way. An outdated SOP that misleads is worse than none. Give me the updated SOP.
description: Update an outdated SOP to reflect how the process actually works now.
useCase: Use this when a process has changed but its SOP has not. Updating it to match reality keeps the documentation trustworthy, since an outdated SOP that teaches the wrong way is worse than none, actively causing errors, and keeping SOPs current is what makes an operations library reliable rather than a liability.
variables: WHAT CHANGED, E.G. NEW TOOLS, STEPS, OR A DIFFERENT WAY OF DOING IT

title: Plan Which SOPs to Create First
promptText: Help me figure out which SOPs to create first for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The recurring processes and tasks in my business are [LIST YOUR PROCESSES AND TASKS]. My goal is [YOUR GOAL, E.G. DELEGATE MORE, WORK LESS IN THE BUSINESS, OR ENSURE CONSISTENCY]. Help me prioritize: which processes would deliver the most benefit from being documented first, which are most error prone or dependent on me, and which to leave for later. Do not overwhelm me by suggesting I document everything at once. Give me a prioritized list of the SOPs to build first and why.
description: Prioritize which SOPs to document first for the biggest benefit.
useCase: Use this when you know you should document your processes but do not know where to start. Prioritizing the SOPs that would deliver the most benefit, like the error prone or you dependent tasks, focuses your effort where it counts, avoiding the paralysis of trying to document everything at once and never starting.
variables: YOUR GOAL, E.G. DELEGATE MORE, WORK LESS IN THE BUSINESS, OR ENSURE CONSISTENCY

title: Turn a Screen Recording Transcript into an SOP
promptText: I recorded myself doing a task and have the narration transcript. Turn it into a written SOP. Here is the transcript of me talking through the process: [PASTE TRANSCRIPT]. Convert my spoken walkthrough into a clean written procedure: clear numbered steps, the tools and clicks involved, decisions I mentioned, and how to know each step is done. Clean up the spoken filler and tangents while preserving every actual step and detail. Flag anything I did but did not explain. Give me the written SOP from my walkthrough.
description: Convert a spoken walkthrough transcript into a clean written SOP.
useCase: Use this when it is faster to talk through a task than write it. Recording yourself doing the process and converting the transcript into an SOP captures every real step naturally, often producing more complete documentation than writing from memory, since narrating as you work surfaces details you would otherwise skip.

title: Create a Template for a Recurring Process
promptText: Help me create a reusable template for a recurring process so I do not start from scratch each time. The recurring process is [THE PROCESS, E.G. CLIENT ONBOARDING, MONTHLY REPORTING, CONTENT PRODUCTION], and what it involves each time is [WHAT IT INVOLVES]. Build a template that captures the repeatable structure with fill in the blank spots for what changes each time, so running it becomes fast and consistent. Include the steps, any standard content, and the variable inputs. The goal is to turn a repeated process into a quick fill in template. Give me the reusable template.
description: Create a fill in the blank template that makes a recurring process fast and consistent.
useCase: Use this for a process you repeat with small variations each time. A reusable template captures the repeatable structure so you only fill in what changes, making the process faster and more consistent, which saves a solo operator meaningful time on frequent work like onboarding or monthly reporting.
variables: THE PROCESS, E.G. CLIENT ONBOARDING, MONTHLY REPORTING, CONTENT PRODUCTION

title: Write a Client Onboarding SOP
promptText: Help me write an SOP for onboarding a new client in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [YOUR SERVICE], and the steps I currently take to onboard a client are [YOUR CURRENT ONBOARDING STEPS, OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Build a clear onboarding SOP covering everything from the moment they say yes: welcome, collecting what I need from them, setup, kickoff, setting expectations, and getting the work started smoothly. Include the tasks, the order, and what to send at each step. A consistent onboarding makes me look professional and prevents dropped steps. Give me the client onboarding SOP.
description: Write a repeatable SOP for onboarding new clients smoothly and consistently.
useCase: Use this to standardize how you onboard clients. A consistent onboarding SOP makes you look professional, prevents dropped steps, and starts every engagement smoothly, turning what could be a scramble each time into a reliable process that sets the tone for the whole client relationship.
variables: YOUR CURRENT ONBOARDING STEPS, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Add Quality Control to an SOP
promptText: Help me add quality control checks to my SOP so the output is consistently good. Here is the SOP: [PASTE YOUR SOP], and what a good final result looks like is [YOUR QUALITY STANDARD]. Add quality checkpoints: what to verify at key steps, a final check before the task is considered done, common quality problems to watch for, and clear standards so anyone following it produces work at my standard. The goal is consistent quality even when someone else does the task. Give me the SOP with quality control built in.
description: Add quality control checkpoints to an SOP for consistent output.
useCase: Use this when you need consistent quality from a process, especially if delegated. Building in checkpoints and clear standards ensures the output meets your bar even when someone else does it, closing the gap where a followed SOP still produces mediocre results because it defined the steps but not what good looks like.

title: Add a Troubleshooting Section to an SOP
promptText: Help me add a troubleshooting section to my SOP so people can handle problems without coming to me. Here is the SOP: [PASTE YOUR SOP], and the common things that go wrong or questions that come up are [COMMON PROBLEMS OR QUESTIONS]. Create a troubleshooting section covering: the common issues, what causes each, how to resolve them, and when to escalate to me versus handle it themselves. The goal is that someone hits a snag and finds the answer in the SOP instead of interrupting me. Give me the troubleshooting section to add.
description: Add a troubleshooting section so people solve problems without interrupting you.
useCase: Use this to make an SOP handle the problems that would otherwise come back to you. A troubleshooting section covering common issues and when to escalate lets whoever follows the SOP solve snags themselves, which is what makes delegation truly free your time rather than just shifting the interruptions to a different set of questions.

title: Get a Quick SOP Draft Fast
promptText: I need a quick SOP draft for this task: [THE TASK]. Here is roughly how it is done: [BRIEF DESCRIPTION OF THE STEPS]. Give me a clean, straightforward SOP with numbered steps, anything needed to start, and a quick note on doing it right. Keep it practical and ready to use with minor edits, no lengthy formatting. Just give me a solid SOP draft I can refine.
description: Get a quick, practical SOP draft you can refine, with minimal effort.
useCase: Use this when you need to document a task quickly without a full writing process. A fast SOP draft gives you a solid starting point to refine, lowering the barrier to documenting processes, so you actually capture them instead of leaving the knowledge in your head because writing it up felt like too much work.

title: Map a Process as a Flow
promptText: Help me map my process as a clear flow so I can see the whole thing and spot issues. The process is [THE PROCESS], and the steps as I understand them are [THE STEPS]. Lay it out as a logical flow showing the sequence, any decision points where the path branches, and what happens in each branch. Describe the flow clearly enough that I could sketch it as a flowchart. Point out any loops, bottlenecks, or unclear branches. The goal is to see the process visually and find where it breaks down. Give me the process flow.
description: Map a process as a clear flow to see the whole thing and spot bottlenecks.
useCase: Use this when a process has branches or decision points that a linear SOP handles poorly. Mapping it as a flow reveals the whole structure and exposes bottlenecks, loops, and unclear branches, helping you understand and improve a complex process that is hard to grasp when written only as a list of steps.

title: Find Ways to Improve a Process
promptText: Before I document this process, help me improve it so I do not just enshrine an inefficient way of doing things. The process is [THE PROCESS], here is how I currently do it: [YOUR CURRENT STEPS], and what frustrates me or slows it down is [PAIN POINTS]. Analyze it for improvements: unnecessary steps to cut, parts to simplify or reorder, anything to automate, and where time or effort is wasted. Suggest a better version of the process, then note what to document. The goal is to optimize before I standardize. Give me the improved process and the reasoning.
description: Improve and streamline a process before you document it as an SOP.
useCase: Use this before writing an SOP for a process that feels inefficient. Improving it first avoids enshrining a wasteful way of working, since documenting a broken process just makes everyone follow it faithfully, and optimizing before you standardize means the SOP captures the best version rather than locking in old friction.

title: Adjust an SOP for Its Audience
promptText: Help me adjust my SOP so it fits the person who will actually use it. Here is the SOP: [PASTE YOUR SOP], and the person following it is [WHO THEY ARE, THEIR EXPERIENCE, AND CONTEXT]. Rewrite it to match their level: add explanation where they lack background, cut over explanation where they are already skilled, adjust the terminology to what they will understand, and make the detail appropriate so it is neither confusing nor patronizing. The right level of detail is what makes an SOP actually usable. Give me the adjusted SOP.
description: Adjust an SOP's detail and language to fit the person who will use it.
useCase: Use this when an SOP is written at the wrong level for its user. Matching the detail and language to their experience makes it usable, since an SOP too advanced confuses a beginner and one too basic annoys an expert, and getting the level right is often what separates an SOP people follow from one they abandon.
variables: WHO THEY ARE, THEIR EXPERIENCE, AND CONTEXT

title: Organize Your SOPs into a System
promptText: Help me organize my growing collection of SOPs into a system I can navigate. My SOPs cover [LIST YOUR SOPS OR PROCESS AREAS]. Help me structure them: how to group and categorize them logically, a naming convention so they are easy to find, an index or table of contents structure, and how to keep the whole system maintainable as it grows. The goal is that I or anyone else can quickly find the right SOP when needed. Give me an organization system and how to set it up.
description: Organize a growing collection of SOPs into a navigable, maintainable system.
useCase: Use this when you have enough SOPs that finding the right one gets hard. A logical organization system with clear naming and an index makes your procedures usable, since documentation nobody can find quickly goes unused, and a maintainable structure is what turns a pile of SOPs into an operations manual you actually rely on.
variables: LIST YOUR SOPS OR PROCESS AREAS

title: Write an Emergency or Exception Procedure
promptText: Help me write a procedure for handling an emergency or exception situation in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [THE EMERGENCY OR EXCEPTION, E.G. A SITE OUTAGE, A MAJOR CLIENT ISSUE, A MISSED DEADLINE], and the goal is to respond quickly and correctly under pressure. Write a clear procedure covering: how to recognize the situation, the immediate steps to take in order, who to notify, how to contain and resolve it, and what to do afterward. Make it calm and clear enough to follow under stress. Give me the emergency procedure.
description: Write a clear procedure for handling an emergency or exception under pressure.
useCase: Use this to prepare for situations where you cannot afford to improvise. A clear procedure for an outage, crisis, or major exception lets you respond quickly and correctly under stress, since the moment something goes wrong is the worst time to figure out the steps, and having them ready prevents costly panic driven mistakes.
variables: THE EMERGENCY OR EXCEPTION, E.G. A SITE OUTAGE, A MAJOR CLIENT ISSUE, A MISSED DEADLINE

title: Audit Your Existing SOPs
promptText: Help me audit my existing SOPs to find which need attention. Here are my SOPs or a description of them: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE YOUR SOPS], and how they are actually used is [ARE THEY FOLLOWED, OUTDATED, IGNORED]. Assess them: which are outdated, which are unclear or unfollowed and why, which processes still lack an SOP, and which are worth the effort to fix versus retire. Prioritize what to improve first. The goal is a lean set of SOPs that are actually current and used, not a graveyard of documents. Give me the audit and priorities.
description: Audit your existing SOPs to find what to update, fix, retire, or create.
useCase: Use this periodically to keep your SOPs healthy. An audit that flags the outdated, unclear, and unused ones prevents your documentation from becoming a graveyard nobody trusts, focusing your effort on a lean current set that people actually follow rather than an accumulating pile of stale procedures.

title: Add Examples to an SOP
promptText: Help me strengthen my SOP by adding examples that show what good looks like. Here is the SOP: [PASTE YOUR SOP], and an example of the task done well is [DESCRIBE OR PASTE A GOOD EXAMPLE]. Add helpful examples throughout: what a correct output looks like, examples of right versus wrong for tricky steps, and samples that make abstract instructions concrete. Examples often teach better than instructions alone. Show me where examples would help most and what each should demonstrate. Give me the SOP enhanced with examples.
description: Add concrete examples to an SOP to show what good execution looks like.
useCase: Use this to make an SOP clearer by showing rather than only telling. Adding examples of correct output and right versus wrong for tricky steps teaches better than instructions alone, closing the gap where someone follows the written steps but still produces the wrong result because they never saw what good actually looks like.
variables: DESCRIBE OR PASTE A GOOD EXAMPLE

title: Write a Job Post
promptText: Write a job post for a role I need to fill in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE TITLE], the work involves [WHAT THEY WILL DO], and it is [FULL TIME, PART TIME, CONTRACT, OR FREELANCE]. What matters most in a candidate is [KEY QUALITIES OR SKILLS]. Write a job post that clearly describes the role and who I am looking for, conveys what it is like to work with me, is honest about the reality of the job, and attracts the right person while filtering out poor fits. Keep it engaging, not a dry corporate listing. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the job post.
description: Write an engaging, honest job post that attracts the right candidate and filters poor fits.
useCase: Use this when you need to hire and want the right applicants, not a flood of wrong ones. A clear honest job post that conveys the real role and who fits attracts strong candidates while filtering poor ones, saving a solo operator hours of screening people who were never a match.
variables: FULL TIME, PART TIME, CONTRACT, OR FREELANCE

title: Define the Role You Actually Need
promptText: Help me figure out what role I actually need to hire for. I am overwhelmed in my [BUSINESS TYPE] and the tasks eating my time are [WHAT IS TAKING YOUR TIME]. The work I most want off my plate is [WHAT YOU WANT TO OFFLOAD], and my budget is [YOUR BUDGET OR WRITE LIMITED]. Help me define the right role: what tasks to bundle into it, whether I need a specialist or a generalist, whether it should be a contractor, part timer, or VA, and what to prioritize hiring for first. Do not assume I need a full time employee. Give me a clear definition of the role to hire for and why.
description: Define the right role to hire for based on what is actually eating your time.
useCase: Use this before hiring, when you know you need help but not exactly what kind. Defining the right role from the work you want off your plate prevents the costly mistake of hiring the wrong type of help, ensuring your first hire actually relieves the bottleneck rather than adding management overhead.

title: Define Your Ideal Candidate
promptText: Help me define my ideal candidate for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and the work involves [WHAT THEY WILL DO]. Help me get clear on: the skills and experience that genuinely matter versus nice to haves, the personality and work style that would fit how I work, the red flags to avoid, and what kind of person thrives in a small business versus needs more structure. Distinguish what I actually need from what sounds good on paper. This clarity will guide my job post and screening. Give me a clear ideal candidate profile.
description: Define the skills, work style, and fit that genuinely matter in a candidate.
useCase: Use this before writing a job post or screening candidates. A clear profile of what genuinely matters, versus what just sounds good, guides your whole hiring process and prevents being dazzled by impressive resumes that do not fit how a small business actually works, where fit and reliability often matter more than credentials.

title: Tailor a Job Post to a Platform
promptText: Help me tailor my job post for the platform I am posting on. The platform is [PLATFORM, E.G. UPWORK, LINKEDIN, A FREELANCE MARKETPLACE, A JOB BOARD, OR A FACEBOOK GROUP], and here is my role and job details: [ROLE AND DETAILS]. Adapt the post to fit that platform's norms, format, and the kind of candidates there: the right length and tone, what to emphasize for that audience, and how to stand out among other listings. Note anything specific that platform's applicants respond to. Give me the platform tailored job post.
description: Tailor a job post to the norms and audience of a specific hiring platform.
useCase: Use this because a post that works on LinkedIn may flop on Upwork or a Facebook group. Tailoring format, tone, and emphasis to each platform's norms and candidate pool helps your listing stand out and attract the right people, since the same generic post rarely performs well across different hiring channels.
variables: PLATFORM, E.G. UPWORK, LINKEDIN, A FREELANCE MARKETPLACE, A JOB BOARD, OR A FACEBOOK GROUP

title: Write Candidate Screening Questions
promptText: Write screening questions to filter applicants for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and what matters most is [KEY REQUIREMENTS AND QUALITIES]. Give me a set of questions to include in the application or a first screen that quickly reveal whether someone is a genuine fit: questions that surface real experience versus buzzwords, that test the key skills, that reveal work style and attitude, and that filter out low effort or mismatched applicants. Include at least one question whose answer quickly separates serious candidates from the rest. Give me the screening questions.
description: Write screening questions that quickly filter genuine fits from mismatched applicants.
useCase: Use this to filter applicants efficiently before investing time in interviews. Well designed screening questions surface real fit and effort, quickly separating serious candidates from the rest, which saves a solo operator from the time drain of interviewing people a good question would have screened out upfront.

title: Prepare Interview Questions
promptText: Help me prepare interview questions for a candidate for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and what I most need to assess is [WHAT YOU NEED TO EVALUATE, E.G. SKILLS, RELIABILITY, FIT, PROBLEM SOLVING]. Give me interview questions that go beyond generic ones: questions that reveal how they actually work and think, that test real competence rather than rehearsed answers, that assess fit with a small business, and that surface potential problems. Note what a good versus concerning answer looks like for the key ones. Give me an interview question set with guidance on reading the answers.
description: Prepare revealing interview questions with guidance on reading the answers.
useCase: Use this before interviewing candidates. Questions that reveal how someone actually works and think, rather than eliciting rehearsed answers, help you assess real competence and fit, and knowing what a good versus concerning answer looks like makes the interview a genuine evaluation rather than a pleasant chat that tells you little.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED TO EVALUATE, E.G. SKILLS, RELIABILITY, FIT, PROBLEM SOLVING

title: Review Applications Efficiently
promptText: Help me review job applications efficiently for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and my must haves are [YOUR NON NEGOTIABLES] while my nice to haves are [NICE TO HAVES]. Help me create a simple way to evaluate applicants consistently: the criteria to score them on, quick signals of a strong versus weak applicant, red flags to watch for, and how to shortlist fairly without spending forever on each one. The goal is to sort many applications down to a strong shortlist efficiently. Give me a review framework I can apply to each application.
description: Create a framework to review applications consistently and shortlist efficiently.
useCase: Use this when facing a stack of applications to sort through. A consistent evaluation framework with clear criteria and signals lets you shortlist efficiently and fairly, avoiding both the time sink of overanalyzing each one and the bias of inconsistent gut judgments when a solo operator reviews applicants without a system.

title: Evaluate a Candidate After an Interview
promptText: Help me evaluate a candidate objectively after interviewing them for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and here are my notes and impressions from the interview: [YOUR NOTES AND IMPRESSIONS]. My must haves are [YOUR REQUIREMENTS]. Help me assess them fairly: how well they meet the real requirements, the strengths and concerns, whether my gut reaction is backed by evidence or bias, and what to probe further if needed. Help me separate genuine fit from surface impression like charisma. Give me an objective evaluation and a clear read on whether to advance them.
description: Objectively evaluate a candidate after an interview, separating fit from surface charm.
