useCase: Use this after an interview to decide clearly. An objective evaluation against your real requirements separates genuine fit from surface impressions like charisma or nerves, protecting against the common hiring mistake of choosing the most likable candidate over the one who would actually do the job well.

title: Write a Brief for a Freelancer
promptText: Help me write a clear brief for a freelancer or contractor I am hiring for a specific project. The project is [THE PROJECT], the outcome I need is [DESIRED OUTCOME], my budget and timeline are [BUDGET AND TIMELINE], and any constraints or preferences are [CONSTRAINTS OR PREFERENCES]. Write a brief that clearly explains what I need, the scope and deliverables, the standards and expectations, what I will provide, and how success is defined. A clear brief prevents misunderstandings and gets better results. Keep it thorough but not overwhelming. Give me the freelancer brief.
description: Write a clear project brief that gets freelancers to deliver what you actually need.
useCase: Use this when hiring a freelancer for a project. A clear brief defining the scope, deliverables, and standards prevents the misunderstandings and disappointing results that come from vague instructions, since freelancers deliver against what you specify, and a strong brief is the single biggest factor in getting work you are happy with.

title: Decide if You Are Ready to Hire
promptText: Help me decide whether I am actually ready to make my first hire in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My situation is [YOUR SITUATION, E.G. REVENUE, WORKLOAD, HOW OVERWHELMED YOU ARE], and what is holding me back is [YOUR HESITATION]. Help me think it through honestly: whether the numbers support a hire, whether hiring would free me for higher value work or just add management burden, whether a contractor or part timer is a safer first step, and the risks either way. Be realistic about both the cost of hiring and the cost of staying stuck doing everything. Give me an honest assessment and recommendation.
description: Decide honestly whether you are ready to make your first hire.
useCase: Use this at the daunting decision of whether to hire for the first time. An honest assessment of whether the numbers and workload support it, and whether a hire would free you or just add burden, helps you make this high stakes call clearly rather than either hiring too soon out of overwhelm or staying stuck too long out of fear.
variables: YOUR SITUATION, E.G. REVENUE, WORKLOAD, HOW OVERWHELMED YOU ARE

title: Decide Between a Contractor and Employee
promptText: Help me decide whether to hire a contractor, freelancer, or employee for the help I need in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The work is [THE WORK], how much of it there is is [ONGOING OR PROJECT BASED, AND HOW MANY HOURS], and my situation is [YOUR SITUATION AND BUDGET]. Help me weigh the options: which fits the type and volume of work, the flexibility and cost tradeoffs, the commitment and management differences, and which is the smarter first step for me. Note this is general business guidance, not legal or tax advice, and classification rules matter. Give me a recommendation with the reasoning.
description: Decide whether a contractor, freelancer, or employee fits your needs best.
useCase: Use this when deciding how to structure your help. Weighing which arrangement fits the work volume, cost, and commitment you need helps you choose the smartest option, since starting with a contractor is often lower risk than an employee for a first hire, though the right answer depends on the work and your situation.
variables: ONGOING OR PROJECT BASED, AND HOW MANY HOURS

title: Plan Your Hiring Budget
promptText: Help me think through the real cost of hiring for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and the pay range I am considering is [PAY RANGE OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Help me understand the full cost beyond just their pay: the additional costs to factor in, what the total realistically comes to, how much revenue or freed up time the hire needs to generate to be worth it, and whether my business can genuinely support it. Note this is general guidance, not tax or accounting advice. Help me plan a hire I can actually afford. Give me the budget breakdown and what to consider.
description: Plan the real, full cost of a hire and what it needs to return to be worth it.
useCase: Use this before committing to a hire to understand the true cost. Factoring in the full expense beyond base pay, and what the hire must return to justify itself, prevents the cash flow strain of a hire you could not really afford, a serious risk for a solo business where one bad hiring decision can be financially destabilizing.
variables: PAY RANGE OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Write a Job Offer Message
promptText: Write a message offering the role to the candidate I have chosen for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], the terms are [PAY, HOURS, START DATE, AND KEY TERMS], and what I liked about them is [WHY YOU CHOSE THEM]. Write a warm professional offer that expresses genuine enthusiasm about them joining, clearly states the key terms, conveys what it will be like to work together, and makes them excited to say yes. Keep it human and personal, not a cold formal letter. Note that formal terms should be confirmed in writing appropriately. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the offer message.
description: Write a warm, clear job offer that makes your chosen candidate excited to say yes.
useCase: Use this to extend an offer to your chosen candidate. A warm offer that conveys genuine enthusiasm and clear terms makes a strong candidate excited to accept, which matters because the best candidates often have other options, and how you make the offer influences whether they choose you over an alternative.
variables: PAY, HOURS, START DATE, AND KEY TERMS

title: Write a Candidate Rejection Message
promptText: Write a respectful rejection message for candidates I am not moving forward with for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and the stage they reached is [DID THEY APPLY, GET SCREENED, OR INTERVIEW]. Write a kind, professional message that thanks them for their time and interest, declines clearly without false hope, and leaves them with a positive impression of me and my business. Keep it warm but concise, and avoid generic coldness. If they were a strong runner up worth keeping in mind, note how to say so genuinely. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the rejection message, adaptable for different stages.
description: Write a respectful candidate rejection that leaves a positive impression.
useCase: Use this to decline candidates gracefully. A kind professional rejection leaves people with a good impression of your business, which matters for your reputation since rejected candidates talk and may be customers or future applicants, and treating them well reflects the character of a business worth working with or buying from.
variables: DID THEY APPLY, GET SCREENED, OR INTERVIEW

title: Make the Final Hiring Decision
promptText: Help me make a final hiring decision between my top candidates for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and here are my candidates with their strengths and concerns: [CANDIDATES AND YOUR NOTES]. My most important criteria are [WHAT MATTERS MOST]. Help me decide: how each stacks up on what truly matters, the risks and upside of each, whether I am weighting the right things, and which is the strongest choice. Warn me if I am leaning toward someone for the wrong reasons. Help me choose confidently. Give me an analysis and a recommendation with the reasoning.
description: Make a confident final hiring decision between your top candidates.
useCase: Use this at the final decision between strong candidates. A structured comparison against what truly matters helps you choose confidently and catches when you are leaning toward someone for the wrong reasons, reducing the risk of a bad hire, which is one of the most costly and disruptive mistakes a small business can make.

title: Hire a Virtual Assistant
promptText: Help me hire a virtual assistant for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The tasks I want to offload are [TASKS FOR THE VA], my budget is [YOUR BUDGET], and I need someone who [KEY REQUIREMENTS, E.G. TIMEZONE, SKILLS, AVAILABILITY]. Help me: define the VA role clearly, write a job post to attract good candidates, decide what to look for and how to screen, and plan how to hand off tasks so it actually saves me time. Include the common mistakes people make hiring a first VA and how to avoid them. Give me a plan and the job post to hire a VA.
description: Plan and post to hire a virtual assistant that actually saves you time.
useCase: Use this to hire a VA, often a solo operator's first and highest leverage hire. A clear plan for defining the role, screening, and handing off tasks ensures a VA actually frees your time, avoiding the common trap where a poorly scoped or poorly managed VA creates more work than they remove.
variables: KEY REQUIREMENTS, E.G. TIMEZONE, SKILLS, AVAILABILITY

title: Plan a New Hire's First Days
promptText: Help me plan the first days and onboarding for my new hire in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and what they will be doing is [THEIR WORK]. Help me plan a smooth start: what to set up and share before they begin, how to welcome and orient them, what to have them do first to build early confidence and wins, what to communicate about how I work and expectations, and how to set them up to succeed rather than sink. A strong start makes a hire productive faster. Give me an onboarding plan for their first days and weeks.
description: Plan a new hire's onboarding so they start strong and become productive fast.
useCase: Use this right after making a hire. A structured onboarding plan gets a new hire productive faster and reduces early turnover, since a chaotic start where someone is left to figure things out alone is a common reason good hires struggle or leave, wasting the effort and cost of hiring them.

title: Plan How to Hand Off Work
promptText: Help me plan how to hand off work to my new hire or contractor so delegation actually works. The tasks I want to hand off are [TASKS TO DELEGATE], and the person is [WHO AND THEIR EXPERIENCE]. Help me plan the handoff: what to document or explain, how to transfer the work gradually versus all at once, how much to check and how to give feedback early, how to let go without micromanaging, and how to build up to trusting them with more. My struggle with delegating is [YOUR DELEGATION STRUGGLE, OR WRITE NONE]. Give me a handoff plan that sets up successful delegation.
description: Plan a work handoff that makes delegation succeed without micromanaging.
useCase: Use this when handing work to a new hire or contractor. A thoughtful handoff plan with gradual transfer and early feedback makes delegation succeed, addressing the real challenge for many solo operators, which is not finding help but letting go effectively so delegation frees their time rather than becoming a source of frustration and rework.
variables: YOUR DELEGATION STRUGGLE, OR WRITE NONE

title: Design a Paid Test Project
promptText: Help me design a small paid test project to evaluate a candidate before committing in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and the real work they would do is [THE WORK]. Design a fair test project that reflects the actual work, is small enough to be reasonable and worth paying for, and reveals their real skill, reliability, and how they communicate and take direction. Explain what to look for in how they handle it. Keep it respectful of their time and fairly compensated. Give me the test project design and what it will reveal about the candidate.
description: Design a fair paid test project that reveals a candidate's real ability before hiring.
useCase: Use this to evaluate a candidate through real work before a full commitment. A small paid test project reveals actual skill, reliability, and communication far better than interviews alone, reducing hiring risk by showing how someone truly performs, while paying for it keeps the process fair and respectful of their time.

title: Add Filters to Screen Out Poor Fits
promptText: Help me add elements to my hiring process that filter out poor fit or low effort applicants for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and the wrong fits I keep attracting or want to avoid are [WHO YOU WANT TO FILTER OUT]. Suggest practical filters: a specific instruction in the job post that low effort applicants will ignore, screening questions that self select the right people, and signals in applications that reliably indicate a poor fit. The goal is fewer wrong applicants so I spend time only on genuine candidates. Give me the filtering tactics to build into my process.
description: Add filters to your hiring process that screen out poor fit applicants early.
useCase: Use this when you keep attracting the wrong applicants. Building in filters like a hidden instruction low effort applicants ignore, and self selecting screening questions, cuts down the wrong applicants before they reach you, saving a solo operator from the significant time drain of reviewing and declining people who were never a fit.
variables: WHO YOU WANT TO FILTER OUT

title: Get a Quick Job Post Fast
promptText: I need a job post quickly for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], the work is [WHAT THEY WILL DO], it is [FULL TIME, PART TIME, OR CONTRACT], and the key requirements are [KEY REQUIREMENTS]. Give me a clear, ready to post job listing that describes the role and who I want, in a [TONE] tone. Keep it concise and ready to publish with minor edits. Just give me a solid job post I can use now.
description: Get a clear, ready to publish job post quickly with minimal effort.
useCase: Use this when you need to post a role fast without a full process. A quick ready to publish job post lets you get a listing up promptly when you need help urgently, capturing candidates sooner rather than delaying the hire because writing the post felt like one more task you did not have time for.
variables: FULL TIME, PART TIME, OR CONTRACT

title: Prepare Reference Check Questions
promptText: Help me prepare questions to ask when checking a candidate's references for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The role is [ROLE], and what I most want to verify or learn is [YOUR CONCERNS OR WHAT TO CONFIRM]. Give me reference check questions that get honest useful information rather than generic praise: questions that reveal how the person actually performed, their reliability and work style, any concerns, and whether the reference would work with them again. Note how to read what references say and do not say. Give me the reference check questions and what to listen for.
description: Prepare reference check questions that get honest, useful information.
useCase: Use this before checking a candidate's references. Questions designed to get honest information rather than generic praise, and knowing how to read what a reference does and does not say, help you catch concerns before hiring, adding a valuable layer of verification that reduces the risk of a costly bad hire.
variables: YOUR CONCERNS OR WHAT TO CONFIRM

title: Plan Your Whole Hiring Process
promptText: Help me plan an efficient hiring process for a role in my [BUSINESS TYPE] so I do not waste time or make a rushed decision. The role is [ROLE], my timeline is [YOUR TIMELINE], and my capacity to spend on hiring is [YOUR AVAILABLE TIME]. Lay out a simple step by step hiring process: from writing the post through screening, interviewing, evaluating, and deciding, with what to do at each stage and how to keep it moving without dragging on or cutting corners. Keep it realistic for a solo operator doing this alone. Give me the hiring process plan.
description: Plan an efficient end to end hiring process realistic for a solo operator.
useCase: Use this before starting to hire, to run a process rather than wing it. A clear step by step plan keeps hiring moving efficiently without either dragging on or rushing a bad decision, which matters for a solo operator hiring alone with no HR support, where a disorganized process wastes time and risks a poor choice.

title: Find What to Automate in Your Business
promptText: Help me find the best automation opportunities in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here are the repetitive tasks and processes I do regularly: [LIST YOUR RECURRING TASKS AND PROCESSES]. My tools are [YOUR TOOLS OR WRITE BASIC]. Analyze this and identify: which tasks are strong candidates for automation, which would save the most time or reduce the most errors, which are realistic to automate with simple tools versus need more setup, and which are not worth automating. Prioritize by impact versus effort. Do not suggest automating things that are better left manual. Give me a ranked list of automation opportunities and why each matters.
description: Identify and rank the highest impact automation opportunities in your business.
useCase: Use this when you sense you are wasting time on repetitive work but do not know where to start automating. A ranked list by impact versus effort focuses you on the automations that actually save meaningful time, avoiding the trap of automating trivial things while the real time drains continue unaddressed.
variables: LIST YOUR RECURRING TASKS AND PROCESSES

title: Assess if a Task Is Worth Automating
promptText: Help me assess whether a specific task is worth automating. The task is [THE TASK], I do it [HOW OFTEN], it takes about [TIME PER INSTANCE], and how I do it now is [HOW YOU DO IT]. Help me evaluate: how much time automating it would actually save over time, the effort and cost to set up the automation, whether the task is consistent enough to automate reliably, the risk if the automation fails or gets it wrong, and whether it is genuinely worth doing versus leaving manual. Give me an honest verdict on whether to automate this task and, if so, roughly how.
description: Get an honest verdict on whether a specific task is worth automating.
useCase: Use this before investing time in automating a task. An honest assessment of the time saved versus setup effort and failure risk prevents the common mistake of spending hours automating something that barely saved time, ensuring your automation effort goes only where the payoff genuinely justifies it.

title: Audit Your Time to Find Automation Wins
promptText: Help me audit where my time goes to find automation opportunities I am not seeing. Here is how I spend my working time and the tasks that fill my days in my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [DESCRIBE HOW YOU SPEND YOUR TIME]. Help me identify: the repetitive low value tasks quietly eating my time, the ones I do so habitually I do not notice their cost, which are automatable, and where the biggest time recovery would come from. Surface the hidden time drains I have normalized. Give me the tasks worth automating ranked by how much time they would free up.
description: Audit how you spend your time to surface hidden, automatable time drains.
useCase: Use this to find the automatable time drains you have stopped noticing. Auditing where your time actually goes surfaces the repetitive low value work you do on autopilot, which is often where the biggest automation wins hide, since the tasks you have normalized are exactly the ones quietly consuming hours you could reclaim.
variables: DESCRIBE HOW YOU SPEND YOUR TIME

title: Prioritize Your Automation Projects
promptText: Help me prioritize which automations to set up first. Here are the automation ideas I am considering for my [BUSINESS TYPE]: [LIST YOUR AUTOMATION IDEAS]. My technical comfort level is [YOUR COMFORT WITH TECH], and my time to set them up is [YOUR AVAILABLE TIME]. Help me rank them by impact versus effort: which deliver the most benefit for the least setup, which are quick wins to build momentum, which are bigger projects to tackle later, and which to skip. Give me a prioritized order to implement them so I get results fast without getting overwhelmed. Give me the ranked plan.
description: Rank your automation ideas by impact versus effort into an implementation order.
useCase: Use this when you have several automation ideas and limited time. Ranking them by impact versus effort, with quick wins first, gets you results fast and builds momentum, preventing the overwhelm of tackling a big complex automation first and stalling before seeing any benefit from the simpler high value ones.

title: Find the Right Tool to Automate a Task
promptText: Help me figure out how to automate a specific task and what kind of tool could do it. The task is [THE TASK], here is how it currently works: [HOW IT WORKS NOW], and the tools I already use are [YOUR CURRENT TOOLS]. Suggest approaches: whether a feature in a tool I already have could do it, whether a connector type tool could link my existing tools, or whether a dedicated tool is needed. Describe the type of solution and what to look for rather than assuming specific current products, since tools change. Note the rough setup effort. Give me practical options to automate this task.
description: Find the right type of tool or approach to automate a specific task.
useCase: Use this when you know what you want to automate but not how. Understanding the type of solution that fits, whether an existing tool's feature, a connector, or a dedicated app, points you in the right direction, and since specific tools change constantly, knowing what to look for matters more than a fixed product recommendation.

title: Find Email and Communication to Automate
promptText: Help me find email and communication tasks to automate in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The repetitive messages and email tasks I handle are [YOUR EMAIL AND MESSAGE TASKS, E.G. INQUIRIES, FOLLOW UPS, CONFIRMATIONS, ONBOARDING]. Identify what could be automated: templated responses, auto sequences for common situations, scheduling and confirmations, follow up reminders, and routing. Note which should stay personal versus which are safe to automate without losing the human touch. The goal is to cut repetitive communication work while keeping the personal feel where it matters. Give me the communication automations worth setting up.
description: Find email and communication tasks to automate without losing the personal touch.
useCase: Use this to cut the repetitive communication that eats a solo operator's day. Identifying which messages can be automated versus which must stay personal frees significant time on routine emails while preserving the human touch where it matters, avoiding the risk of automating away the personal feel that sets a small business apart.
variables: YOUR EMAIL AND MESSAGE TASKS, E.G. INQUIRIES, FOLLOW UPS, CONFIRMATIONS, ONBOARDING

title: Automate Your Client Workflow
promptText: Help me automate parts of my client workflow in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My client process from inquiry to completion looks like [DESCRIBE YOUR CLIENT WORKFLOW]. Identify where automation could smooth the journey: inquiry handling, scheduling, onboarding, reminders, delivery, follow up, and payment steps. For each opportunity, note what to automate and where a human touch should remain. The goal is a client experience that runs smoothly with less manual effort from me, while still feeling personal and professional. Give me the client workflow automations worth setting up and where to keep the human element.
description: Automate parts of your client workflow while keeping it personal and professional.
useCase: Use this to smooth your client journey with less manual work. Automating scheduling, reminders, onboarding, and follow ups reduces the administrative burden of managing clients while keeping the experience professional, letting a solo operator handle more clients smoothly without dropping the balls that manual tracking inevitably causes.

title: Automate Social Media Tasks
promptText: Help me find social media and content tasks to automate in my [BUSINESS TYPE] without making my presence feel robotic. My social media work involves [YOUR SOCIAL MEDIA TASKS, E.G. POSTING, SCHEDULING, REPURPOSING, ENGAGING]. Identify what is safe and smart to automate, such as scheduling and cross posting, versus what should stay genuinely human, like real engagement and replies. Warn me against automations that make my presence feel spammy or fake. The goal is to save time on the mechanical parts while keeping the authentic human connection that actually works. Give me the social automations worth using and what to keep manual.
description: Automate the mechanical parts of social media while keeping engagement authentic.
useCase: Use this to save time on social media without going robotic. Automating scheduling and repurposing while keeping real engagement human frees time on the mechanical parts, avoiding the trap of over automating social presence into something spammy, since authentic connection is exactly what makes social media work for a small business.
variables: YOUR SOCIAL MEDIA TASKS, E.G. POSTING, SCHEDULING, REPURPOSING, ENGAGING

title: Automate Admin and Backend Tasks
promptText: Help me find the boring admin and backend tasks to automate in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The administrative work that eats my time is [YOUR ADMIN TASKS, E.G. INVOICING, SCHEDULING, DATA ENTRY, FILE ORGANIZATION, REPORTING]. Identify which of these dull but necessary tasks could be automated or streamlined, what would save the most tedious time, and which are the easiest to set up. These are the tasks I least want to do anyway, so automating them is doubly valuable. Give me the admin automations worth setting up ranked by how much tedium they remove.
description: Find the tedious admin and backend tasks worth automating to remove drudgery.
useCase: Use this to offload the boring admin work that drains time and motivation. Automating tedious tasks like invoicing, scheduling, and data entry removes work you dislike anyway, making it doubly valuable, since these necessary but draining tasks are both time consuming and demoralizing for a solo operator who would rather focus on real work.
variables: YOUR ADMIN TASKS, E.G. INVOICING, SCHEDULING, DATA ENTRY, FILE ORGANIZATION, REPORTING

title: Find Quick Automation Wins
promptText: Help me find quick automation wins I can set up fast for immediate relief in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My repetitive tasks are [YOUR REPETITIVE TASKS], and my tech comfort is [YOUR COMFORT LEVEL]. Focus only on the easiest, fastest automations that need little setup and no complex tools, the kind I could put in place today or this week. Skip anything that needs significant time or technical skill. The goal is fast momentum and immediate time savings from simple automations. Give me the quick wins I can implement right away and how to approach each.
description: Find the fastest, simplest automations you can set up today for immediate relief.
useCase: Use this when you want automation relief without a big project. Focusing only on quick wins that need little setup gives immediate time savings and builds momentum, which is often the best way to start automating, since early easy successes make the bigger automation projects feel worth tackling later.

title: Plan a No Code Automation
promptText: Help me plan a no code automation for a task, since I am not technical. The task I want to automate is [THE TASK], how it works now is [CURRENT PROCESS], and the tools involved are [TOOLS INVOLVED]. Walk me through the automation in plain terms: what the trigger would be, what steps or actions should follow, what the end result looks like, and the general type of no code approach that could achieve it. Explain it simply enough that I can set it up or brief someone to. Note what could go wrong and how to test it. Give me a clear no code automation plan.
description: Plan a no code automation in plain terms you can set up without technical skills.
useCase: Use this to automate something when you are not technical. A plain language plan of the trigger, steps, and result makes automation accessible without coding, letting a non technical solo operator either build it with no code tools or clearly brief someone, capturing automation's benefits without needing to become a developer.

title: Map a Workflow Before Automating It
promptText: Help me map out a workflow clearly before I automate it, so I automate the right thing. The process is [THE PROCESS], and here is how it currently works: [DESCRIBE THE CURRENT STEPS]. Help me map it: the trigger that starts it, each step and decision point, the inputs and outputs, and where the manual effort actually is. Then identify which parts should be automated versus kept manual, and whether the process should be improved before automating rather than after. Mapping it first prevents automating a messy process. Give me the mapped workflow and where automation fits.
description: Map a workflow clearly before automating so you automate the right, improved process.
useCase: Use this before automating any multi step process. Mapping the workflow first reveals where the real manual effort is and whether the process should be improved before automating, preventing the common mistake of automating a messy process, which just makes an inefficient way of working run faster.

title: Automate Your Content Workflow
promptText: Help me automate and streamline my content workflow in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My content process from idea to published is [DESCRIBE YOUR CONTENT WORKFLOW]. Identify where automation or streamlining could help: capturing and organizing ideas, repurposing across platforms, scheduling and publishing, and the repetitive mechanical steps, while keeping the actual creative work human. Note what to automate versus what must stay a human creative act. The goal is to spend more time creating and less on the mechanical parts around it. Give me the content workflow automations worth setting up.
description: Automate the mechanical parts of your content workflow so you can focus on creating.
useCase: Use this to spend more time creating and less on content logistics. Automating the mechanical steps around content, like repurposing, scheduling, and publishing, while keeping the creative work human, removes the friction that makes consistent content hard for a solo operator, who often spends more time on logistics than on the creating itself.

title: Balance Automation with a Personal Touch
promptText: Help me decide where to automate and where to keep the human touch in my [BUSINESS TYPE], so I save time without losing what makes my business feel personal. The customer facing interactions and touchpoints I have are [YOUR CUSTOMER TOUCHPOINTS]. Help me draw the line: which touchpoints are safe to automate without customers noticing or minding, which are the moments where the personal human touch is a competitive advantage I should protect, and how to automate the backend while keeping the front feeling personal. Give me clear guidance on what to automate and what to keep human, and why.
description: Decide where to automate and where to protect the personal touch that sets you apart.
useCase: Use this to automate without eroding the personal feel that draws customers to a small business. Drawing a clear line between backend tasks safe to automate and human moments worth protecting captures efficiency while preserving your competitive advantage, since over automating the personal touch surrenders the very edge a solo business has over larger competitors.

title: Audit Your Existing Automations
promptText: Help me audit the automations I already have to make sure they are still serving me well. My current automations are [LIST YOUR EXISTING AUTOMATIONS AND WHAT THEY DO]. Help me review them: which are genuinely saving time and working well, which may be broken, outdated, or no longer needed, which might be causing problems I have not noticed, and where I am over automated or missing a check. Also flag any automation that should have a human review step. The goal is a clean set of automations that actually help. Give me the audit and what to fix, remove, or add a safeguard to.
description: Audit your existing automations to find what is broken, outdated, or risky.
useCase: Use this to keep your automations healthy over time. Auditing what is still working, broken, or over automated catches the silent failures and outdated automations that can quietly cause problems, since a broken automation you have stopped watching can do damage unnoticed, and cluttered automations add confusion rather than saving time.
variables: LIST YOUR EXISTING AUTOMATIONS AND WHAT THEY DO

title: Add Safeguards to an Automation
promptText: Help me add safeguards to an automation so it does not cause problems if something goes wrong. The automation is [DESCRIBE THE AUTOMATION AND WHAT IT DOES], and the risk if it fails or misfires is [WHAT COULD GO WRONG]. Help me build in protection: where a human check or approval step should sit, how to catch errors before they reach a customer, how to know if the automation stops working, and what to monitor. The goal is to keep the time savings while preventing the automation from silently doing damage. Give me the safeguards to add to this automation.
description: Add safeguards to an automation so it does not silently cause damage if it fails.
useCase: Use this for any automation where a failure could cause real harm. Adding human checks, error catching, and monitoring keeps the time savings while preventing the automation from silently damaging customer relationships or your business, since an unwatched automation that misfires can quietly cause problems far costlier than the time it saved.
variables: DESCRIBE THE AUTOMATION AND WHAT IT DOES

title: Find Tasks AI Could Help Automate
promptText: Help me find tasks in my [BUSINESS TYPE] where AI tools could save me time. The tasks I regularly do that involve writing, thinking, summarizing, researching, or repetitive knowledge work are [YOUR RELEVANT TASKS]. Identify where AI could genuinely help: drafting, summarizing, brainstorming, research, repurposing, or first drafts I refine. For each, note how AI would fit into my workflow and what still needs my judgment and review. Be realistic about where AI helps versus where it creates work or risk. Give me the tasks where AI could save real time and how to use it well for each.
description: Find where AI tools could genuinely save time in your knowledge work.
useCase: Use this to spot where AI can realistically speed up your work. Identifying tasks like drafting, summarizing, and research where AI genuinely helps, while noting what still needs your judgment, lets a solo operator capture real time savings from AI without the disappointment of applying it where it creates more work or risk than it removes.

title: Automate Data Entry and Transfers
promptText: Help me automate the data entry and information transfers that waste my time in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The manual data work I do is [YOUR DATA TASKS, E.G. COPYING BETWEEN TOOLS, ENTERING FORM RESPONSES, UPDATING SPREADSHEETS, MOVING INFO BETWEEN APPS]. Identify where information could flow automatically between my tools instead of me copying it manually, which transfers are the biggest time sinks, and the general approach to connect them. Manual data transfer is error prone as well as slow, so note where automating also reduces mistakes. Give me the data automations worth setting up and how to approach each.
description: Automate manual data entry and transfers between your tools to save time and errors.
useCase: Use this to eliminate the manual copying of data between tools. Automating information transfers saves time and reduces the errors that manual data entry inevitably causes, removing one of the most tedious and mistake prone parts of running a business where a solo operator often re enters the same data across multiple disconnected tools.
variables: YOUR DATA TASKS, E.G. COPYING BETWEEN TOOLS, ENTERING FORM RESPONSES, UPDATING SPREADSHEETS, MOVING INFO BETWEEN APPS

title: Automate Scheduling and Booking
promptText: Help me automate scheduling and booking in my [BUSINESS TYPE] to end the back and forth. How I currently handle scheduling is [YOUR CURRENT SCHEDULING PROCESS], and what I book is [WHAT YOU SCHEDULE, E.G. CALLS, APPOINTMENTS, CONSULTATIONS]. Help me set up automated scheduling: how self booking could work, what reminders and confirmations to automate to reduce no shows, how to protect my time with rules and buffers, and how to keep it feeling professional. The goal is to eliminate scheduling back and forth and no shows. Give me a plan to automate my scheduling and what to look for in an approach.
description: Automate scheduling and booking to end back and forth and reduce no shows.
useCase: Use this to eliminate the time wasted on scheduling back and forth. Automated self booking with reminders and buffers ends the email tennis of finding a time and cuts no shows, reclaiming meaningful time and mental energy for a solo operator while making the booking experience smoother and more professional for clients.
variables: WHAT YOU SCHEDULE, E.G. CALLS, APPOINTMENTS, CONSULTATIONS

title: Calculate the Payoff of an Automation
promptText: Help me calculate whether an automation is worth the investment. The task is [THE TASK], I do it [HOW OFTEN], it takes [TIME PER INSTANCE], and setting up the automation would take roughly [SETUP TIME AND ANY COST]. Help me work out the payoff: how much time or money the automation saves over weeks and months, how long until the setup effort pays for itself, and whether the return justifies doing it now versus later or not at all. Factor in ongoing maintenance too. Give me an honest calculation of whether this automation is worth it and when it pays off.
description: Calculate the real time and money payoff of an automation before building it.
useCase: Use this to justify or reject an automation with real numbers. Calculating how long until the setup effort pays for itself, and factoring in maintenance, prevents both under automating high value tasks and over investing in automations that never break even, grounding the decision in payoff rather than the appeal of automating for its own sake.

title: Create Your Automation Starter Plan
promptText: Help me create a simple starter plan to begin automating my [BUSINESS TYPE] without getting overwhelmed. I am new to automation, my repetitive tasks are [YOUR REPETITIVE TASKS], and my comfort with tech is [YOUR COMFORT LEVEL]. Give me a beginner friendly plan: the one or two easiest highest value automations to start with, a simple approach to set them up, how to build confidence before tackling more, and a rough path from there. Do not overwhelm me with a huge list of tools and projects. The goal is to start small and build. Give me my automation starter plan.
description: Create a beginner friendly starter plan to begin automating without overwhelm.
useCase: Use this if automation feels overwhelming and you do not know where to begin. A simple starter plan focused on one or two easy high value automations gets you moving without the paralysis of too many options, building the confidence and momentum that makes tackling bigger automations feel achievable rather than daunting.

title: Check if You Are Over Automating
promptText: Help me check whether I am over automating my [BUSINESS TYPE] in ways that hurt more than help. My current automations are [YOUR AUTOMATIONS], and any signs of friction I have noticed are [ANY PROBLEMS, E.G. CUSTOMERS FEELING PROCESSED, THINGS FEELING IMPERSONAL, COMPLEXITY]. Help me assess: whether any automations are making my business feel cold or robotic, where automation has added complexity or fragility that outweighs the time saved, and where a return to a human or simpler manual approach would actually be better. Give me an honest read on where I have over automated and what to pull back.
description: Check whether you have over automated in ways that hurt your business.
useCase: Use this if automation may have gone too far. An honest check on where automations make your business feel cold or add fragile complexity catches the over automation that quietly costs customer relationships or creates more problems than it solves, since more automation is not always better and some things genuinely work better human or manual.
variables: ANY PROBLEMS, E.G. CUSTOMERS FEELING PROCESSED, THINGS FEELING IMPERSONAL, COMPLEXITY

title: Spot Repetitive Tasks Worth Systematizing
promptText: Help me spot the repetitive tasks in my [BUSINESS TYPE] that I keep doing manually and should either automate, templatize, or systematize. Walk me through my typical week and work: [DESCRIBE YOUR TYPICAL WORK AND TASKS]. Identify the tasks I repeat that follow a pattern, and for each suggest whether the best fix is automation, a template, a checklist, or a system, since not everything needs full automation. The goal is to stop reinventing the wheel on things I do over and over. Give me the repetitive tasks and the right way to systematize each.
description: Spot repetitive tasks and identify the right fix, whether automation, template, or system.
useCase: Use this to stop reinventing the wheel on repeated work. Spotting the tasks that follow a pattern and matching each to the right fix, whether automation, a template, or a checklist, addresses repetition efficiently, since not everything needs full automation and often a simple template or system saves time faster than building an automation would.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR TYPICAL WORK AND TASKS

title: Create a Logo Design Brief
promptText: Help me create a clear logo design brief for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My business is called [BUSINESS NAME], it serves [TARGET AUDIENCE], and what I do is [WHAT YOU DO]. The personality I want my brand to convey is [BRAND PERSONALITY], and brands whose look I admire are [BRANDS YOU LIKE OR WRITE NONE]. Help me define: the feeling the logo should evoke, the style directions that would fit, colors and mood to explore, what to avoid, and where the logo needs to work such as a website, social avatar, and print. This brief will guide whether I design it myself, use AI, or hire someone. Give me the finished logo brief.
description: Create a clear logo design brief that guides any direction you take for a logo.
useCase: Use this before designing or commissioning a logo. A clear brief that defines the feeling, style, and constraints prevents wasted effort and vague results, since whether you use AI, DIY, or a designer, the quality of the outcome depends almost entirely on the clarity of the brief you start from.
variables: BRANDS YOU LIKE OR WRITE NONE

title: Explore Logo Style Directions
promptText: Help me explore logo style directions for my [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME], serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My brand should feel [BRAND PERSONALITY]. Walk me through the main logo styles with a note on how each would suit my business: wordmark, lettermark or monogram, icon or symbol, combination mark, and emblem. For each, explain the tradeoffs, when it works best, and whether it fits my brand and how I will use it. Then recommend the styles most worth exploring for me and why. Help me choose a direction before generating or designing anything. Give me the style exploration and a recommendation.
description: Explore the main logo styles and find the right direction for your brand.
useCase: Use this early to choose a logo style deliberately rather than defaulting to the first idea. Understanding how a wordmark, icon, or combination mark each suit your brand and usage helps you pick a direction that works everywhere you need it, avoiding a logo that looks fine on a website but fails as a tiny social avatar.

title: Write a Midjourney Prompt for a Logo
promptText: Help me write an effective Midjourney prompt to generate logo concepts for my [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME]. The style I want is [LOGO STYLE, E.G. MINIMAL WORDMARK, GEOMETRIC ICON, PLAYFUL MASCOT], the feeling is [BRAND FEELING], and relevant imagery or symbolism is [ANY SYMBOLS OR CONCEPTS, OR WRITE NONE]. Write a strong image prompt that describes the logo style, mood, colors, and any symbolism clearly, using terms that produce clean logo style results such as simple, vector, flat, minimal on a plain background. Give me the main prompt plus 2 or 3 variations to explore different directions. Note that AI logos are best used as inspiration to refine, not final files.
description: Write a strong Midjourney prompt to generate logo concept directions to explore.
useCase: Use this to generate logo concepts and directions to react to. A well constructed image prompt produces varied concepts that spark ideas and clarify what you want, which is valuable early exploration, though AI generated logos work best as inspiration you refine into a clean final mark rather than as finished usable files.
variables: LOGO STYLE, E.G. MINIMAL WORDMARK, GEOMETRIC ICON, PLAYFUL MASCOT
ANY SYMBOLS OR CONCEPTS, OR WRITE NONE

title: Refine a Logo Generation Prompt
promptText: Help me refine my logo image prompt to get better results. Here is my current prompt: [PASTE YOUR PROMPT], and what I am getting that I do not want is [WHAT IS WRONG WITH THE RESULTS]. What I actually want is [DESIRED RESULT]. Help me diagnose why the prompt is producing the wrong output and rewrite it: adjust the descriptive terms, add or remove style keywords, clarify the composition, and use the right language to steer it toward clean logo results. Give me the improved prompt and explain what changed so I learn how to steer image generation better.
description: Diagnose and refine a logo image prompt that is producing the wrong results.
useCase: Use this when your logo prompt keeps generating the wrong style or messy output. Diagnosing why and adjusting the keywords and composition language steers the generator toward what you actually want, and learning what changed builds the prompting skill that makes every future image generation more controllable.
variables: WHAT IS WRONG WITH THE RESULTS

title: Define Your Brand Visual Direction
promptText: Help me define an overall visual direction for my brand before I settle on a logo. My [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME] serves [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I want it to feel [BRAND PERSONALITY]. Help me establish the visual foundation: the overall aesthetic and mood, the kinds of colors that fit, the typographic feel, the imagery style, and how it all should come together into a coherent look. This gives me a direction that the logo, colors, and everything else can align with. Explain how a defined visual direction keeps my brand consistent. Give me the visual direction for my brand.
description: Define an overall visual direction that keeps your whole brand coherent.
useCase: Use this to establish a visual foundation before designing individual pieces. A defined direction for aesthetic, color, and type keeps your logo, website, and content coherent, since a brand that looks consistent everywhere feels far more professional and trustworthy than one where each piece was designed in isolation.

title: Build a Brand Color Palette
promptText: Help me build a color palette for my brand. My [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME] serves [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the feeling I want is [BRAND FEELING]. Any colors I am drawn to or want to avoid are [COLOR PREFERENCES OR WRITE NONE]. Help me create a cohesive palette: a primary color and what it should convey, supporting and accent colors, and neutrals, with guidance on the mood each choice sets and how they work together. Explain the psychology behind the choices for my audience and industry. Note how to keep the palette consistent across everything. Give me a recommended color palette with the reasoning.
description: Build a cohesive brand color palette with reasoning tied to your audience.
useCase: Use this to choose brand colors deliberately rather than picking what looks nice in the moment. A cohesive palette grounded in the mood you want and your audience creates instant recognition and the right emotional response, since consistent color is one of the fastest ways a small brand starts to feel established and intentional.

title: Choose Colors by What They Convey
promptText: Help me understand what colors would best convey the right message for my brand. My [BUSINESS TYPE] serves [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the qualities I want my brand to communicate are [QUALITIES, E.G. TRUST, ENERGY, LUXURY, WARMTH, CALM]. Explain which colors tend to convey each of these qualities and why, how my specific audience and industry might perceive different colors, which colors would reinforce my desired message and which would work against it, and any cultural or industry associations to consider. Then recommend the color directions that best fit what I want to communicate. Give me the guidance and a recommendation.
description: Choose brand colors based on the qualities and message you want to convey.
useCase: Use this to ground your color choices in what they actually communicate. Understanding which colors convey trust, energy, or luxury for your specific audience lets you pick a palette that reinforces your message rather than working against it, since color sends a signal before anyone reads a word, and the wrong one quietly undermines your positioning.
variables: QUALITIES, E.G. TRUST, ENERGY, LUXURY, WARMTH, CALM

title: Generate Icon and Symbol Ideas for a Logo
promptText: Help me brainstorm icon and symbol ideas for my logo. My [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME] does [WHAT YOU DO], serves [TARGET AUDIENCE], and should feel [BRAND FEELING]. Help me generate symbol concepts: the obvious literal symbols and, more valuably, the less obvious metaphorical or abstract ideas that represent what my business stands for or does. For each idea, note what it represents and why it could work. Push beyond the clichés everyone in my industry uses. Then flag the most promising and distinctive concepts. The goal is symbol ideas to explore or generate. Give me the icon and symbol concepts.
description: Brainstorm distinctive icon and symbol concepts for a logo beyond the clichés.
useCase: Use this to find a distinctive symbol rather than the obvious cliché everyone in your industry uses. Generating metaphorical and abstract concepts alongside literal ones surfaces ideas with real meaning and originality, which is what makes a logo memorable rather than another generic mark that blends in with competitors.

title: Plan a Brand Mood Board
promptText: Help me plan a mood board to define my brand's visual feel. My [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME] serves [TARGET AUDIENCE] and should feel [BRAND PERSONALITY]. Help me plan what to gather: the kinds of images, colors, textures, typography, and visual references that would capture the feeling I want, what themes to look for, and how to tell whether a visual fits my brand or not. Give me a clear guide for what to collect and search for so my mood board actually clarifies my direction rather than becoming a random collection. Give me the mood board plan and what to look for.
description: Plan a focused brand mood board that clarifies your visual direction.
useCase: Use this before gathering visual references so your mood board actually guides you. A focused plan for what to collect turns a mood board into a real clarifying tool rather than a random pile of pretty images, giving you and any designer a shared reference for the feel you are after before a single asset is made.

title: Decide What Type of Logo You Need
promptText: Help me decide what type of logo makes the most sense for my situation. My [BUSINESS TYPE] is called [BUSINESS NAME], my name length and style is [SHORT, LONG, OR UNUSUAL], and where I will use the logo most is [WHERE YOU WILL USE IT, E.G. WEBSITE, SOCIAL, PRODUCTS, SIGNAGE]. My budget and skill for creating it is [YOUR SITUATION]. Help me decide between a simple text based logo, an icon plus text, or a standalone symbol, based on my name, usage, budget, and how established my brand is. Recommend the practical choice and explain why it fits my situation better than the alternatives. Give me a clear recommendation.
description: Decide the practical logo type that fits your name, usage, and budget.
useCase: Use this to choose a logo type that fits your real situation, not just what looks good. Matching the choice to your name, how you will use it, and your budget leads to a practical logo that works everywhere you need it, since a symbol only mark, for example, demands brand recognition a new business does not yet have.
variables: WHERE YOU WILL USE IT, E.G. WEBSITE, SOCIAL, PRODUCTS, SIGNAGE

title: Decide Whether to DIY or Hire for Your Logo
promptText: Help me decide how to actually get my logo made. My situation is [YOUR BUDGET, SKILL, AND TIMELINE], my business stage is [JUST STARTING, ESTABLISHED, OR REBRANDING], and how important a polished logo is to my business is [HOW MUCH IT MATTERS FOR YOU]. Help me weigh the options: designing it myself with tools, using AI generation as a base, using an affordable design service, or hiring a professional designer. For each, note the cost, quality, and effort tradeoffs for my situation, and recommend the smartest approach for where I am now. A logo can be upgraded later, so note if starting simple is fine. Give me a recommendation.
description: Decide the smartest way to get your logo made for your budget and stage.
useCase: Use this to choose how to get your logo made without overspending or underinvesting. Weighing DIY, AI, an affordable service, or a professional against your stage and budget leads to a sensible choice, since a brand new business often needs only a clean simple logo now and can invest in a professional one once it is established.
variables: HOW MUCH IT MATTERS FOR YOU

title: Write a Logo Brief for a Designer
promptText: Help me write a brief to give a designer so they create the logo I actually want. My [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME] serves [TARGET AUDIENCE] and should feel [BRAND PERSONALITY]. What I want is [YOUR VISION OR WRITE OPEN], examples I like are [REFERENCES OR WRITE NONE], and where I will use the logo is [USAGE]. Write a clear designer brief covering the business context, the feeling and style I want, practical requirements like where it will be used and formats needed, what to avoid, and how I will judge success. A strong brief gets a better result and fewer costly revision rounds. Give me the designer brief.
description: Write a clear logo brief that gets a designer to deliver what you actually want.
useCase: Use this when hiring a designer for your logo. A clear brief covering context, the feeling you want, and practical requirements gets a better result with fewer revision rounds, since designers deliver against what you communicate, and a vague brief is the main reason logo projects drag on through frustrating back and forth and disappointing drafts.

title: Articulate Feedback on Logo Drafts
promptText: Help me give clear useful feedback on logo drafts I received. Here is what I am reacting to: [DESCRIBE THE DRAFTS AND YOUR GUT REACTION, E.G. SOMETHING FEELS OFF, TOO CORPORATE, NOT QUITE RIGHT]. My brand should feel [BRAND FEELING]. Help me turn my vague reactions into specific, actionable feedback: what exactly is or is not working, the words to describe what I want changed, and how to communicate it so the designer or my next generation attempt gets closer. Translate my gut feeling into precise design feedback. Give me clear feedback I can pass on.
description: Turn vague gut reactions to logo drafts into precise, actionable feedback.
useCase: Use this when a logo draft feels off but you cannot say why. Translating your gut reaction into specific feedback, like too corporate or the spacing feels heavy, lets a designer or your next attempt actually improve it, since it is not quite right gives nothing to act on and leads to endless frustrating revision rounds.
variables: DESCRIBE THE DRAFTS AND YOUR GUT REACTION, E.G. SOMETHING FEELS OFF, TOO CORPORATE, NOT QUITE RIGHT

title: Evaluate a Logo Before Finalizing
promptText: Help me evaluate a logo before I commit to it. The logo is [DESCRIBE THE LOGO], for my [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME] that should feel [BRAND FEELING]. Help me judge it against what matters: whether it works at small sizes like a social avatar and large like a banner, whether it works in one color and in black and white, whether it is simple and memorable, whether it fits my brand and audience, whether it is distinct from competitors, and whether it will still feel right in a few years. Walk me through each check honestly and flag any problems. Give me an evaluation and whether it is ready to finalize.
description: Evaluate a logo against the practical tests that matter before committing.
useCase: Use this before finalizing a logo you will use everywhere for years. Checking it against real tests like scalability, single color use, and distinctiveness catches problems while they are still fixable, since a logo that looks great in the design file but fails as a tiny avatar or in black and white causes ongoing headaches once it is in use.

title: Plan Where Your Logo Needs to Work
promptText: Help me think through all the places my logo needs to work, so I design or choose one that functions everywhere. My [BUSINESS TYPE] uses visuals in these places: [WHERE YOU SHOW UP, E.G. WEBSITE, SOCIAL PROFILES, INVOICES, PRODUCTS, EMAIL, SIGNAGE]. Help me map the practical requirements: where it needs to be tiny versus large, where it needs to work on light versus dark backgrounds, where a horizontal versus stacked version is needed, and what logo variations I should have ready. The goal is to plan for real usage so my logo works everywhere, not just in one context. Give me the usage map and variations I will need.
description: Map everywhere your logo must work and the variations you will need.
useCase: Use this to design or choose a logo that works in every real context. Mapping where it needs to be tiny, on dark backgrounds, or horizontal versus stacked reveals the variations you will need, preventing the common problem of a logo that looks great on the website but breaks as a favicon, on a dark header, or in a square social avatar.
variables: WHERE YOU SHOW UP, E.G. WEBSITE, SOCIAL PROFILES, INVOICES, PRODUCTS, EMAIL, SIGNAGE

title: Generate Logo Concepts with Gemini
promptText: Help me write a prompt to generate logo concepts for my [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME]. The style I want is [LOGO STYLE], the feeling is [BRAND FEELING], and any symbolism to include is [SYMBOLISM OR WRITE NONE]. Write a clear detailed prompt describing the logo concept, style, mood, colors, and composition so an image generator produces clean logo style concepts on a simple background. Include guidance to keep it minimal and vector like rather than detailed or photographic. Give me the main prompt and a couple of variations to explore different looks. Remind me that generated logos are starting points to refine, not final production files.
description: Write a clear prompt to generate clean logo concepts with an image generator.
useCase: Use this to generate logo concept directions to explore and react to. A detailed prompt describing style, mood, and composition produces varied clean concepts that help you discover what you want, serving as strong early inspiration you then refine into a polished final mark, since raw generated logos are rarely production ready as is.

title: Choose Typography for Your Brand
promptText: Help me choose typography that fits my brand. My [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME] serves [TARGET AUDIENCE] and should feel [BRAND PERSONALITY]. Help me understand what typography would suit my brand: the general type styles that convey the right feeling such as modern, classic, friendly, or bold, how many fonts I actually need and how they should pair, where readability matters most, and the qualities to look for. Describe the type directions rather than assuming specific current fonts, since availability changes. Explain how type shapes brand perception. Give me typography direction and what to look for when choosing fonts.
description: Choose typography direction that conveys the right feeling for your brand.
useCase: Use this to choose fonts that reinforce your brand rather than clash with it. Understanding what type styles convey your desired feeling and how to pair them shapes how professional and coherent your brand looks, since typography silently sets tone across everything you make, and mismatched or amateur font choices quietly undercut an otherwise good brand.

title: Plan Your Logo Variations
promptText: Help me plan the set of logo variations I should have for practical use. My main logo is [DESCRIBE YOUR LOGO], and where I use my brand is [YOUR USAGE CONTEXTS]. Help me determine the variations to create or request: a primary version, a stacked or horizontal alternate, a simplified or icon only mark for small spaces, single color and reversed versions for dark backgrounds, and any others my usage needs. Explain when each variation gets used. Having the right set prevents the scramble of not having the version you need. Give me the list of logo variations to prepare and where each is used.
description: Plan the practical set of logo variations you need for real world use.
useCase: Use this once you have a logo, to prepare the variations real usage demands. Having a stacked version, an icon only mark, and single color and reversed versions ready prevents the recurring scramble of not having the format you need, which is a constant small friction for businesses that only ever made one version of their logo.

title: Create a Simple Brand Consistency Guide
promptText: Help me create a simple brand guide so my visuals stay consistent everywhere. My brand elements are: logo [DESCRIBE YOUR LOGO], colors [YOUR COLORS], fonts [YOUR FONTS], and overall feel [YOUR BRAND FEELING]. Help me document the essentials in a lightweight practical guide: how and where to use the logo and its variations, the exact colors and when to use each, the fonts and how to apply them, and simple do and do not rules to keep everything consistent. Keep it short and usable for a solo operator, not a huge corporate manual. Give me the simple brand consistency guide.
description: Create a lightweight brand guide that keeps your visuals consistent everywhere.
useCase: Use this to lock in consistency once your brand basics exist. A simple guide documenting your logo, colors, and fonts and how to use them keeps everything cohesive as you create more, which is what makes a small brand look established, and it becomes essential the moment you hand any design work to someone else.

title: Analyze Competitor Logos to Stand Out
promptText: Help me analyze my competitors' logos and branding so mine can stand apart. Here is what my competitors' logos and visual branding look like: [DESCRIBE COMPETITOR LOGOS AND BRANDING]. My [BUSINESS TYPE] serves [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me spot the patterns: the colors, styles, and symbols they all tend to use, where they look similar to each other, and the visual space none of them occupy. Then recommend how my logo and branding could deliberately differ to stand out while still fitting my industry. The goal is a distinct visual identity, not another lookalike. Give me the analysis and how to differentiate visually.
description: Analyze competitor logos to find the visual space to make yours distinct.
useCase: Use this so your logo stands out instead of blending in. Analyzing the colors, styles, and symbols competitors all share reveals the visual space none occupy, letting you differentiate deliberately, since in industries where everyone's branding looks alike, a distinct visual identity is an immediate and lasting competitive advantage.

title: Plan a Logo Refresh
promptText: Help me decide whether and how to refresh my existing logo. My current logo is [DESCRIBE YOUR CURRENT LOGO], and what feels off about it is [WHAT BOTHERS YOU, E.G. OUTDATED, AMATEUR, NO LONGER FITS]. My business is [YOUR SITUATION AND HOW ESTABLISHED YOUR BRAND IS]. Help me think it through: whether a full redesign or a lighter refresh is the right call, what to keep for recognition versus change, how to modernize without losing brand equity, and how to roll out the change. Be honest if the current logo is fine and a refresh is not worth it. Give me a recommendation and a refresh plan if warranted.
description: Decide whether to refresh your logo and plan it without losing brand equity.
useCase: Use this when your logo feels dated or amateur but changing it feels risky. Weighing a full redesign against a lighter refresh, and what to keep for recognition, lets you modernize without discarding the brand equity you have built, avoiding both clinging to a logo that undercuts you and a needless rebrand that resets recognition.
variables: WHAT BOTHERS YOU, E.G. OUTDATED, AMATEUR, NO LONGER FITS
YOUR SITUATION AND HOW ESTABLISHED YOUR BRAND IS

title: Get Quick Logo Concept Directions Fast
promptText: I need quick logo concept ideas for my [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME], serving [TARGET AUDIENCE], that should feel [BRAND FEELING]. Give me a handful of distinct logo directions fast: for each, a short description of the style, any symbol or concept, and the feeling it conveys. Keep it quick and varied so I have several directions to consider or generate from. No long explanation, just solid concept starting points I can react to right now.
description: Get several quick, distinct logo concept directions to react to fast.
useCase: Use this when you want fast logo directions to react to. A handful of quick distinct concepts gives you starting points to explore or generate from without a lengthy process, useful early when reacting to a few concrete directions clarifies your thinking faster than staring at a blank page trying to imagine one.

title: Translate Brand Personality into Visuals
promptText: Help me translate my brand personality into concrete visual choices. My [BUSINESS TYPE] called [BUSINESS NAME] has a personality I would describe as [YOUR BRAND PERSONALITY IN A FEW WORDS], serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me turn those personality traits into specific visual direction: what colors, shapes, typography, and overall style would express each trait, and how they combine into a coherent look that feels like my brand. Bridge the gap between abstract personality words and actual design decisions. Give me the visual choices that would authentically express my brand personality.
description: Translate abstract brand personality traits into concrete visual design choices.
useCase: Use this to bridge the gap between how your brand should feel and actual design decisions. Translating personality traits into specific color, shape, and type choices ensures your visuals authentically express your brand, since knowing you want to feel approachable or premium only helps once it becomes concrete design direction you can actually act on.
variables: YOUR BRAND PERSONALITY IN A FEW WORDS

title: Answer Key Questions Before Designing a Logo
promptText: Help me think through the key questions I should answer before creating my logo, since I am not sure where to start. My [BUSINESS TYPE] is called [BUSINESS NAME] and serves [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Ask me the important questions that will clarify my logo direction: about my brand personality, what makes me different, how I want people to feel, where I will use the logo, and what I am drawn to. Then help me use my answers to arrive at an initial direction. Guide me from not knowing what I want to having a clear starting point. Give me the questions and help me work through to a direction.
description: Work through the key questions that clarify your logo direction from scratch.
useCase: Use this when you need a logo but have no idea what you want. Working through the right clarifying questions about personality, differentiation, and usage turns a blank starting point into a clear direction, which is essential since attempting to design or generate a logo without this clarity produces scattered results and endless second guessing.

title: Plan a Social Media Graphic Concept
promptText: Help me plan a graphic for a social media post. My [BUSINESS TYPE] is posting about [POST TOPIC OR MESSAGE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] and my colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me plan the graphic concept: what it should communicate at a glance, the key visual idea, what text if any should be on it, the layout approach, and how to make it stop the scroll while staying on brand. Keep it achievable for someone without deep design skills. Give me a clear concept I can create or generate from.
description: Plan a clear, scroll stopping social graphic concept that stays on brand.
useCase: Use this before making a social graphic so it communicates fast and fits your brand. A planned concept that stops the scroll and conveys the message at a glance performs far better than a graphic thrown together, since on social feeds you have a split second to catch attention before someone keeps scrolling.

title: Design a Quote Graphic
promptText: Help me design a quote graphic for social media. The quote or text is [YOUR QUOTE OR TEXT], for my [BUSINESS TYPE] targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] with colors [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me plan the design: how to lay out the text so it is readable and striking, what to emphasize, the background and color approach, and how to keep it clearly on brand and shareable. Note how to make it work on [PLATFORM] specifically. Keep it simple enough to build in a basic design tool. Give me the design plan for the quote graphic.
description: Design a readable, shareable quote graphic that stays on brand.
useCase: Use this to turn a strong line or insight into a shareable quote graphic. A well laid out quote graphic gets saved and shared, extending your reach, and getting the text emphasis and brand look right is what separates a graphic people repost from one that looks amateur and gets ignored.

title: Write an Image Prompt for a Social Graphic
promptText: Help me write an image generation prompt to create a background or visual for a social media graphic. The post is about [POST TOPIC], the mood I want is [MOOD OR FEELING], my brand colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE], and it is for [PLATFORM] so the shape should be [ORIENTATION, E.G. SQUARE, PORTRAIT, LANDSCAPE]. Write a clear image prompt describing the visual, style, mood, colors, and composition, leaving space for text if I will add it. Note the aspect ratio to request. Give me the main prompt plus a couple of variations. Remind me to leave clear areas for any text overlay.
description: Write an image prompt for a social graphic background with room for text.
useCase: Use this to generate a custom background or visual for a social graphic. A clear image prompt that specifies mood, colors, and the right aspect ratio, and leaves space for text, produces a usable on brand visual, giving your posts a distinctive look rather than the generic stock imagery everyone else uses.

title: Plan a Carousel Graphic Series
promptText: Help me plan a carousel of graphics for [PLATFORM]. My [BUSINESS TYPE] wants to teach or share [THE TOPIC OR CONTENT] with [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan the carousel slide by slide: a cover slide that hooks and earns the swipe, content slides that each carry one clear point with minimal text, a consistent visual layout across slides, and a final slide with a call to action. Note what visual and text goes on each slide and how to keep them cohesive. Keep text minimal and scannable. Give me the slide by slide carousel plan.
description: Plan a cohesive carousel series slide by slide with a strong hook and CTA.
useCase: Use this to plan a carousel, one of the highest engagement formats on Instagram and LinkedIn. A cohesive series with a hooking cover and one idea per slide keeps people swiping, which the algorithm rewards, and planning it slide by slide ensures the visual consistency and clear flow that make carousels perform.

title: Design a Carousel Cover Slide
promptText: Help me design a cover slide for my carousel that earns the swipe. The carousel is about [CAROUSEL TOPIC] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], and the payoff of swiping is [WHAT THEY GET]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me design a cover that stops the scroll and compels the swipe: the hook text and how to phrase it, the visual approach, what to emphasize, and how to signal there is valuable content inside. The cover determines whether anyone sees the rest. Give me the cover slide design and a few hook text options to test.
description: Design a carousel cover slide that stops the scroll and earns the swipe.
useCase: Use this because the carousel cover determines whether anyone sees the rest. A cover that stops the scroll and compels the swipe with a strong hook is the single biggest factor in carousel performance, since even great content inside goes unseen if the cover fails to earn that first swipe.

title: Create Reusable Social Graphic Templates
promptText: Help me plan a set of reusable social media graphic templates so I can create on brand posts quickly. My [BUSINESS TYPE] regularly posts these types of content: [YOUR RECURRING POST TYPES, E.G. QUOTES, TIPS, ANNOUNCEMENTS, TESTIMONIALS]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] and colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. For each recurring post type, plan a template: the layout, where text and visuals go, the consistent brand elements, and what changes each time versus stays fixed. The goal is templates I can quickly fill in to post consistently without designing from scratch. Give me the template plans.
description: Plan reusable, on brand graphic templates for your recurring post types.
useCase: Use this once to make posting consistent graphics fast. Reusable templates for your recurring post types let you fill in and publish quickly instead of designing from scratch each time, which is what makes consistent posting sustainable and gives your feed the cohesive branded look that builds recognition.
variables: YOUR RECURRING POST TYPES, E.G. QUOTES, TIPS, ANNOUNCEMENTS, TESTIMONIALS

title: Plan Text Overlay for a Graphic
promptText: Help me plan the text overlay for a social media graphic so it is readable and impactful. The message is [YOUR MESSAGE OR TEXT], on a [DESCRIBE THE BACKGROUND OR IMAGE], for [PLATFORM]. Help me with the text: how to trim it to the essential words for a graphic, what to emphasize as the focal point versus secondary, how to ensure readability against the background, and how to arrange it for impact. Graphics fail when text is too long or hard to read. Give me the refined text and a layout approach for the overlay.
description: Plan readable, impactful text overlay that does not overwhelm a graphic.
useCase: Use this when adding text to a graphic, where too much or unreadable text is the most common failure. Trimming to essential words, setting a clear focal point, and ensuring readability against the background makes a graphic land at a glance, since cluttered or hard to read text causes people to scroll straight past.

title: Get the Right Graphic Sizes per Platform
promptText: Help me understand what graphic dimensions and formats I need for the platforms I post on. I post on [YOUR PLATFORMS], and the types of posts I make are [YOUR POST TYPES, E.G. FEED POSTS, STORIES, CAROUSELS, PROFILE IMAGES]. Explain the general aspect ratios and orientation each platform and post type needs, why using the right size matters for how the graphic displays, and how to design once and adapt across formats efficiently. Focus on the principles and aspect ratios rather than exact pixel counts, since those change. Give me a practical guide to sizing my graphics for each platform and post type.
description: Learn the right aspect ratios and formats for graphics on each platform.
useCase: Use this to design graphics that display properly on each platform. Using the right aspect ratios prevents the awkward cropping and wasted space that make posts look amateur, and knowing how to adapt one design across formats efficiently saves time when you post the same content to multiple platforms.
variables: YOUR POST TYPES, E.G. FEED POSTS, STORIES, CAROUSELS, PROFILE IMAGES

title: Generate a Social Visual with Gemini
promptText: Help me write a prompt to generate a visual for a social media post. The post is about [POST TOPIC], the mood is [MOOD], my brand colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE], and the format is [ORIENTATION] for [PLATFORM]. Write a clear detailed image prompt describing the scene or visual, style, mood, colors, and composition, with space left for any text I will add. Specify the aspect ratio. Give me the main prompt and a couple of variations to explore different looks. Remind me to check the generated image works with my brand and leaves room for text where needed.
description: Write a detailed prompt to generate an on brand social post visual.
useCase: Use this to generate a custom visual for a post. A detailed prompt specifying mood, colors, composition, and aspect ratio produces a usable on brand image with room for text, giving your posts original visuals instead of the recycled stock photos that make small business feeds blur together with everyone else's.

title: Plan a Cohesive Visual Feed
promptText: Help me plan a cohesive visual look for my social media feed so it looks intentional and on brand. My [BUSINESS TYPE] posts on [PLATFORM] for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] with colors [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me define a consistent visual system: the recurring colors, styles, fonts, and layout patterns that should tie my posts together, how much variety to allow while staying cohesive, and how to make my feed recognizable at a glance. A cohesive feed signals professionalism. Give me the visual system and simple rules to keep my feed consistent.
description: Plan a cohesive visual system so your feed looks intentional and recognizable.
useCase: Use this to make your feed look professional and recognizable as a whole. A consistent visual system across posts signals professionalism and builds brand recognition, since a cohesive feed makes a small business look established, while a random mix of styles reads as amateur even when the individual posts are fine.

title: Design an Announcement Graphic
promptText: Help me design a graphic to announce something for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The announcement is [WHAT YOU ARE ANNOUNCING, E.G. A LAUNCH, SALE, EVENT, OR NEWS] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] and colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me design an announcement graphic that grabs attention, communicates the key information clearly at a glance such as what, when, and any action, creates appropriate excitement or urgency, and drives the action I want. Keep the essential info prominent and uncluttered. Give me the design plan and the text to put on the graphic.
description: Design an announcement graphic that grabs attention and drives action.
useCase: Use this to announce a launch, sale, or event with a graphic that gets noticed.
