An announcement graphic that communicates the key info at a glance and drives action outperforms a plain text post, since a strong visual creates the attention and excitement that gets people to actually notice and respond to what you are announcing.
variables: WHAT YOU ARE ANNOUNCING, E.G. A LAUNCH, SALE, EVENT, OR NEWS

title: Design a Promotional or Sale Graphic
promptText: Help me design a promotional graphic for an offer or sale from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The offer is [YOUR OFFER OR PROMOTION], the key details are [PRICE, DISCOUNT, DEADLINE, OR TERMS], for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] and colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me design a graphic that makes the offer clear and enticing, highlights the value and any urgency, includes the key details without clutter, and drives people to act, while staying on brand rather than looking like a spammy sales flyer. Give me the design plan and the text for the promotional graphic.
description: Design a promotional graphic that sells the offer without looking spammy.
useCase: Use this to promote an offer with a graphic that converts. A promotional graphic that makes the value and urgency clear while staying on brand drives action without the spammy sales flyer look that erodes trust, since a clean compelling offer graphic gets both attention and clicks where a cluttered one repels.

title: Plan an Infographic
promptText: Help me plan an infographic that presents information visually for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The information or data is [WHAT YOU WANT TO SHOW], for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan it: how to structure the information visually so it is easy to follow, what to show as the key takeaway, how to represent any numbers or steps simply, the visual flow, and how to keep it clear rather than cramming in too much. Infographics get shared when they make information easy and satisfying to grasp. Give me the infographic structure and content plan.
description: Plan a clear, shareable infographic that makes information easy to grasp.
useCase: Use this to turn information or data into a shareable infographic. A well structured infographic that makes information easy and satisfying to grasp gets saved and shared widely, positioning you as a helpful expert, while a cluttered one that crams in too much overwhelms and gets ignored.

title: Turn Data into a Simple Visual
promptText: Help me turn some data or numbers into a simple clear visual for social media. The data is [YOUR DATA OR NUMBERS], and the point I want it to make is [THE KEY MESSAGE], for [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me simplify it: the single clearest way to visualize this so the takeaway is obvious at a glance, what to cut so it is not cluttered, how to label it simply, and how to make the key number or comparison stand out. The goal is a visual anyone grasps instantly, not a busy chart. Give me the simplest effective way to present this data visually and how to lay it out.
description: Turn data into a simple visual where the key takeaway is obvious at a glance.
useCase: Use this to make data shareable and instantly understandable. Simplifying numbers into a clear visual where the takeaway is obvious at a glance makes your point land and gets shared, since a clean simple data visual communicates far more effectively on social than a busy chart that makes people work to understand it.

title: Design a Before and After Graphic
promptText: Help me design a before and after graphic to show a transformation from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The before is [THE BEFORE STATE], the after is [THE AFTER STATE], and it demonstrates [WHAT IT PROVES ABOUT YOUR WORK]. For [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me design it: how to lay out the before and after for clear contrast, what to emphasize about the transformation, any text to frame it, and how to make the improvement obvious and compelling. Before and afters are powerful proof. Give me the design plan for the before and after graphic.
description: Design a before and after graphic that makes a transformation clear and compelling.
useCase: Use this to showcase results with a before and after, one of the most persuasive social formats. A clear contrast that makes the transformation obvious provides powerful proof of your work, since seeing a real result is far more convincing to prospects than any claim, and these graphics reliably earn attention and shares.
variables: WHAT IT PROVES ABOUT YOUR WORK

title: Design a Testimonial Graphic
promptText: Help me design a graphic to feature a customer testimonial from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The testimonial is [PASTE THE TESTIMONIAL], from [CUSTOMER NAME OR DESCRIPTOR], for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] and colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me design it: how to present the quote so it is readable and credible, what to emphasize, how to attribute it, and how to make it feel like genuine social proof rather than bragging. Trim the quote to its most powerful part if needed. Give me the design plan and the final text layout for the testimonial graphic.
description: Design a testimonial graphic that reads as genuine social proof, not bragging.
useCase: Use this to turn a customer testimonial into a social proof graphic. Presenting a trimmed powerful quote with credible attribution makes proof persuasive and shareable, and framing it as genuine social proof rather than bragging is what lets testimonials build trust with prospects instead of coming across as self promotion.

title: Adapt a Graphic Across Platforms
promptText: Help me adapt one graphic to work across my different platforms efficiently. My original graphic is [DESCRIBE THE GRAPHIC AND ITS FORMAT], and I want to use it on [YOUR OTHER PLATFORMS AND FORMATS]. Help me plan the adaptations: how to reformat it for each platform's dimensions without awkward cropping, what to adjust for each platform's style and audience, and how to keep it looking intentional rather than a stretched or squished copy. The goal is to get maximum use from one design across platforms. Give me a plan to adapt this graphic for each platform.
description: Adapt one graphic across platforms without awkward cropping or a stretched look.
useCase: Use this to get more from one graphic across your platforms. Adapting it properly for each format, rather than posting an awkwardly cropped copy, lets one design serve everywhere while still looking intentional, saving significant design time for a solo operator who cannot make custom graphics for every platform separately.
variables: DESCRIBE THE GRAPHIC AND ITS FORMAT

title: Get Feedback on a Social Graphic
promptText: Help me evaluate a social media graphic I made before I post it. Here is what it looks like: [DESCRIBE YOUR GRAPHIC IN DETAIL], for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], and my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me assess it honestly: whether the message is clear at a glance, whether the text is readable, whether it will stop the scroll, whether it looks on brand and professional, and what specifically would make it stronger. Point out anything that looks cluttered, off, or amateur. Give me honest feedback and specific improvements before I post.
description: Get honest feedback on a social graphic and specific improvements before posting.
useCase: Use this before posting a graphic you are unsure about. Honest feedback on clarity, readability, and whether it looks professional catches the issues that would make it underperform or look amateur, letting you fix them before posting rather than publishing something that quietly undercuts your brand.

title: Check Graphics for Brand Consistency
promptText: Help me check whether my social media graphics are consistent with my brand. My brand elements are colors [YOUR COLORS], fonts [YOUR FONTS], and overall feel [YOUR BRAND FEELING]. Here are the graphics or types of posts I make: [DESCRIBE YOUR GRAPHICS]. Help me assess: whether they consistently reflect my brand colors, fonts, and style, where they drift off brand or look inconsistent with each other, and simple rules I could follow to keep everything cohesive. The goal is a recognizable consistent visual brand across all my graphics. Give me the consistency check and rules to tighten it up.
description: Check whether your graphics are consistent with your brand and tighten them up.
useCase: Use this to audit your graphics for brand consistency. Checking whether they consistently use your colors, fonts, and style catches the drift that makes a feed look scattered, and simple consistency rules keep your visual brand recognizable, which is what makes a small business look established and intentional rather than ad hoc.

title: Design a Story or Vertical Graphic
promptText: Help me design a vertical story graphic for [PLATFORM] stories. The content is [WHAT THE STORY IS ABOUT], for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and any action I want is [DESIRED ACTION, E.G. TAP LINK, REPLY, SWIPE, OR WRITE NONE]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me design it for the full screen vertical format: how to use the vertical space, keeping text large and readable on mobile, where to place any interactive element or call to action, and making it feel native to stories rather than a repurposed square post. Give me the story graphic design plan and text.
description: Design a vertical story graphic that feels native to the full screen format.
useCase: Use this to design for stories, which need a different approach than feed posts. A graphic built for the full screen vertical format with large mobile readable text feels native and performs better than a repurposed square post, which looks awkward and out of place in the immersive full screen story experience.
variables: DESIRED ACTION, E.G. TAP LINK, REPLY, SWIPE, OR WRITE NONE

title: Design Your Profile Visuals
promptText: Help me plan the profile visuals for my [BUSINESS TYPE] on [PLATFORM], such as my profile picture and any header or cover image. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING], my colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE], and what I want my profile to convey is [THE IMPRESSION YOU WANT]. Help me plan: what my profile image should be so it is clear and recognizable even when tiny, what a header or cover should show and communicate, and how to make my profile instantly signal who I am and what I offer. These are the first thing visitors see. Give me a plan for my profile visuals.
description: Plan profile and cover visuals that make a strong first impression.
useCase: Use this to get your profile visuals right, since they are the first thing every visitor sees. A clear recognizable profile image and a cover that signals who you are and what you offer make a strong first impression, which shapes whether visitors follow or bounce, making these among the highest impact visuals you have.

title: Get a Quick Social Graphic Idea Fast
promptText: I need a quick idea for a social media graphic. My [BUSINESS TYPE] wants to post about [TOPIC OR MESSAGE] for [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. Give me a fast, clear graphic concept: what the visual should be, what text to put on it, and the layout, so I can make it quickly in a basic design tool. Keep it simple and ready to build, no long explanation. Just give me a solid graphic idea I can create right now.
description: Get a fast, simple, ready to build social graphic concept.
useCase: Use this when you need a graphic idea quickly to keep posting. A fast simple concept with the visual, text, and layout lets you create and post without a long planning process, which helps a solo operator maintain consistent posting even on busy days when the alternative is skipping the post entirely.

title: Choose the Right Tool to Make Graphics
promptText: Help me figure out the best approach and type of tool to create my social media graphics. My design skill is [YOUR SKILL LEVEL], my budget is [YOUR BUDGET OR WRITE MINIMAL], the graphics I need to make are [WHAT YOU NEED TO CREATE], and how often is [HOW OFTEN]. Help me decide: whether a template based design tool, AI generation, or hiring out fits my situation, what to look for in an approach given my skill and needs, and how to create good graphics efficiently without becoming a designer. Focus on the type of approach rather than assuming specific current products. Give me a recommendation for how to create my graphics.
description: Choose the right approach and type of tool for creating your social graphics.
useCase: Use this to find a sustainable way to make graphics that fits your skill and budget. Choosing the right approach, whether a template tool, AI, or hiring out, lets you create good graphics efficiently without becoming a designer, which is what makes consistent visual content realistic for a solo operator rather than a constant struggle.

title: Plan Seasonal or Timely Graphics
promptText: Help me plan a graphic for a seasonal or timely moment for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The occasion is [THE HOLIDAY, SEASON, OR EVENT], and how it connects to my business is [THE CONNECTION OR WRITE GENERAL]. For [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan a graphic that fits the moment genuinely, ties it to my brand or offer naturally, and feels timely rather than a forced generic holiday post. Note how to keep it on brand while embracing the occasion. Give me the seasonal graphic concept and text.
description: Plan a seasonal or timely graphic that fits the moment and stays on brand.
useCase: Use this to create timely graphics that ride seasonal attention. A graphic that genuinely fits the occasion and ties naturally to your brand feels relevant and gets engagement, while staying on brand avoids the forced generic holiday post that adds nothing, letting you capitalize on timely moments without looking like you are just filling the calendar.

title: Plan a Product Mockup Concept
promptText: Help me plan a product mockup for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The product is [YOUR PRODUCT, E.G. A DIGITAL PRODUCT, PHYSICAL ITEM, PACKAGING, OR APP], and I need the mockup for [WHERE IT WILL BE USED, E.G. WEBSITE, SOCIAL, SALES PAGE]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me plan the mockup concept: how to present the product so it looks appealing and real, the setting or context that would make it desirable, what to emphasize, and the overall style. The goal is a mockup that makes people want the product. Give me a clear mockup concept I can create or generate from.
description: Plan a product mockup concept that presents your product as appealing and real.
useCase: Use this before creating a product mockup so it sells rather than just shows. A planned concept that presents the product appealingly in the right context makes it desirable, which matters because for digital and physical products alike, a professional looking mockup strongly influences whether people perceive the product as worth buying.
variables: YOUR PRODUCT, E.G. A DIGITAL PRODUCT, PHYSICAL ITEM, PACKAGING, OR APP
WHERE IT WILL BE USED, E.G. WEBSITE, SOCIAL, SALES PAGE

title: Create a Digital Product Mockup
promptText: Help me create a mockup to visually present my digital product so it feels tangible and valuable. My digital product is [YOUR DIGITAL PRODUCT, E.G. AN EBOOK, COURSE, TEMPLATE, GUIDE, OR SOFTWARE], and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Digital products are hard to sell because they have no physical form, so help me plan how to give it a visual presence: whether to show it on a device screen, as a book or box representation, or as a stylized graphic, and how to make an intangible product look substantial and worth the price. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Give me the best way to mock up my digital product and the concept to create.
description: Create a mockup that gives an intangible digital product visual weight and value.
useCase: Use this to give a digital product a visual presence that justifies its price. Since digital products have no physical form, a mockup showing it on a device or as a stylized representation makes it feel substantial and worth buying, addressing the real challenge that intangible products struggle to look valuable without a strong visual.
variables: YOUR DIGITAL PRODUCT, E.G. AN EBOOK, COURSE, TEMPLATE, GUIDE, OR SOFTWARE

title: Write a Midjourney Prompt for a Product Mockup
promptText: Help me write a Midjourney prompt to generate a product mockup or product scene. The product is [DESCRIBE YOUR PRODUCT], the setting or context I want is [DESIRED SCENE OR BACKGROUND], the mood is [MOOD], and my brand colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Write a clear image prompt describing the product, the scene, lighting, style, and mood to produce an appealing professional product shot. Note the aspect ratio for where I will use it. Give me the main prompt plus a couple of variations for different settings. Remind me that generated product images work best for lifestyle scenes and inspiration, and to check the product depiction is accurate to what I actually sell.
description: Write a Midjourney prompt to generate an appealing product mockup or scene.
useCase: Use this to generate a professional looking product scene or lifestyle shot. A clear prompt specifying the product, setting, and lighting produces appealing imagery that makes a product desirable, useful for creating polished product visuals without a photoshoot, though the depiction should be checked for accuracy against what you actually sell.

title: Plan a Lifestyle Context for Your Product
promptText: Help me plan a lifestyle context to show my product in use rather than in isolation. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], used by [TARGET AUDIENCE] to [WHAT IT HELPS THEM DO]. Help me plan a context or scene that shows the product in a real appealing setting, connects it to the life or outcome my audience wants, and makes it aspirational, not just a plain product shot on white. Suggest the setting, mood, and what the scene should convey about using the product. Showing a product in context makes it more desirable. Give me the lifestyle scene concept for my product mockup.
description: Plan an aspirational lifestyle context that shows your product in use.
useCase: Use this to make a product desirable by showing it in a real appealing context. A lifestyle scene that connects the product to the life or outcome your audience wants is more persuasive than a plain product shot, since people buy the result and the feeling, and seeing the product in an aspirational context helps them imagine owning it.

title: Plan an Ebook or Guide Cover Mockup
promptText: Help me plan a cover and mockup for my ebook or guide called [TITLE], which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT IT DELIVERS]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] and colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me plan two things: the cover design itself, meaning the layout, title treatment, and visual that makes it look professional and appealing, and how to mock it up as a 3D book, tablet, or stacked presentation so it looks like a real substantial product. The cover and mockup strongly affect perceived value. Give me the cover concept and the best mockup presentation for my ebook.
description: Plan an ebook cover and 3D mockup that make it look like a substantial product.
useCase: Use this for an ebook, guide, or lead magnet cover and presentation. A professional cover mocked up as a 3D book or on a device makes a digital document look like a substantial valuable product, strongly affecting perceived value, since a bare PDF feels worth little while a well presented one feels worth paying for or opting in to receive.

title: Plan a Device Mockup for Your Product
promptText: Help me plan a device mockup showing my product on a screen. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT, E.G. A COURSE, APP, WEBSITE, TEMPLATE, OR SOFTWARE], and I want to show it on [DEVICE TYPE, E.G. LAPTOP, PHONE, TABLET, OR MULTIPLE]. My audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE] and brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan: which device or combination best suits my product, what to show on the screen to convey the value, the setting around the device, and how to make it look professional and real. A device mockup makes digital products feel tangible. Give me the device mockup concept and what to display.
description: Plan a device mockup that shows your digital product on screen convincingly.
useCase: Use this to present a digital product on a device convincingly. A device mockup showing your course, app, or template on a screen makes it feel tangible and real, which helps digital products sell, since seeing the product displayed on a familiar device helps buyers understand and value what they are actually getting.
variables: YOUR PRODUCT, E.G. A COURSE, APP, WEBSITE, TEMPLATE, OR SOFTWARE
DEVICE TYPE, E.G. LAPTOP, PHONE, TABLET, OR MULTIPLE

title: Plan a Product Packaging Mockup
promptText: Help me plan a packaging mockup for my physical product. The product is [YOUR PRODUCT], the packaging type is [PACKAGING, E.G. BOX, POUCH, BOTTLE, LABEL], for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] with colors [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me plan how to present the packaging: the mockup style and angle that shows it off best, the setting, and what makes packaging look premium and appealing versus cheap. Also note what the packaging design itself should convey. The goal is a mockup that makes the product look professionally packaged and desirable. Give me the packaging mockup concept.
description: Plan a packaging mockup that makes a physical product look premium and desirable.
useCase: Use this to present physical product packaging appealingly. A packaging mockup shown at the right angle and setting makes a product look premium and professionally made, which strongly affects buyer perception, since packaging is often the first thing customers judge and a polished mockup signals quality before they ever hold the product.
variables: PACKAGING, E.G. BOX, POUCH, BOTTLE, LABEL

title: Plan App or Software Screenshot Mockups
promptText: Help me plan mockups of my app or software screenshots that show it off well. My product is [YOUR APP OR SOFTWARE], which helps [TARGET AUDIENCE] with [WHAT IT DOES]. Help me plan: which screens or features to showcase and in what order, how to present screenshots so they look polished such as in device frames or a clean layout, what to highlight about the value in each, and how to make the product look easy and appealing to use. The goal is screenshot mockups that make people understand and want the product. Give me a plan for which screens to show and how to present them.
description: Plan polished screenshot mockups that show off your app or software's value.
useCase: Use this to present app or software screenshots that sell the product. Choosing which screens to show and framing them in device mockups makes the product look polished and easy to use, which drives sign ups, since prospects deciding whether to try software judge heavily on how it looks and whether they can picture themselves using it.

title: Plan a Product Bundle Mockup
promptText: Help me plan a mockup showing my product bundle so it looks like great value. The bundle includes [WHAT IS IN THE BUNDLE], for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan a mockup that displays all the included items together attractively, makes the bundle feel abundant and worth the price, and shows the value of getting everything. Whether the items are digital or physical, note how to present them so the bundle looks substantial and appealing. Seeing everything included justifies the price. Give me the bundle mockup concept and how to arrange the items.
description: Plan a bundle mockup that displays everything included and looks like great value.
useCase: Use this to present a bundle so its value is visually obvious. A mockup showing all the included items together makes the bundle feel abundant and worth the price, since seeing everything laid out justifies the cost far better than a text list, helping buyers grasp the value of getting the whole package at once.

title: Generate a Product Visual with Gemini
promptText: Help me write a prompt to generate a product visual or mockup scene. The product is [DESCRIBE YOUR PRODUCT], the scene or setting is [DESIRED SETTING], the mood is [MOOD], and my brand colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Write a clear detailed image prompt describing the product, the scene, lighting, composition, and mood to produce an appealing professional product visual. Specify the aspect ratio for where I will use it. Give me the main prompt and a couple of variations for different settings. Remind me to verify the generated product looks accurate to what I actually sell and is suitable for commercial use.
description: Write a detailed prompt to generate a professional product visual or scene.
useCase: Use this to generate a product visual or lifestyle scene with an image generator. A detailed prompt specifying product, setting, and lighting produces appealing professional imagery without a photoshoot, useful for product visuals across your site and social, with a reminder to verify accuracy and commercial suitability of the generated result.

title: Plan Mockups for a Sales Page
promptText: Help me plan the product mockups I need for my sales page. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], the sales page is for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the key points I am selling are [YOUR KEY SELLING POINTS]. Help me plan which mockups the page needs and where: a hero mockup that makes a strong first impression, mockups that visually support each key benefit or feature, and any that show the product in use or its results. Explain how strategic mockups throughout a sales page increase conversions. Give me a plan for which mockups to place where on the sales page and what each should show.
description: Plan the strategic product mockups a sales page needs to drive conversions.
useCase: Use this to plan mockups that boost sales page conversions. Placing a strong hero mockup and visuals that support each benefit helps prospects understand and desire the product, since a sales page with strategic product imagery converts far better than one that describes the product in text alone without letting buyers see it.

title: Choose the Right Mockup Style
promptText: Help me choose the right style of mockup for my product and brand. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING], and my audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Walk me through the main mockup styles and which fits me: a clean minimal mockup on a plain background, a lifestyle scene showing the product in context, a flat lay from above, a device mockup, or a bold stylized presentation. For each, note when it works best and whether it suits my product and brand. Then recommend the style that best fits. Give me guidance and a recommendation on my mockup style.
description: Choose the mockup style that best fits your product, brand, and audience.
useCase: Use this to pick a mockup style deliberately rather than defaulting. Choosing between a clean minimal shot, a lifestyle scene, or a device mockup based on your product and brand ensures the presentation fits, since the right style makes a product look intentional and appealing while a mismatched one can make even a good product feel off.

title: Plan a Flat Lay Product Mockup
promptText: Help me plan a flat lay mockup, shot from above, for my product. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], and any complementary items or props that fit my brand are [PROPS OR RELATED ITEMS, OR WRITE NONE]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING] and colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me plan the flat lay: how to arrange the product and any props attractively, the background and color approach, the composition and spacing, and how to make it look styled and intentional rather than cluttered. Flat lays work well for social and lifestyle branding. Give me the flat lay concept and arrangement.
description: Plan a styled flat lay mockup arranged attractively from above.
useCase: Use this to create a styled flat lay, a popular format for social and lifestyle branding. A well arranged flat lay with thoughtful composition and props looks intentional and on brand, giving your product an aesthetic presentation that performs well on visual platforms, where styled overhead shots consistently draw engagement.
variables: PROPS OR RELATED ITEMS, OR WRITE NONE

title: Check a Mockup for Accuracy and Honesty
promptText: Help me make sure my product mockup accurately represents what customers will actually get. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], and my mockup shows [DESCRIBE THE MOCKUP]. Help me check: whether the mockup could set expectations that the real product does not meet, whether anything shown is misleading about size, contents, quality, or features, and how to make it appealing while staying honest. An impressive mockup that overpromises leads to disappointed customers and refunds. Flag anything that could mislead and how to keep it both attractive and accurate. Give me the honesty check and any adjustments.
description: Check that a product mockup is appealing without misleading customers.
useCase: Use this to ensure a mockup is honest as well as appealing. Checking that it does not overpromise on size, contents, or quality prevents the disappointed customers and refunds that follow when the real product does not match its mockup, protecting your reputation, since a misleading mockup wins a sale but loses trust and invites complaints.

title: Get Feedback on a Product Mockup
promptText: Help me evaluate my product mockup before I use it. The mockup shows [DESCRIBE YOUR MOCKUP IN DETAIL], for my product [YOUR PRODUCT] aimed at [TARGET AUDIENCE], with a brand feel of [BRAND FEELING]. Help me assess it honestly: whether it makes the product look appealing and desirable, whether it looks professional versus amateur, whether it clearly conveys what the product is, whether it fits my brand, and what specifically would make it stronger. Point out anything that looks off, cheap, or unclear. Give me honest feedback and specific improvements before I use this mockup.
description: Get honest feedback on a product mockup and specific improvements before using it.
useCase: Use this before using a mockup you are unsure about. Honest feedback on whether it looks professional, appealing, and clear catches the issues that would undercut the product's perceived value, letting you fix them first, since a mockup that looks cheap or unclear can lower how much people think your product is worth.

title: Choose How to Create Your Mockups
promptText: Help me figure out the best way to create product mockups for my situation. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], my design skill is [YOUR SKILL LEVEL], my budget is [YOUR BUDGET OR WRITE MINIMAL], and how often I need mockups is [HOW OFTEN]. Help me decide the approach: whether a mockup template tool, AI image generation, DIY photography, or hiring out fits my product and situation, and what to look for in each approach. Focus on the type of approach rather than assuming specific current products, since tools change. Give me a recommendation for how to create my product mockups efficiently.
description: Choose the best approach to create product mockups for your skill and budget.
useCase: Use this to find a practical way to make mockups that fits your situation. Choosing between a mockup tool, AI generation, DIY, or hiring out based on your product and budget lets you create professional visuals efficiently, which matters since good mockups significantly affect sales but should not require becoming a designer or spending heavily.

title: Plan a Clean Product Shot
promptText: Help me plan a clean simple product shot that shows my product clearly and professionally. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], and I need a clean image for [WHERE IT WILL BE USED, E.G. A LISTING, WEBSITE, OR CATALOG]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan a clean product presentation: the background and lighting approach, the angle that shows the product best, how to keep it simple and uncluttered so the product is the focus, and how to make it look professional. Sometimes a clean clear shot outperforms an elaborate scene. Give me the clean product shot plan.
description: Plan a clean, professional product shot where the product is the clear focus.
useCase: Use this when you need a clean clear product image rather than an elaborate scene. A simple professional shot with good lighting and the right angle shows the product clearly, which is exactly what listings and catalogs need, since sometimes a clean uncluttered image where the product is the focus converts better than a busy lifestyle scene.
variables: WHERE IT WILL BE USED, E.G. A LISTING, WEBSITE, OR CATALOG

title: Plan a Set of Product Mockups
promptText: Help me plan a full set of mockups I need for my product across all its uses. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], and I need images for [WHERE YOU NEED THEM, E.G. WEBSITE, SALES PAGE, SOCIAL, LISTINGS, ADS]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan the set: which types of mockups and shots I need for each use, how to get variety while keeping them cohesive, and an efficient way to produce the whole set. The goal is a complete consistent set of product images rather than scrambling for one at a time. Give me the mockup set plan and what to create for each use.
description: Plan a complete, cohesive set of product mockups for all your uses at once.
useCase: Use this to plan all your product images at once rather than one at a time. A complete cohesive set covering your website, sales page, social, and ads is more efficient to produce together and keeps your product looking consistent everywhere, avoiding the scramble of needing a specific mockup you do not have at the worst moment.
variables: WHERE YOU NEED THEM, E.G. WEBSITE, SALES PAGE, SOCIAL, LISTINGS, ADS

title: Plan a Mockup for an Online Course
promptText: Help me plan a mockup to visually represent my online course so it looks like a substantial valuable program. My course is [COURSE NAME], which teaches [TARGET AUDIENCE] to [OUTCOME], and it includes [WHAT IS INCLUDED, E.G. MODULES, VIDEOS, WORKBOOKS, BONUSES]. My brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Courses are intangible, so help me plan a mockup that makes it feel real and worth the price: showing it across devices, representing the modules or materials visually, and conveying the scope and value. Give me the course mockup concept and what to show to make it look substantial.
description: Plan a mockup that makes an intangible online course look substantial and valuable.
useCase: Use this to give an online course a visual presence that justifies its price. A mockup showing the course across devices and representing its modules and materials makes an intangible program feel real and substantial, which is crucial since courses often cost significant money and buyers hesitate to pay for something they cannot see or hold.
variables: WHAT IS INCLUDED, E.G. MODULES, VIDEOS, WORKBOOKS, BONUSES

title: Get a Quick Mockup Idea Fast
promptText: I need a quick product mockup idea. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and I need it for [WHERE IT WILL BE USED]. Give me a fast, clear mockup concept: how to present the product, the setting or style, and what to show, so I can create or generate it quickly. Keep it simple and ready to act on, no long explanation. Just give me a solid mockup idea I can create right now.
description: Get a fast, simple product mockup concept ready to create.
useCase: Use this when you need a mockup idea quickly. A fast clear concept for presenting your product lets you create or generate a visual without a long planning process, useful when you need a product image promptly for a post, listing, or page and just want a solid direction to act on immediately.

title: Integrate Your Branding into a Mockup
promptText: Help me integrate my branding into my product mockup so it looks cohesive with my brand. My mockup shows [DESCRIBE YOUR MOCKUP], and my brand elements are colors [YOUR COLORS], style [YOUR BRAND STYLE], and feel [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan how to weave my branding in: the background and color choices that match my brand, where my logo or brand elements should appear if at all, and how to make the mockup unmistakably part of my brand rather than generic. A branded mockup builds recognition. Give me how to integrate my branding into this mockup.
description: Integrate your branding into a mockup so it looks cohesive and builds recognition.
useCase: Use this to make product mockups reinforce your brand. Integrating your colors, style, and elements so the mockup is unmistakably yours builds recognition across everywhere the image appears, since a branded mockup strengthens your identity while a generic one, however polished, does nothing to make your product memorable as yours.

title: Plan a Mockup for a Physical Product
promptText: Help me plan a mockup for my physical product that shows it off well. My product is [YOUR PHYSICAL PRODUCT], its key appealing features are [WHAT MAKES IT APPEALING, E.G. MATERIAL, DESIGN, SIZE, DETAIL], for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and my brand feel is [BRAND FEELING]. Help me plan: the angles and shots that best showcase the product's appeal, how to highlight its quality and key features, the setting or background, and whether to show scale or the product in use. The goal is a mockup that makes the physical product look as good as it does in person. Give me the physical product mockup plan.
description: Plan a mockup that showcases a physical product's quality and key features.
useCase: Use this to present a physical product so it looks as good as it does in person. A mockup that showcases the right angles, highlights quality and features, and shows scale helps buyers appreciate the product, which matters for online selling where customers cannot touch the item and rely entirely on images to judge whether it is worth buying.
variables: WHAT MAKES IT APPEALING, E.G. MATERIAL, DESIGN, SIZE, DETAIL

title: Plan Product Mockups for Ads
promptText: Help me plan product mockups specifically for paid ads. My product is [YOUR PRODUCT], the ad is for [PLATFORM], targeting [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the main message or offer is [YOUR AD MESSAGE OR OFFER]. Help me plan a mockup that works in an ad: grabs attention fast in a feed, makes the product instantly appealing, supports the ad message, and drives the click. Note how ad mockups differ from regular product shots, needing to stop the scroll and sell quickly. Give me the ad mockup concept and what makes it work for advertising.
description: Plan attention grabbing product mockups built specifically for paid ads.
useCase: Use this to create product mockups for paid advertising, where the bar is different. An ad mockup must stop the scroll and sell fast to earn its cost, so one that grabs attention and drives the click makes your ad spend work harder, since a weak product image wastes budget while a compelling one lifts the entire campaign's return.

title: Generate Ad Creative Concepts
promptText: Generate ad creative concepts for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am advertising [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], and the main benefit or offer is [YOUR BENEFIT OR OFFER]. Generate several distinct ad creative concepts, each with a different angle such as the problem it solves, the desired outcome, social proof, curiosity, or a bold claim. For each concept, describe the visual idea, the headline or hook, and why that angle would work for my audience. Give me a range to test rather than one idea. Mark the two or three most promising concepts. Give me the ad creative concepts.
description: Generate a range of distinct ad creative concepts with different angles to test.
useCase: Use this when planning ads and needing strong creative directions. A range of concepts with different angles gives you options to test, which is essential since ad performance is hard to predict and testing several angles finds the winner far faster than betting everything on one idea you assume will work.

title: Generate Ad Hooks
promptText: Generate scroll stopping hooks for my ad. I am advertising [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], and the key message is [YOUR MESSAGE]. Give me 12 hook options, meaning the opening line or headline that stops the scroll and pulls people in. Vary them: some that call out the audience, some that name the pain, some that promise the outcome, some that spark curiosity, and some that challenge an assumption. Keep them short and punchy for an ad. Mark the strongest few. The hook determines whether the ad gets seen at all. Give me the ad hooks.
description: Generate scroll stopping ad hooks across multiple proven angles to test.
useCase: Use this because the hook decides whether an ad gets seen. Scroll stopping opening lines earn the attention everything else depends on, and testing varied hooks finds what grabs your audience, since even a great offer and visual fail if the hook does not stop people from scrolling past.

title: Write an Image Prompt for an Ad
promptText: Help me write an image generation prompt for an ad visual. The ad promotes [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE], the concept is [YOUR AD CONCEPT OR ANGLE], the mood is [MOOD], and my brand colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Write a clear image prompt describing a scroll stopping ad visual, its style, mood, composition, and colors, leaving space for headline text if I will add it. Specify the aspect ratio for [PLATFORM]. Give me the main prompt plus a couple of variations. Remind me the visual needs to stop the scroll and support the ad message, and to verify it is suitable and accurate for advertising.
description: Write an image prompt for a scroll stopping ad visual with room for text.
useCase: Use this to generate an attention grabbing ad visual. A clear prompt specifying the concept, mood, and aspect ratio, with space for text, produces a scroll stopping image that supports the message, giving your ads custom visuals that perform better than generic stock, which audiences increasingly recognize and scroll past.

title: Explore Different Ad Angles
promptText: Help me explore different angles to advertise my [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. The same product can be sold many ways, so help me brainstorm distinct advertising angles: leading with the problem, the outcome or dream result, fear of missing out, social proof, a surprising insight, a specific use case, price or value, or an us versus the old way angle. For each, explain how the ad would frame the product and which audience mindset it targets. Then recommend which angles are most worth testing for my offer. Give me the ad angles and a recommendation.
description: Explore distinct advertising angles for the same product and pick which to test.
useCase: Use this to find the angle that sells your product best. The same offer can be advertised many ways, and exploring distinct angles like problem, outcome, or social proof reveals which resonates, since often the difference between an ad that flops and one that converts is not the product or visual but the angle it leads with.

title: Write Ad Copy
promptText: Write ad copy for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am advertising [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. The main benefit is [KEY BENEFIT], the offer is [YOUR OFFER OR WRITE NONE], and the action I want is [DESIRED ACTION]. Write ad copy that hooks with a strong opening, speaks to what my audience wants or struggles with, conveys the benefit clearly, and drives the action with a clear call to action. Match the length and style to [PLATFORM]. Give me two or three versions with different tones or angles to test. Keep it compelling, not generic. Give me the ad copy.
description: Write compelling ad copy that hooks, conveys the benefit, and drives action.
useCase: Use this to write ad copy that converts. Copy that hooks fast, speaks to the audience's wants, and drives a clear action turns attention into clicks, and having a few versions to test finds the strongest, since ad copy is a major lever on performance and small wording changes can meaningfully shift results.

title: Plan a Video Ad Concept
promptText: Help me plan a short video ad for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am promoting [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], and the key message is [YOUR MESSAGE]. Help me plan the video concept: a hook in the first few seconds that stops the scroll, how the ad unfolds to hold attention and convey the value, the key beats or scenes, and a clear call to action at the end. Note that the first seconds are critical since most viewers drop off fast. Keep it achievable to produce simply. Give me the video ad concept and a rough shot or beat outline.
description: Plan a short video ad concept with a strong hook and clear beats.
useCase: Use this to plan a video ad that holds attention. A concept with a strong opening hook and clear beats keeps viewers watching, which matters because most drop off in the first seconds, and a video ad that fails to hook immediately wastes its budget no matter how good the rest of it is.

title: Design a Scroll Stopping Ad Visual
promptText: Help me design an ad visual that stops the scroll for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am promoting [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. Help me plan a visual built to grab attention in a busy feed: what makes people stop scrolling, how to use contrast, movement, faces, or pattern interrupts, how to make it stand out from typical polished ads without looking like spam, and how to support the message. Note the balance between attention grabbing and on brand. Give me the scroll stopping visual concept and what makes it stop the scroll.
description: Design an ad visual engineered to stop the scroll in a busy feed.
useCase: Use this to design an ad visual that earns attention in a crowded feed. Understanding what actually stops the scroll, like contrast, faces, or pattern interrupts, makes your ad get noticed, since the visual's only job in the first instant is to halt the scroll, and one that blends in never gets the chance to sell anything.

title: Plan a User Generated Style Ad
promptText: Help me plan a user generated content style ad for my [BUSINESS TYPE], the authentic looking kind that does not feel like a polished ad. I am promoting [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. Help me plan a natural, authentic feeling ad concept: how to make it feel like a genuine recommendation or real person sharing rather than a corporate ad, what to show and say, and the casual style that builds trust. This style often outperforms polished ads because it feels real. Note how to keep it honest. Give me the UGC style ad concept and script or talking points.
description: Plan an authentic user generated style ad that does not feel like an ad.
useCase: Use this to create ads that feel authentic rather than corporate. A user generated style ad that feels like a real recommendation builds trust and often outperforms polished production, since audiences increasingly tune out obvious ads but engage with content that feels genuine and human, making this style highly effective when done honestly.

title: Generate an Ad Visual with Gemini
promptText: Help me write a prompt to generate an ad visual. The ad promotes [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE], the concept is [YOUR CONCEPT], the mood is [MOOD], and my brand colors are [YOUR COLORS OR WRITE NONE]. Write a clear detailed image prompt describing an eye catching ad visual, its style, composition, mood, and colors, with space for headline text if needed. Specify the aspect ratio for [PLATFORM]. Give me the main prompt and a couple of variations to test different looks. Remind me to check the generated image is attention grabbing, on brand, accurate, and suitable for a paid ad.
description: Write a detailed prompt to generate an eye catching ad visual to test.
useCase: Use this to generate ad visuals to test. A detailed prompt describing the concept, mood, and aspect ratio produces attention grabbing imagery, and generating variations lets you test different looks cheaply, which suits advertising where testing multiple visuals is how you find the creative that actually performs rather than guessing.

title: Write and Optimize Ad Calls to Action
promptText: Help me write strong calls to action for my ad promoting [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM]. The action I want is [DESIRED ACTION, E.G. SHOP, LEARN MORE, SIGN UP, BOOK]. Give me several call to action options that clearly tell people what to do next and motivate the click, varying the urgency, framing, and wording. Note which suit a cold audience seeing me for the first time versus a warmer one. The CTA converts attention into action, so it needs to be clear and compelling. Give me the ad CTA options and when to use each.
description: Write clear, compelling ad calls to action matched to audience temperature.
useCase: Use this to convert ad attention into clicks. A clear compelling call to action tells people exactly what to do and motivates them, and matching it to a cold versus warm audience improves results, since a vague or weak CTA loses the action even after your hook and visual successfully earned the viewer's attention.
variables: DESIRED ACTION, E.G. SHOP, LEARN MORE, SIGN UP, BOOK

title: Create Ad Variations to Test
promptText: Help me create variations of my ad to test what works best. My base ad promotes [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], with this hook and message: [YOUR CURRENT AD]. Create variations that each change one element so I can learn what drives performance: one with a different hook, one with a different angle, one with a different visual concept, and one with a different call to action. For each, note what it is testing and what a win would tell me. Testing one variable at a time reveals what actually moves results. Give me the ad variations to test.
description: Create controlled ad variations that isolate one variable to test performance.
useCase: Use this to improve ads through testing rather than guessing. Variations that each change one element reveal what actually drives performance, which is how profitable ad accounts are built, since systematic testing beats intuition and isolating variables tells you exactly what to keep and what to change in the next round.

title: Diagnose an Underperforming Ad
promptText: Help me diagnose why my ad is not performing. The ad promotes [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], here is the ad: [DESCRIBE YOUR AD, HOOK, VISUAL, AND COPY], and the problem is [WHAT IS UNDERPERFORMING, E.G. LOW CLICKS, LOW ATTENTION, CLICKS BUT NO SALES]. Help me pinpoint the likely issue: whether the hook fails to stop the scroll, the visual is weak, the angle does not resonate, the copy is unclear, the CTA is weak, or there is a mismatch with the landing page. For the likely cause, suggest what to change. Give me a diagnosis and specific fixes.
description: Diagnose why an ad is underperforming and what specifically to fix.
useCase: Use this when an ad is spending but not delivering. Pinpointing whether the problem is the hook, visual, angle, copy, or CTA tells you what to fix rather than scrapping the whole ad, which saves budget and time, since ads usually fail at one specific point and fixing that is faster than starting over blindly.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR AD, HOOK, VISUAL, AND COPY
WHAT IS UNDERPERFORMING, E.G. LOW CLICKS, LOW ATTENTION, CLICKS BUT NO SALES

title: Write a Problem Agitate Solve Ad
promptText: Help me write an ad using the problem agitate solve structure for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My audience [TARGET AUDIENCE] struggles with [THE PROBLEM], and my [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] solves it by [HOW IT SOLVES IT]. Write an ad that names the problem so they feel understood, agitates it briefly by touching on the frustration or cost of it, then presents my solution as the relief, ending with a clear call to action. Keep the agitation honest and empathetic, not manipulative. This structure works because it meets people in their pain. Give me the problem agitate solve ad.
description: Write a problem agitate solve ad that meets the audience in their pain.
useCase: Use this to write an ad using a proven persuasive structure. Naming the problem, briefly agitating it, then presenting your solution meets people in their pain and makes your offer feel like relief, which converts well when done empathetically, since prospects who feel understood are far more receptive to the solution that follows.

title: Plan a Carousel Ad
promptText: Help me plan a carousel ad for [PLATFORM] promoting my [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My key selling points are [YOUR SELLING POINTS OR OFFER DETAILS]. Help me plan the carousel cards: a first card that hooks and earns the swipe, cards that each build the case with a benefit, feature, or proof point, a logical flow that leads toward the sale, and a final card with a strong call to action. Note what visual and text goes on each card. A carousel ad lets me tell a fuller story than a single image. Give me the carousel ad plan card by card.
description: Plan a carousel ad card by card that builds a case toward the sale.
useCase: Use this to plan a carousel ad, which tells a fuller story than a single image. Cards that hook then build the case with benefits and proof let you make a complete argument, useful for products that need more explanation, since the swipeable format holds attention while walking prospects from interest to the call to action.
variables: YOUR SELLING POINTS OR OFFER DETAILS

title: Plan a Retargeting Ad
promptText: Help me plan a retargeting ad for people who already engaged with my [BUSINESS TYPE] but did not buy. They previously [WHAT THEY DID, E.G. VISITED THE SITE, VIEWED THE PRODUCT, ABANDONED CART], and my offer is [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING]. Help me plan an ad tailored to a warm audience that already knows me: how to acknowledge they were interested, address the likely reason they did not act, add reassurance or an incentive, and bring them back to complete the action. Retargeting audiences convert well with the right nudge. Give me the retargeting ad concept and message.
description: Plan a retargeting ad that brings warm prospects back to complete the action.
useCase: Use this to convert warm prospects who engaged but did not buy. A retargeting ad tailored to people who already showed interest, addressing why they hesitated and adding a nudge, converts well, since these warm audiences are far closer to buying than cold ones and often just need a reminder or reassurance to complete what they started.
variables: WHAT THEY DID, E.G. VISITED THE SITE, VIEWED THE PRODUCT, ABANDONED CART

title: Plan an Ad Built on Social Proof
promptText: Help me plan an ad built around social proof for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My proof is [YOUR PROOF, E.G. TESTIMONIALS, RESULTS, NUMBER OF CUSTOMERS, REVIEWS], and I am promoting [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me plan an ad that puts the proof front and center: how to feature a testimonial or result as the hook, how to make the proof credible and relatable, and how to connect it to the action I want. Social proof reduces the skepticism that stops people buying from someone new. Keep it genuine. Give me the social proof ad concept and how to present the proof.
description: Plan an ad that leads with social proof to overcome buyer skepticism.
useCase: Use this to build an ad around proof, which overcomes the skepticism prospects feel toward an unfamiliar business. Leading with a genuine testimonial or result makes the ad credible and relatable, since people trust the experiences of others far more than a business's own claims, making social proof one of the most persuasive ad angles.
variables: YOUR PROOF, E.G. TESTIMONIALS, RESULTS, NUMBER OF CUSTOMERS, REVIEWS

title: Match Your Ad to Your Audience
promptText: Help me make sure my ad actually resonates with the specific audience I am targeting. My ad promotes [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING], here is the ad concept: [DESCRIBE YOUR AD], and my target audience is [TARGET AUDIENCE AND WHAT YOU KNOW ABOUT THEM]. Help me assess: whether the hook, angle, visual, and language actually fit how this audience thinks and what they care about, where it might miss or feel generic, and how to sharpen it to speak directly to them. An ad that feels made for the viewer outperforms a generic one. Give me the audience match check and how to tailor the ad more tightly.
description: Check whether your ad truly resonates with your specific target audience.
useCase: Use this to ensure an ad speaks directly to its audience rather than generically. Checking whether the hook, angle, and language fit how your audience thinks, and tightening it to them, lifts performance, since an ad that feels made specifically for the viewer resonates far more than a generic one trying to appeal to everyone.
variables: TARGET AUDIENCE AND WHAT YOU KNOW ABOUT THEM

title: Balance Visual and Text in an Ad
promptText: Help me get the balance of visual and text right in my ad creative. My ad promotes [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] on [PLATFORM], and my concept is [YOUR CONCEPT]. Help me decide: how much text should be on the visual itself versus in the caption or copy, what the essential words on the image should be, how to keep the visual clean and attention grabbing rather than cluttered with text, and how the on image text and the caption should work together. Too much text on an ad image hurts both attention and reach. Give me guidance on the visual and text balance for my ad.
description: Balance on image text and caption so your ad stays clean and attention grabbing.
useCase: Use this to get the text balance right on an ad. Deciding what belongs on the image versus in the caption keeps the visual clean and attention grabbing, which matters because too much text on an ad image hurts both attention and reach on many platforms, while the right minimal on image text lands the message fast.

title: Get a Quick Ad Concept Fast
promptText: I need a quick ad concept. My [BUSINESS TYPE] is promoting [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], and the key benefit is [KEY BENEFIT]. Give me a fast, clear ad concept: the hook, the visual idea, and the call to action, ready to create or test. Keep it simple and actionable, no long explanation. Just give me a solid ad concept I can use right now.
description: Get a fast, complete ad concept with hook, visual, and CTA ready to test.
useCase: Use this when you need an ad concept quickly. A fast complete concept with hook, visual, and CTA lets you launch or test without a long process, useful for capitalizing on a timely moment or getting a test running, since a solid concept acted on quickly often beats a perfect one that takes too long to develop.

title: Frame Your Offer in an Ad
promptText: Help me frame my offer compellingly in an ad. My offer is [YOUR OFFER AND ITS DETAILS], for [TARGET AUDIENCE], and the real value is [THE VALUE OR OUTCOME]. Help me frame it in the ad for maximum appeal: how to present the offer so the value feels obvious, whether to lead with the price, the saving, the outcome, or the transformation, how to add urgency or reason to act now if genuine, and how to make it feel like a clear yes. The same offer can be framed weakly or powerfully. Give me the strongest way to frame my offer in the ad and the wording.
description: Frame your offer in an ad so its value feels obvious and compelling.
useCase: Use this to present an offer so it feels like an obvious yes. Framing it around the right element, whether outcome, saving, or transformation, and adding genuine urgency makes the value clear, since the same offer can be framed weakly or powerfully, and how you present it in the ad heavily influences whether people act.

title: Storyboard a Video Ad
promptText: Help me storyboard my video ad so I know exactly what to film or create. My video ad promotes [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM], and the concept is [YOUR CONCEPT]. Help me break it into a shot by shot storyboard: the opening hook shot, each following scene or shot with what it shows and any text or voiceover, the pacing, and the closing call to action shot. Keep it simple enough to produce with basic tools or a phone. A storyboard makes filming efficient and the ad coherent. Give me the shot by shot storyboard for my video ad.
description: Storyboard a video ad shot by shot so it is efficient to film and coherent.
useCase: Use this to plan a video ad before filming. A shot by shot storyboard covering the hook, scenes, and CTA makes production efficient and the ad coherent, saving time and frustration, since filming without a plan leads to wasted effort and a disjointed result, while a clear storyboard means you know exactly what to capture.

title: Check an Ad for Policy and Claim Issues
promptText: Help me check my ad for issues that could get it rejected or cause problems. My ad promotes [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] on [PLATFORM], and here is the ad: [DESCRIBE YOUR AD AND COPY]. Help me spot potential issues: claims that are too strong or unsubstantiated, anything that could violate common ad platform policies, misleading framing, or wording that promises results I cannot guarantee. Suggest how to keep the ad compelling while making the claims honest and defensible. Note this is general guidance, not legal or platform specific advice. Give me the check and any wording to fix.
description: Check an ad for overreaching claims and policy issues before running it.
useCase: Use this before running an ad to catch claim and policy problems. Spotting overreaching claims or misleading framing helps you avoid rejections and the trust damage of overpromising, keeping the ad both compelling and defensible, since exaggerated claims risk platform rejection and the disappointed customers and complaints that follow when reality falls short.

title: Turn a Winning Post into an Ad
promptText: Help me turn a post that performed well organically into a paid ad for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The post was about [POST TOPIC] and did well because [WHY YOU THINK IT WORKED, OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Here is the post: [DESCRIBE OR PASTE THE POST]. I want to promote [WHAT YOU ARE PROMOTING] to [TARGET AUDIENCE]. Help me adapt it into an ad: what made it resonate that I should keep, how to add a clear offer and call to action without killing what worked, and how to frame it for a paid audience that does not already follow me. Proven organic content often makes the best ads. Give me the ad adaptation.
description: Adapt a well performing organic post into a paid ad that keeps what worked.
useCase: Use this to turn proven organic content into ads. A post that already resonated is validated creative, and adapting it into an ad while keeping what worked and adding an offer often outperforms concepts built from scratch, since real audience response is the best signal of what will convert when you put budget behind it.
variables: WHY YOU THINK IT WORKED, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Write a Professional Invoice
promptText: Help me write a clear professional invoice for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the work or products are [WHAT YOU DELIVERED], the amount is [AMOUNT], and my payment terms are [PAYMENT TERMS, E.G. DUE IN 14 DAYS]. Help me lay out an invoice that includes everything it needs: a clear description of what they are paying for, the amount and any breakdown, the due date, how to pay, my details, and an invoice number. Keep it professional and easy to understand so there is no confusion or reason to delay payment. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the invoice content and layout.
description: Write a clear, professional invoice that leaves no confusion about what is owed.
useCase: Use this to send invoices that get paid without back and forth. A clear professional invoice with an obvious amount, due date, and payment method removes the confusion and friction that delay payment, which matters for a solo operator whose cash flow depends on getting paid promptly and looking organized to clients.
variables: PAYMENT TERMS, E.G. DUE IN 14 DAYS

title: Write an Invoice Email
promptText: Write a friendly professional email to send with an invoice to my client. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the invoice is for [WHAT IT IS FOR], the amount is [AMOUNT], and it is due [DUE DATE]. Write an email that thanks them, clearly notes the invoice is attached with the amount and due date, states how to pay, and keeps a warm professional tone that makes paying feel easy and expected. Keep it brief. The email should reduce any friction to prompt payment while maintaining the relationship. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the subject line and the email.
description: Write a warm invoice email that makes prompt payment feel easy and expected.
useCase: Use this when sending an invoice, since the email framing affects how fast you get paid. A warm professional note that clearly states the amount, due date, and how to pay reduces friction and sets the expectation of prompt payment, while keeping the client relationship positive rather than transactional.

title: Write a Gentle Payment Reminder
promptText: Write a gentle payment reminder for an invoice that is due soon or just past due. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the invoice is for [WHAT IT IS FOR], the amount is [AMOUNT], and it was due [DUE DATE OR HOW OVERDUE]. Write a friendly, low pressure reminder that assumes good faith, such as it simply slipping their mind, restates the invoice details and how to pay, and makes it easy to resolve without any awkwardness. Keep it warm and professional so it protects the relationship. This is an early reminder, not a demand. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the subject line and the reminder.
description: Write a friendly, good faith payment reminder for a recently due invoice.
useCase: Use this for a first reminder on a due or slightly late invoice. A gentle good faith nudge that assumes the client simply forgot gets most invoices paid without damaging the relationship, since many late payments are honest oversights and a warm reminder resolves them while keeping the client comfortable.

title: Write a Firm Payment Reminder
promptText: Write a firm but still professional payment reminder for an invoice that is now clearly overdue after earlier gentle reminders. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the invoice is for [WHAT IT IS FOR], the amount is [AMOUNT], and it is now [HOW OVERDUE]. Write a reminder that is more direct and firm than a gentle nudge, clearly states the invoice is overdue and needs to be paid, notes any late terms or next steps if relevant, and requests prompt payment, while remaining professional rather than hostile. Firmness is warranted now, but keep the door open. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the subject line and the reminder.
description: Write a firm but professional reminder for a clearly overdue invoice.
useCase: Use this when gentle reminders have not worked and an invoice is meaningfully overdue. A firm professional message that clearly states the invoice must be paid signals you are serious without burning the relationship, which is often what finally prompts payment from a client who has been letting it slide.

title: Build a Payment Reminder Sequence
promptText: Help me build a sequence of payment reminders that escalates appropriately for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My payment terms are [YOUR TERMS], and I want a repeatable set of reminders to send at the right intervals. Build the sequence: a friendly reminder just before or at the due date, a gentle nudge shortly after, a firmer reminder further out, and a final notice, each with suggested timing and the tone shift. Give me the messages I can reuse for any client by filling in the details. A consistent reminder system means I get paid without agonizing over each message. Give me the full reminder sequence with timing.
description: Build a reusable, escalating payment reminder sequence with the right timing.
useCase: Use this once to systematize chasing payment so you never dread it. A ready sequence that escalates from friendly to firm at set intervals means you follow up consistently on every late invoice without writing each message from scratch, which is how a solo operator keeps cash flowing without letting invoices slip.

title: Write a Final Payment Notice
promptText: Write a final payment notice for a seriously overdue invoice where earlier reminders have failed. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the invoice is for [WHAT IT IS FOR], the amount is [AMOUNT], and it is now [HOW OVERDUE]. Write a serious, professional final notice that clearly states this is a final request before I take further steps such as [YOUR NEXT STEP, E.G. PAUSING WORK, LATE FEES, OR OTHER ACTION], gives a clear deadline, and remains firm and factual rather than emotional or threatening. This is a last attempt to resolve it directly. Note this is general guidance, not legal advice. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the subject line and the final notice.
description: Write a serious, professional final notice for a seriously overdue invoice.
useCase: Use this as a last direct attempt before escalating a badly overdue invoice. A firm factual final notice with a clear deadline and stated next step often prompts payment when softer reminders failed, and keeping it professional rather than emotional protects you while making clear you will not simply absorb the loss.
variables: YOUR NEXT STEP, E.G. PAUSING WORK, LATE FEES, OR OTHER ACTION

title: Set Clear Payment Terms
promptText: Help me set clear payment terms for my [BUSINESS TYPE] serving [TARGET AUDIENCE]. My work is [WHAT YOU DO], and my current terms are [YOUR TERMS OR WRITE NONE]. Help me define terms that protect my cash flow and reduce late payments: when payment is due, whether to require deposits or upfront payment, whether to charge late fees, accepted payment methods, and how to communicate the terms upfront so they are agreed before work starts. Explain how each choice affects getting paid on time. Note this is general guidance, not legal or accounting advice. Give me recommended payment terms and how to state them.
description: Set clear payment terms that protect cash flow and reduce late payments.
useCase: Use this to establish terms that get you paid reliably. Clear terms agreed upfront, covering due dates, deposits, and late fees, prevent the payment problems that come from vague arrangements, since most late payment issues trace back to terms that were never clearly set or communicated before the work began.

title: Request a Deposit or Upfront Payment
promptText: Write a message requesting a deposit or upfront payment before I begin work for a client. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the project is [THE PROJECT], the total is [TOTAL AMOUNT], and the deposit I want is [DEPOSIT AMOUNT OR PERCENTAGE]. Write a message that requests the deposit confidently and professionally, frames it as standard practice that protects both sides, explains what happens once it is paid, and makes it easy to pay. Requesting a deposit should feel normal, not awkward. Keep it warm but clear. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the deposit request message.
description: Confidently request a deposit or upfront payment as standard practice.
useCase: Use this to request deposits without awkwardness. Framing a deposit as standard practice that protects both sides makes it feel normal and secures payment before you invest work, which protects your cash flow and filters out clients who were never serious, a key safeguard for a solo operator.

title: Write a Late Fee Policy
promptText: Help me write a clear late fee policy for my [BUSINESS TYPE] to encourage on time payment. My payment terms are [YOUR TERMS], and the late fee I am considering is [YOUR LATE FEE OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Help me write a policy that states when a payment is considered late, what the late fee is and how it applies, and how it is communicated to clients upfront so it is fair and expected. Explain how to enforce it without damaging good client relationships, and when to waive it. Note this is general guidance, not legal advice, and late fee rules can vary by location. Give me the late fee policy and how to communicate it.
description: Write a fair late fee policy that encourages on time payment.
useCase: Use this to add a late fee that motivates prompt payment. A clear policy communicated upfront gives clients a real incentive to pay on time and compensates you when they do not, and knowing when to enforce versus waive it lets you protect cash flow without souring relationships with otherwise good clients.
variables: YOUR LATE FEE OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Build a Reusable Invoice Template
promptText: Help me build a reusable invoice template for my [BUSINESS TYPE] so I can invoice quickly and consistently. My work is [WHAT YOU INVOICE FOR], and my payment terms are [YOUR TERMS]. Create a clean invoice template with all the standard fields I need and fill in the blank spots for what changes each time, such as the client, description, amount, and dates. Include everything a proper invoice should have so I never leave out something important. The goal is a professional template I can fill in fast for any client. Give me the reusable invoice template.
description: Build a reusable invoice template you can fill in fast for any client.
useCase: Use this once to make invoicing fast and consistent. A reusable template with all the standard fields means you invoice in minutes without forgetting anything important, which keeps your billing professional and prompt, since a solo operator who dreads making invoices tends to delay them and delay getting paid.

title: Set Up Recurring Invoices
promptText: Help me set up recurring invoices for clients on retainer or subscription in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The recurring arrangement is [DESCRIBE IT, E.G. MONTHLY RETAINER, SUBSCRIPTION], the amount is [AMOUNT], and the cadence is [HOW OFTEN]. Help me plan: how to handle recurring billing consistently, what the recurring invoice should say, how to communicate the recurring charge clearly so there are no surprises, and how to keep the process reliable so I never forget to bill. Note approaches rather than assuming specific tools. Give me a plan and the recurring invoice content to bill retainer clients smoothly.
description: Set up reliable recurring invoices for retainer and subscription clients.
useCase: Use this to bill recurring clients consistently without forgetting. A reliable recurring invoice process ensures retainer and subscription income arrives on schedule, which protects the predictable revenue that stabilizes a solo business, since forgetting to bill or billing late on recurring work quietly costs money and looks unprofessional.
variables: DESCRIBE IT, E.G. MONTHLY RETAINER, SUBSCRIPTION

title: Have a Conversation About Late Payment
promptText: Help me handle a direct conversation with a client about a seriously late payment, whether by phone or a personal message. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION AND HOW OVERDUE], and my relationship with them is [YOUR RELATIONSHIP]. Help me prepare: how to open the conversation without hostility, how to firmly ask for payment while staying professional, how to respond if they give excuses or ask for more time, and how to reach a resolution such as a payment plan if needed. Help me get paid while handling it maturely. My tone is [TONE]. Give me talking points for the conversation.
description: Prepare to handle a direct late payment conversation firmly and professionally.
useCase: Use this when a late payment needs a real conversation, not just another email. Preparing how to firmly ask for payment, handle excuses, and reach a resolution helps you recover what you are owed while staying professional, which matters when reminders have failed and a direct honest conversation is the path to actually getting paid.
variables: DESCRIBE THE SITUATION AND HOW OVERDUE

title: Propose a Payment Plan
promptText: Help me propose a payment plan to a client who owes me but cannot pay the full amount at once. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the amount owed is [AMOUNT], and their situation is [THEIR SITUATION OR WHAT THEY SAID]. Write a message that offers a reasonable payment plan to recover what I am owed while working with their situation: proposing installments and timing, keeping it clear and firm about the commitment, and making it easy for them to agree and follow through. Getting paid over time beats not getting paid at all. Note this is general guidance, not legal advice. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the payment plan proposal.
description: Propose a reasonable payment plan to recover what you are owed over time.
useCase: Use this when a client genuinely cannot pay in full but wants to resolve the debt. A clear reasonable payment plan recovers your money over time, which beats writing it off, and offering a workable structure often turns a stuck unpaid invoice into a resolved one while preserving some goodwill with a client hitting a rough patch.
variables: THEIR SITUATION OR WHAT THEY SAID

title: Respond to an Invoice Dispute
promptText: Help me respond to a client disputing my invoice. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the invoice is for [WHAT IT IS FOR], the amount is [AMOUNT], and their dispute is [WHAT THEY ARE DISPUTING OR OBJECTING TO]. Here is the actual situation: [THE FACTS FROM YOUR SIDE]. Write a calm professional response that addresses their concern factually, references what was agreed, holds firm on legitimate charges while being fair, and works toward resolving it so I get paid. Avoid getting defensive or emotional. Note this is general guidance, not legal advice. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the response to the invoice dispute.
description: Respond calmly and factually to a client disputing your invoice.
useCase: Use this when a client pushes back on an invoice. A calm factual response that references what was agreed and holds firm on legitimate charges resolves disputes while getting you paid, since disputes handled professionally rather than defensively usually settle in your favor when the work and terms are clear.
variables: WHAT THEY ARE DISPUTING OR OBJECTING TO

title: Write a Payment Confirmation and Thank You
promptText: Write a short message confirming I received a client's payment and thanking them. The client is [CLIENT NAME], and the payment was for [WHAT IT WAS FOR]. Write a brief warm message that confirms the payment was received, thanks them genuinely, and where natural reinforces the good working relationship or notes what comes next. A quick payment confirmation is professional and closes the loop nicely. Keep it short and appreciative. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the payment confirmation message.
description: Write a warm message confirming payment received and thanking the client.
useCase: Use this to close the loop after a client pays. A quick warm confirmation and thank you looks professional, reassures the client the payment landed, and strengthens the relationship, a small courtesy most solo operators skip that leaves clients feeling valued and more likely to pay promptly next time.

title: Turn an Estimate into an Invoice
promptText: Help me turn my estimate or quote into a proper invoice now that the work is done.
