Here is the original estimate: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE ESTIMATE], and what actually happened versus the estimate is [ANY CHANGES OR EXTRAS, OR WRITE MATCHED THE ESTIMATE]. Help me create the final invoice that reflects the completed work, clearly accounts for any changes or extras with explanation, matches what was agreed, and leaves no confusion about the final amount. If there are extras beyond the estimate, help me present them so they are understood and accepted. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the final invoice content.
description: Turn an estimate into a clear final invoice that accounts for any changes.
useCase: Use this to invoice accurately after quoting a job. A final invoice that clearly reflects the completed work and explains any extras beyond the estimate prevents payment disputes, since unexplained differences between a quote and the final bill are a common source of client pushback that clear presentation avoids.
variables: ANY CHANGES OR EXTRAS, OR WRITE MATCHED THE ESTIMATE

title: Generate a Quick Invoice Fast
promptText: I need to create an invoice quickly. My business is [BUSINESS NAME], the client is [CLIENT NAME], the work is [WHAT YOU DID], the amount is [AMOUNT], and it is due [DUE DATE]. Give me a clean, complete invoice with all the standard details, ready to send with minimal editing. Keep it professional and clear. Just give me a solid invoice I can use right now.
description: Generate a clean, complete, ready to send invoice quickly.
useCase: Use this when you need to invoice fast and just want it done. A quick complete invoice lets you bill promptly without a lengthy process, which helps a solo operator get paid sooner, since the faster an invoice goes out after work is finished, the faster the payment tends to come back.

title: Plan How to Track Unpaid Invoices
promptText: Help me set up a simple system to track my unpaid and overdue invoices so none slip through the cracks in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Right now I track invoices by [HOW YOU CURRENTLY TRACK, OR WRITE NOT AT ALL]. Help me plan a lightweight system: what to track for each invoice such as who owes what and when it is due, how to see at a glance what is outstanding and overdue, when to trigger reminders, and how to keep it current without much effort. The goal is to always know who owes me money and follow up on time. Give me a simple invoice tracking system.
description: Set up a simple system to track unpaid and overdue invoices reliably.
useCase: Use this so unpaid invoices never get forgotten. A lightweight tracking system shows at a glance who owes you and what is overdue, triggering timely follow ups, which directly protects cash flow, since a solo operator juggling many things can easily lose track of an unpaid invoice and simply never chase money they are owed.
variables: HOW YOU CURRENTLY TRACK, OR WRITE NOT AT ALL

title: Clarify Confusing Invoice Wording
promptText: Help me make my invoice wording clearer so clients understand exactly what they are paying for. Here is my current invoice description or line items: [PASTE YOUR INVOICE WORDING]. Help me rewrite the descriptions so they are clear and specific about what was delivered, easy for the client to understand, and leave no ambiguity that could cause questions or delay payment. Vague invoice descriptions cause confusion and slow payment. Keep it professional and precise. Give me the clearer invoice wording.
description: Rewrite vague invoice wording so clients understand exactly what they owe for.
useCase: Use this when invoice descriptions are too vague and cause client questions. Clear specific wording about what was delivered removes the confusion that delays payment, since a client who does not understand a line item often sets the invoice aside to ask about it later, and clarity upfront gets you paid faster.

title: Plan How to Prevent Late Payments
promptText: Help me reduce late payments in my [BUSINESS TYPE] by fixing the causes rather than just chasing overdue invoices. My clients are [TARGET AUDIENCE], my current payment process is [DESCRIBE HOW YOU INVOICE AND GET PAID], and the late payment issues I have are [YOUR ISSUES]. Help me identify why payments come late and how to prevent it: clearer terms upfront, deposits, easier payment methods, better invoice timing, or reminders before the due date. Give me the highest impact changes to get paid on time more consistently. Prevention beats chasing. Give me a plan to prevent late payments.
description: Plan the changes that prevent late payments rather than just chasing them.
useCase: Use this to fix chronic late payment at its source. Identifying why payments come late and addressing it through clearer terms, deposits, or easier payment reduces the problem far more than endlessly chasing overdue invoices, freeing a solo operator from the constant stress and cash flow strain of getting paid late.
variables: DESCRIBE HOW YOU INVOICE AND GET PAID

title: Communicate Payment Method Options
promptText: Help me clearly communicate the ways clients can pay me to make paying as easy as possible. My accepted payment methods are [YOUR PAYMENT METHODS], for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Help me write clear payment instructions I can include on invoices and in emails: how to pay by each method, any details they need, and which is easiest or preferred, phrased so paying is simple and frictionless. The easier I make it to pay, the faster I get paid. Keep it clear and concise. Give me the payment options wording for my invoices and emails.
description: Communicate payment options clearly so paying you is simple and fast.
useCase: Use this to remove payment friction. Clear instructions on how to pay by each method make it effortless for clients to pay, which speeds up payment, since even a willing client delays when the process is unclear, and reducing that friction is one of the simplest ways to get paid faster.

title: Write a Quick Payment Nudge Text
promptText: Write a short, friendly text or message to nudge a client about an invoice, for a client I have a casual relationship with. The client is [CLIENT NAME], the invoice is for [WHAT IT IS FOR], the amount is [AMOUNT], and it is [DUE OR HOW OVERDUE]. Write a brief warm text that reminds them about the invoice in a low key, friendly way that fits a casual relationship and makes it easy to pay, without feeling formal or heavy. Keep it short and human. Give me a couple of quick text versions to choose from.
description: Write a short, friendly text to nudge a casual client about an invoice.
useCase: Use this for a quick payment nudge to a client you have a casual relationship with. A brief friendly text fits the relationship and often gets a faster response than a formal email, since for informal client relationships a light human reminder feels natural and prompts payment without the stiffness of a formal notice.

title: Build a Simple Business Budget
promptText: Help me build a simple budget for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My average monthly revenue is [YOUR REVENUE OR RANGE], my known regular expenses are [YOUR EXPENSES], and my financial goal is [YOUR GOAL, E.G. PROFIT TARGET, SAVING, PAYING MYSELF MORE]. Help me build a straightforward budget that lays out expected income, allocates money to expense categories, sets aside for taxes and savings, and shows what is left as profit or to reinvest. Keep it realistic and simple to maintain for a solo operator, not a complex corporate budget. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a simple business budget and how to use it.
description: Build a simple, realistic business budget a solo operator can actually maintain.
useCase: Use this to get control of your business finances with a real budget. A simple budget that allocates income to expenses, taxes, savings, and profit gives you a plan to run the business by, replacing the reactive guesswork of a solo operator who tracks money loosely with a clear picture of what should go where.
variables: YOUR GOAL, E.G. PROFIT TARGET, SAVING, PAYING MYSELF MORE

title: Decide How to Allocate Your Budget
promptText: Help me decide how to allocate my business budget across categories in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My monthly income to work with is [YOUR INCOME], and the areas I spend on or want to invest in are [YOUR CATEGORIES, E.G. TOOLS, MARKETING, CONTRACTORS, TAXES, SAVINGS, YOUR PAY]. Help me think through sensible allocations: rough percentages or amounts for each area, what to prioritize, how much to hold for taxes and a buffer, and how to balance reinvesting in growth versus paying myself. Explain the reasoning so I can adjust. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a suggested budget allocation and the thinking behind it.
description: Decide sensible budget allocations across your spending and investment areas.
useCase: Use this when you have income but no plan for dividing it. Thoughtful allocation across expenses, taxes, savings, growth, and your own pay ensures money goes where it should, preventing the common solo operator problem of spending reactively and reaching month end unsure whether anything was left for profit or taxes.
variables: YOUR CATEGORIES, E.G. TOOLS, MARKETING, CONTRACTORS, TAXES, SAVINGS, YOUR PAY

title: Plan a Profit First Style Budget
promptText: Help me set up a budget that ensures I actually take a profit rather than spending everything, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My revenue is [YOUR REVENUE], and right now my profit tends to be [WHAT IS LEFT OVER, OR WRITE LITTLE OR NOTHING]. Help me plan an approach where I allocate money to profit, my own pay, taxes, and operating expenses in a deliberate order so profit is protected first rather than being whatever is left. Suggest a sensible starting split and how to adjust it. Note this is general guidance inspired by common budgeting approaches, not financial advice. Give me a profit protecting budget plan.
description: Plan a budget that protects profit first instead of leaving it to chance.
useCase: Use this if your business earns but never seems to leave a profit. Allocating to profit, pay, and taxes deliberately before operating expenses ensures you actually keep money, addressing the trap where a solo operator lets expenses expand to consume all revenue and ends up working hard for little real profit.
variables: WHAT IS LEFT OVER, OR WRITE LITTLE OR NOTHING

title: Track Budget Against Actual Spending
promptText: Help me compare my actual results against my budget for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My budget was [YOUR BUDGET BY CATEGORY], and my actual income and spending were [YOUR ACTUALS]. Help me see clearly where I came in over or under on income and each expense category, the overall variance, what drove the differences, and what it means. Then tell me what to adjust in my spending or my budget going forward. The goal is to learn from the gap between plan and reality and keep improving. Give me the budget versus actual analysis and recommended adjustments.
description: Compare budget against actual results to learn and adjust going forward.
useCase: Use this to make your budget a living tool rather than a one time exercise. Comparing plan against reality reveals where you overspend or underearn and why, so you can adjust, since a budget only helps if you check actuals against it, and this feedback loop is what steadily improves a solo operator's financial control.

title: Budget for Starting a Business
promptText: Help me budget the costs of starting my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My business idea is [YOUR IDEA], and what I think I will need to get going is [WHAT YOU EXPECT TO NEED, OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Help me think through the realistic startup costs: the one time setup costs, the initial tools and services, any inventory or equipment, and the early running costs before revenue comes in. Flag costs people commonly forget, and separate the true essentials from things I can delay. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a realistic startup budget and what I actually need to begin.
description: Budget realistic startup costs and separate essentials from what can wait.
useCase: Use this when starting out and unsure what it will really cost. A realistic startup budget that flags forgotten costs and separates essentials from delayable ones prevents both underfunding and overspending before revenue arrives, helping a new solo operator start lean and avoid the cash crunch that sinks many businesses early.
variables: WHAT YOU EXPECT TO NEED, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Plan Your Marketing Budget
promptText: Help me plan a marketing budget for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My monthly revenue is [YOUR REVENUE], my growth goal is [YOUR GOAL], and the marketing I do or want to do is [YOUR MARKETING ACTIVITIES]. Help me decide how much to allocate to marketing and how to split it across activities: what portion of revenue is reasonable to spend, how to balance paid versus free efforts, how to budget in a way I can sustain, and how to track whether the spending is working. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a marketing budget plan and how to allocate and monitor it.
description: Plan a sustainable marketing budget and how to allocate and track it.
useCase: Use this to spend on marketing deliberately rather than randomly. A planned budget with a reasonable portion of revenue and a sensible split across activities makes marketing sustainable and measurable, preventing both the underspending that stalls growth and the overspending on unproven channels that can drain a solo business.

title: Plan How Much to Pay Yourself
promptText: Help me figure out how much to pay myself from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My monthly revenue is [YOUR REVENUE], my business expenses are [YOUR EXPENSES], and my personal financial needs are [WHAT YOU NEED TO LIVE ON]. Help me think through a sustainable owner pay: what the business can support after expenses, taxes, and a buffer, how to pay myself consistently rather than erratically, and how to balance taking pay now versus reinvesting. Be realistic about both my needs and the business's health. Note this is general guidance, not financial or tax advice. Give me a plan for paying myself sustainably.
description: Plan a sustainable, consistent amount to pay yourself from the business.
useCase: Use this to pay yourself in a planned way rather than taking whatever is around. Determining what the business can sustainably support after expenses, taxes, and buffer lets you pay yourself consistently, addressing a core struggle for solo operators who either underpay themselves or take money erratically in ways that destabilize the business.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED TO LIVE ON

title: Plan a Lean Bootstrap Budget
promptText: Help me plan a lean budget to run my [BUSINESS TYPE] on as little as possible while I build it up. My situation is [YOUR SITUATION, E.G. LOW REVENUE, BOOTSTRAPPING, TIGHT ON CASH], and my current or planned expenses are [YOUR EXPENSES]. Help me strip the budget to essentials: what I truly need to operate, what I can cut, delay, or find free or cheaper alternatives for, and how to keep my costs minimal without crippling the business. The goal is maximum runway on minimum spend. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a lean budget and where to trim to the essentials.
description: Plan a lean bootstrap budget that runs the business on minimum spend.
useCase: Use this when cash is tight and you need to extend your runway. A lean budget stripped to essentials, with cheaper alternatives for the rest, minimizes your burn rate while keeping the business functional, which is how a bootstrapping solo operator survives long enough to reach profitability without running out of money.
variables: YOUR SITUATION, E.G. LOW REVENUE, BOOTSTRAPPING, TIGHT ON CASH

title: Plan Your Annual Budget
promptText: Help me plan an annual budget for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My expected revenue for the year is [YOUR REVENUE EXPECTATION], my known recurring costs are [YOUR RECURRING COSTS], and my goals or planned investments for the year are [YOUR GOALS OR PLANNED SPENDING]. Help me lay out a yearly budget: projected income and expenses across the year, allowance for taxes and savings, room for planned investments and unexpected costs, and target profit. Note any seasonal variation I should plan for. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me an annual budget plan and how to break it into a monthly guide.
description: Plan an annual budget with room for taxes, investments, and seasonality.
useCase: Use this to plan your finances for the year ahead. An annual budget projecting income, expenses, taxes, and planned investments gives you a financial roadmap and reveals seasonal patterns to prepare for, letting a solo operator make decisions with a full year view rather than lurching month to month without a plan.

title: Budget for a Seasonal Business
promptText: Help me budget for my [BUSINESS TYPE] that has seasonal or uneven income. My busy periods are [WHEN YOU EARN MORE], my slow periods are [WHEN YOU EARN LESS], and my rough income pattern is [DESCRIBE THE PATTERN]. Help me plan a budget that handles the ups and downs: how to set money aside during good periods to cover the lean ones, how to smooth my income so slow months are not a crisis, how to plan expenses around the cycle, and how to avoid overspending when cash is flowing. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a budget approach for my seasonal income.
description: Budget for seasonal income by smoothing the highs to cover the lows.
useCase: Use this if your income swings by season. A budget that sets aside surplus in busy periods to cover slow ones smooths the cycle and prevents the lean months from becoming a crisis, which is essential for a solo operator with uneven income, where the danger is overspending when cash flows and scrambling when it does not.

title: Evaluate Budget Spending by Return
promptText: Help me evaluate whether my budget spending is actually delivering a return in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is where my money goes and what I get from each: [LIST YOUR SPENDING AREAS AND WHAT THEY DELIVER]. Help me assess which spending drives real results versus which is not paying off, where I should spend more because the return is strong, where to cut because it is not working, and how to shift my budget toward what actually generates return. The goal is a budget weighted toward what works. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me the analysis and how to reallocate toward better returns.
description: Evaluate which budget spending delivers real returns and reallocate toward it.
useCase: Use this to make your budget work harder. Assessing which spending actually drives results, then shifting money toward it and away from what does not, improves your return on every dollar, which is how a solo operator grows efficiently rather than spreading a budget evenly across things that pay off very unevenly.
variables: LIST YOUR SPENDING AREAS AND WHAT THEY DELIVER

title: Decide What to Cut When Money Is Tight
promptText: Help me decide what to cut from my budget because money is tight in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My current spending is [LIST YOUR EXPENSES WITH AMOUNTS], and how much I need to cut is [HOW MUCH YOU NEED TO REDUCE, OR WRITE AS MUCH AS POSSIBLE]. Help me prioritize cuts intelligently: what I can cut immediately with no real harm, what to reduce or downgrade, what is painful but survivable, and what I must protect because cutting it would hurt the business's ability to earn. Order the cuts from easiest to most damaging. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a prioritized list of what to cut and in what order.
description: Prioritize budget cuts from painless to protect the business's ability to earn.
useCase: Use this when you need to cut costs under financial pressure. A prioritized cut list from painless to damaging lets you reduce spending intelligently while protecting what the business needs to keep earning, avoiding the panic cutting that can cripple a solo business by slashing the very things that generate its revenue.
variables: HOW MUCH YOU NEED TO REDUCE, OR WRITE AS MUCH AS POSSIBLE

title: Budget for Taxes
promptText: Help me plan how to set aside money for taxes in my [BUSINESS TYPE] so I am not caught short. My revenue is [YOUR REVENUE], and how I handle taxes now is [HOW YOU HANDLE IT NOW, OR WRITE I DON'T]. Help me plan a simple approach: setting aside a portion of income regularly for taxes, roughly what percentage to reserve as a general starting point, where to keep it separate so I do not spend it, and how to stay ready for tax deadlines. Be very clear that tax rates and obligations vary by location and situation, this is general guidance not tax advice, and I must confirm the right amount with a qualified tax professional. Give me a practical approach to budgeting for taxes.
description: Plan to set aside money for taxes so you are never caught short.
useCase: Use this to avoid the tax time panic of owing money you already spent. Regularly reserving a portion of income in a separate place means the tax bill is covered when it comes, which protects a solo operator from one of the most common and stressful financial mistakes, though a professional should confirm the right percentage for your situation.
variables: HOW YOU HANDLE IT NOW, OR WRITE I DON'T

title: Build a Cash Buffer into Your Budget
promptText: Help me build a cash buffer or emergency fund for my [BUSINESS TYPE] so an unexpected drop or cost does not sink me. My monthly running costs are [YOUR MONTHLY COSTS], and my current savings buffer is [WHAT YOU HAVE SET ASIDE, OR WRITE NONE]. Help me plan: how big a buffer makes sense for my business, how to build it up gradually from my budget without straining, where to keep it, and when it is okay to use it. A buffer turns a potential crisis into a manageable bump. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a plan to build and maintain a cash buffer.
description: Plan a business cash buffer to weather unexpected drops or costs.
useCase: Use this to protect the business against surprises. A cash buffer built gradually from your budget turns an unexpected slow month or sudden cost from a crisis into a manageable bump, providing the financial resilience that lets a solo operator weather the inevitable rough patches without panic or debt.
variables: WHAT YOU HAVE SET ASIDE, OR WRITE NONE

title: Adjust Your Budget as Things Change
promptText: Help me adjust my budget because something has changed in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My current budget is [YOUR CURRENT BUDGET], and what has changed is [WHAT CHANGED, E.G. INCOME DROPPED, A NEW EXPENSE, GROWTH, A NEW GOAL]. Help me revise the budget sensibly to fit the new reality: what to adjust in response, how to rebalance the numbers, what the change means for my profit and plans, and how to keep the budget realistic and useful. A budget should flex with the business, not stay frozen. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me the adjusted budget and the reasoning.
description: Adjust your budget sensibly when income, costs, or goals change.
useCase: Use this when a change makes your current budget outdated. Revising it to fit new income, costs, or goals keeps the budget realistic and useful, since a frozen budget quickly becomes irrelevant as the business shifts, and a solo operator needs a plan that flexes with reality rather than one abandoned the moment things change.
variables: WHAT CHANGED, E.G. INCOME DROPPED, A NEW EXPENSE, GROWTH, A NEW GOAL

title: Plan How Much to Reinvest
promptText: Help me decide how much to reinvest back into my [BUSINESS TYPE] versus take as profit. My monthly profit after expenses is [YOUR PROFIT], my growth goals are [YOUR GOALS], and the things I could invest in are [POSSIBLE INVESTMENTS, E.G. TOOLS, MARKETING, HELP, SKILLS]. Help me think through the balance: how much to reinvest to fuel growth versus keep as my income, which investments would most move the business forward, and how to reinvest without starving my own pay or leaving no cushion. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a reinvestment plan and how to balance growth with taking profit.
description: Plan how much profit to reinvest for growth versus take as income.
useCase: Use this to balance growing the business against paying yourself. Deciding how much to reinvest and where fuels growth without starving your income or cushion, resolving a constant solo operator tension, since reinvesting too little stalls the business while reinvesting too much leaves you working for no personal return.
variables: POSSIBLE INVESTMENTS, E.G. TOOLS, MARKETING, HELP, SKILLS

title: Budget for a Specific Project
promptText: Help me budget for a specific project or initiative in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The project is [THE PROJECT], my goal for it is [THE GOAL], and my available budget or constraints are [YOUR BUDGET OR LIMITS]. Help me plan the project budget: the costs involved, how to allocate the budget across them, where to leave room for overruns, and how to judge whether the projected return justifies the spend. Flag any costs I might be underestimating. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a project budget and whether it looks worth the investment.
description: Budget a specific project and judge whether the return justifies the spend.
useCase: Use this before committing money to a project or initiative. A clear project budget with room for overruns and a check on projected return prevents overspending on a single effort, helping a solo operator decide whether a project is worth the investment rather than diving in and discovering the costs only partway through.

title: Work Out Your Break Even Point
promptText: Help me work out the break even point for my [BUSINESS TYPE] so I know what I need to earn to cover costs. My fixed monthly costs are [YOUR FIXED COSTS], my variable costs per sale or client are [YOUR VARIABLE COSTS], and my price or average sale is [YOUR PRICE]. Help me calculate roughly how much revenue or how many sales I need to break even each month, what I need beyond that to hit a target profit, and what this means for my budget and goals. Explain the numbers simply. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me my break even and what it means for my targets.
description: Calculate your break even point and what you need to earn to profit.
useCase: Use this to know the number you must hit to cover costs. Understanding your break even, and what it takes to reach a profit target, grounds your goals and budget in reality, giving a solo operator a clear minimum to aim for each month rather than working without knowing the point at which the business actually starts making money.

title: Plan a Budget Spreadsheet
promptText: Help me plan a simple budget spreadsheet for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I want to track [WHAT YOU WANT TO BUDGET AND SEE, E.G. MONTHLY INCOME, EXPENSES BY CATEGORY, PROFIT, VARIANCE]. Help me design the structure: what sections and columns to include, how to lay out income, expenses, and totals, how to compare budget to actual, and how to keep it simple to update each month. Describe the layout clearly enough that I can build it. The goal is a practical budget spreadsheet that gives me a clear financial picture without complexity. Give me the spreadsheet structure and how to set it up.
description: Plan a simple, practical budget spreadsheet structure you can maintain.
useCase: Use this to build a budget spreadsheet you will actually keep up. A clear structure covering income, expenses, profit, and budget versus actual gives you financial visibility without complexity, suiting a solo operator who wants a hands on view of the numbers without paying for or learning complicated budgeting software.
variables: WHAT YOU WANT TO BUDGET AND SEE, E.G. MONTHLY INCOME, EXPENSES BY CATEGORY, PROFIT, VARIANCE

title: Budget with an Irregular Income
promptText: Help me budget when my income is irregular and unpredictable in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My income varies like this: [DESCRIBE YOUR INCOME VARIABILITY], and my essential monthly costs are [YOUR ESSENTIAL COSTS]. Help me build an approach that works with unpredictable income: how to budget around my baseline essential needs, how to handle good months versus lean months, how to pay myself a steady amount despite variable income, and how to avoid the feast and famine cycle. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a budgeting approach for irregular income.
description: Budget effectively when your income is irregular and unpredictable.
useCase: Use this when unpredictable income makes budgeting hard. An approach built around covering baseline essentials and smoothing good months into lean ones lets you pay yourself steadily and escape the feast and famine cycle, which is one of the hardest and most stressful parts of solo business finances to manage well.

title: Get a Quick Budget Snapshot Fast

title: Respond to Rising Costs
promptText: Help me respond to rising costs that are squeezing my margins in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My costs have risen because [WHAT IS DRIVING THE COST INCREASE], and it is affecting [WHAT IT IS DOING TO YOUR MARGINS]. Help me plan a response: how much I would need to raise prices to protect my margin, whether to absorb some or pass it all on, how to reduce the rising costs, and how to communicate any increase to customers. Balance protecting profit with keeping customers. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a plan to protect my profitability against rising costs.
description: Plan how to protect your margins when costs rise, through pricing or cost cuts.
useCase: Use this when rising costs eat your margins. A plan balancing price increases, cost reduction, and customer communication protects profitability without driving customers away, which matters because costs rise over time and a solo operator who absorbs every increase watches their margin steadily shrink until the business stops being worthwhile.
variables: WHAT IS DRIVING THE COST INCREASE
WHAT IT IS DOING TO YOUR MARGINS

title: Get an Overall Profit Analysis
promptText: Help me get an overall picture of my business's profitability in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my financial picture: revenue [YOUR REVENUE], costs [YOUR COSTS], and expenses [YOUR EXPENSES], over [THE PERIOD]. Help me analyze the overall health: my actual profit and profit margin, how much of my revenue I keep, whether my profitability is healthy for my type of business, and the biggest factors helping or hurting it. Then highlight the most important thing to work on to improve profit. The goal is a clear read on how profitable my business really is. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me the overall profit analysis and top priority.
description: Get a clear overall analysis of your business's profitability and top priority.
useCase: Use this to understand how profitable your business really is overall. An analysis of your actual profit, margin, and the biggest factors helping or hurting it gives a clear read on financial health and what to fix first, which many solo operators lack, since being busy and generating revenue is not the same as running a genuinely profitable business.

title: Analyze Pricing on Value Not Just Cost
promptText: Help me think about whether I could price based on the value I deliver rather than just my costs in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I sell is [WHAT YOU SELL], the outcome or value it delivers to customers is [THE VALUE OR RESULT], and my current cost based price is [YOUR PRICE]. Help me analyze: whether the value I deliver could justify a higher price than my costs suggest, how to gauge what that value is worth to customers, and what a value informed price range might look like. This can dramatically improve margins where the value is high. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me the value based pricing analysis.
description: Analyze whether the value you deliver could justify pricing above your costs.
useCase: Use this to escape the trap of pricing only on costs. Analyzing whether the value you deliver justifies a higher price can dramatically improve margins where the outcome is worth far more than delivery costs, a shift that lets a solo operator capture the real worth of their work rather than leaving money on the table by pricing off cost alone.

title: Get a Quick Margin Check
promptText: Help me quickly check the margin on something I sell. My price is [YOUR PRICE] and my cost to deliver it is [YOUR COST]. Quickly tell me my profit per sale, my margin as a percentage, and whether that looks healthy or thin at a glance. Keep it fast and clear, no long explanation. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Just give me a quick margin read right now.
description: Get a fast margin check on a price and cost at a glance.
useCase: Use this for a quick gut check on a margin. A fast read on profit per sale and margin percentage tells you at a glance whether something is healthy or thin without a full analysis, useful when a solo operator just wants to sanity check a price or cost quickly before deciding whether it needs a closer look.

title: Analyze the Profitability of Pricing Tiers
promptText: Help me analyze the profitability across my pricing tiers or packages in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My tiers, with their prices and rough costs to deliver, are [LIST YOUR TIERS WITH PRICES AND COSTS]. Help me see the margin on each tier, which is most and least profitable, whether my tiers are priced to guide customers toward the profitable ones, and whether any tier is a poor deal for me. Then suggest adjustments so my tier structure works in my favor. The goal is tiers that are each profitable and steer customers well. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me the tier profitability analysis and adjustments.
description: Analyze margins across pricing tiers and structure them to work in your favor.
useCase: Use this if you offer tiered pricing or packages. Analyzing the margin on each tier reveals which are profitable and whether the structure steers customers toward good deals for you, letting you adjust so every tier makes money, since poorly structured tiers can quietly push customers toward the option that pays a solo operator the least.
variables: LIST YOUR TIERS WITH PRICES AND COSTS

title: Balance Cost Based and Market Pricing
promptText: Help me reconcile what my costs say I should charge with what the market will bear in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My cost based price to hit a good margin is [YOUR COST BASED PRICE], but the market or competitors are around [MARKET PRICE OR WHAT CUSTOMERS EXPECT]. Help me navigate the gap: what to do if my needed price is above what the market pays, how to close the gap through costs or value rather than just cutting price, and how to find a price that is both profitable and competitive. The goal is a price that works financially and in the market. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me how to reconcile my costs with the market.
description: Reconcile the price your costs require with what the market will actually pay.
useCase: Use this when your costs demand a higher price than the market seems to pay. Navigating the gap through reducing costs or raising perceived value, rather than just cutting price into unprofitability, finds a price that works both financially and competitively, resolving a genuine bind for a solo operator caught between needing a margin and facing market price pressure.
variables: MARKET PRICE OR WHAT CUSTOMERS EXPECT

title: Find Where Profit Is Leaking
promptText: Help me find where profit is leaking out of my [BUSINESS TYPE], because I feel busy but do not keep as much as I should. My situation is [DESCRIBE YOUR REVENUE, COSTS, AND WHERE MONEY SEEMS TO GO]. Help me hunt for the leaks: thin margins, unprofitable offerings or clients, excessive discounting, scope creep and unbilled work, rising costs, inefficiency, or overhead creep. Identify the most likely places profit is escaping and what to do about each. The goal is to plug the leaks so more of my revenue becomes profit. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me the likely profit leaks and how to fix them.
description: Find where profit is leaking out of the business and how to plug each leak.
useCase: Use this when you feel busy but do not keep enough profit. Hunting systematically for leaks like thin margins, discounting, unbilled scope creep, and overhead creep finds where money escapes, so you can plug it, addressing the frustrating and common situation where a solo operator generates good revenue yet keeps far less profit than the numbers should allow.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR REVENUE, COSTS, AND WHERE MONEY SEEMS TO GO

title: Set Up Profitability Tracking
promptText: Help me set up a simple way to track profitability over time in my [BUSINESS TYPE], not just revenue. What I want to keep an eye on is [WHAT MATTERS TO YOU, E.G. MARGIN, PROFIT PER OFFERING, OVERALL PROFIT]. Help me plan a lightweight tracking approach: the few profit focused numbers worth watching regularly, how to calculate and record them simply, how often to review, and how to spot when profitability is slipping. The goal is ongoing visibility of profit, not just how much money is coming in. Note this is general guidance, not financial advice. Give me a simple profitability tracking approach.
description: Set up simple ongoing tracking of profitability, not just revenue.
useCase: Use this to keep profit visible over time, not just revenue. A lightweight system tracking the few profit numbers that matter catches slipping profitability early, which is vital because revenue is easy to watch while profit quietly erodes, and a solo operator focused only on money coming in can miss margins declining until it becomes a serious problem.
variables: WHAT MATTERS TO YOU, E.G. MARGIN, PROFIT PER OFFERING, OVERALL PROFIT

title: Draft a Service Agreement Starting Point
promptText: Help me draft a plain language service agreement starting point for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [WHAT YOU PROVIDE], my typical client is [YOUR CLIENT], and the key things I want covered are [WHAT MATTERS TO YOU, E.G. SCOPE, PAYMENT, TIMELINE]. Draft a clear service agreement covering the common sections such as the parties, services provided, deliverables, payment terms, timeline, responsibilities of each side, and how it can end. Keep it clear and readable, not dense legalese. Be very clear this is a general educational starting draft, not legal advice, that laws vary by location, and that a qualified lawyer should review it before I use it. Give me the service agreement draft and note what to confirm with a lawyer.
description: Draft a clear, plain language service agreement starting point to refine.
useCase: Use this to create a first draft of a service agreement to then have reviewed. A clear starting point covering the essential sections gives you something concrete to refine and take to a lawyer, saving time and cost, though a qualified professional should review it before use since contract law varies by location and getting it wrong carries real risk.
variables: WHAT MATTERS TO YOU, E.G. SCOPE, PAYMENT, TIMELINE

title: Define Scope of Work in an Agreement
promptText: Help me write a clear scope of work section for my service agreement in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The work I am providing is [DESCRIBE THE WORK AND DELIVERABLES], and what is NOT included is [WHAT IS OUT OF SCOPE, OR WRITE HELP ME THINK THROUGH THIS]. Help me draft a scope section that clearly defines exactly what I will deliver, what is included and excluded, any assumptions, and what would count as additional work. A precise scope prevents disputes and scope creep. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review the full agreement. Give me the scope of work section and note what to clarify.
description: Write a precise scope of work section that prevents disputes and scope creep.
useCase: Use this to define scope clearly and prevent the disputes that vague scope causes. A precise section stating what is and is not included protects against scope creep and disagreements about what was promised, which is one of the most common and costly problems for a solo operator, since unclear scope leads to unpaid extra work and client conflict.
variables: WHAT IS OUT OF SCOPE, OR WRITE HELP ME THINK THROUGH THIS

title: Draft Payment Terms for an Agreement
promptText: Help me draft clear payment terms for my service agreement in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My pricing is [YOUR PRICING], and how I want to be paid is [YOUR PAYMENT PREFERENCES, E.G. DEPOSIT, MILESTONES, ON COMPLETION, LATE FEES]. Draft a payment terms section covering the fees, when payment is due, any deposit or milestone schedule, accepted methods, what happens if payment is late, and any other protections. Make the terms clear and enforceable sounding while remaining fair. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review it, especially any late fee provisions which are regulated in some places. Give me the payment terms section.
description: Draft clear, fair payment terms that protect you in a service agreement.
useCase: Use this to write payment terms that protect your cash flow. Clear terms on when and how you get paid, deposits, and late payment consequences reduce the payment problems that plague service businesses, though a lawyer should review them since some provisions like late fees are regulated and vary by location.
variables: YOUR PAYMENT PREFERENCES, E.G. DEPOSIT, MILESTONES, ON COMPLETION, LATE FEES

title: Review a Service Agreement for Gaps
promptText: Help me review my service agreement to spot gaps and unclear areas before a lawyer looks at it in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my agreement: [PASTE YOUR AGREEMENT]. Help me identify: important things that seem missing, vague or ambiguous wording that could cause disputes, areas that seem one sided or unfair, and questions to raise with a lawyer. The goal is to catch obvious issues myself so my professional review is more focused and useful. Be very clear this is general educational feedback, not legal advice, and a qualified lawyer must review the agreement properly. Give me the gaps and questions to raise.
description: Review a service agreement for gaps and ambiguities before professional review.
useCase: Use this to catch obvious issues in an agreement before paying a lawyer to review it. Spotting missing pieces and vague wording yourself makes professional review more focused and productive, helping a solo operator get better value from legal help, though the lawyer must still properly review it since this is educational feedback, not legal advice.

title: Understand Confusing Contract Clauses
promptText: Help me understand contract clauses or terms that confuse me in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The clauses or terms I do not understand are [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE CLAUSES OR TERMS]. Explain in plain language what each one means, why it is typically included, what it would mean for me practically, and anything I should be cautious about. Help me actually understand what I might be agreeing to or including. Be very clear this is general educational explanation, not legal advice, that meaning and enforceability vary by location, and that a lawyer should advise on anything specific. Give me plain explanations of these clauses and what to watch for.
description: Understand confusing contract clauses explained in plain language.
useCase: Use this to understand contract language before signing or including it. title: Add Scope Creep Protection to an Agreement
promptText: Help me add protection against scope creep to my service agreement in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [YOUR SERVICE], and the scope creep I keep experiencing is [WHAT CLIENTS TEND TO ASK FOR BEYOND SCOPE]. Help me draft language that handles out of scope requests: how additional work is defined, how changes are requested and approved, how extra work is priced and billed, and how revisions or rounds are limited. The goal is language that lets me get paid for extra work instead of absorbing it. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review it. Give me the scope protection language.
description: Add clear scope creep protection so you get paid for out of scope work.
useCase: Use this to stop absorbing unpaid extra work. Clear language defining how out of scope requests are approved and billed lets you charge for additional work instead of doing it for free, addressing a persistent drain for service based solo operators, where clients gradually expand requests and unclear terms mean the extra work goes uncompensated.

title: Draft Cancellation and Termination Terms
promptText: Help me draft cancellation and termination terms for my service agreement in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [YOUR SERVICE], and how I want to handle ending the arrangement is [YOUR PREFERENCES, E.G. NOTICE PERIOD, DEPOSITS NON REFUNDABLE, WORK PAID TO DATE]. Draft terms covering how either party can end the agreement, notice required, what happens to payments and work in progress, any non refundable amounts, and how to wind things down fairly. Protect me from being left unpaid for work done. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review it since cancellation terms are regulated in some places. Give me the cancellation and termination terms.
description: Draft fair cancellation and termination terms that protect you if a deal ends.
useCase: Use this to protect yourself when an engagement ends early. Clear terms on notice, payment for work done, and non refundable amounts prevent being left unpaid or in dispute if a client cancels, a real risk for a solo operator, though a lawyer should review them since cancellation and refund terms are regulated differently across locations.

title: Draft Revision and Approval Terms
promptText: Help me draft clear revision and approval terms for my service agreement in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My work involves [YOUR WORK AND DELIVERABLES], and my revision policy is [HOW MANY ROUNDS OR YOUR PREFERENCE, OR WRITE HELP ME DECIDE]. Draft terms covering how many revision rounds are included, how additional revisions are handled and charged, how work gets approved and signed off, and what counts as final delivery. The goal is to prevent endless unpaid revisions and unclear approval. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review the full agreement. Give me the revision and approval terms.
description: Draft revision and approval terms that prevent endless unpaid revisions.
useCase: Use this if clients request endless revisions that eat your profit. Clear terms defining included revision rounds and how extra ones are charged, plus how work gets approved, prevent the unpaid revision spiral, protecting a solo operator from the common trap where undefined revisions turn a fixed price project into unlimited unbilled work.

title: Spot Red Flags in a Contract Sent to You
promptText: Help me spot potential red flags in a contract a client or company sent me to sign for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is the contract: [PASTE THE CONTRACT]. Help me identify anything that looks concerning or unfair to me: terms heavily favoring the other side, unusual or risky obligations, vague language that could be used against me, missing protections I would want, or anything I should question or negotiate. Flag what to look at closely and raise with a lawyer. Be very clear this is general educational feedback to help me spot issues, not legal advice, and a qualified lawyer must review it before I sign. Give me the potential red flags and what to question.
description: Spot potential red flags in a contract sent to you before signing.
useCase: Use this before signing a contract someone else drafted. Spotting one sided terms, risky obligations, and missing protections helps you know what to question or negotiate, protecting you from unfair agreements, though a lawyer must review it before signing since this is educational feedback and enforceability depends on law that varies by location.

title: Check if an Agreement Is Fair to Both Sides
promptText: Help me check whether my service agreement is fair and balanced or too one sided in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my agreement: [PASTE YOUR AGREEMENT]. Help me assess: whether it protects me adequately without being so one sided that it scares off good clients, where it might be unfair to the client, where it leaves me exposed, and how to strike a fair balance that protects me while feeling reasonable. A fair agreement builds trust and still protects me. Be clear this is general educational feedback, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review it. Give me an assessment of the balance and how to make it fair.
description: Check whether your agreement is fairly balanced or too one sided either way.
useCase: Use this to strike the right balance in your agreement. Checking whether it protects you without being so one sided it deters good clients helps you build trust while staying protected, since an overly aggressive contract can cost you clients while a too soft one leaves you exposed, though a lawyer should review the balance for your situation.

title: Understand Liability and Protection Clauses
promptText: Help me understand liability and protection concepts for my service agreement in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Be very clear that liability law is complex, varies significantly by location, and these clauses may or may not be enforceable, this is general educational information only not legal advice, and I must have a qualified lawyer draft and advise on any liability protection. promptText: Help me rewrite my NDA in plainer language so people actually read and sign it in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my current NDA: [PASTE YOUR NDA]. Help me make it clear and readable while keeping the protections intact: simplify the dense legalese, make the obligations understandable to a normal person, and keep it professional and still protective. A short readable NDA gets signed faster than an intimidating one. Be clear this is general educational rewriting, not legal advice, that simplification must not accidentally weaken the protections, and that a lawyer should confirm the plain version still protects me. Give me the plain language NDA.
description: Rewrite an NDA in plain language so people read and sign it faster.
useCase: Use this to reduce the friction of getting NDAs signed. A short readable NDA gets signed faster than an intimidating wall of legalese that makes people nervous or send it to their own lawyer, though a lawyer should confirm the simplified version still protects you, since clarity must not accidentally weaken the terms.

title: Draft an NDA for Partnership Discussions
promptText: Help me draft an NDA for exploratory partnership or collaboration discussions in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The discussions are with [WHO], about [WHAT YOU MIGHT DO TOGETHER], and what we would share is [WHAT BOTH SIDES MIGHT DISCLOSE]. Draft a mutual NDA suited to early exploratory talks: covering both sides' information, clarifying that discussing does not commit either side to anything, allowing each to walk away freely, and keeping it light enough not to chill a friendly conversation. Note that heavy NDAs at the exploratory stage can kill the conversation. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review it. Give me the partnership discussion NDA.
description: Draft a light mutual NDA suited to early exploratory partnership talks.
useCase: Use this before exploratory partnership conversations where both sides share information. A light mutual NDA that protects both sides while making clear neither is committed keeps the conversation comfortable, since a heavy one sided NDA at the exploratory stage can kill a promising discussion before it starts.

title: Get a Quick NDA Draft Fast
promptText: I need a quick simple NDA draft. My business is [BUSINESS TYPE], the situation is [WHY YOU NEED IT], what needs protecting is [WHAT IS CONFIDENTIAL], and it should be [MUTUAL OR ONE WAY]. Give me a clear, concise NDA covering the essentials, ready for me to refine and have reviewed. Keep it short and readable rather than dense. Be clear this is a general educational starting draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review it before use. Just give me a solid starting draft I can work from.
description: Get a quick, concise NDA starting draft to refine.
useCase: Use this when you need an NDA draft quickly. A fast concise starting point covering the essentials gives you something to refine and have reviewed, useful when a conversation is imminent, though a lawyer should review it before use since it is an educational starting draft rather than a finished agreement.

title: Narrow an Overly Broad NDA
promptText: Help me narrow an NDA that is too broad, whether one I wrote or one I was asked to sign, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is the NDA: [PASTE THE NDA], and what feels too broad is [WHAT SEEMS EXCESSIVE]. Help me tighten it: narrowing the definition of confidential information to what actually needs protecting, limiting the duration to something reasonable, removing obligations that go beyond confidentiality, and making it proportionate. Note that a focused NDA is often more enforceable and more likely to be signed than a sweeping one. Be clear this is general educational guidance, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review any changes. Give me the narrowed version and what changed.
description: Narrow an overly broad NDA into something proportionate and more enforceable.
useCase: Use this when an NDA feels excessive, whether yours or one handed to you. Narrowing the definition, duration, and obligations to what actually needs protecting makes it proportionate, and a focused NDA is often both more enforceable and more likely to be signed than a sweeping one that alarms the other side.

title: Decide When an NDA Needs a Lawyer
promptText: Help me decide whether an NDA situation needs a lawyer or whether a reviewed template is enough for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION, WHAT IS AT STAKE, AND WHO IS INVOLVED]. Help me think through: when professional help is genuinely warranted such as high stakes information or a sophisticated counterparty, when a solid template reviewed once can cover routine situations, the factors that raise the stakes, and how to be smart about legal costs. Be clear this is general guidance to inform the decision, not legal advice, that anything high stakes warrants professional help, and that when in doubt consulting a lawyer is safer. Give me guidance on whether this NDA needs a lawyer.
description: Decide when an NDA genuinely needs a lawyer versus a reviewed template.
useCase: Use this to spend legal budget wisely on NDAs. Understanding when professional help is genuinely warranted, such as high stakes information or a sophisticated counterparty, versus when a template reviewed once suffices for routine situations, helps a solo operator stay protected without lawyering every conversation.
variables: DESCRIBE THE SITUATION, WHAT IS AT STAKE, AND WHO IS INVOLVED

title: Draft Client Onboarding Terms
promptText: Help me draft clear onboarding terms to set expectations with new clients in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [WHAT YOU DO], my typical client is [YOUR CLIENT], and the misunderstandings I want to prevent are [WHAT GOES WRONG, OR WRITE HELP ME ANTICIPATE]. Draft terms a client agrees to at the start covering what I will deliver, what I need from them, how we communicate, timelines, payment, and how changes are handled. Keep it clear and welcoming rather than cold and defensive, since this is a client's first impression. Be very clear this is a general educational starting draft, not legal advice, and a qualified lawyer should review it before use. Give me the onboarding terms and what to confirm with a lawyer.
description: Draft clear onboarding terms that set expectations without feeling cold.
useCase: Use this to set expectations at the start of a client relationship. Clear onboarding terms covering deliverables, communication, and what you need from them prevent most misunderstandings before they start, and getting the tone right matters since this is a client's first real impression of how you work.

title: Write an Expectations Document
promptText: Help me write a document that sets clear expectations with new clients in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [WHAT YOU DO], and the expectations I need to set are [WHAT CLIENTS OFTEN ASSUME WRONGLY, E.G. TIMELINES, AVAILABILITY, WHAT IS INCLUDED]. Write a clear friendly document that explains how the work will go, what they can expect from me and when, what I need from them, how we will communicate, and what is and is not included. The goal is that nobody is surprised later. Frame it as helpful orientation rather than a list of rules. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review anything binding. Give me the expectations document.
description: Write a friendly document that sets clear expectations so nobody is surprised.
useCase: Use this to prevent the mismatched assumptions that sour client relationships. A friendly orientation explaining how the work will go, what is included, and what you need from them means nobody is surprised later, which is where most client friction actually originates rather than in the work itself.

title: Set Boundaries at Client Onboarding
promptText: Help me set healthy boundaries with clients right at the start in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The boundary issues I struggle with are [YOUR ISSUES, E.G. AFTER HOURS MESSAGES, ENDLESS REQUESTS, EXPECTING INSTANT REPLIES, SCOPE CREEP]. Help me write onboarding terms that establish boundaries kindly but clearly: my working hours and response times, the right channels to reach me, how requests and changes are handled, and what falls outside our arrangement. Frame boundaries as how I deliver good work rather than as restrictions. Setting these upfront is far easier than enforcing them later. Be clear this is general guidance, not legal advice. Give me the boundary setting terms.
description: Set client boundaries kindly at the start rather than enforcing them later.
useCase: Use this to establish boundaries before bad habits form. Stating working hours, response times, and how requests are handled at onboarding is far easier than clawing them back after a client has been messaging at midnight for a month, and framing them as how you deliver good work makes them land well.
variables: YOUR ISSUES, E.G. AFTER HOURS MESSAGES, ENDLESS REQUESTS, EXPECTING INSTANT REPLIES, SCOPE CREEP

title: Plan a Client Welcome Packet
promptText: Help me plan a welcome packet for new clients in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [WHAT YOU DO], and my onboarding process involves [WHAT HAPPENS AT THE START]. Help me plan what to include: a warm welcome, what happens next and when, what I need from them, how we will work together and communicate, key terms and expectations, and answers to questions clients commonly ask at this stage. Make it feel professional and reassuring rather than bureaucratic. A good welcome packet builds confidence and prevents questions. Be clear this is general guidance, not legal advice, and any binding terms should be lawyer reviewed. Give me the welcome packet structure and content.

useCase: Use this to make new clients feel confident they hired well. A welcome packet covering what happens next, what you need, and common questions builds trust and heads off the flurry of early questions, making a solo operator look organized in exactly the moment a client is deciding whether they made a good choice.

title: Draft Communication Terms for Clients
promptText: Help me draft clear communication terms for new clients in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. How I prefer to work is [YOUR PREFERENCES, E.G. EMAIL ONLY, SCHEDULED CALLS, ONE CHANNEL], my response time is [YOUR REALISTIC RESPONSE TIME], and my working hours are [YOUR HOURS]. Draft terms covering the channels to use, expected response times from both sides, how urgent matters are handled, meeting arrangements, and keeping communication in one place. description: Draft communication terms covering channels, response times, and availability.
useCase: Use this to prevent the communication chaos of clients reaching you across five channels expecting instant replies. Clear terms on channels and realistic response times protect your focus and set expectations you can actually meet, which is better than overpromising availability and disappointing them.

title: Build a Client Intake Questionnaire
promptText: Help me build an intake questionnaire for new clients in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [WHAT YOU DO], and what I need to know before starting is [WHAT INFORMATION YOU NEED]. Build a questionnaire that gathers everything I need upfront: their goals, relevant background, materials or access I need, their expectations and constraints, and anything that would otherwise surface awkwardly later. Include questions that reveal potential problems early such as unrealistic expectations or unclear decision makers. Keep it focused rather than exhausting. Be clear this is general guidance, not legal advice, and note any sensitive data collected may carry obligations. Give me the client intake questionnaire.
description: Build an intake questionnaire that gathers what you need and surfaces problems early.
useCase: Use this to gather what you need before starting and catch problems early. title: Define What Clients Are Responsible For
promptText: Help me define what clients are responsible for in my onboarding terms for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My work needs clients to [WHAT YOU NEED FROM THEM, E.G. PROVIDE MATERIALS, GIVE FEEDBACK ON TIME, GRANT ACCESS, MAKE DECISIONS], and the delays I experience are [WHERE CLIENTS HOLD THINGS UP]. Draft terms covering what they must provide and by when, the consequences of delays on their side such as timeline shifts, and how we handle it if they go quiet. Frame it as a shared partnership rather than a list of demands. This protects me from being blamed for delays I did not cause. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review binding terms. Give me the client responsibility terms.
description: Define client responsibilities so their delays do not become your problem.
useCase: Use this to protect yourself from client caused delays. Terms defining what they must provide and by when, and what happens if they go quiet, prevent you being blamed for timelines their inaction wrecked, a frequent frustration where the provider absorbs the fallout of a client's slow feedback.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED FROM THEM, E.G. PROVIDE MATERIALS, GIVE FEEDBACK ON TIME, GRANT ACCESS, MAKE DECISIONS

title: Set Timeline Expectations at Onboarding
promptText: Help me set realistic timeline expectations with new clients in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My typical project takes [YOUR REALISTIC TIMELINE], and the timeline pressure I face is [WHAT CLIENTS EXPECT OR PUSH FOR]. Draft terms that set honest expectations: how long things realistically take, what the milestones are, what depends on their input, how delays on either side affect the schedule, and how rush requests are handled. Build in buffer rather than promising your best case. Overpromising on timelines is a common way to start a relationship badly. Be clear this is general guidance, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review binding commitments. Give me the timeline terms.
description: Set honest timeline expectations with buffer rather than promising best case.
useCase: Use this to avoid starting a client relationship with a promise you cannot keep. Setting realistic timelines with buffer, and clarifying what depends on their input, prevents the damage of missed deadlines, since overpromising to win the work and then slipping erodes trust far more than an honest timeline would have.

title: Set Payment Expectations at Onboarding
promptText: Help me set clear payment expectations with new clients at onboarding in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My payment structure is [YOUR STRUCTURE, E.G. DEPOSIT THEN BALANCE, MILESTONES, MONTHLY], and my terms are [YOUR TERMS]. Draft onboarding wording that makes payment clear from the start: when payments are due, how to pay, what happens if payment is late, and what triggers each payment. Frame it matter of factly as part of how the engagement works rather than as a warning. Clients who understand the payment rhythm from day one pay more reliably. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review binding terms, especially late payment provisions. Give me the payment expectation wording.
description: Set clear payment expectations at onboarding so clients pay reliably.
useCase: Use this to establish the payment rhythm from day one. Clients who understand when and how they pay from the start pay more reliably than those who encounter the terms only when an invoice lands, and framing it matter of factly as part of how things work avoids it feeling like a warning.

title: Write an Onboarding FAQ
promptText: Help me write an FAQ for new clients in my [BUSINESS TYPE] to answer the questions they always ask at the start. My service is [WHAT YOU DO], and the questions new clients commonly ask are [COMMON QUESTIONS, OR WRITE HELP ME ANTICIPATE]. title: Set Revision Expectations at Onboarding
promptText: Help me set revision expectations with new clients at onboarding in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My work involves [YOUR DELIVERABLES], and my revision policy is [YOUR POLICY]. Draft onboarding wording that explains how revisions work before we start: how many rounds are included, how feedback should be given to make revisions productive, what counts as a revision versus a new request, and how additional rounds are handled. Be clear this is a general educational draft, not legal advice, and a lawyer should review binding terms. Give me the revision expectation wording.

useCase: Use this to head off revision creep before it starts. title: Clarify Scope During Onboarding
promptText: Help me clarify scope with a new client during onboarding in my [BUSINESS TYPE], before any confusion sets in. useCase: Use this to name exclusions plainly while it is still easy. Restating what is included and explicitly what is not at onboarding feels slightly awkward but prevents the far worse conversation weeks later when a client expects something you never agreed to deliver.

title: Spot Client Red Flags During Onboarding

Help me assess honestly: which behaviors genuinely predict a difficult engagement, which are harmless, what to clarify before proceeding, and whether this looks like a client worth taking. Note that onboarding behavior usually predicts how the whole engagement will go. Be honest rather than reassuring. Be clear this is general guidance, not legal advice. Give me an honest read on the warning signs and what to do.
description: Spot client warning signs at onboarding while you can still address or decline.
useCase: Use this when a new client feels slightly off during onboarding. title: Decline a Client Gracefully at Onboarding

title: Build a Client Onboarding Checklist
promptText: Help me build a checklist so nothing gets missed when onboarding a new client in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. title: Check Your Onboarding Terms for Gaps
promptText: Help me check my onboarding terms for gaps in my [BUSINESS TYPE], based on where things actually go wrong. Here are my terms: [PASTE YOUR TERMS], and the problems I keep having with clients are [WHAT GOES WRONG REPEATEDLY]. Help me identify: which of my recurring problems my terms fail to address, what is missing or too vague to rely on, and what to add so the same issues stop recurring. Use my actual pain points as the guide rather than a generic list. Recurring client problems usually trace back to something onboarding never set. title: Write a Professional Email
promptText: Help me write a professional email for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The recipient is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], what I need to communicate is [YOUR MESSAGE], and what I want to happen is [DESIRED OUTCOME]. Write a clear professional email that gets to the point quickly, communicates what matters without padding, makes any request explicit, and ends with a clear next step. Keep it concise, since long emails get skimmed or ignored. My tone is [TONE]. Give me a subject line and the email.

useCase: Use this for any email where the wording matters and you want it right. A concise email that states its purpose quickly and makes the request explicit gets read and acted on, unlike the rambling emails that get skimmed, since busy people decide within a line or two whether to engage.

title: Write Email Subject Lines That Get Opened
promptText: Help me write a subject line for an email. The email is about [WHAT IT IS ABOUT], the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE], and what I want them to do is [DESIRED ACTION]. Give me several subject line options that are clear and specific rather than vague, tell the recipient what the email is actually about, and make it easy to find later. Vary them: some direct, some that signal urgency where genuine, and some that spark curiosity. Avoid clickbait or anything misleading. Mark the strongest. Give me the subject line options.
description: Write clear, specific subject lines that get emails opened and found later.
useCase: Use this because the subject line decides whether your email gets opened. Clear specific subject lines that say what the email is about outperform vague ones like quick question, and they also make the email findable later, which busy recipients quietly appreciate.

title: Adjust the Tone of an Email
promptText: Help me adjust the tone of an email before I send it. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and the tone I want is [DESIRED TONE, E.G. WARMER, FIRMER, MORE FORMAL, MORE CASUAL]. Rewrite it in the right tone while keeping the substance intact. Point out anything in my draft that reads differently than I intended, such as sounding cold, passive aggressive, apologetic, or harsh. Tone is easy to get wrong in writing since there is no voice or face to soften it. Give me the adjusted email and what changed.
description: Adjust an email's tone and catch anything that reads differently than intended.
useCase: Use this when an email needs to land a certain way. Tone is easy to misjudge in writing since there is no voice or expression to soften it, and catching that your draft reads as cold, passive aggressive, or overly apologetic before sending prevents a lot of unnecessary friction.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP
DESIRED TONE, E.G. WARMER, FIRMER, MORE FORMAL, MORE CASUAL

title: Cut an Email Down to Size
promptText: Help me cut this email down. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT]. Trim it to the essentials: cut the padding, throat clearing, unnecessary context, and over explanation, while keeping everything that actually matters and preserving the tone. Make the ask clearer and easier to spot. Long emails get skimmed, so a shorter one is usually more effective. Tell me what you cut and why. Give me the shortened email.
description: Cut a long email down to the essentials without losing what matters.
useCase: Use this when your email has grown too long. Cutting the padding and over explanation while keeping the substance makes it more likely to be read and acted on, since a long email invites skimming and the request buried in paragraph four often gets missed entirely.

title: Write a Cold Email That Gets a Reply
promptText: Help me write a cold email to someone who does not know me, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The recipient is [WHO THEY ARE AND WHY YOU ARE REACHING OUT], what I want is [YOUR ASK], and what I know about them is [ANY RELEVANT CONTEXT, OR WRITE NOT MUCH]. Write a short email that opens with something specific to them rather than a generic pitch, explains quickly why I am reaching out, makes a small easy ask rather than a big one, and respects their time. Keep it short, since long cold emails get deleted. Be genuine rather than salesy. Give me a subject line and the cold email.
description: Write a short, specific cold email that respects their time and gets a reply.
useCase: Use this when reaching out to someone who does not know you. A short email that opens with something specific to them and makes a small easy ask gets replies, unlike the generic pitch that gets deleted, since the bar for a stranger's attention is high and brevity plus relevance is what clears it.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHY YOU ARE REACHING OUT
ANY RELEVANT CONTEXT, OR WRITE NOT MUCH

title: Make Your Ask Clear in an Email
promptText: Help me make my ask clear in an email, because I tend to bury it or be vague. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], and what I actually want is [WHAT YOU REALLY WANT THEM TO DO]. Help me: state the ask explicitly rather than hinting at it, put it somewhere they will actually see it, make it specific and easy to say yes to, and remove the hedging that makes it sound optional. Being direct about what I want is not rude, it is helpful. Give me the revised email with a clear ask.
description: Make your ask explicit and easy to act on instead of buried or hedged.
useCase: Use this when you hint at what you want rather than asking. A buried or hedged ask often gets no response simply because the recipient did not register that anything was needed, and being direct about what you want is helpful rather than rude, since it lets them act.
variables: WHAT YOU REALLY WANT THEM TO DO

title: Draft a Reply to an Email
promptText: Help me reply to an email. Here is the email I received: [PASTE THEIR EMAIL], my relationship with them is [YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and what I want to convey is [YOUR RESPONSE OR POSITION]. Draft a reply that addresses what they actually said rather than talking past it, answers any questions they asked, conveys my position clearly, and moves things forward. Note anything in their email I should address that I might overlook. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the reply.
description: Draft a reply that addresses what they actually said and moves things forward.
useCase: Use this when a reply needs care. Addressing what they actually said, answering their questions, and catching the point you might have overlooked prevents the frustrating exchange where two people talk past each other across three rounds of email.

title: Soften an Email That Reads Too Harsh
promptText: Help me soften an email that might read harsher than I intend. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE], and the situation is [ANY CONTEXT, E.G. YOU ARE FRUSTRATED, IT IS BAD NEWS, YOU ARE PUSHING BACK]. Soften it so it stays clear and firm on the substance but does not read as cold, blunt, or annoyed. Keep the message intact rather than watering it down into vagueness. Point out the specific phrases that were landing badly. Softening tone is not the same as backing down. Give me the softened email.
description: Soften a harsh sounding email without watering down the message.
useCase: Use this when frustration or bluntness has crept into a draft. Softening how it reads while keeping the substance firm prevents an email that damages a relationship over tone, and knowing which phrases were landing badly helps you catch it yourself next time.
variables: ANY CONTEXT, E.G. YOU ARE FRUSTRATED, IT IS BAD NEWS, YOU ARE PUSHING BACK

title: Make an Email Firmer Without Being Rude
promptText: Help me make an email firmer, because my draft is too soft and apologetic. Here it is: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], and what I need is [WHAT YOU NEED TO HOLD FIRM ON]. Rewrite it to be clear and confident: cut the excessive apologizing, hedging, and qualifiers that undermine my position, state what I need directly, and sound professional and assured rather than tentative. Remaining polite while being firm is entirely possible. Point out the phrases that were weakening me. Give me the firmer email.
description: Make a soft, apologetic email firm and confident while staying polite.
useCase: Use this when your draft undermines you with hedging and apology. Cutting the qualifiers that weaken your position and stating what you need directly reads as confident rather than rude, and it matters because a tentative email invites the other side to push back on things that were not negotiable.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED TO HOLD FIRM ON

title: Check an Email for Clarity Before Sending
promptText: Help me check this email before I send it. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], and the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE]. Check it for: anything unclear or open to misreading, whether my point and ask are obvious, whether the tone matches my intent, anything that could be taken the wrong way, and anything missing they would need to respond. Read it as the recipient would rather than as I meant it. Flag what to fix. Give me the feedback and a tightened version.
description: Check an email for clarity and misreading risk before you send it.
useCase: Use this before sending an email that matters. Reading it as the recipient would rather than as you meant it catches the ambiguity and unintended tone that cause misunderstandings, which is the main gap in email since you cannot clarify in the moment the way you can in conversation.

title: Write an Introduction Email
promptText: Help me write an email introducing myself for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The recipient is [WHO THEY ARE], the context is [WHY YOU ARE INTRODUCING YOURSELF, E.G. NEW CLIENT, REFERRAL, NETWORKING], and what I want from the introduction is [YOUR GOAL]. Write an email that introduces me clearly without a life story, explains why I am reaching out and any connection between us, conveys what I do briefly and relevantly, and suggests a clear next step. Keep it warm and short. Give me a subject line and the introduction email.
description: Write a warm, short introduction email that leads to a clear next step.
useCase: Use this when introducing yourself to someone new. An introduction that stays brief, names any connection, and suggests a next step lands far better than a long self description, since the recipient's question is simply why this matters to them and how much of their time it will take.
variables: WHY YOU ARE REACHING OUT, E.G. NEW CLIENT, REFERRAL, NETWORKING

title: Write an Email Using a Referral
promptText: Help me write an email to someone I was referred to, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The referrer is [WHO REFERRED YOU AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP TO THEM], the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE], and what I want is [YOUR ASK]. Write an email that leads with the referral since that is my strongest credential here, mentions the referrer naturally rather than name dropping, explains why I am reaching out, and makes a clear reasonable ask. A referral opens doors, so use it well without overplaying it. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a referral email that uses the connection well without overplaying it.
