useCase: Use this when someone referred you to a contact. Leading with the referral gets the email read since it is your strongest credential with a stranger, and mentioning the referrer naturally rather than name dropping keeps it from feeling like you are trading on someone else's relationship.
variables: WHO REFERRED YOU AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP TO THEM

title: Write a Thank You Email
promptText: Help me write a thank you email for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am thanking [WHO] for [WHAT THEY DID], and my relationship with them is [YOUR RELATIONSHIP]. Write a genuine thank you that is specific about what I am grateful for rather than generic, feels sincere rather than formulaic, and where natural keeps the relationship warm for the future. Keep it short, since a brief genuine thank you beats a long performative one. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the thank you email.
description: Write a specific, genuine thank you email that does not feel formulaic.
useCase: Use this to thank someone properly. Being specific about what you are grateful for makes a thank you feel genuine rather than obligatory, and these small notes quietly build the goodwill that leads to referrals and repeat work, which most people skip sending entirely.

title: Write an Apology Email
promptText: Help me write an apology email for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What happened is [WHAT WENT WRONG], who it affected is [WHO], and what I am doing about it is [YOUR FIX OR RESPONSE]. Write an apology that takes clear responsibility without excessive self flagellation or defensive explaining, acknowledges the actual impact on them, states what I am doing to fix it and prevent it recurring, and moves forward constructively. Avoid the non apology of sorry you feel that way. Own it and move on. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the apology email.
description: Write an apology that owns the mistake without excessive grovelling or excuses.
useCase: Use this when you need to apologize professionally. Taking clear responsibility and focusing on the fix, rather than defensive explaining or excessive grovelling, is what actually repairs trust, and handling a mistake well often leaves a client with more confidence in you than if it had never happened.

title: Write an Email Delivering Bad News
promptText: Help me write an email delivering bad news for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The bad news is [WHAT YOU NEED TO TELL THEM, E.G. A DELAY, A PROBLEM, A PRICE INCREASE, DECLINING SOMETHING], the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE], and any context or solution is [WHAT YOU CAN OFFER, OR WRITE NOTHING]. Write an email that delivers the news clearly and early rather than burying it, explains briefly without over justifying, offers a solution or path forward where possible, and respects them enough to be direct. Do not bury bad news at the bottom of a cheerful email. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the email.
description: Deliver bad news clearly and early rather than burying it in padding.
useCase: Use this when you must tell someone something they will not like. Delivering it clearly and early respects them and preserves trust, since burying bad news under cheerful padding is transparent and makes the recipient trust you less, while directness paired with a path forward usually lands better than expected.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED TO TELL THEM, E.G. A DELAY, A PROBLEM, A PRICE INCREASE, DECLINING SOMETHING
WHAT YOU CAN OFFER, OR WRITE NOTHING

title: Write an Email Saying No
promptText: Help me write an email declining something for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I am declining is [WHAT YOU ARE SAYING NO TO], the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and my reason is [YOUR REASON, OR WRITE PREFER NOT TO SAY]. Write a clear no that is unambiguous rather than a soft maybe, stays warm and respectful, gives a brief reason without over explaining or making excuses, and where appropriate offers an alternative or points them elsewhere. Do not leave false hope or invite negotiation I do not want. Give me the email.
description: Write a clear, warm no that does not leave false hope or invite negotiation.
useCase: Use this when you need to decline something. A clear warm no is kinder than a vague maybe that leaves someone waiting, and over explaining tends to invite negotiation, so a brief reason and a firm answer serves both sides better than a softened response that is not actually a yes.
variables: WHAT YOU ARE SAYING NO TO
WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP
YOUR REASON, OR WRITE PREFER NOT TO SAY

title: Write an Email Sharing Good News
promptText: Help me write an email sharing good news for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The news is [THE GOOD NEWS], the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE], and what I want from it is [YOUR GOAL, E.G. JUST SHARING, BUILDING MOMENTUM, PROMPTING NEXT STEPS]. Write an email that leads with the good news, conveys genuine energy without overhyping, notes anything they need to know or do next, and where natural strengthens the relationship. Keep it warm and brief. My tone is [TONE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write an email sharing good news with genuine energy and a clear next step.
useCase: Use this to share a win or positive update. Leading with the news and conveying genuine energy without overhyping lands well, and these emails are worth sending since positive touchpoints build the relationship warmth that makes future asks and renewals easier.
variables: YOUR GOAL, E.G. JUST SHARING, BUILDING MOMENTUM, PROMPTING NEXT STEPS

title: Build a Library of Email Templates
promptText: Help me build a library of reusable email templates for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The emails I write over and over are [YOUR REPEATED EMAILS, E.G. INQUIRIES, QUOTES, ONBOARDING, FOLLOW UPS, INVOICES]. For each, draft a template with clear fill in the blank spots for what changes each time, written in my voice and covering what that email needs to accomplish. Note where personalization matters so they do not feel canned. The goal is to stop rewriting the same emails from scratch. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the template library.
description: Build reusable templates for the emails you write over and over.
useCase: Use this once to stop rewriting the same emails. Templates for your recurring messages save real time and ensure your best wording gets used every time rather than whatever you improvise when tired, while noting where personalization matters keeps them from feeling canned.
variables: YOUR REPEATED EMAILS, E.G. INQUIRIES, QUOTES, ONBOARDING, FOLLOW UPS, INVOICES

title: Respond to an Inquiry
promptText: Help me respond to an inquiry from a potential customer in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is their inquiry: [PASTE THEIR MESSAGE], and my service is [WHAT YOU OFFER]. Write a response that answers what they actually asked, conveys competence and warmth, gives them what they need to move forward, asks any question I need answered, and proposes a clear next step. Respond promptly and helpfully, since the first reply shapes whether they choose me. Do not dump my entire pitch on them. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the response.
description: Respond to a customer inquiry helpfully with a clear next step.
useCase: Use this when a potential customer reaches out. The first reply shapes whether they choose you, so answering what they actually asked and proposing a clear next step converts better than a generic pitch dump, which reads as though you did not read their message.

title: Define Your Email Voice
promptText: Help me define a consistent voice for my emails in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My audience is [WHO YOU EMAIL], how I want to come across is [YOUR DESIRED IMPRESSION, E.G. WARM AND PROFESSIONAL, DIRECT AND EFFICIENT, FRIENDLY AND CASUAL], and here are examples of emails I have written: [PASTE A FEW OF YOUR EMAILS, OR WRITE NONE]. Help me define my email voice: the tone, level of formality, typical greetings and sign offs, sentence style, and what to avoid. Give me a short voice guide I can apply consistently so my emails sound like me rather than varying with my mood. Give me the email voice guide.
description: Define a consistent email voice so your messages sound like you every time.
useCase: Use this to make your emails consistently sound like you. A defined voice covering tone, formality, and sign offs keeps your emails coherent rather than swinging between stiff and casual depending on your mood, which is what makes a small business feel professional and recognizable.
variables: YOUR DESIRED IMPRESSION, E.G. WARM AND PROFESSIONAL, DIRECT AND EFFICIENT, FRIENDLY AND CASUAL
PASTE A FEW OF YOUR EMAILS, OR WRITE NONE

title: Draft an Email You Are Dreading
promptText: Help me write an email I have been putting off in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [DESCRIBE THE SITUATION AND WHY IT IS HARD], the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE], and what I need to say is [WHAT NEEDS SAYING]. Help me draft it: what to actually say, how to be honest without being harsh, how to handle my own discomfort without letting it make the email worse, and how to keep it professional. Being direct is usually less painful than the avoidance. Note anything I should not say. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the email.
description: Draft the difficult email you have been avoiding, honestly and professionally.
useCase: Use this for the email you keep putting off. Getting it drafted honestly and professionally breaks the avoidance, and being direct is almost always less painful than the delay, since the situation rarely improves while you postpone and the discomfort compounds.
variables: DESCRIBE THE SITUATION AND WHY IT IS HARD

title: Get a Quick Email Draft Fast
promptText: I need a quick email. It is to [WHO], about [WHAT IT IS ABOUT], and I want them to [DESIRED ACTION]. Give me a short clear professional email with a subject line, ready to send with minor edits. Keep it concise, no padding. Just give me a solid email I can send right now.
description: Get a fast, concise, ready to send email draft.
useCase: Use this when you just need an email written and sent. A quick clear draft gets it off your list without deliberation, which matters on a busy day when the alternative is the email sitting unwritten for hours while you avoid the small friction of starting it.

title: Proofread an Email Before Sending
promptText: Proofread this email before I send it: [PASTE YOUR EMAIL]. Check for typos, grammar errors, awkward phrasing, wrong names or details, and anything that would look careless or unprofessional. Fix what needs fixing while keeping my voice and tone intact rather than rewriting it into something generic. Flag anything that reads oddly. Give me the corrected email and note what you changed.
description: Proofread an email for errors while keeping your voice intact.
useCase: Use this before sending an email where a typo would look bad. Catching errors and awkward phrasing while preserving your voice protects your professionalism, since a misspelled name or sloppy sentence in a client email quietly undermines the impression you are working to build.

title: Write a Meeting Request Email
promptText: Help me write an email requesting a meeting for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], the purpose is [WHY YOU WANT TO MEET], and what I want out of it is [YOUR GOAL]. Write a short email that states clearly why I want to meet and what is in it for them, proposes a specific length so they know the commitment, offers concrete times or an easy way to book, and makes saying yes simple. Keep it brief, since a long meeting request signals a long meeting. My tone is [TONE]. Give me a subject line and the email.
description: Write a short meeting request that makes the purpose and commitment clear.
useCase: Use this when asking someone for a meeting. Stating the purpose, the length, and what is in it for them makes yes easy, since vague open ended requests get ignored, and naming a specific short duration reassures a busy person that this will not eat their afternoon.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Clarify Why the Meeting Should Happen
promptText: Help me clarify the purpose of a meeting I want to request in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I want to meet [WHO] because [YOUR ROUGH REASON], and what I hope comes out of it is [YOUR HOPED OUTCOME]. Help me sharpen this: what the meeting is genuinely for, what specific outcome would make it worthwhile, whether the purpose is clear enough to state in one sentence, and how to frame it so it sounds worth their time rather than vague. A meeting request without a clear purpose usually gets declined or wasted. Give me a clear one sentence purpose and how to frame it.
description: Sharpen a vague meeting purpose into one clear sentence worth someone's time.
useCase: Use this before requesting a meeting you have not fully thought through. Sharpening the purpose into one clear sentence is what gets it accepted and makes it productive, since a request that cannot state its own purpose gets declined, and a meeting without one wastes everyone's time even when accepted.

title: Check Whether a Meeting Is Needed at All
promptText: Help me decide whether I actually need a meeting or whether this could be handled another way in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I need is [WHAT YOU NEED TO ACCOMPLISH OR DECIDE], the people involved are [WHO], and the context is [ANY CONTEXT]. Help me assess honestly: whether a live conversation genuinely adds something here, or whether an email, a message, a shared document, or a short recorded update would do it faster for everyone. If a meeting is warranted, say what makes it so. If not, suggest the better alternative. Do not default to yes. Give me an honest recommendation.
description: Honestly check whether a meeting is needed or could be handled async.
useCase: Use this before putting a meeting on anyone's calendar. Honestly checking whether a live conversation adds anything, or whether an email or shared doc would be faster, protects your time and theirs, since much of what becomes a meeting could have been resolved in writing in a fraction of the time.
variables: WHAT YOU NEED TO ACCOMPLISH OR DECIDE

title: Propose an Alternative to a Meeting
promptText: Help me propose handling something without a meeting in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Someone wants to meet about [WHAT THEY WANT TO MEET ABOUT], and I think it could be handled by [YOUR PROPOSED ALTERNATIVE, E.G. EMAIL, A DOC, A QUICK MESSAGE]. Write a message that suggests the alternative without seeming dismissive or unwilling to engage, frames it as saving them time rather than avoiding them, offers to meet if the alternative does not resolve it, and keeps the relationship warm. Declining a meeting gracefully is a skill. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the message.
description: Propose handling something async without seeming dismissive or unwilling.
useCase: Use this when someone requests a meeting that does not need to be one. Suggesting an alternative framed as saving their time, while offering to meet if it does not resolve, protects your calendar without damaging the relationship, which is what makes declining a meeting gracefully a genuinely useful skill.
variables: WHAT THEY WANT TO MEET ABOUT
YOUR PROPOSED ALTERNATIVE, E.G. EMAIL, A DOC, A QUICK MESSAGE

title: Propose Meeting Times Without the Back and Forth
promptText: Help me propose meeting times in a way that ends the scheduling back and forth in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My availability is [YOUR AVAILABILITY], the meeting length is [HOW LONG], and the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND WHERE, IF TIMEZONES MATTER]. Write wording that offers a few specific concrete options rather than asking when works for you, handles timezones clearly if relevant, gives an easy fallback if none work, and makes it a one reply decision. Vague availability questions cause the endless email tennis. Give me the scheduling wording.
description: Propose specific times so scheduling takes one reply instead of five emails.
useCase: Use this to end scheduling ping pong. Offering a few concrete options rather than asking when works for you turns scheduling into a one reply decision, since open ended availability questions force the other person to do the work and generate the email tennis everyone hates.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHERE, IF TIMEZONES MATTER

title: Frame a Meeting as Worth Their Time
promptText: Help me frame a meeting request so it sounds worth the other person's time in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I want to meet [WHO THEY ARE], the meeting is about [THE TOPIC], and honestly what is in it for them is [THE BENEFIT TO THEM, OR WRITE HELP ME FIND IT]. Help me frame it around their interest rather than mine: what they would gain, why it is relevant to them, and how to convey that briefly and honestly without overselling. If the meeting mainly benefits me, help me be upfront about that rather than dressing it up. Give me the framing and wording.
description: Frame a meeting request around their benefit rather than yours.
useCase: Use this when requesting time from someone busy or senior. Framing the meeting around what they gain, rather than what you want, is what earns a yes, and being upfront when the meeting mainly benefits you reads as more honest than a thin pretext they will see through anyway.
variables: THE BENEFIT TO THEM, OR WRITE HELP ME FIND IT

title: Request a Meeting with a Stranger
promptText: Help me request a meeting with someone who does not know me, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE AND WHY YOU WANT TO MEET THEM], what I know about them is [ANY CONTEXT], and my ask is [WHAT YOU WANT]. Write a short request that opens with something specific to them rather than a generic pitch, explains briefly why I am reaching out to them specifically, asks for a small amount of time rather than an hour, and makes it easy to say yes or no. Keep it short and respectful of the fact they owe me nothing. Give me a subject line and the request.
description: Request a meeting from a stranger with a small, specific, respectful ask.
useCase: Use this to ask a stranger for time. Opening with something specific to them and asking for a small amount of time rather than an hour respects that they owe you nothing, which is exactly why short specific requests get accepted while generic pitches for an hour get deleted.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHY YOU WANT TO MEET THEM

title: Follow Up on an Ignored Meeting Request
promptText: Help me follow up on a meeting request that got no reply in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I asked [WHO] about [WHAT YOU ASKED FOR] on [WHEN YOU ASKED], and I have heard nothing. Write a brief follow up that assumes good faith rather than sounding annoyed, adds something useful rather than just bumping the thread, makes it even easier to respond, and gives them an easy out if they are not interested. Keep it light and short. Note how many follow ups is reasonable before letting it go. Give me the follow up message.
description: Follow up on an unanswered meeting request without sounding annoyed.
useCase: Use this when a meeting request gets no reply. A light follow up that assumes good faith and offers an easy out often gets a response, since most silence is a busy inbox rather than rejection, though knowing when to stop matters as much as sending the first nudge.

title: Decline a Meeting Request Gracefully
promptText: Help me decline a meeting request in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The request is [WHAT THEY WANT TO MEET ABOUT], the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and my reason is [YOUR REASON, E.G. NO TIME, NOT A FIT, NOT NEEDED, OR PREFER NOT TO SAY]. Write a message that declines clearly without leaving false hope, stays warm and respectful, gives a brief reason without over explaining, and where appropriate offers an alternative such as a shorter call, an async option, or another time. A clear no is kinder than an indefinite maybe. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the message.
description: Decline a meeting clearly and warmly without leaving false hope.
useCase: Use this to say no to a meeting. A clear warm decline is kinder than an indefinite maybe that leaves someone chasing you, and offering a smaller alternative where appropriate lets you protect your time without shutting the relationship down entirely.
variables: WHAT THEY WANT TO MEET ABOUT
WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP
YOUR REASON, E.G. NO TIME, NOT A FIT, NOT NEEDED, OR PREFER NOT TO SAY

title: Reschedule a Meeting Gracefully
promptText: Help me reschedule a meeting in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The meeting was [WHAT AND WHEN], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], my reason is [YOUR REASON, OR WRITE PREFER NOT TO DETAIL], and how much notice I am giving is [HOW MUCH NOTICE]. Write a message that apologizes briefly without grovelling, proposes specific alternative times immediately rather than leaving it open, and keeps it easy for them. Note how the approach should differ if I am cancelling last minute versus with plenty of notice. Rescheduling is normal, but leaving it vague is what annoys people. Give me the message.
description: Reschedule a meeting with a brief apology and immediate alternative times.
useCase: Use this when you need to move a meeting. A brief apology paired with specific alternative times handles it cleanly, since what actually annoys people is not the reschedule but being left to chase you for a new time, and last minute changes need more care than early ones.
variables: YOUR REASON, OR WRITE PREFER NOT TO DETAIL

title: Cancel a Meeting Professionally
promptText: Help me cancel a meeting in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The meeting was [WHAT AND WHEN], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], my reason is [YOUR REASON], and whether I want to reschedule is [RESCHEDULE OR CANCEL ENTIRELY]. Write a message that cancels clearly and promptly, apologizes proportionately to the disruption caused, explains briefly, and either proposes a new time or closes it out cleanly so they are not left wondering. Cancelling late deserves a real apology, cancelling early just needs clarity. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the message.
description: Cancel a meeting clearly with an apology proportionate to the disruption.
useCase: Use this when cancelling a meeting. Cancelling promptly and clearly, with an apology sized to the actual disruption, handles it well, and either proposing a new time or closing it out cleanly prevents the limbo where the other person does not know whether to expect you to follow up.

title: Confirm a Meeting Before It Happens
promptText: Help me write a meeting confirmation message for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The meeting is [WHAT, WHEN, AND HOW LONG], with [WHO], and it is happening [WHERE OR ON WHAT PLATFORM]. Write a brief confirmation that restates the time clearly including timezone if relevant, confirms the location or link, notes what we will cover so they arrive prepared, and mentions anything they should bring or think about. Keep it short. A confirmation reduces no shows and makes the meeting more productive. Give me the confirmation message.
description: Confirm a meeting with the time, link, and what will be covered.
useCase: Use this after a meeting is booked. A brief confirmation restating the time, link, and agenda reduces no shows and means people arrive prepared rather than asking what this is about in the first five minutes, which is where a lot of meeting time quietly disappears.

title: Send a Meeting Reminder
promptText: Help me write a short reminder for an upcoming meeting in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The meeting is [WHAT AND WHEN], with [WHO], and it is [WHERE OR THE LINK]. Write a brief friendly reminder that restates the time and link, keeps it light rather than nagging, and includes anything they need to have ready. Keep it very short. A quick reminder the day before meaningfully cuts no shows. Give me the reminder message.
description: Send a short, friendly meeting reminder that cuts no shows.
useCase: Use this the day before a meeting. A brief reminder with the time and link meaningfully reduces no shows, which protects the slot you reserved, and keeping it light rather than nagging means it reads as helpful rather than as chasing someone who already committed.

title: Follow Up After a No Show
promptText: Help me follow up after someone missed a meeting in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The meeting was [WHAT AND WHEN], the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and whether I still want to meet is [DO YOU STILL WANT TO, OR ARE YOU DONE]. Write a message that assumes good faith rather than sounding annoyed, checks whether everything is okay, offers to reschedule if I still want to, and keeps the door open or closes it gracefully. Note when a no show is worth another attempt versus a signal to move on. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the message.
description: Follow up after a no show without sounding annoyed, and know when to move on.
useCase: Use this when someone misses a meeting. Assuming good faith rather than firing off an annoyed note is usually right since things genuinely come up, though recognizing when a no show is a signal to stop investing matters too, especially with a prospect who was never that interested.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP
DO YOU STILL WANT TO, OR ARE YOU DONE

title: Ask for a Shorter Meeting
promptText: Help me ask to shorten a meeting in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The meeting is [WHAT AND HOW LONG IT IS SCHEDULED FOR], with [WHO], and I think it needs [HOW LONG YOU THINK IT NEEDS]. Write a message that proposes the shorter length without seeming dismissive of them or the topic, frames it as keeping things focused rather than as me being too busy for them, and suggests how to make the shorter time work such as a tight agenda. Most meetings expand to fill the time booked. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the message.
description: Ask to shorten a meeting without seeming dismissive of the person or topic.
useCase: Use this when a meeting is booked for longer than it needs. Proposing a shorter slot framed as keeping things focused protects your time without insulting anyone, and it works because meetings genuinely expand to fill whatever was booked regardless of what the topic actually requires.
variables: WHAT AND HOW LONG IT IS SCHEDULED FOR
HOW LONG YOU THINK IT NEEDS

title: Request a Discovery Call with a Prospect
promptText: Help me request a discovery call with a potential client for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The prospect is [WHO THEY ARE AND HOW THEY CAME TO YOU], my service is [WHAT YOU OFFER], and what I want from the call is [YOUR GOAL]. Write a message that proposes a short call framed around understanding their situation rather than pitching at them, sets a clear expectation of what the call is and how long, makes booking easy, and removes any sense of a hard sell. People avoid calls they think are pitches. My tone is [TONE]. Give me a subject line and the message.
description: Request a discovery call framed around them, not as a disguised pitch.
useCase: Use this to get a prospect on a call. Framing it around understanding their situation rather than pitching, with a clear short duration, removes the resistance people have to sales calls, since the fear of being pitched at for an hour is exactly what makes prospects avoid booking.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND HOW THEY CAME TO YOU

title: Include an Agenda in a Meeting Request
promptText: Help me include a brief agenda in my meeting request for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The meeting is about [THE TOPIC], with [WHO], for [HOW LONG], and what needs to be covered is [WHAT TO COVER]. Write a short agenda to include in the request that shows the meeting has a plan and will not ramble, sets the expectation of what we will and will not cover, and helps them prepare. Keep it to a few lines rather than a formal document. An agenda in the request itself makes acceptance more likely. Give me the request wording with the agenda.
description: Include a brief agenda in a meeting request to show it has a plan.
useCase: Use this to make a meeting request more likely to be accepted. A few lines of agenda signal that the meeting has a plan and will not ramble, which reassures a busy person, and it also means both sides arrive knowing what will and will not be covered.

title: Request a Meeting with Someone Senior
promptText: Help me request a meeting with someone significantly more senior or busy than me, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], my connection to them is [YOUR CONNECTION, OR WRITE NONE], and what I want is [YOUR ASK]. Write a request that is respectful of their time without being obsequious, gets to the point in the first line, asks for a small specific amount of time, makes clear why them specifically, and gives an easy no. Confidence without arrogance is the balance. Do not apologize for existing. Give me a subject line and the request.
description: Request time from someone senior with confidence rather than obsequiousness.
useCase: Use this when asking someone senior or very busy for time. Getting to the point immediately and asking for something small reads as confident and respectful, while excessive apologizing for taking their time actually makes the ask harder to say yes to by signalling you do not think it is worth it.

title: Propose a Recurring Meeting
promptText: Help me propose a recurring meeting in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The meeting would be [WHAT IT IS FOR], with [WHO], at a cadence of [HOW OFTEN], lasting [HOW LONG]. Write a proposal that explains why a regular rhythm helps rather than ad hoc meetings, proposes the cadence and length, notes what each session would cover, and builds in a check to review whether it is still worth it later. Recurring meetings tend to outlive their usefulness, so proposing a review point upfront is smart. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the proposal message.
description: Propose a recurring meeting with a built in point to review whether it still earns its place.
useCase: Use this when proposing a standing meeting. Explaining why a rhythm helps and building in a review point upfront is smart, since recurring meetings reliably outlive their usefulness and become far harder to cancel once they are simply part of everyone's week.

title: Fix the Tone of a Meeting Request
promptText: Help me fix the tone of my meeting request in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], and the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP]. Check whether it reads as I intend: whether it sounds desperate, presumptuous, apologetic, or demanding, whether the ask is proportionate to the relationship, and whether it makes yes easy. Point out the specific phrases that land badly and why. Meeting requests fail on tone more often than on substance. Give me the feedback and a fixed version.
description: Fix a meeting request that reads as desperate, presumptuous, or apologetic.
useCase: Use this before sending a meeting request that matters. Meeting requests fail on tone more often than substance, and catching that your draft reads as desperate or presumptuous, and which specific phrases cause it, is what turns an ignored request into an accepted one.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Share a Booking Link Without Seeming Cold
promptText: Help me share my booking link in a way that does not feel cold or presumptuous in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and the meeting is [WHAT IT IS FOR]. Write wording that offers the booking link as a convenience for them rather than as me being too important to check my calendar, keeps the human warmth around it, and offers to just pick a time manually if they prefer. Note when a booking link is appropriate versus when it reads as dismissive, such as with someone senior. Give me the wording.
description: Share a booking link warmly rather than in a way that reads as dismissive.
useCase: Use this when sending someone a booking link. Framing it as a convenience for them, with an offer to just pick a time manually, keeps the warmth, and knowing when a link reads as dismissive matters, since sending one to a senior contact can land as though their time matters less than yours.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Get a Quick Meeting Request Fast
promptText: I need a quick meeting request. It is to [WHO], about [THE PURPOSE], and I want [HOW LONG] of their time. Give me a short clear message with a subject line that states the purpose, proposes a specific length, and offers a couple of times. Keep it brief and ready to send. Just give me a solid meeting request I can send right now.
description: Get a fast, ready to send meeting request with a clear purpose and times.
useCase: Use this when you just need a meeting request written and sent. A quick message stating the purpose, length, and a couple of times covers everything a good request needs without deliberation, which is all most meeting requests actually require.

title: Decide When to Ask for the Meeting
promptText: Help me decide when and how to time a meeting request in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], the context is [WHAT IS GOING ON, E.G. YOU JUST MET THEM, THEY JUST BOUGHT, YOU HAVE BEEN TALKING A WHILE], and my ask is [WHAT YOU WANT]. Help me think about timing: whether now is the right moment or too early, what would make them more receptive, whether to build more rapport first, and how the timing changes the framing. Asking too early can burn a contact who would have said yes later. Give me a recommendation on timing and framing.
description: Decide whether now is the right moment to ask for a meeting.
useCase: Use this before asking someone for time, especially a new contact. Timing matters because asking too early can burn a relationship that would have produced a yes later, and thinking about what would make them receptive is often worth more than perfecting the wording of a premature ask.
variables: WHAT IS GOING ON, E.G. YOU JUST MET THEM, THEY JUST BOUGHT, YOU HAVE BEEN TALKING A WHILE

title: Write a Follow Up Email
promptText: Help me write a follow up email for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I originally contacted [WHO THEY ARE] about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT] on [WHEN], and I have heard nothing back. Write a short follow up that assumes good faith rather than sounding impatient, adds something useful rather than just saying bumping this, restates the ask clearly and simply, and makes it easy to reply even with a no. Keep it brief, since a long follow up on an ignored email rarely lands better. My tone is [TONE]. Give me a subject line and the follow up.
description: Write a brief follow up that assumes good faith and makes replying easy.
useCase: Use this when someone has not replied. A short follow up that adds something useful rather than just bumping the thread gets responses, since most silence is a busy inbox rather than rejection, and making it easy to reply with a no often produces the clarity you actually needed.

title: Decide When to Follow Up
promptText: Help me decide when to follow up in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I contacted [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP] about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT] on [WHEN], and the urgency is [HOW TIME SENSITIVE IT IS]. Help me judge: whether enough time has passed to follow up without seeming impatient, how the right timing changes based on who they are and what I asked for, whether the urgency justifies moving sooner, and what a sensible gap looks like here. Following up too fast reads as pushy, too slow and the thread goes cold. Give me a recommendation on timing and why.
description: Judge the right moment to follow up without seeming pushy or letting it go cold.
useCase: Use this when unsure whether it is too soon to chase. Timing depends on who they are and what you asked, and getting it right matters because following up too fast reads as pushy while waiting too long lets the thread go cold and the context fade from their memory.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Add Value to a Follow Up Instead of Just Bumping
promptText: Help me make my follow up worth opening rather than just a bump in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am following up with [WHO THEY ARE] about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT], and what I could offer or share is [ANYTHING USEFUL YOU COULD ADD, OR WRITE HELP ME FIND SOMETHING]. Help me find a genuine reason to reappear in their inbox: a useful resource, a relevant update, a new angle, an answer to a likely objection, or something that makes the follow up feel helpful rather than needy. If I have nothing to add, tell me and suggest how to follow up honestly anyway. Give me the value adding follow up.
description: Find a genuine reason to follow up rather than sending an empty bump.
useCase: Use this when your follow up would otherwise just say checking in. Adding something genuinely useful makes reappearing in someone's inbox feel helpful rather than needy, and being told honestly when you have nothing to add is more useful than manufacturing a thin pretext they will see through.
variables: ANYTHING USEFUL YOU COULD ADD, OR WRITE HELP ME FIND SOMETHING

title: Replace Just Checking In with Something Better
promptText: Help me write a follow up without falling back on just checking in, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am following up with [WHO THEY ARE] about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT], and the situation is [WHERE THINGS STAND]. Explain briefly why just checking in and similar phrases are weak, since they put the work on the recipient and give them nothing to respond to, then write a follow up that has an actual purpose, a specific question they can answer easily, or a concrete next step. Give them something to react to rather than a vague nudge. Give me the improved follow up.
description: Write a follow up with an actual purpose instead of a vague checking in nudge.
useCase: Use this to stop sending follow ups that give the recipient nothing to work with. Just checking in puts the effort on them and invites no response, whereas a specific question or concrete next step is easy to answer, which is why purposeful follow ups reliably outperform vague nudges.

title: Plan a Follow Up Sequence
promptText: Help me plan a follow up sequence for my [BUSINESS TYPE] so I stop following up randomly or forgetting entirely. The situation is [WHAT YOU FOLLOW UP ON, E.G. PROPOSALS, INQUIRIES, QUOTES], and my typical contact is [WHO THEY ARE]. Plan a sequence: how many follow ups is reasonable, the gap between each, how the message and tone should shift across them, what each one should add, and a clear final one that closes the loop. Give me the messages I can reuse. A planned sequence means I follow up consistently without agonizing each time. Give me the sequence with timing.
description: Plan a reusable follow up sequence with timing so you never chase randomly.
useCase: Use this once to systematize following up. A planned sequence with set gaps and reusable messages means you follow up consistently without deciding from scratch each time, which is how a solo operator stops losing deals to the simple failure of never sending the second email.
variables: WHAT YOU FOLLOW UP ON, E.G. PROPOSALS, INQUIRIES, QUOTES

title: Write a Final Follow Up That Closes the Loop
promptText: Help me write a final follow up to close out a thread in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I have followed up with [WHO THEY ARE] about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT] [HOW MANY TIMES] with no reply. Write a final message that closes the loop gracefully: notes I will stop following up, keeps the door genuinely open for them to come back, avoids guilt tripping or passive aggression, and leaves a good impression. Note that a clean closing message often gets a reply when nothing else did, since it releases the pressure. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the final follow up.
description: Write a graceful final follow up that closes the loop without guilt tripping.
useCase: Use this to end a thread you have chased enough. A clean closing message that releases the pressure often gets a reply when nothing else did, and even when it does not, it leaves a good impression rather than the resentment a passive aggressive last word would create.

title: Decide When to Stop Following Up
promptText: Help me decide whether to keep following up or let it go in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I have contacted [WHO THEY ARE] about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT] [HOW MANY TIMES] over [HOW LONG], and the signals so far are [ANY RESPONSE OR SILENCE]. Help me assess honestly: whether the silence means no, whether more follow up would help or just annoy them, whether this is worth more of my energy, and when persistence turns into pestering. Be honest rather than encouraging me to keep chasing something dead. Note that my energy may be better spent elsewhere. Give me an honest read on whether to continue.
description: Honestly assess whether to keep following up or let a dead thread go.
useCase: Use this when you are not sure whether to keep chasing. An honest read on whether silence means no protects both your energy and the relationship, since persistence has a point past which it becomes pestering, and chasing a dead thread costs attention better spent on prospects who are actually responsive.

title: Sharpen the Question in Your Follow Up
promptText: Help me sharpen the question in my follow up so it is easy to answer in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], and what I actually need to know is [WHAT YOU NEED]. Help me turn a vague open question into one they can answer in seconds: making it specific, making it a yes or no or a pick one where possible, and removing anything that requires them to compose a thoughtful reply. The easier a question is to answer, the more likely it gets answered. Give me the sharpened follow up.
description: Sharpen a vague follow up question into one answerable in seconds.
useCase: Use this when your follow up asks something that requires effort to answer. Turning an open question into a yes or no or a pick one dramatically raises reply rates, since a busy person will answer a five second question but postpone one that needs a thoughtful composed response.

title: Follow Up on a Proposal or Quote
promptText: Help me follow up on a proposal or quote in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I sent [WHAT YOU SENT] to [WHO THEY ARE] on [WHEN], and where things stood was [THEIR LAST SIGNAL OR INTEREST LEVEL]. Write a follow up that checks in without sounding desperate for the work, offers to answer questions or clarify anything, gently surfaces any hesitation they might have, and makes it easy to say yes, no, or not yet. Note that a no is more useful to me than indefinite silence. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the proposal follow up.
description: Follow up on a proposal without sounding desperate, and surface hesitation.
useCase: Use this after sending a proposal that has gone quiet. Surfacing hesitation and making it easy to say no gets you the answer you need, since a clear no lets you move on and stop mentally holding the deal open, which is more valuable than the false hope of indefinite silence.
variables: THEIR LAST SIGNAL OR INTEREST LEVEL

title: Surface the Real Objection in a Follow Up
promptText: Help me surface the real reason someone has gone quiet in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I was talking to [WHO THEY ARE] about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT], and they went silent after [WHAT HAPPENED LAST]. Write a follow up that gently invites the honest answer: naming the likely hesitations myself so they do not have to raise them, giving explicit permission to say it is not right, and making honesty easier than avoidance. People often go quiet because saying no feels awkward. Naming the obstacle myself often unlocks the truth. Give me the follow up.
description: Write a follow up that names likely hesitations and invites an honest answer.
useCase: Use this when someone has gone quiet and you suspect an unspoken objection. Naming the likely hesitation yourself and giving permission to say no makes honesty easier than avoidance, which is why this often unlocks the real answer when direct questions have failed.

title: Follow Up After Being Ghosted
promptText: Help me handle being ghosted in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT WAS HAPPENING], we were [HOW FAR ALONG YOU WERE], and they went silent [WHEN]. Write a follow up that does not let my frustration leak through, gives them an easy way back without awkwardness, and either revives the thread or closes it cleanly. Note that ghosting usually says more about their situation than about me, and that taking it personally leads to messages I regret. Help me respond well rather than emotionally. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the follow up.
description: Follow up after being ghosted without letting frustration leak into the message.
useCase: Use this when someone disappears mid conversation. Responding without letting frustration show gives them an easy way back and protects the relationship, since ghosting usually reflects their own situation rather than anything about you, and a message written from irritation is one you tend to regret.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT WAS HAPPENING

title: Write a Reminder About Something Owed
promptText: Help me remind someone about something they owe me in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I am waiting on is [WHAT THEY OWE YOU, E.G. FEEDBACK, MATERIALS, A DECISION, ACCESS], the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and it is holding up [WHAT IT IS BLOCKING]. Write a reminder that is friendly but clear, explains what their delay is holding up so the urgency is understood, restates exactly what I need, and makes it easy to deliver. Note that people often do not realize their delay is blocking anything. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the reminder.
description: Remind someone about something owed by making clear what their delay is blocking.
useCase: Use this when waiting on feedback, materials, or a decision. Explaining what the delay is actually holding up creates urgency without pressure, and it works because people frequently do not realize their slow reply is blocking anything at all on your end.
variables: WHAT THEY OWE YOU, E.G. FEEDBACK, MATERIALS, A DECISION, ACCESS
WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Escalate a Reminder When Nothing Moves
promptText: Help me escalate a reminder in my [BUSINESS TYPE] since gentle ones have not worked. I need [WHAT YOU NEED] from [WHO THEY ARE], I have reminded them [HOW MANY TIMES], and the consequence of continued delay is [WHAT HAPPENS IF THEY KEEP STALLING, E.G. DEADLINE SLIPS, WORK PAUSES]. Write a firmer reminder that states the situation plainly, names the real consequence factually rather than as a threat, sets a clear deadline, and stays professional rather than annoyed. Firmness and hostility are different things. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the escalated reminder.
description: Escalate a reminder firmly and factually without turning hostile.
useCase: Use this when gentle reminders have been ignored. Stating the situation plainly with a real consequence and a clear deadline usually moves things, and keeping it factual rather than annoyed matters because firmness gets results while hostility just damages the relationship you still need.
variables: WHAT HAPPENS IF THEY KEEP STALLING, E.G. DEADLINE SLIPS, WORK PAUSES

title: Check a Follow Up for Passive Aggression
promptText: Help me check my follow up for tone problems before I send it in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], and honestly I am feeling [YOUR ACTUAL FEELING, E.G. FRUSTRATED, ANXIOUS, ANNOYED]. Check whether my frustration or anxiety is leaking through: passive aggressive phrasing, pointed reminders of how long it has been, guilt tripping, or desperation. Point out the specific phrases that give it away and rewrite them. Note that as I mentioned and per my last email are classic tells. Give me the feedback and a clean version.
description: Catch passive aggression or desperation leaking into a follow up before sending.
useCase: Use this before sending a follow up written while frustrated. Catching the phrases that give away irritation, like the classic as I mentioned or per my last email, prevents a message that damages the relationship, since the recipient hears the edge even when you thought you had hidden it.
variables: YOUR ACTUAL FEELING, E.G. FRUSTRATED, ANXIOUS, ANNOYED

title: Set Up a Follow Up Tracking System
promptText: Help me set up a simple system to track what I am waiting on in my [BUSINESS TYPE], since things slip through the cracks. What I follow up on is [WHAT YOU CHASE, E.G. PROPOSALS, INVOICES, FEEDBACK, INQUIRIES], and how I track it now is [HOW YOU TRACK, OR WRITE I DON'T]. Help me build a lightweight system: what to record for each open thread, how to see at a glance what needs a nudge, when to trigger each follow up, and how to keep it current without much effort. Note that most lost opportunities are forgotten rather than rejected. Give me a simple follow up tracking system.
description: Set up a lightweight system so open threads never slip through the cracks.
useCase: Use this because most lost opportunities are forgotten rather than rejected. A simple system showing what is outstanding and when to nudge means you actually follow up, which is where a solo operator quietly loses work, not to competitors but to threads they simply never returned to.
variables: WHAT YOU CHASE, E.G. PROPOSALS, INVOICES, FEEDBACK, INQUIRIES
HOW YOU TRACK, OR WRITE I DON'T

title: Write a Batch of Follow Ups Fast
promptText: Help me write follow ups for several stalled threads at once in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here are the threads I need to follow up on: [LIST EACH ONE, WHO IT IS, WHAT IT WAS ABOUT, AND HOW LONG IT HAS BEEN]. For each, write a short tailored follow up that fits that specific situation rather than a copy paste template. Keep each brief and easy to reply to. Batching this saves me from putting it off. Give me the follow up messages, one per thread.
description: Write tailored follow ups for several stalled threads in one batch.
useCase: Use this to clear a backlog of follow ups in one sitting. Batching them tailored rather than copy pasted gets them all sent, which matters because follow ups are easy to postpone individually and a solo operator often has several sitting undone at once.
variables: LIST EACH ONE, WHO IT IS, WHAT IT WAS ABOUT, AND HOW LONG IT HAS BEEN

title: Follow Up After a Meeting or Call
promptText: Help me follow up after a meeting or call in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I met with [WHO THEY ARE], we discussed [WHAT YOU COVERED], and what was agreed or left open is [OUTCOMES AND OPEN ITEMS]. Write a follow up that thanks them briefly, confirms what was agreed so there is a record, names who is doing what by when, and keeps momentum toward the next step. Send it promptly while it is fresh. Note that the follow up is where a good meeting either converts into action or quietly dies. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the follow up.
description: Follow up after a meeting to confirm what was agreed and keep momentum.
useCase: Use this right after a meeting. A prompt follow up confirming what was agreed and who does what turns a good conversation into action, since without it the energy fades and the meeting quietly produces nothing, which is how most well intentioned meetings actually end.

title: Follow Up on a Warm Lead
promptText: Help me follow up on a warm lead in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], they showed interest by [WHAT THEY DID, E.G. INQUIRED, DOWNLOADED SOMETHING, HAD A CALL], and that was [WHEN]. Write a follow up that references their specific interest rather than starting cold, picks up naturally where we left off, offers a clear easy next step, and does not squander the warmth by being generic. A warm lead that goes unfollowed becomes a cold one fast. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the follow up.
description: Follow up on a warm lead before the interest goes cold.
useCase: Use this when someone showed interest and you have not followed up. Referencing their specific interest and offering an easy next step capitalizes on warmth that decays quickly, since a lead that felt promising last week has usually moved on mentally by the time you get around to it.
variables: WHAT THEY DID, E.G. INQUIRED, DOWNLOADED SOMETHING, HAD A CALL

title: Follow Up After a Long Gap
promptText: Help me follow up with someone after a long silence in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR HISTORY], it has been [HOW LONG], and why I am reaching out now is [YOUR REASON]. Write a message that acknowledges the gap without over apologizing for it, gives a genuine reason for reconnecting now, reminds them of the context without assuming they remember everything, and makes it easy to pick things back up. Note that a long gap is normal and does not need a lengthy excuse. My tone is [TONE]. Give me the message.
description: Reconnect after a long silence without over apologizing for the gap.
useCase: Use this to revive a contact you have not spoken to in months. Acknowledging the gap briefly and giving a genuine reason for reconnecting works better than an elaborate apology, since long silences are normal in business and treating it as awkward makes it awkward.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR HISTORY

title: Switch Channels When Email Is Ignored
promptText: Help me decide whether to try a different channel since my emails are being ignored in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], I have emailed [HOW MANY TIMES], and the other channels available are [OTHER WAYS TO REACH THEM, E.G. PHONE, MESSAGE, SOCIAL, IN PERSON]. Help me judge: whether switching channels is reasonable or would feel intrusive, which channel fits the relationship, and how to approach it without seeming like I am hunting them down. Then write the message for that channel. Note that changing channels can work but can also cross a line. Give me a recommendation and the message.
description: Judge whether switching channels is reasonable or would feel intrusive.
useCase: Use this when email is not getting through. Switching channels sometimes works because your email simply got buried, but it can also feel like being hunted down, so judging whether it fits the relationship matters before you appear somewhere they did not invite you.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP
OTHER WAYS TO REACH THEM, E.G. PHONE, MESSAGE, SOCIAL, IN PERSON

title: Write Subject Lines for Follow Ups
promptText: Help me write a subject line for a follow up email. The original email was about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT], the recipient is [WHO THEY ARE], and this is follow up number [WHICH FOLLOW UP]. Give me several options: some that reply on the existing thread and some that start fresh, and explain when each approach is better. Avoid guilt inducing subject lines like following up again. Make them clear and easy to act on. Mark the strongest. Give me the subject line options.
description: Write follow up subject lines that get opened without inducing guilt.
useCase: Use this to get a follow up opened. The subject line decides it, and knowing when to reply on the existing thread versus start fresh matters, while guilt inducing lines like following up again make the recipient feel bad and less likely to engage, not more.

title: Cut a Follow Up Down to Size
promptText: Help me trim my follow up, it is too long. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT]. Cut it hard: remove the throat clearing, the over explaining, the apologizing for following up, and the restating of everything from the original email. Keep only what is needed to prompt a reply. Note that a follow up should be shorter than the original email, not longer. Tell me what you cut. Give me the trimmed version.
description: Trim a bloated follow up down to only what prompts a reply.
useCase: Use this when your follow up has grown longer than the email it is chasing. Cutting the apologizing and re explaining leaves only what prompts a reply, which works because a follow up should be shorter than the original, and a long one signals you are trying too hard.

title: Get a Quick Follow Up Message Fast
promptText: I need a quick follow up message. It is to [WHO], about [WHAT IT WAS ABOUT], it has been [HOW LONG], and I want them to [WHAT YOU WANT THEM TO DO]. Give me a short friendly follow up with a subject line, ready to send. Keep it brief and easy to reply to, no guilt tripping or padding. Just give me a solid follow up I can send right now.
description: Get a fast, brief, ready to send follow up message.
useCase: Use this when you just need the follow up written and sent. A short ready to send message gets it off your list, which matters because follow ups are the easiest thing to postpone and the cost of not sending one is usually higher than sending an imperfect one.
variables: WHAT YOU WANT THEM TO DO

title: Get Over the Fear of Following Up
promptText: Help me get past my reluctance to follow up in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What holds me back is [YOUR HESITATION, E.G. FEELING PUSHY, FEAR OF ANNOYING THEM, FEAR OF REJECTION, FEELING DESPERATE], and the situation is [WHAT YOU ARE AVOIDING FOLLOWING UP ON]. Help me reframe this honestly: whether my fear matches reality, why most people expect and do not mind reasonable follow up, what actually reads as pushy versus what is normal, and how to follow up in a way I feel fine about. Be honest rather than just reassuring me. Note that not following up costs more than following up ever does. Give me a reframe and a way forward.
description: Reframe the fear of following up and find an approach you feel fine about.
useCase: Use this when reluctance stops you following up at all. Most people expect and do not mind reasonable follow up, and understanding what actually reads as pushy versus normal helps, since not following up costs a solo operator far more work than the imagined annoyance ever would.
variables: YOUR HESITATION, E.G. FEELING PUSHY, FEAR OF ANNOYING THEM, FEAR OF REJECTION, FEELING DESPERATE
WHAT YOU ARE AVOIDING FOLLOWING UP ON

title: Plan a Difficult Conversation
promptText: Help me plan a difficult conversation I need to have in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [DESCRIBE WHAT HAPPENED AND WITH WHOM], what I need to address is [THE ISSUE], and the outcome I want is [YOUR GOAL]. Help me prepare: what the conversation is genuinely about versus what is just frustration, the two or three points that actually need making, how to open it, what I want to walk away with, and what I am willing to compromise on. Help me separate the essential from the venting. Going in prepared is the difference between resolving something and making it worse. Give me a plan for the conversation.
description: Plan a difficult conversation so you address what matters, not just vent.
useCase: Use this before any conversation you are dreading. Separating the real issue from accumulated frustration and knowing your two or three points beforehand is what makes the difference between resolving something and inflaming it, since unprepared hard conversations tend to sprawl into everything that has ever bothered you.
variables: DESCRIBE WHAT HAPPENED AND WITH WHOM

title: Open a Difficult Conversation Well
promptText: Help me open a difficult conversation in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The topic is [WHAT YOU NEED TO DISCUSS], the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and how I expect them to react is [THEIR LIKELY REACTION]. Help me write an opening that names the topic directly rather than burying it in pleasantries, avoids putting them instantly on the defensive, signals I want to resolve this rather than attack them, and invites their side. The first thirty seconds set whether this goes well or badly. Do not sandwich it in fake compliments, people see through it. Give me a few opening options.
description: Open a hard conversation directly without instantly triggering defensiveness.
useCase: Use this because the first thirty seconds decide how a hard conversation goes. Naming the topic directly while signalling you want to resolve rather than attack keeps them listening, whereas burying it in pleasantries or a fake compliment sandwich makes people brace for what is coming anyway.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Separate the Facts from Your Feelings
promptText: Help me separate the facts from my feelings before I address something in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is everything I am thinking about the situation: [WRITE IT ALL OUT, UNFILTERED]. Help me sort it: what actually happened as observable fact, what is my interpretation or assumption, what I am feeling and why, and what of this genuinely needs raising versus what I need to let go. Then tell me what the core issue actually is beneath the frustration. I am not asking you to validate me, I am asking you to help me see it clearly. Give me the facts, the interpretations, and the real issue.
description: Sort facts from interpretation and feeling to find the real issue underneath.
useCase: Use this to think clearly before a charged conversation. Sorting what actually happened from what you assumed and felt reveals the real issue, which is often smaller and more solvable than the story built around it, and going in with facts rather than accumulated interpretation makes resolution far more likely.

title: Check Your Assumptions Before Confronting Someone
promptText: Help me check my assumptions before I confront someone in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I believe happened is [WHAT YOU THINK IS GOING ON], and what I actually know for certain is [THE HARD FACTS]. Challenge me honestly: what am I assuming about their intent that I cannot actually know, what innocent explanations could account for this, what information might I be missing, and what would I want to verify before raising it. Push back on my story rather than agreeing with it. Note that most conflict comes from assuming intent behind behavior. Give me the assumptions I am making and what to check first.
description: Challenge your assumptions about someone's intent before confronting them.
useCase: Use this before confronting anyone. Most conflict comes from assuming intent behind behavior, and being honestly challenged on what you actually know versus what you have inferred prevents the confrontation you would regret once the innocent explanation surfaces afterward.
variables: WHAT YOU THINK IS GOING ON

title: Rehearse a Difficult Conversation
promptText: Help me rehearse a difficult conversation before I have it in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [THE SITUATION], the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY ARE LIKE], and what I plan to say is [YOUR INTENDED APPROACH]. Play out how this might go: how they might realistically respond including defensively or emotionally, what they might say that would throw me, how I could respond to each, and where I might get pulled off track. Then tell me what to hold firm on and where flexibility is fine. Rehearsing the hard parts means I am not improvising under pressure. Give me the likely responses and how to handle each.
description: Rehearse the likely reactions so you are not improvising under pressure.
useCase: Use this before a conversation you expect to go badly. Playing out the defensive or emotional responses and how you would answer means you are not improvising in the moment, which is when people either freeze, over concede, or say the thing they cannot take back.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY ARE LIKE

title: Decide Whether to Have It Live or in Writing
promptText: Help me decide whether to handle a difficult matter live or in writing in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [THE SITUATION], the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and my instinct is [WHAT YOU WANT TO DO]. Help me weigh it honestly: whether a live conversation would resolve this faster and more humanly, whether writing gives useful clarity and a record, whether I am choosing writing to avoid discomfort rather than because it is better, and whether the matter needs a record for practical reasons. Be honest if I am hiding behind email. Give me a recommendation and why.
description: Decide honestly whether a hard matter needs a live conversation or writing.
useCase: Use this when tempted to send an email instead of picking up the phone. Being honest about whether writing serves the situation or just spares you discomfort matters, since many issues resolve in five live minutes that would take four painful email rounds, though some genuinely need a record.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Give Critical Feedback Without Crushing Someone
promptText: Help me give critical feedback in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE, E.G. A CONTRACTOR, A COLLABORATOR, A HELPER], the problem is [WHAT NEEDS TO CHANGE], and the impact is [WHY IT MATTERS]. Help me deliver it well: focusing on the specific behavior or work rather than their character, being concrete rather than vague, explaining the impact, being clear about what needs to change, and leaving them able to act on it rather than just feeling bad. Be direct without being harsh. Vague kindness helps nobody improve. Give me how to deliver the feedback.
description: Give critical feedback that is specific, actionable, and not personal.
useCase: Use this when someone's work needs to change. Focusing on specific behavior with concrete examples and clear expectations lets them actually improve, whereas vague softened feedback leaves people confused about what was wrong and changes nothing, which serves neither side.
variables: WHO THEY ARE, E.G. A CONTRACTOR, A COLLABORATOR, A HELPER

title: Respond Well to Criticism You Received
promptText: Help me respond to criticism I have received in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is what they said: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE THE CRITICISM], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], and my honest reaction is [HOW YOU ACTUALLY FEEL ABOUT IT]. Help me: separate what is fair and worth acting on from what is not, decide what to acknowledge, and respond in a way that is not defensive but also does not just capitulate on things I disagree with. Help me hear it without either collapsing or fighting. Then draft a response. Be honest if the criticism has merit. Give me an honest read and a response.
description: Respond to criticism without collapsing into apology or fighting back.
useCase: Use this when criticism stings and you are about to reply. Sorting what is fair from what is not, then responding without defensiveness or capitulation, protects both the relationship and your position, since the two instinctive reactions of fighting back or over apologizing both tend to make things worse.
variables: HOW YOU ACTUALLY FEEL ABOUT IT

title: Respond to an Angry Message
promptText: Help me respond to an angry message in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is what they sent: [PASTE THEIR MESSAGE], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], and the actual situation is [WHAT REALLY HAPPENED FROM YOUR VIEW]. Help me respond in a way that de escalates rather than matches their energy: acknowledging their frustration without necessarily accepting every accusation, addressing the legitimate part, staying calm and factual, and moving toward resolution. Note that responding immediately while angry is almost always a mistake and waiting an hour rarely hurts. Give me the response and tell me if I should wait before sending.
description: Respond to an angry message calmly and de escalate rather than match their energy.
useCase: Use this when someone sends you something furious. Acknowledging the frustration while staying factual de escalates, and being told to wait before sending is often the most valuable part, since responding immediately while angry is how a recoverable situation becomes a permanent one.
variables: WHAT REALLY HAPPENED FROM YOUR VIEW

title: De Escalate a Conflict That Is Heating Up
promptText: Help me de escalate a conflict that is getting worse in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [WHAT IS HAPPENING AND HOW IT ESCALATED], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], and where it stands is [THE CURRENT STATE]. Help me cool this down: what is actually driving their reaction, what I might be doing that is fuelling it, how to break the escalation cycle, what to concede or acknowledge that costs me little, and how to move toward resolution. Note that being right is not the same as resolving it. Tell me honestly if I am contributing to the escalation. Give me a de escalation approach.
description: Break an escalation cycle and move a heating conflict toward resolution.
useCase: Use this when a disagreement is spiralling. Understanding what drives their reaction and what you might be fuelling breaks the cycle, and the honest observation that being right is not the same as resolving it is often what a solo operator needs when a client conflict is consuming their week.
variables: WHAT IS HAPPENING AND HOW IT ESCALATED

title: Disagree Professionally
promptText: Help me disagree with someone professionally in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. They think [THEIR POSITION], I think [YOUR POSITION], the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and the stakes are [WHAT DEPENDS ON THIS]. Help me express disagreement in a way that is clear rather than mushy, acknowledges what is valid in their view, explains my reasoning rather than just asserting, and keeps it about the substance rather than about who is right. Note that I can disagree firmly and still be respectful. Also tell me honestly if their position has merit I am dismissing. Give me the disagreement.
description: Disagree clearly and respectfully while keeping it about substance.
useCase: Use this when you need to push back on a client or collaborator. Disagreeing clearly while acknowledging what is valid in their view keeps it about substance rather than ego, and being told honestly when their position has merit you are dismissing is worth more than help defending a weak stance.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Say It Without Triggering Defensiveness
promptText: Help me say something difficult without immediately putting the person on the defensive in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is what I want to say: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT OR DESCRIBE YOUR POINT], and the person is [WHO THEY ARE]. Rewrite it to reduce defensiveness: removing accusatory framing and always or never statements, describing the impact rather than assigning blame, focusing on the specific instance rather than a pattern of character, and inviting their perspective. Keep the substance fully intact, I am not trying to soften the message into meaninglessness. Point out what was triggering defensiveness. Give me the revised version.
description: Rephrase a hard message to reduce defensiveness without weakening it.
useCase: Use this when your point is right but the phrasing will make them stop listening. Removing accusatory framing and character generalizations while keeping the substance intact means the message actually lands, since a defensive person stops processing what you are saying and starts building their rebuttal.
variables: PASTE YOUR DRAFT OR DESCRIBE YOUR POINT

title: Set a Boundary with Someone
promptText: Help me set a boundary with someone in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], what is happening is [THE BEHAVIOR, E.G. MESSAGING AT ALL HOURS, EXPANDING SCOPE, EXPECTING INSTANT REPLIES], and how long it has been going on is [HOW LONG]. Help me address it: how to raise it without a big confrontation, how to state the boundary clearly rather than hinting, how to hold it if they push back, and how to do this without damaging the relationship. Note that boundaries set late are harder but still worth setting, and that hinting never works. Give me how to have this conversation.
description: Set a boundary clearly rather than hinting, and hold it under pushback.
useCase: Use this when someone has been overstepping and you have let it slide. Stating the boundary clearly rather than hinting is what actually changes behavior, and while boundaries set late are harder than ones set at the start, they still work, whereas continued hinting reliably does not.
variables: THE BEHAVIOR, E.G. MESSAGING AT ALL HOURS, EXPANDING SCOPE, EXPECTING INSTANT REPLIES

title: Hold a Boundary When They Push Back
promptText: Help me hold a boundary I set that is being tested in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The boundary I set was [WHAT YOU SAID], and they are pushing back by [HOW THEY ARE TESTING OR IGNORING IT]. Help me respond: restating the boundary calmly without re justifying or over explaining it, not being drawn into debating whether it is reasonable, staying warm while being immovable, and what to do if they keep pushing. Note that over explaining a boundary invites negotiation of it. A boundary only exists if I hold it. Give me the response.
description: Hold a boundary calmly under pushback without re justifying it.
useCase: Use this when someone tests a boundary you set. Restating it calmly without re justifying is what makes it real, since over explaining invites negotiation and a boundary that collapses under the first push teaches the other person that your limits are opening offers rather than limits.
variables: HOW THEY ARE TESTING OR IGNORING IT

title: Address Someone's Behavior
promptText: Help me address someone's behavior in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], the behavior is [WHAT THEY ARE DOING], and its effect is [THE IMPACT ON YOU OR THE WORK]. Help me raise it: describing the specific behavior and its effect rather than characterizing them as a person, giving a concrete example rather than speaking generally, saying clearly what I need instead, and staying open to their side since there may be context I lack. Note the difference between you are unreliable and the last three deadlines moved. Give me how to raise it.
description: Address behavior with specific examples rather than characterizing the person.
useCase: Use this when someone's conduct is affecting the work. Describing the specific behavior and its effect rather than labelling them makes the conversation actionable, since you are unreliable invites a fight while the last three deadlines moved invites a solution.
variables: THE IMPACT ON YOU OR THE WORK

title: Tell a Client Your Prices Are Going Up
promptText: Help me tell an existing client my prices are increasing in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The client is [WHO THEY ARE AND HOW LONG YOU HAVE WORKED TOGETHER], the increase is [FROM WHAT TO WHAT], and it takes effect [WHEN]. Help me have this conversation: stating the increase clearly and confidently without a long apologetic justification, giving reasonable notice, briefly noting the reason without over explaining, and handling their likely pushback. Note that over apologizing signals the price is negotiable. Also help me decide what I will do if they say no. Give me how to have this conversation.
description: Tell a client about a price increase confidently without over apologizing.
useCase: Use this when raising prices on an existing client. Stating it clearly with reasonable notice and without a long apology is what makes it stick, since over explaining signals the number is negotiable, and deciding in advance what you will do if they refuse means you are not improvising under pressure.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND HOW LONG YOU HAVE WORKED TOGETHER

title: Have an Awkward Money Conversation
promptText: Help me have an awkward conversation about money in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [THE MONEY ISSUE, E.G. THEY OWE YOU, THEY WANT A DISCOUNT, THEY QUESTIONED YOUR RATE, THEY EXPECT FREE WORK], and the person is [WHO THEY ARE]. Help me handle it: how to talk about money without apologizing for needing to be paid, how to state my position plainly, how to respond to pushback, and how to stay warm while being firm. Note that discomfort with money conversations is common and costs people real income. Do not help me cave, help me be fair and clear. Give me how to handle it.
description: Handle an awkward money conversation firmly without apologizing for being paid.
useCase: Use this when money needs discussing and you would rather avoid it. Talking about money plainly without apology is a learnable skill, and it matters because discomfort here costs solo operators real income, whether through unclaimed work, accepted discounts, or invoices they never chased.
variables: THE MONEY ISSUE, E.G. THEY OWE YOU, THEY WANT A DISCOUNT, THEY QUESTIONED YOUR RATE, THEY EXPECT FREE WORK

title: Tell Someone You Made a Mistake
promptText: Help me tell someone I made a mistake in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What happened is [WHAT YOU DID OR MISSED], who it affects is [WHO AND HOW], and what I can do about it is [YOUR FIX, OR WRITE NOT SURE YET]. Help me have this conversation: telling them promptly rather than hoping they do not notice, owning it without excessive grovelling or defensive explaining, being clear about the impact and the fix, and rebuilding confidence. Note that how a mistake is handled often matters more than the mistake, and that hiding it is what actually destroys trust. Give me how to tell them.
description: Own a mistake promptly without grovelling or defensive explaining.
useCase: Use this when you have messed up and are dreading telling them. Owning it promptly with a clear fix often leaves a client more confident in you than if nothing had gone wrong, whereas hiding it and hoping they miss it is what actually destroys trust when it inevitably surfaces.
variables: YOUR FIX, OR WRITE NOT SURE YET

title: Deliver Bad News in a Conversation
promptText: Help me deliver bad news in a live conversation in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The news is [WHAT YOU HAVE TO TELL THEM], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], and how they will likely take it is [THEIR LIKELY REACTION]. Help me prepare: how to lead with it rather than building up to it, how much explanation is enough without over justifying, how to handle their reaction in the moment, what to do if they get upset, and how to move to what happens next. Note that people handle bad news better when it is direct and they can see a path forward. Give me how to deliver it and handle the reaction.
description: Deliver bad news directly and handle the reaction in the moment.
useCase: Use this before telling someone something they will not want to hear. Leading with the news rather than building up to it, and being ready for the reaction, works because people handle bad news better when it is direct and paired with a path forward than when they sense it coming through five minutes of preamble.
variables: WHAT YOU HAVE TO TELL THEM

title: End a Client Relationship
promptText: Help me end a client relationship in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The client is [WHO THEY ARE AND HOW LONG], my reason is [WHY YOU WANT TO END IT], and where the work stands is [ANY WORK IN PROGRESS OR OBLIGATIONS]. Help me do it well: how much reason to give without inviting debate, handling any work in progress and obligations fairly, giving reasonable notice, keeping it professional so they do not badmouth me, and being firm if they push to stay. Note that a bad fit client costs more than the revenue is worth. Do not help me stay out of guilt. Give me how to end it.
description: End a client relationship professionally and firmly without inviting debate.
useCase: Use this when a client is costing you more than they are worth. Ending it professionally with fair handling of work in progress protects your reputation while freeing your capacity, since a draining client blocks the good work you would otherwise take and guilt is a poor reason to keep one.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND HOW LONG
WHY YOU WANT TO END IT
ANY WORK IN PROGRESS OR OBLIGATIONS

title: Clear Up a Misunderstanding
promptText: Help me clear up a misunderstanding in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I think happened is [YOUR VERSION], what they seem to think happened is [THEIR APPARENT VERSION], and the friction it has caused is [THE RESULT].
