Help me untangle it: where the wires likely crossed, how to clarify without making it about who was wrong, how to acknowledge my part if I contributed, and how to get both of us back on the same page. Note that insisting on being right usually prolongs a misunderstanding rather than resolving it. Give me how to clear it up.
description: Untangle a misunderstanding without making it about who was wrong.
useCase: Use this when crossed wires have caused friction. Clarifying without assigning blame and acknowledging your own part resolves it quickly, since insisting on being right prolongs the misunderstanding, and most crossed wires trace to an ambiguity neither side noticed rather than anyone's fault.

title: Check What Not to Say
promptText: Help me check what I should not say in a difficult conversation in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [THE SITUATION], and here is what I am planning to say: [YOUR PLANNED APPROACH OR DRAFT]. Flag the landmines: anything that would escalate rather than resolve, anything I would regret, anything that sounds like an accusation or ultimatum I do not mean, anything I cannot back up, and anything better left unsaid even if true. Note that in a charged moment the satisfying line is usually the costly one. Give me what to cut and why.
description: Flag the lines you would regret before a difficult conversation.
useCase: Use this before a charged conversation to catch what you should not say. Flagging the escalating or regrettable lines protects you, since in a heated moment the most satisfying thing to say is almost always the most expensive, and it cannot be unsaid once it lands.

title: Repair Things After a Conversation Went Badly
promptText: Help me repair things after a conversation went badly in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What happened is [HOW IT WENT WRONG], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], and my part in it was [WHAT YOU DID OR SAID, HONESTLY]. Help me: acknowledge my part without abandoning my actual position, reopen the conversation more constructively, address any damage, and find a way forward. Note that a badly handled conversation is usually recoverable if addressed soon rather than left to harden. Do not help me pretend it was entirely their fault. Give me a repair message and approach.
description: Repair a relationship after a conversation went badly, without abandoning your position.
useCase: Use this when a hard conversation went wrong. Acknowledging your part while holding your actual position reopens things constructively, and moving soon matters because a badly handled conversation is usually recoverable early but hardens into a permanent rift if left alone.
variables: WHAT YOU DID OR SAID, HONESTLY

title: Follow Up After a Difficult Conversation
promptText: Help me follow up after a difficult conversation in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The conversation was about [THE TOPIC], with [WHO THEY ARE], and what was agreed or left unresolved is [THE OUTCOME]. Write a follow up that confirms what was agreed so there is a shared record, keeps the tone warm rather than clinical, does not relitigate what was discussed, and moves things forward. Note that a good follow up prevents the conversation being remembered differently by each side, and that a warm one signals the relationship survived. Give me the follow up.
description: Follow up after a hard conversation to confirm what was agreed and signal the relationship survived.
useCase: Use this after a difficult conversation resolves. Confirming what was agreed prevents each side remembering it differently, and keeping the tone warm rather than clinical signals the relationship survived, which is often the thing both people are quietly wondering afterward.

title: Stop Avoiding a Conversation You Need to Have
promptText: Help me stop avoiding a conversation I know I need to have in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The conversation is [WHAT YOU ARE AVOIDING], I have been putting it off for [HOW LONG], and what I am afraid of is [YOUR FEAR]. Help me honestly: examine whether my fear matches the likely reality, weigh what the avoidance is costing me versus what the conversation would, break it into a manageable first step, and find the smallest version of it I could start with. Be honest rather than just encouraging. Note that avoided problems almost always grow. Give me an honest read and a first step.
description: Break the avoidance on a conversation you keep putting off.
useCase: Use this when you have been dodging something for weeks. Honestly weighing what the avoidance costs against what the conversation would, and finding a manageable first step, breaks the paralysis, since avoided problems almost always grow while the dreaded conversation is usually smaller than the dread.

title: Get a Quick Script for a Hard Conversation
promptText: I need a quick script for a difficult conversation. The situation is [THE SITUATION], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], and what I need to say is [WHAT NEEDS SAYING]. Give me a short practical script: how to open, the key points to make, and how to close. Keep it concise and usable, no long analysis. Note anything I should avoid saying. Just give me a script I can use right now.
description: Get a fast, practical script for a difficult conversation.
useCase: Use this when a hard conversation is imminent and you need words now. A short script covering the open, key points, and close gives you something to hold onto, which matters because the moment you most need clarity is usually the moment your mind goes blank.

title: Ask Someone for an Introduction
promptText: Help me ask someone for an introduction in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person I am asking is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], who I want to meet is [THE TARGET AND WHY], and my reason is [WHAT YOU WANT FROM THE INTRO]. Write a request that makes it easy to say yes: explaining briefly why I want the intro, why it might be worthwhile for the target too, and giving them an easy way to decline without awkwardness. Note that asking for an intro spends someone's relationship capital, so make it low effort for them. Give me the request message.
description: Ask for an introduction in a way that makes it easy to say yes or decline.
useCase: Use this when you want someone to connect you to a contact. Making it low effort and giving an easy out respects that an intro spends their relationship capital, which is why the requests that succeed are the ones that do the thinking for the connector rather than handing them a vague ask.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP
WHAT YOU WANT FROM THE INTRO

title: Write a Forwardable Intro Blurb
promptText: Help me write a forwardable blurb someone can paste when introducing me, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I do [WHAT YOU DO], the person being introduced to me is [THE TARGET], and the reason for the intro is [THE REASON]. Write a short paragraph the connector can forward as is, covering who I am, why I am worth their time, and what the ask is, written so it sounds like the connector could send it without editing. Note that giving someone a forwardable blurb dramatically raises the chance the intro actually happens, since it removes all the work. Give me the blurb.
description: Write a paste ready blurb that makes an introduction effortless to forward.
useCase: Use this alongside any intro request. Handing the connector something they can forward without editing dramatically raises the odds the intro happens, since most intros die not from unwillingness but from the small friction of having to compose the message themselves.

title: Make an Introduction Between Two People
promptText: Help me introduce two people to each other in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Person one is [WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY DO], person two is [WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY DO], and why they should meet is [THE REASON]. Write the intro email: framing why each is valuable to the other, giving enough context that neither has to ask what this is about, and handing the thread over cleanly so they can take it from there. Also explain the double opt in approach of checking with both first, and when it is worth doing versus when a direct intro is fine. Give me the intro email.
description: Introduce two people well, with context both need and a clean handover.
useCase: Use this when connecting two contacts. Framing why each is valuable to the other and handing the thread over cleanly makes the intro land, and understanding double opt in matters since introducing people without checking can put both in an awkward position they did not ask for.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY DO

title: Check Before Making an Introduction
promptText: Help me check with someone before introducing them to a contact, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person I want to introduce them to is [THE OTHER PERSON AND WHY], and my relationship with the person I am checking with is [YOUR RELATIONSHIP]. Write a short message asking whether they would welcome the intro, giving them enough context to decide, and making it genuinely easy to say no without explaining themselves. Note that checking first protects everyone and costs nothing, while a surprise intro can put someone in an awkward spot. Give me the permission check message.
description: Check whether someone welcomes an intro before making it, with an easy out.
useCase: Use this before connecting two people. Checking first costs nothing and protects everyone, since a surprise introduction can land someone in an obligation they did not want, and giving a genuine easy out means their yes actually means something.

title: Respond When Someone Introduces You
promptText: Help me respond to an introduction someone just made in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is the intro: [PASTE THE INTRO EMAIL], and what I want from the connection is [YOUR GOAL]. Write a reply that thanks the connector and moves them to bcc so the thread does not clutter their inbox, opens warmly with the new contact, references something specific from the intro, and proposes a clear easy next step. Note that responding promptly reflects well on the connector who vouched for me. Give me the reply.
description: Respond to an intro promptly, move the connector to bcc, and propose a next step.
useCase: Use this the moment an intro lands in your inbox. Replying promptly, moving the connector to bcc, and proposing a clear next step handles it properly, and it matters because slow or vague responses reflect badly on the person who spent their credibility vouching for you.

title: Thank Someone for an Introduction
promptText: Help me thank someone for making an introduction in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The connector is [WHO THEY ARE], they introduced me to [THE CONTACT], and what came of it is [THE OUTCOME, OR WRITE TOO EARLY TO TELL]. Write a thank you that is specific rather than generic, closes the loop by telling them what happened since connectors rarely hear back, and keeps the relationship warm. Note that reporting back is what makes someone willing to introduce you again, and almost nobody does it. Give me the thank you message.
description: Thank a connector and close the loop by reporting what came of the intro.
useCase: Use this after someone introduces you. Reporting back on what happened is what makes a connector willing to introduce you again, and almost nobody does it, which means this small message quietly sets you apart and keeps a valuable relationship warm.
variables: THE OUTCOME, OR WRITE TOO EARLY TO TELL

title: Decline an Introduction Gracefully
promptText: Help me decline an introduction in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [WHAT THEY ARE ASKING, E.G. TO INTRODUCE YOU TO SOMEONE, OR TO BE INTRODUCED], the person asking is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP], and my reason is [WHY YOU DO NOT WANT TO, E.G. NOT A GOOD FIT, DO NOT KNOW THEM WELL ENOUGH, PROTECTING THE RELATIONSHIP]. Write a decline that is warm and clear, gives a brief honest reason without a lecture, and where possible offers something else useful. Note that declining protects both my judgment and the contact's trust in me. Give me the message.
description: Decline an intro request warmly while protecting your judgment and relationships.
useCase: Use this when asked for an intro you should not make. Declining warmly with a brief reason protects your contact's trust in you, since every introduction you make is a small bet with your own credibility, and making one you doubt costs more than the awkwardness of saying no.
variables: WHAT THEY ARE ASKING, E.G. TO INTRODUCE YOU TO SOMEONE, OR TO BE INTRODUCED
WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP
WHY YOU DO NOT WANT TO, E.G. NOT A GOOD FIT, DO NOT KNOW THEM WELL ENOUGH, PROTECTING THE RELATIONSHIP

title: Write the First Message to a New Contact
promptText: Help me write the first real message to a new contact in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], how we connected is [HOW YOU MET OR WERE INTRODUCED], and what I want is [YOUR GOAL, OR WRITE JUST BUILDING THE RELATIONSHIP]. Write a message that references how we connected specifically, gives them a reason to engage, and proposes something concrete and small rather than a vague let us stay in touch. Note that let us keep in touch is where most new connections quietly die. Give me the first message.
description: Write a first message to a new contact that proposes something concrete.
useCase: Use this after meeting or being introduced to someone. Proposing something small and concrete rather than a vague let us stay in touch is what keeps a connection alive, since that phrase is where the overwhelming majority of promising new contacts quietly go to die.
variables: HOW YOU MET OR WERE INTRODUCED
YOUR GOAL, OR WRITE JUST BUILDING THE RELATIONSHIP

title: Follow Up After Meeting Someone at an Event
promptText: Help me follow up with someone I met at an event, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The event was [THE EVENT], the person is [WHO THEY ARE], what we talked about is [WHAT YOU DISCUSSED], and what I want is [YOUR GOAL, OR WRITE JUST TO CONNECT]. Write a follow up that references our specific conversation so they remember me among the dozens of people they met, sends promptly while I am still fresh in their mind, and proposes something small and concrete. Note that most event connections die because nobody follows up within the window where they still remember you. Give me the follow up.
description: Follow up after an event while they still remember who you are.
useCase: Use this within days of meeting someone at an event. Referencing your specific conversation is what separates you from the dozens of other people they met, and sending promptly matters because the window where they remember your face closes fast.
variables: YOUR GOAL, OR WRITE JUST TO CONNECT

title: Keep a Connection Warm Over Time
promptText: Help me keep a connection warm without being weird about it in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR HISTORY], the last contact was [WHEN AND ABOUT WHAT], and why I want to stay connected is [YOUR REASON]. Write a message that reappears naturally rather than out of nowhere, gives a genuine reason for reaching out such as something useful I can share or a relevant update, and asks nothing. Note that the connections worth having are maintained before you need them, and a message that only ever arrives with an ask trains people to dread your name. Give me the message.
description: Reappear naturally to keep a connection warm without asking for anything.
useCase: Use this to maintain a relationship you value. Reaching out with something useful and no ask is what keeps a connection alive, since the contacts worth having are maintained before you need them, and someone who only ever hears from you when you want something learns to expect it.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR HISTORY

title: Decide When to Ask for an Introduction
promptText: Help me judge whether now is the right time to ask someone for an introduction in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], our relationship is [HOW WELL YOU KNOW THEM AND HOW LONG], what I have given or done for them is [ANYTHING YOU HAVE CONTRIBUTED, OR WRITE NOTHING YET], and the ask is [WHAT YOU WANT]. Help me assess honestly: whether the relationship can carry this ask yet, whether asking now would spend goodwill I have not built, and what would need to happen first. Be honest if it is too early. Note that a premature ask can cost a relationship that would have delivered later. Give me an honest read.
description: Judge honestly whether a relationship can carry an intro ask yet.
useCase: Use this before asking someone to spend their credibility on you. An honest read on whether the relationship can carry the ask prevents the premature request that costs you a contact who would have said yes in six months, since asking too early is how promising connections quietly end.
variables: HOW WELL YOU KNOW THEM AND HOW LONG
ANYTHING YOU HAVE CONTRIBUTED, OR WRITE NOTHING YET

title: Give Before You Ask
promptText: Help me find a way to be useful to someone before I ask them for anything, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY DO], what I know about their situation is [ANYTHING YOU KNOW ABOUT THEIR GOALS OR PROBLEMS], and what I can offer is [YOUR SKILLS AND CONNECTIONS]. Help me identify something genuinely useful I could do for them: an introduction they would value, a resource, a relevant opportunity, or a specific piece of help. It must be genuinely useful rather than a transparent setup for an ask. If I have nothing real to offer, tell me honestly. Give me options and how to offer them.
description: Find something genuinely useful to offer someone before asking for anything.
useCase: Use this to build a relationship you may want something from later. Finding something genuinely useful to give first changes the dynamic entirely, though it only works when the offer is real, since a transparent setup for an ask is worse than no contact at all.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY DO
ANYTHING YOU KNOW ABOUT THEIR GOALS OR PROBLEMS

title: Offer an Introduction Nobody Asked For
promptText: Help me proactively offer an introduction I think would be valuable in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The two people are [PERSON ONE AND WHAT THEY NEED] and [PERSON TWO AND WHAT THEY OFFER], and why I think they should meet is [YOUR REASONING]. Write a message offering the intro to each: explaining why I thought of them, what I think is in it for them, and making it genuinely easy to decline. Note that offering a good unprompted intro is one of the highest value things I can do for a relationship, and it costs me almost nothing. Give me the offer messages.
description: Proactively offer a valuable introduction that neither party asked for.
useCase: Use this when you spot two contacts who should know each other. Offering a good unprompted intro is one of the highest value things you can do for a relationship and it costs you almost nothing, which is why the people who do it habitually end up with networks that work for them.
variables: PERSON ONE AND WHAT THEY NEED
PERSON TWO AND WHAT THEY OFFER

title: Track Introductions So They Do Not Die
promptText: Help me set up a simple way to track introductions in my [BUSINESS TYPE], since I lose track of them. What I need to track is [WHAT YOU WANT TO KEEP TABS ON, E.G. INTROS RECEIVED, INTROS OWED, CONNECTIONS TO MAINTAIN]. Help me build a lightweight system: what to record for each intro, when to follow up, when to report back to the connector, and how to see at a glance what needs attention. Note that an intro someone spent their credibility on and I then let die is worse than never asking, because it teaches them not to do it again. Give me a simple tracking system.
description: Track introductions so they do not quietly die after being made.
useCase: Use this if intros keep slipping through the cracks. A lightweight system for tracking what needs follow up and who needs reporting back protects your reputation with connectors, since an intro someone vouched for and you let die teaches them never to do it again.
variables: WHAT YOU WANT TO KEEP TABS ON, E.G. INTROS RECEIVED, INTROS OWED, CONNECTIONS TO MAINTAIN

title: Write a LinkedIn Connection Request
promptText: Help me write a LinkedIn connection request note for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], why I want to connect is [YOUR REASON], and our connection point is [HOW YOU KNOW OF THEM, E.G. MUTUAL CONTACT, THEIR POST, SAME INDUSTRY, EVENT]. Write a short note within the character limit that gives a specific reason for connecting, references something real rather than being generic, and does not pitch anything. Note that a connection request that pitches immediately gets ignored or reported, and that a blank request from a stranger usually gets declined. Give me the connection note.
description: Write a LinkedIn connection note that is specific and does not pitch.
useCase: Use this when connecting with someone on LinkedIn. A short specific note referencing something real gets accepted where a blank request from a stranger gets declined, and not pitching matters because an immediate sales message is the fastest way to get ignored or reported.
variables: HOW YOU KNOW OF THEM, E.G. MUTUAL CONTACT, THEIR POST, SAME INDUSTRY, EVENT

title: Start a Real Conversation After Connecting
promptText: Help me start an actual conversation with someone after they accepted my connection request, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], why I connected is [YOUR REASON], and what I want is [YOUR GOAL, OR WRITE JUST TO BUILD THE RELATIONSHIP]. Write a message that opens a genuine conversation rather than launching into a pitch, gives them something worth responding to, and does not ask for anything yet. Note that the accepted connection is permission to talk, not permission to sell, and that pitching immediately after connecting is why most people ignore new connections entirely. Give me the opening message.
description: Start a genuine conversation after connecting rather than launching into a pitch.
useCase: Use this after someone accepts your connection request. Opening a real conversation rather than pitching is what turns a connection into a relationship, since an accepted request is permission to talk and not permission to sell, and the immediate pitch is why most people ignore new connections.
variables: YOUR GOAL, OR WRITE JUST TO BUILD THE RELATIONSHIP

title: Introduce Yourself with No Mutual Connection
promptText: Help me introduce myself to someone with no mutual connection at all, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE], why I want to reach them is [YOUR REASON], and what I know about them is [ANYTHING SPECIFIC YOU KNOW ABOUT THEIR WORK]. Write an introduction that opens with something specific and genuine about their work rather than flattery, explains briefly why I am reaching out to them specifically rather than anyone, makes a small ask or none at all, and respects that they owe me nothing. Note that specificity is the only thing separating this from spam. Give me the introduction.
description: Introduce yourself cold with specificity rather than flattery or spam.
useCase: Use this when there is no mutual connection to lean on. Opening with something specific about their work and explaining why them rather than anyone is the only thing separating a cold introduction from spam, since generic outreach reads as a mail merge and gets treated like one.
variables: ANYTHING SPECIFIC YOU KNOW ABOUT THEIR WORK

title: Reconnect with Someone from Your Past
promptText: Help me reconnect with someone I have not spoken to in years, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The person is [WHO THEY ARE AND HOW YOU KNEW THEM], it has been [HOW LONG], and why I am reaching out is [YOUR REASON, HONESTLY]. Write a message that acknowledges the gap without an elaborate apology, reminds them who I am without assuming they remember details, is honest about why I am reaching out now, and does not pretend this is a purely social catch up if it is not. Note that people respect honesty about an ask far more than a transparently fake reconnection. Give me the message.
description: Reconnect after years honestly, without faking a social catch up.
useCase: Use this to revive a dormant contact. Being honest about why you are reaching out lands better than a fake social catch up that pivots to an ask in paragraph three, since people see through that immediately and it costs you the relationship you were trying to revive.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND HOW YOU KNEW THEM

title: Explain What You Do in One Line
promptText: Help me explain what I do in one clear line for introductions in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I actually do is [DESCRIBE YOUR WORK], who I do it for is [YOUR CLIENTS], and the result they get is [THE OUTCOME]. Write a few one line options that a normal person would understand immediately, avoid jargon and vague phrases like helping businesses grow, and make it specific enough that someone could tell whether they need me. Note that if my introduction line is vague, nobody can refer me because they cannot recognize who I am for. Give me the options and mark the strongest.
description: Write a one line explanation of what you do that people can actually repeat.
useCase: Use this because a vague self description makes you unreferrable. If people cannot immediately grasp who you help and how, they cannot recognize a fit when they meet one, which means every conversation about you dies rather than turning into a referral.

title: Make Yourself Easy to Refer
promptText: Help me make it easier for people to refer me in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I do is [YOUR WORK], my ideal client is [WHO YOU WANT], and the trigger that means someone needs me is [THE SITUATION THAT SIGNALS A FIT]. Help me articulate: the specific signals someone should watch for that mean a person needs me, how to explain that to my network so they can spot it, and what to give them so referring me is effortless. Note that people want to refer others but usually cannot because they do not know what to look for. Give me how to make myself referrable.
description: Make yourself easy to refer by naming the signals that mean someone needs you.
useCase: Use this to unlock referrals from people who already like you. Most people want to refer others but cannot because they do not know what to watch for, so naming the specific trigger that means someone needs you turns goodwill into actual introductions.
variables: THE SITUATION THAT SIGNALS A FIT

title: Check an Intro Message for Tone
promptText: Help me check my intro or networking message before I send it, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my draft: [PASTE YOUR DRAFT], and the person is [WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP]. Check whether it reads as I intend: whether it sounds desperate, transactional, sycophantic, or entitled, whether the ask is proportionate to the relationship, whether it does the work for them or hands them a chore, and whether a stranger would find it worth answering. Point out the specific phrases that land badly. Note that networking messages fail on tone more than on substance. Give me the feedback and a fixed version.
description: Check a networking message for desperation, entitlement, or sycophancy.
useCase: Use this before sending a message that matters. Networking messages fail on tone more than substance, and catching that yours reads as desperate, transactional, or entitled before it lands is the difference between a reply and a contact who quietly writes you off.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP

title: Handle an Introduction That Went Nowhere
promptText: Help me handle an introduction that went nowhere in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The connector is [WHO MADE THE INTRO], the contact is [WHO YOU WERE INTRODUCED TO], and what happened is [HOW IT FIZZLED, E.G. THEY NEVER REPLIED, IT WENT QUIET, IT WAS NOT A FIT]. Help me: decide whether to follow up with the contact once more or let it go, and report back to the connector honestly without making them feel their intro was wasted. Note that reporting back even on a dead intro is what keeps a connector willing to try again. Give me both messages.
description: Handle a dead introduction and report back to the connector honestly.
useCase: Use this when an intro fizzles out. Reporting back honestly even on a dead intro is what keeps a connector willing to try again, since silence leaves them wondering whether they wasted their credibility and quietly discourages them from helping next time.
variables: HOW IT FIZZLED, E.G. THEY NEVER REPLIED, IT WENT QUIET, IT WAS NOT A FIT

title: Get a Quick Intro Message Fast
promptText: I need a quick intro message. It is to [WHO], the context is [HOW YOU KNOW THEM OR WHY YOU ARE REACHING OUT], and what I want is [YOUR GOAL]. Give me a short warm message that gets to the point, references something specific, and proposes an easy next step. Keep it brief and ready to send, no filler. Just give me a solid intro message I can send right now.
description: Get a fast, ready to send introduction message.
useCase: Use this when you just need the intro written and sent. A short specific message with an easy next step covers what most introductions actually need, which matters because the intro you never send because you were overthinking the wording helps nobody.
variables: HOW YOU KNOW THEM OR WHY YOU ARE REACHING OUT

title: Network Without Feeling Like a Fraud
promptText: Help me approach networking without it feeling gross in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What puts me off is [YOUR HESITATION, E.G. FEELING TRANSACTIONAL, FEAR OF IMPOSING, HATING SMALL TALK, FEELING LIKE A FRAUD], and what I actually need is [WHAT YOU ARE TRYING TO ACHIEVE]. Help me honestly: reframe what networking actually is when done well, find an approach that fits how I am rather than forcing a persona, and identify what I could do that would not feel like a betrayal of myself. Be honest rather than just reassuring. Note that the transactional version people hate is not the only version. Give me a reframe and a first step.
description: Find an approach to networking that does not require becoming someone else.
useCase: Use this if networking feels gross and you avoid it entirely. Reframing what it actually is when done well, and finding an approach that fits your temperament rather than forcing a persona, matters because the transactional version most people picture is not the only one available.
variables: YOUR HESITATION, E.G. FEELING TRANSACTIONAL, FEAR OF IMPOSING, HATING SMALL TALK, FEELING LIKE A FRAUD
WHAT YOU ARE TRYING TO ACHIEVE

title: Write an Out of Office Message
promptText: Help me write an out of office message for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am away from [START DATE] to [END DATE], the reason is [REASON, OR WRITE PREFER NOT TO SAY], and what I want people to do meanwhile is [WHAT THEY SHOULD DO]. Write a message that states clearly when I am back, sets a realistic expectation of when they will hear from me rather than promising a fast reply I cannot deliver, tells them what to do if it is urgent, and sounds like a person rather than a corporate template. Keep it short. Give me the out of office message.
description: Write a clear out of office message that sets realistic expectations.
useCase: Use this before any time away. A message that states when you are back and sets a realistic reply expectation prevents the frustration of people waiting on you, and being honest about the timeline is better than promising a quick response you will not deliver.
variables: REASON, OR WRITE PREFER NOT TO SAY

title: Handle Being Away with Nobody to Cover You
promptText: Help me write an out of office message when there is nobody else to cover for me in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am away [WHEN AND FOR HOW LONG], and my clients are [WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY MIGHT NEED]. Most out of office advice assumes a team to redirect people to, and I do not have one. Help me write a message that is honest about this without sounding unprofessional or fragile, sets expectations clearly, tells people what genuinely constitutes an emergency versus what can wait, and does not apologize for taking time off. Note that being solo is a fact, not a failing. Give me the message.
description: Write an out of office message when there is genuinely nobody to redirect people to.
useCase: Use this when you are the whole business and there is no colleague to hand off to. Being honest about it without apologizing works better than the corporate template that promises a colleague who does not exist, since clients of solo operators already know and respect the directness.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT THEY MIGHT NEED

title: Define What Counts as Urgent While You Are Away
promptText: Help me define what counts as urgent in my out of office message for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My work is [WHAT YOU DO], what genuinely cannot wait is [REAL EMERGENCIES IN YOUR WORK], and what people wrongly treat as urgent is [WHAT THEY ESCALATE THAT COULD WAIT]. Help me write wording that names specifically what qualifies as urgent and how to reach me for those, while making clear that everything else waits until I am back. Note that if I say contact me for anything urgent without defining it, everyone decides their thing is urgent and I have no break at all. Give me the urgency wording.
description: Define specifically what counts as urgent so your time off is not eaten by everything.
useCase: Use this to protect actual time off. If you say contact me if urgent without defining urgent, everyone decides their thing qualifies and you have no break, whereas naming specifically what does and does not count gives people a standard to measure against.
variables: WHAT THEY ESCALATE THAT COULD WAIT

title: Write a Vacation Out of Office
promptText: Help me write a vacation out of office for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am away [DATES], whether I am checking email at all is [CHECKING OCCASIONALLY OR FULLY OFFLINE], and my clients are [WHO THEY ARE]. Write a message that is warm and human, states plainly whether I am reachable or not rather than being vague about it, gives a clear return date, and does not apologize for taking a holiday. My tone is [TONE]. Note that vagueness about whether I am checking invites people to try, so pick a position and state it. Give me the vacation message.
description: Write a warm vacation out of office that states plainly whether you are reachable.
useCase: Use this before a holiday. Stating plainly whether you are checking email or fully offline prevents the ambiguity that invites people to try anyway, and not apologizing for taking time off sets a healthier tone than a message that treats a holiday as an imposition.

title: Write an Out of Office for Illness
promptText: Help me write an out of office because I am unwell in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My situation is [HOW LONG YOU EXPECT TO BE OUT, OR WRITE NOT SURE], and how much I want to disclose is [YOUR PREFERENCE]. Write a message that communicates I am unavailable without oversharing medical details, handles the fact I may not know when I will be back, sets expectations for delayed replies, and does not over apologize. Note that I owe nobody a diagnosis, and that unknown return dates are better stated honestly than invented. Give me the message.
description: Write an out of office for illness without oversharing or inventing a return date.
useCase: Use this when you are unwell and need to communicate it. Saying you are unavailable without disclosing medical details is entirely reasonable, and stating honestly that you are not sure when you will be back beats inventing a return date you then miss.
variables: HOW LONG YOU EXPECT TO BE OUT, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Write an Out of Office When You Do Not Know When You Are Back
promptText: Help me write an out of office when I genuinely do not know when I will be back, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The situation is [WHAT IS GOING ON, AS MUCH AS YOU WANT TO SAY]. Write a message that handles the uncertainty honestly rather than inventing a date I will miss, tells people what to expect, gives them a way to decide whether to wait or go elsewhere, and commits to updating them. Note that an invented return date I then blow past damages trust more than admitting I do not know. Give me the message.
description: Write an out of office with an honest unknown return rather than an invented date.
useCase: Use this when the timeline is genuinely uncertain. Admitting you do not know beats inventing a date you then miss, and giving people enough to decide whether to wait or go elsewhere respects them more than leaving them hanging on a fiction.
variables: WHAT IS GOING ON, AS MUCH AS YOU WANT TO SAY

title: Write an Out of Office for Extended Leave
promptText: Help me write an out of office for an extended absence in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am away from [START] to [END], the reason is [REASON, E.G. PARENTAL LEAVE, SABBATICAL, EXTENDED TRAVEL, PERSONAL], and my clients are [WHO THEY ARE]. Write a message suited to a long absence rather than a week: acknowledging the length clearly, telling people what to do with work that cannot wait months, and being clear about whether the business continues or pauses. Note that a long absence needs different handling to a holiday, since people cannot simply wait it out. Give me the message.
description: Write an out of office for a long absence where waiting it out is not an option.
useCase: Use this for parental leave, a sabbatical, or extended travel. A long absence needs different handling to a week away because clients cannot simply wait, so being clear about whether the business pauses and what they should do meanwhile prevents them quietly finding someone else.
variables: REASON, E.G. PARENTAL LEAVE, SABBATICAL, EXTENDED TRAVEL, PERSONAL

title: Tell Clients About Time Off in Advance
promptText: Help me tell my clients in advance that I will be away, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am away [DATES], my active clients are [WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT IS IN PROGRESS], and what I will do to prepare is [ANY PREP, E.G. DELIVERING EARLY, PAUSING WORK]. Write a heads up message that gives them fair notice, explains how their work is affected, asks for anything I need from them before I go, and reassures them nothing will fall through. Note that an out of office is not enough on its own, since a client discovering my absence from an auto reply feels blindsided. Give me the advance notice message.
description: Give clients advance notice of time off so they are not blindsided by an auto reply.
useCase: Use this before any significant absence. An auto reply alone is not enough, since a client discovering you are gone from an automated message feels blindsided, whereas advance notice lets them plan and signals you have thought about their work.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHAT IS IN PROGRESS
ANY PREP, E.G. DELIVERING EARLY, PAUSING WORK

title: Prepare Your Business Before Time Off
promptText: Help me prepare my [BUSINESS TYPE] before I take time off. I am away [DATES], my active work is [WHAT IS IN PROGRESS], and my worries are [WHAT YOU ARE AFRAID WILL BREAK]. Build me a checklist of what to handle before I go: work to deliver or pause, clients to notify, invoices to send, auto replies to set, deadlines to move, and anything that would become a problem in my absence. Note that the anxiety of being away usually comes from things left unhandled rather than from the absence itself. Give me a pre departure checklist.
description: Build a checklist of what to handle before taking time off.
useCase: Use this before any break. The anxiety of being away usually comes from loose ends rather than the absence itself, so working through what to deliver, notify, invoice, and pause beforehand is what lets you actually switch off rather than checking email from a beach.
variables: WHAT YOU ARE AFRAID WILL BREAK

title: Set Expectations for When You Return
promptText: Help me set realistic expectations in my out of office for what happens when I am back, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am away [HOW LONG], and the backlog I expect is [WHAT YOU EXPECT TO COME BACK TO]. Help me write wording that does not promise I will reply to everything the moment I return, since I will have a full inbox and existing work, but instead sets a realistic window and tells people what to do if they need a faster answer. Note that promising an immediate reply on return is a promise I will break on day one. Give me the return expectation wording.
description: Set realistic reply expectations for your return rather than promising instant catch up.
useCase: Use this so you do not return to an impossible promise. Saying you will reply immediately on your return is a commitment you break on day one, whereas a realistic window means people wait calmly instead of chasing you the morning you get back.
variables: WHAT YOU EXPECT TO COME BACK TO

title: Plan Your First Day Back
promptText: Help me plan my first day back after time away from my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I was away [HOW LONG], what waits for me is [WHAT YOU EXPECT], and my instinct is [WHAT YOU NORMALLY DO, E.G. PANIC AND ANSWER EVERYTHING]. Help me plan a sane first day: what to tackle first, how to triage rather than answering chronologically, what can wait, and how to avoid burning the benefit of the break in a single frantic day. Note that returning and immediately drowning undoes the rest, and that most of the inbox is not as urgent as it looks. Give me a first day back plan.
description: Plan a sane first day back so you do not undo the break in a single frantic day.
useCase: Use this before returning from time off. Triaging rather than answering chronologically protects the benefit of the break, since coming back and immediately drowning undoes the rest, and most of what accumulated is far less urgent than it looks at first glance.
variables: WHAT YOU NORMALLY DO, E.G. PANIC AND ANSWER EVERYTHING

title: Triage the Inbox You Came Back To
promptText: Help me triage the backlog I came back to in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What is waiting is [DESCRIBE THE PILE, E.G. NUMBER OF EMAILS, TYPES, ANYTHING URGENT YOU KNOW OF]. Help me sort it: what needs a real reply, what needs a holding message, what resolved itself while I was away, what can be deleted or ignored entirely, and the order to work through it. Note that a meaningful share of any backlog resolves itself or stops mattering, so answering everything chronologically is the slowest possible approach. Give me a triage plan.
description: Triage a post absence backlog instead of grinding through it chronologically.
useCase: Use this on your first day back facing a full inbox. Sorting by what actually needs a reply rather than working chronologically is far faster, since a meaningful share of any backlog resolved itself or stopped mattering while you were gone.
variables: DESCRIBE THE PILE, E.G. NUMBER OF EMAILS, TYPES, ANYTHING URGENT YOU KNOW OF

title: Reply to Something That Sat While You Were Away
promptText: Help me reply to a message that sat unanswered while I was away, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is their message: [PASTE IT], how long it sat is [HOW LONG], and my response is [YOUR ANSWER OR POSITION]. Write a reply that acknowledges the delay briefly without grovelling, gets straight to what they asked, and does not use my absence as an excuse for a bad answer. Note that a short acknowledgment beats a paragraph of apology, and that people mostly just want the answer. Give me the reply.
description: Reply to a delayed message with a brief acknowledgment rather than an apology paragraph.
useCase: Use this when catching up on messages that waited. A brief acknowledgment of the delay followed by the actual answer serves people better than a paragraph of apology, since what they have been waiting for is the response, not your remorse.

title: Send a Holding Reply Before a Real One
promptText: Help me write a holding reply for something I cannot answer properly yet, in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The message is about [WHAT THEY ASKED], and when I can actually respond properly is [WHEN]. Write a short holding reply that confirms I have seen it, tells them when a real answer is coming, and closes the loop enough that they stop wondering. Keep it very brief. Note that a ten second holding reply prevents a week of them wondering whether I got it, and costs almost nothing. Give me the holding reply.
description: Send a quick holding reply so people stop wondering whether you received it.
useCase: Use this when you cannot answer properly yet. A ten second holding reply confirming receipt and naming when a real answer is coming costs almost nothing and prevents a week of them wondering whether their message reached you at all.

title: Write an Auto Reply for New Inquiries
promptText: Help me write an auto reply for new inquiries in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [WHAT YOU OFFER], my typical response time is [HOW FAST YOU REALLY REPLY], and what would help me most is [WHAT INFO YOU NEED FROM THEM UPFRONT]. Write an auto reply that confirms I got their message, sets a realistic response time, and usefully asks for the information I will need anyway so the next exchange is faster. Note that an auto reply is the first impression a prospect gets, so it should feel human rather than robotic. Give me the inquiry auto reply.
description: Write an inquiry auto reply that confirms receipt and gathers what you need upfront.
useCase: Use this on your contact form or inbox. It is the first impression a prospect gets, so making it feel human while gathering the information you will need anyway saves a round trip and reassures someone who just reached out to a stranger.
variables: WHAT INFO YOU NEED FROM THEM UPFRONT

title: Set a Realistic Response Time
promptText: Help me decide what response time to promise in my auto replies for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. How fast I actually reply is [YOUR HONEST TYPICAL SPEED], my worst case is [YOUR SLOWEST], and my clients expect [WHAT THEY SEEM TO EXPECT]. Help me pick a promise I can consistently keep rather than my best case, since a promise I break routinely is worse than a slower one I always meet. Then write the wording. Note that under promising and over delivering beats the reverse, and that most people are fine waiting if they know how long. Give me a response time and the wording.
description: Pick a response time promise you can actually keep every time.
useCase: Use this before publishing any response time. Promising your best case means routinely breaking it, whereas a slightly slower promise you always meet builds trust, and most people are entirely fine waiting as long as they know how long.

title: Write a Form Submission Confirmation
promptText: Help me write a confirmation message for when someone submits a form on my [BUSINESS TYPE] site. The form is for [WHAT THE FORM IS FOR], and what happens next is [YOUR NEXT STEP AND TIMING]. Write a confirmation that reassures them it went through, tells them exactly what happens next and when, and sounds like a person rather than a system notification. Note that a vague thanks for your submission leaves people wondering if it worked, and that this is often the first thing they read from me. Give me the confirmation message.
description: Write a form confirmation that tells people exactly what happens next.
useCase: Use this for any form on your site. A vague thanks for your submission leaves people wondering whether it worked, whereas confirming receipt and naming what happens next and when reassures someone who just handed their details to a stranger.

title: Write an After Hours Auto Reply
promptText: Help me write an after hours auto reply for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My working hours are [YOUR HOURS AND TIMEZONE], my clients are [WHO THEY ARE AND WHERE], and the problem I am trying to solve is [YOUR ISSUE, E.G. PEOPLE EXPECTING NIGHT REPLIES, TIMEZONE CONFUSION]. Write an auto reply that states my hours clearly, sets the expectation of a reply during them, and does so without sounding rigid or unhelpful. Note that replying at midnight once teaches people to expect it, and an after hours auto reply is how I stop that pattern before it starts. Give me the after hours reply.
description: Write an after hours auto reply that establishes your working hours.
useCase: Use this if clients expect replies at all hours. An after hours auto reply establishes your working hours before the expectation forms, which matters because replying at midnight once teaches people that midnight replies are available, and that pattern is hard to reverse.
variables: YOUR ISSUE, E.G. PEOPLE EXPECTING NIGHT REPLIES, TIMEZONE CONFUSION

title: Use Auto Replies to Hold a Boundary
promptText: Help me use auto replies to hold a boundary in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The boundary I want to hold is [WHAT YOU WANT, E.G. NO WEEKEND WORK, DEFINED HOURS, SLOWER REPLIES, FOCUS BLOCKS], and what keeps happening is [HOW IT GETS VIOLATED]. Help me use an auto reply to make the boundary visible and automatic rather than something I have to defend in every conversation. Write the wording, and note where an auto reply is the wrong tool and I need an actual conversation instead. Note that automation states a boundary without me having to say no repeatedly. Give me the auto reply and honest advice on its limits.
description: Use auto replies to make a boundary automatic rather than defended conversation by conversation.
useCase: Use this to stop defending the same boundary repeatedly. An auto reply states it automatically without you saying no each time, though knowing when automation is the wrong tool matters too, since some boundaries need an actual conversation rather than a message the person barely reads.
variables: WHAT YOU WANT, E.G. NO WEEKEND WORK, DEFINED HOURS, SLOWER REPLIES, FOCUS BLOCKS

title: Handle Timezone Gaps in Auto Replies
promptText: Help me handle timezone differences in my auto replies for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am in [YOUR LOCATION AND TIMEZONE], my clients are mostly in [THEIR LOCATIONS], and the friction this causes is [THE PROBLEM, E.G. THEY EXPECT REPLIES WHILE YOU SLEEP, OVERLAP IS TINY]. Write auto reply wording that makes the timezone gap clear and workable rather than a source of frustration, sets expectations about overlap and reply timing, and frames it as a fact of the arrangement rather than an apology. Note that clients working with someone in another timezone knew that when they hired me. Give me the wording.
description: Make a timezone gap clear and workable rather than a source of friction.
useCase: Use this when you work across timezones from your clients. Making the gap explicit and setting expectations about overlap turns it into a workable fact rather than a recurring frustration, and framing it without apology is fair since clients knew where you were when they hired you.
variables: THE PROBLEM, E.G. THEY EXPECT REPLIES WHILE YOU SLEEP, OVERLAP IS TINY

title: Write an Auto Reply When You Are at Capacity
promptText: Help me write an auto reply for when I am fully booked in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My situation is [YOUR CAPACITY, E.G. BOOKED FOR MONTHS, NOT TAKING NEW WORK], when I next have space is [WHEN, OR WRITE NOT SURE], and what I want to happen with new inquiries is [WAITLIST, REFER OUT, OR TURN AWAY]. Write an auto reply that is honest about my capacity without sounding like I am brushing people off, offers a waitlist or referral where appropriate, and leaves a good impression so they come back later. Note that being booked is a good signal, and handling it well means they wait rather than forget me. Give me the capacity auto reply.
description: Write an auto reply for being fully booked that keeps people warm rather than losing them.
useCase: Use this when you are at capacity and inquiries keep arriving. Being honest while offering a waitlist or referral leaves a good impression, which matters because handling a full book well means people wait for you or return later, rather than writing you off.
variables: YOUR CAPACITY, E.G. BOOKED FOR MONTHS, NOT TAKING NEW WORK
WAITLIST, REFER OUT, OR TURN AWAY

title: Announce a Holiday Closure
promptText: Help me announce a holiday closure for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. I am closed [DATES], the occasion is [THE HOLIDAY OR REASON], my clients are [WHO THEY ARE AND WHERE, SINCE HOLIDAYS DIFFER BY COUNTRY], and what still happens during the closure is [ANYTHING CONTINUING, OR WRITE NOTHING]. Write both an advance announcement and an auto reply for the closure period, warm and clear about dates, noting anything clients need to do before I close. Note that clients elsewhere may not share the holiday, so it needs stating rather than assuming. Give me both messages.
description: Announce a holiday closure with advance notice and a closure period auto reply.
useCase: Use this before closing for a holiday. Giving advance notice plus an auto reply covers both the clients who plan ahead and those who message anyway, and stating the dates explicitly matters since clients in other countries may not share or even know about the holiday.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND WHERE, SINCE HOLIDAYS DIFFER BY COUNTRY

title: Make an Auto Reply Sound Like a Person
promptText: Help me make my auto reply sound human rather than robotic in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is my current version: [PASTE IT], and my brand voice is [YOUR VOICE]. Rewrite it to sound like a person wrote it: cutting the corporate filler like your message is important to us, keeping the useful information intact, and letting some personality through without being try hard. Note that an auto reply is often the only thing a prospect reads before deciding whether I am worth their time, so sounding like a form letter costs me. Give me the humanized version.
description: Rewrite a robotic auto reply to sound like an actual person wrote it.
useCase: Use this on any auto reply that reads like a template. Cutting the corporate filler while keeping the information matters because an auto reply is often the only thing a prospect reads before judging you, and sounding like a form letter tells them exactly nothing worth knowing.

title: Audit All Your Auto Replies
promptText: Help me audit the automated messages going out from my [BUSINESS TYPE], since I have lost track of them. The ones I know about are [LIST WHAT YOU KNOW OF, E.G. FORM CONFIRMATIONS, BOOKING CONFIRMATIONS, OUT OF OFFICE, EMAIL SEQUENCES]. Help me work out: what automated messages are likely going out that I have forgotten, which are stale or wrong now, which contradict each other, and which say things that are no longer true. Note that automated messages keep sending long after the information in them expires, and I am usually the last to notice. Give me an audit checklist and what to look for.
description: Audit the automated messages still sending on your behalf, including forgotten ones.
useCase: Use this if you have accumulated automations over time. Automated messages keep sending long after their information expires and you are usually the last to notice, so an audit catches the confirmation still promising a response time you abandoned a year ago.
variables: LIST WHAT YOU KNOW OF, E.G. FORM CONFIRMATIONS, BOOKING CONFIRMATIONS, OUT OF OFFICE, EMAIL SEQUENCES

title: Take Time Off Without the Guilt
promptText: Help me take time off without feeling guilty about it in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What stops me is [YOUR HESITATION, E.G. FEAR OF LOSING CLIENTS, GUILT, FEELING IRREPLACEABLE, WORRYING IT LOOKS UNPROFESSIONAL], and how long it has been since a real break is [HOW LONG]. Help me honestly: examine whether the fear matches reality, since clients take holidays too and rarely mind, weigh what never stopping is actually costing me, and find the smallest break I could take. Be honest rather than just reassuring. Note that a business that cannot survive my absence for a week is a problem worth naming, not a badge. Give me an honest read and a first step.
description: Examine the guilt that stops you taking time off, honestly.
useCase: Use this if you have not had a real break in a long time. Examining whether the fear matches reality helps, since clients take holidays themselves and rarely mind, and a business that cannot survive a week without you is a structural problem worth naming rather than a work ethic to be proud of.
variables: YOUR HESITATION, E.G. FEAR OF LOSING CLIENTS, GUILT, FEELING IRREPLACEABLE, WORRYING IT LOOKS UNPROFESSIONAL

title: Get a Quick Out of Office Fast
promptText: I need a quick out of office message. I am away [DATES], the reason is [REASON, OR LEAVE BLANK], and people should [WHAT THEY SHOULD DO MEANWHILE]. Give me a short clear out of office that states my return date, sets a realistic reply expectation, and sounds human. Keep it brief, no corporate filler. Just give me an out of office I can set right now.
description: Get a fast, human out of office message you can set immediately.
useCase: Use this when you are leaving shortly and need the message set now. A short clear one covering your return date and reply expectations is all most absences need, which matters because the out of office you never set because you were busy leaving helps nobody.

title: Find Keywords Worth Targeting
promptText: Help me find keywords worth targeting for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I do is [YOUR SERVICE OR PRODUCT], my customers are [WHO THEY ARE], and where I operate is [YOUR LOCATION OR MARKET, OR WRITE ONLINE ONLY]. Help me build a starting list: the terms my customers would actually type when looking for what I offer, grouped by what they are trying to accomplish. Focus on realistic terms a small site could rank for rather than the huge competitive ones. Note that I cannot verify search volume, so treat this as a hypothesis list I should check in a keyword tool before committing. Give me the keyword list grouped by intent.
description: Build a starting keyword list grouped by what searchers are trying to accomplish.
useCase: Use this to get a first keyword list when you have no idea where to start. Grouping by intent rather than volume gives you a hypothesis list to check in a real tool, and being told upfront that volume needs verifying prevents you building a strategy on guessed numbers.
variables: YOUR LOCATION OR MARKET, OR WRITE ONLINE ONLY

title: Work Out What a Searcher Actually Wants
promptText: Help me understand the intent behind keywords for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My keywords are [PASTE YOUR KEYWORD LIST], and what I sell is [YOUR OFFER]. For each keyword, tell me what the person is likely trying to do: learn something, compare options, find a specific site, or buy right now. Then tell me which ones are worth my effort given that I want [YOUR GOAL, E.G. SALES, EMAIL SIGNUPS, TRAFFIC]. Note that ranking for a term whose searchers do not want what I sell produces traffic that never converts. Give me the intent breakdown and which to prioritize.
description: Classify keyword intent so you do not chase traffic that never converts.
useCase: Use this before committing to a keyword list. Understanding whether searchers want to learn, compare, or buy prevents the common mistake of ranking for a high volume term whose searchers had no intention of buying anything, which produces traffic that looks good and does nothing.
variables: YOUR GOAL, E.G. SALES, EMAIL SIGNUPS, TRAFFIC

title: Find Long Tail Keywords You Can Actually Win
promptText: Help me find long tail keywords for my [BUSINESS TYPE] that a small site could realistically rank for. My broad topic is [YOUR MAIN TOPIC], and my niche or angle is [WHAT MAKES YOU SPECIFIC]. Generate longer, more specific phrases where competition is thinner: question phrasings, problem specific wording, and terms with qualifiers. Explain why each is more winnable than the broad term. Note that lower volume terms that convert beat high volume terms I will never rank for. Give me the long tail list and why each is worth trying.
description: Find longer, more specific keywords a small site can realistically rank for.
useCase: Use this when the obvious keywords in your niche are dominated by big sites. Longer specific phrases have thinner competition and often better intent, which matters because a small site ranking for a modest term that converts beats endlessly chasing a head term it will never reach.

title: Find the Questions People Ask
promptText: Help me find the questions people ask about my topic in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My topic is [YOUR TOPIC], and my audience is [WHO THEY ARE AND HOW MUCH THEY KNOW]. Generate the real questions someone would type or ask an AI about this: beginner confusions, comparison questions, how to questions, and the awkward questions they might not ask a person. Group them by where the person is in their thinking. Note that question keywords often have clear intent and are easier to answer well than broad terms. Give me the questions grouped by stage.
description: Find the real questions people ask about your topic, grouped by their stage.
useCase: Use this to build content around what people actually want to know. Question keywords carry clear intent and are easier to answer thoroughly than broad terms, and grouping them by stage shows you which ones attract someone ready to buy versus someone just starting to look.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND HOW MUCH THEY KNOW

title: Check Whether You Can Actually Rank
promptText: Help me judge honestly whether I can rank for a keyword in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The keyword is [YOUR TARGET KEYWORD], my site is [DESCRIBE YOUR SITE, ITS AGE, SIZE, AND ANY AUTHORITY], and who currently ranks is [WHO IS ON PAGE ONE, IF YOU LOOKED]. Help me assess: whether this is realistic for a site like mine or a fantasy, what would need to be true for me to compete, and whether my effort is better spent elsewhere. Be honest rather than encouraging. Note that a new small site chasing terms owned by established brands wastes months. Give me an honest assessment.
description: Honestly assess whether your site can realistically rank for a target keyword.
useCase: Use this before investing months in a keyword. An honest read on whether a small site can compete with whoever currently owns page one saves you from the most common SEO mistake, which is spending a year producing content aimed at terms that were never winnable.
variables: DESCRIBE YOUR SITE, ITS AGE, SIZE, AND ANY AUTHORITY
WHO IS ON PAGE ONE, IF YOU LOOKED

title: Find Keywords Your Competitors Missed
promptText: Help me find keyword gaps my competitors have left open in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My competitors are [WHO THEY ARE], what they cover well is [WHAT THEY RANK FOR OR WRITE ABOUT], and my angle is [WHAT YOU DO DIFFERENTLY OR KNOW BETTER]. Help me identify: topics they neglect, angles they handle badly or generically, audiences they ignore, and where my specific perspective could win. Note that competing head on with an established site is harder than finding what they overlooked. Give me the gaps and why each is an opening.
description: Find the topics and angles competitors have neglected or handled badly.
useCase: Use this instead of trying to outrank competitors on their strongest terms. Finding what they neglect or handle generically is a far easier path than competing head on, since an established site is hard to displace where it is strong and easy to beat where it never bothered.
variables: WHAT THEY RANK FOR OR WRITE ABOUT
WHAT YOU DO DIFFERENTLY OR KNOW BETTER

title: Narrow Your Keywords to Something Winnable
promptText: Help me narrow my keyword focus to something I can actually win in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My broad topic is [THE BROAD TOPIC], and what makes me or my customers specific is [YOUR SPECIFICITY, E.G. LOCATION, INDUSTRY, CUSTOMER TYPE, APPROACH]. Help me narrow the broad term into specific variations where I have a real shot: by location, by audience, by use case, by problem. Then tell me which narrow slice to own first. Note that owning a small niche completely beats being invisible in a large one. Give me the narrowed keyword territory.
description: Narrow a broad topic into a specific slice you can realistically own.
useCase: Use this when your topic is too broad to compete in. Narrowing by location, audience, or use case gives you a slice you can genuinely own, which works because being the obvious answer in a small niche produces more business than being invisible on page nine of a large one.
variables: YOUR SPECIFICITY, E.G. LOCATION, INDUSTRY, CUSTOMER TYPE, APPROACH

title: Find Local Search Keywords
promptText: Help me find local search keywords for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My service is [WHAT YOU DO], my area is [YOUR CITY, REGION, OR SERVICE AREA], and my customers are [WHO THEY ARE]. Help me build a local keyword list: service plus location combinations, neighbourhood or suburb variations, near me style phrasing, and the local terms people actually use rather than official place names. Note that local search is often far less competitive than national terms and the searchers are ready to act. Give me the local keyword list.
description: Build a local keyword list using the place names people actually use.
useCase: Use this if you serve a specific area. Local terms are far less competitive than national ones and the people searching them are usually ready to act, and using the informal place names locals actually type beats the official ones nobody says out loud.
variables: YOUR CITY, REGION, OR SERVICE AREA

title: Find Keywords That Bring Buyers
promptText: Help me find keywords that attract people ready to buy in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I sell is [YOUR OFFER], my price point is [ROUGH PRICE], and my buyer is [WHO ACTUALLY PAYS]. Help me identify the terms someone types when they are close to purchasing rather than just researching: comparison terms, pricing terms, hiring or buying language, and problem terms where they want it solved now. Note that a handful of buyer intent terms can be worth more than thousands of informational visitors. Give me the commercial keywords and why each signals readiness.
description: Find the keywords people type when they are close to buying rather than researching.
useCase: Use this to prioritize traffic that converts. A handful of buyer intent terms is worth more than thousands of informational visitors, and knowing which language signals someone is close to purchasing tells you where to spend your limited content effort first.

title: Decide Which Keywords to Do First
promptText: Help me prioritize my keyword list for my [BUSINESS TYPE], since I cannot do them all. My keywords are [PASTE YOUR LIST], my site is [DESCRIBE ITS AGE AND AUTHORITY], and my capacity is [HOW MUCH CONTENT YOU CAN REALISTICALLY PRODUCE]. Help me rank them by what to tackle first, weighing how winnable each is against how valuable the traffic would be. Tell me which to do now, which to hold for later once I have authority, and which to drop entirely. Note that doing five keywords properly beats doing thirty badly. Give me the prioritized order and reasoning.
description: Prioritize a keyword list by winnability and value, given your real capacity.
useCase: Use this when your keyword list is longer than your capacity. Ranking by winnability against value tells you where to start, and being told what to drop matters because doing five keywords properly beats spreading yourself across thirty and ranking for none.
variables: HOW MUCH CONTENT YOU CAN REALISTICALLY PRODUCE

title: Map Keywords to Pages
promptText: Help me map my keywords to actual pages for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My keywords are [PASTE YOUR LIST], and my existing pages are [LIST YOUR PAGES, OR WRITE STARTING FROM SCRATCH]. Help me work out: which keywords belong together on one page because they are the same intent, which need their own page, which existing pages could target which terms, and what new pages I actually need. Note that one page per keyword creates a bloated site of thin pages, while cramming unrelated terms onto one page ranks for none of them. Give me the keyword to page map.
description: Map keywords to pages, grouping same intent terms and separating different ones.
useCase: Use this before writing anything. Grouping same intent keywords onto one page and separating genuinely different ones prevents both failure modes, since a page per keyword bloats your site with thin content while cramming unrelated terms together ranks for none of them.
variables: LIST YOUR PAGES, OR WRITE STARTING FROM SCRATCH

title: Build a Topic Cluster Around a Keyword
promptText: Help me build a topic cluster around a main keyword for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My main topic is [YOUR CORE TOPIC], and my audience is [WHO THEY ARE]. Help me plan: the central page covering the topic broadly, the supporting pages covering specific sub questions in depth, how they would link to each other, and the order to build them in. Note that a cluster of connected pages on one topic tends to outperform scattered unrelated posts, because it signals depth on a subject. Give me the cluster structure and build order.
description: Plan a connected cluster of pages around one topic rather than scattered posts.
useCase: Use this to build depth on a topic instead of publishing scattered posts. A connected cluster covering one subject thoroughly tends to outperform unrelated one off articles, since it signals genuine depth rather than a site that touches everything and covers nothing.

title: Check Whether Your Pages Compete with Each Other
promptText: Help me check whether my own pages are competing with each other in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My pages and their topics are [LIST YOUR PAGES AND WHAT EACH TARGETS]. Help me spot: pages targeting the same intent that should be merged, pages so similar that search engines cannot tell which to rank, and where I have accidentally split one topic across several thin pages. Then tell me what to merge, keep, or redirect. Note that two mediocre pages on the same topic usually rank worse than one good one. Give me the overlaps and what to do.
description: Spot pages on your own site competing for the same intent.
useCase: Use this if you have published a lot over time. Pages targeting the same intent split your effort and confuse search engines about which to rank, and merging them into one strong page usually outperforms leaving two mediocre ones to compete with each other.
variables: LIST YOUR PAGES AND WHAT EACH TARGETS

title: Find Keywords in Content You Already Have
promptText: Help me find keyword opportunities in content I have already published on my [BUSINESS TYPE] site. My existing content is [LIST OR DESCRIBE YOUR PAGES AND POSTS]. Help me identify: pages that are close to targeting something valuable but not quite, posts that could be improved to rank rather than replaced, topics I have covered accidentally that deserve proper treatment, and where a small edit could unlock more than a new page would. Note that improving existing content is usually faster than writing new content from scratch. Give me the opportunities and what to change.
description: Find keyword opportunities hiding in content you have already published.
useCase: Use this before writing anything new. Improving an existing page that nearly ranks is usually faster and more effective than starting from scratch, and most sites have posts sitting at the bottom of page one that a small edit could lift.
variables: LIST OR DESCRIBE YOUR PAGES AND POSTS

title: Use the Words Your Customers Actually Use
promptText: Help me find the words my customers actually use rather than my industry jargon, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. What I call my service is [YOUR INDUSTRY TERM], what it actually does for people is [THE REAL BENEFIT], and my customers are [WHO THEY ARE AND HOW MUCH THEY KNOW]. Help me identify: the plain language people would search for this, the wrong or informal terms they might use, and where my professional vocabulary is invisible to the people looking for me. Note that customers search for their problem in their words, not my job title. Give me the customer language list.
description: Find the plain language customers search for instead of your industry jargon.
useCase: Use this if you suspect nobody searches for what you call yourself. Customers search their problem in their own words rather than your professional vocabulary, which means the industry term on your homepage may be invisible to exactly the people looking for what you do.
variables: WHO THEY ARE AND HOW MUCH THEY KNOW

title: Plan for Seasonal Search Demand
promptText: Help me plan around seasonal search demand in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My business is [WHAT YOU DO], and my busy and quiet periods are [YOUR SEASONALITY, OR WRITE NOT SURE]. Help me think through: which of my keywords likely spike at certain times, how far ahead I need to publish for content to be ranking when demand arrives, and what to work on during quiet periods. Note that publishing seasonal content when the season starts is too late, since ranking takes time. Also note I should verify seasonality in a real tool. Give me a seasonal plan.
description: Plan seasonal content early enough that it ranks before demand arrives.
useCase: Use this if your business has busy and quiet periods. Publishing seasonal content when the season starts is too late because ranking takes time, so working out how far ahead to publish is what separates content that catches the wave from content that arrives after it passed.
variables: YOUR SEASONALITY, OR WRITE NOT SURE

title: Make Sense of Keyword Tool Data
promptText: Help me interpret the keyword data I pulled from a tool for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. Here is the data: [PASTE YOUR KEYWORD DATA WITH VOLUME AND DIFFICULTY], and my site is [DESCRIBE ITS AUTHORITY]. Help me read it properly: what the volume and difficulty numbers actually mean for a site like mine, which terms are worth it despite low volume, which high volume terms are traps, and what the numbers do not tell me. Note that difficulty scores are estimates and vary between tools, so they inform a decision rather than making it. Give me an interpretation and what to target.
description: Interpret keyword tool volume and difficulty data for a site like yours.
useCase: Use this when a keyword tool hands you numbers you are not sure how to read. Understanding what volume and difficulty actually mean for your site, and which high volume terms are traps, turns raw data into a decision, though the scores are estimates rather than facts.
variables: PASTE YOUR KEYWORD DATA WITH VOLUME AND DIFFICULTY

title: Do Keyword Research Without Paid Tools
promptText: Help me do keyword research without paying for tools, for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My topic is [YOUR TOPIC], and my budget is [YOUR BUDGET, OR WRITE ZERO]. Help me find what I can learn from free sources: search suggestions and related searches, what competitors are visibly targeting, questions in forums and communities where my audience gathers, my own customer emails and inquiries, and any free tiers worth using. Note that free research is slower and less precise but genuinely workable, and my own customer conversations are a better source than most people realize. Give me a free research approach.
description: Do workable keyword research using free sources rather than paid tools.
useCase: Use this when paid keyword tools are not in the budget. Free research from search suggestions, competitor pages, and community questions is slower but genuinely workable, and your own customer inquiries are a better source of real search language than most paid tools provide.

title: Read the Search Results Before You Write
promptText: Help me understand what a search results page is telling me before I write for a keyword in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My keyword is [YOUR KEYWORD], and what I see on page one is [DESCRIBE WHAT IS RANKING, THE FORMATS, AND WHO OWNS THEM]. Help me read it: what format search engines seem to reward for this term, what the ranking pages have in common, whether the intent matches what I want to publish, and whether there is room for me. Note that the current page one is the clearest available evidence of what will rank. Give me what the results are telling me.
description: Read what page one is telling you about format, intent, and whether there is room.
useCase: Use this before writing for any keyword. The current page one is the clearest evidence available of what search engines reward for that term, and checking whether the ranking format matches what you planned to write prevents producing something that was never going to fit.
variables: DESCRIBE WHAT IS RANKING, THE FORMATS, AND WHO OWNS THEM

title: Think About Keywords in an AI Search World
promptText: Help me think about how AI answers change my keyword approach in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My topic is [YOUR TOPIC], and my current keyword approach is [WHAT YOU DO NOW]. Help me think through: which of my keywords are the kind of simple question an AI answers directly without a click, which need depth or judgment that keeps people clicking through, and how to weight my effort accordingly. Be honest that this is a shifting area with no settled answers. Note that content whose whole value is a fact anyone can state is more exposed than content with genuine depth. Give me an honest read.
description: Think through which keywords are exposed to AI answers and which are not.
useCase: Use this to think about where search is heading. Simple factual queries increasingly get answered without a click, so weighting effort toward topics needing depth or judgment is a reasonable hedge, though this is a shifting area and anyone claiming certainty about it is guessing.

title: Set Realistic Expectations for SEO
promptText: Help me set realistic expectations for what SEO can do for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My site is [DESCRIBE IT AND ITS AGE], my capacity is [WHAT YOU CAN ACTUALLY PUT IN], and what I am hoping for is [YOUR HOPE]. Be honest with me about: how long this realistically takes for a site like mine, what results are plausible versus fantasy, whether SEO is even the right channel for my business, and what would need to be true for it to work. Do not encourage me if the honest answer is that my effort is better spent elsewhere. Give me an honest assessment.
description: Get an honest assessment of what SEO can realistically do for your business.
useCase: Use this before committing to SEO as a channel. An honest read on the timeline and whether it suits your business matters because SEO is slow and not right for everyone, and finding out a year in that your effort belonged elsewhere is an expensive way to learn it.
variables: WHAT YOU CAN ACTUALLY PUT IN

title: Decide What Keyword Results to Track
promptText: Help me decide what to track for my keywords in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My target keywords are [YOUR KEYWORDS], my goal is [WHAT YOU ACTUALLY WANT, E.G. LEADS, SALES, SIGNUPS], and what I track now is [WHAT YOU TRACK, OR WRITE NOTHING]. Help me pick the few things worth watching: what actually indicates progress, how often to check without obsessing, and what to ignore. Note that rankings move constantly and checking daily tells me nothing but wrecks my week, and that traffic which never converts is not a win. Give me what to track and how often.
description: Pick the few keyword metrics worth tracking, and how often to check them.
useCase: Use this to avoid obsessing over rankings. Picking a few meaningful indicators and a sensible checking rhythm keeps you sane, since rankings fluctuate constantly and daily checking tells you nothing useful while quietly consuming your attention.
variables: WHAT YOU ACTUALLY WANT, E.G. LEADS, SALES, SIGNUPS
WHAT YOU TRACK, OR WRITE NOTHING

title: Work Out Why a Page Is Not Ranking
promptText: Help me work out why a page is not ranking in my [BUSINESS TYPE]. The page is about [THE TOPIC], the keyword is [YOUR TARGET], it has been live [HOW LONG], and what is happening is [WHAT YOU SEE, E.G. NO TRAFFIC, PAGE FIVE, RANKING FOR THE WRONG TERM]. Help me diagnose the likely causes: whether the keyword was too competitive, whether the intent mismatches, whether it needs more time, whether the content is thinner than what ranks, or whether something technical is wrong. Note that not enough time is the most common answer and the hardest to accept. Give me the likely causes and what to check.
description: Diagnose why a page is not ranking, including the possibility it just needs time.
useCase: Use this when a page you had hopes for is going nowhere. Working through whether the keyword was too competitive, the intent mismatched, or the content is simply thinner than what ranks helps, though the most common answer is that not enough time has passed.
variables: WHAT YOU SEE, E.G. NO TRAFFIC, PAGE FIVE, RANKING FOR THE WRONG TERM

title: Get a Quick Keyword List
promptText: I need a quick keyword list. My business is [BUSINESS TYPE], I do [WHAT YOU DO], and my customers are [WHO THEY ARE]. Give me a fast list of realistic search terms people would use to find what I offer, favouring specific winnable phrases over broad competitive ones. Keep it practical, no explanation of SEO theory. Note these are guesses I should verify in a tool. Just give me a starting keyword list.
description: Get a fast starting keyword list of realistic, specific search terms.
useCase: Use this when you just need a keyword list to start from. A quick set of specific realistic terms beats staring at a blank page, and knowing upfront that they are guesses to verify keeps you from treating a first draft list as a finished strategy.

title: Handle Searches for Your Own Name
promptText: Help me handle branded searches for my [BUSINESS TYPE]. My business name is [YOUR NAME], and the situation is [ANY ISSUE, E.G. A COMMON NAME, SOMEONE ELSE RANKS FOR IT, NOBODY SEARCHES IT YET]. Help me think through: whether people searching my name find me, what else ranks for it and whether that is a problem, what people searching my name are actually looking for, and how to make sure the right things appear. Note that branded searches are usually my highest converting traffic since those people already know who I am. Give me what to check and fix.
description: Make sure people searching your business name actually find you.
useCase: Use this to check that your own name leads to you. Branded searches are usually the highest converting traffic a business gets because those people already decided to look you up, so anything competing for your name is intercepting customers at the last step.
variables: ANY ISSUE, E.G. A COMMON NAME, SOMEONE ELSE RANKS FOR IT, NOBODY SEARCHES IT YET
